TL;DR: PriOrGen is a novel framework for automated pathology report generation from Whole Slide Images that addresses long-tail biases in multi-organ scenarios using a Visual-Prototype Anchored Bottleneck module and a Meta-Report Anchored Bank module, achieving superior performance across both head and tail organ categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pathology report generation methods are developed under single-organ settings, overlooking multi-organ clinical scenarios where organ types follow a long-tailed distribution. Two critical biases exist: visual representation bias favoring head-class patterns, and textual decoding bias overfitting to head-class narrative patterns.

Method: Two modules: (1) Visual-Prototype Anchored Bottleneck - uses information bottleneck principle with learnable anchor representations to selectively retain diagnostically relevant visual information while filtering out head-biased redundancy; (2) Meta-Report Anchored Bank - constructs organ-specific meta-report anchored bank and retrieves organ-faithful textual priors to steer decoder away from head-class narrative patterns.

Result: On a multi-organ pathology dataset, the method effectively mitigates long-tail biases and achieves superior report generation performance across both head and tail organ categories compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The proposed PriOrGen framework successfully addresses long-tail distribution challenges in multi-organ pathology report generation, outperforming existing SOTA methods.

Abstract: Automated pathology report generation from Whole Slide Images (WSIs) has
  attracted increasing attention in digital pathology. However, existing methods
  are predominantly developed under single-organ settings, overlooking the
  multi-organ scenarios encountered in clinical practice, where organ types
  typically follow a long-tailed distribution. To address this gap, we identify
  two critical biases: (1) visual representation bias, where the encoder favors
  head-class patterns over tail-class discriminative features, and (2) textual
  decoding bias, where the decoder overfits to head-class narrative patterns,
  yielding diagnostically unreliable outputs for tail-class organs. To mitigate
  these two biases, we propose a novel Prior-anchored multi-Organ pathology
  report Generation framework (PriOrGen). Specifically, a Visual-Prototype
  Anchored Bottleneck module leverages the information bottleneck principle with
  learnable anchor representations to selectively retain diagnostically relevant
  visual information while filtering out head-biased redundancy. Secondly, a
  Meta-Report Anchored Bank module constructs an organ-specific meta-report
  anchored bank and retrieves organ-faithful textual priors to steer the decoder
  away from head-class narrative patterns. Extensive experiments on a multi-
  organ pathology dataset demonstrate that our method effectively mitigates
  long-tail biases and achieves superior report generation performance across
  both head and tail organ categories compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [55] [AnF-DiffPET: Anatomy- and Frequency-Guided Diffusion for PET/CT Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00509)
*Xuepeng Liu,Ruili Li,Zetong Liu,Renyiming Li,Yan Li,Yin Dai,Chao Li,Yueyang Teng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnF-DiffPET is an anatomy- and frequency-guided diffusion framework for low-dose PET denoising that uses CT guidance, multi-scale cross-transformers, and frequency-contrastive learning to outperform existing methods across four datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-dose PET images suffer from high noise and unreliable uptake quantification due to reduced injected activity or acquisition time. Existing diffusion-based denoising methods face challenges with insufficient anatomical guidance, unstable multi-scale feature propagation, and uncertain frequency domain uptake recovery.

Method: Proposes AnF-DiffPET with three key components: (1) Anatomical-Frequency Guidance (AFG) for CT-conditioned denoising, (2) Multi-Scale Cross-Transformer Reconstruction (MSCTR) for stable multi-scale feature propagation, and (3) Frequency-Contrastive Hard Mining (FCHM) to enhance frequency domain consistency.

Result: Evaluated on four PET/CT datasets, AnF-DiffPET achieves superior image fidelity, anatomical consistency, and quantitative fidelity compared to CNN-based, GAN-based, transformer-based, and other diffusion-based methods.

Conclusion: The proposed anatomy- and frequency-guided diffusion framework effectively addresses key challenges in LD-to-HD PET denoising, and the code/models will be publicly released.

Abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) provides essential functional information for disease assessment, however reducing injected activity or acquisition time produces low-dose (LD) PET with stronger count dependent noise and less reliable uptake quantification. Diffusion models offer a promising solution for PET denoising by progressively recovering high-dose (HD) PET images from LD inputs. However, LD-to-HD PET denoising is still challenging due to insufficient anatomical guidance, unstable multi-scale feature propagation, and uncertain frequency domain uptake recovery. We propose AnF-DiffPET, an anatomy- and frequency-guided diffusion framework for computed tomography (CT) conditioned LD PET denoising. The framework integrates Anatomical-Frequency Guidance (AFG), Multi-Scale Cross-Transformer Reconstruction (MSCTR), and Frequency-Contrastive Hard Mining (FCHM) to enhance anatomy aware feature modulation and frequency domain consistency during denoising. Experimental results across four PET/CT datasets show that the proposed method improves image fidelity, anatomical consistency, and quantitative fidelity over representative CNN-based, GAN-based, transformer-based, and diffusion-based methods. The code and trained models will be publicly released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [56] [Cross4D-JEPA: Dense Cross-modal Correspondence Distillation for 4D Point Cloud Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00514)
*Trung Thanh Nguyen,Hai Nguyen-Truong,Tu Vo,Hoang M. Truong,Tuan-Anh Vu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cross4D-JEPA distills frozen 2D/video foundation models into 4D point cloud encoders via dense cross-modal correspondence (mapping each 3D point to its projected teacher patch feature) and per-point latent matching, outperforming intra-modal and global cross-modal baselines on four benchmarks with improved label efficiency and transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 4D point cloud annotation is expensive, existing self-supervised methods are mostly intra-modal, and prior cross-modal approaches use only a single global embedding per clip, wasting rich per-patch semantics from 2D foundation models.

Method: A teacher-student framework where a frozen 2D/video foundation model (DINOv2 or V-JEPA 2) teaches a 4D point encoder via (1) dense cross-modal correspondence projecting each 3D point to teacher patch features and (2) a per-point latent matching objective without masking, negatives, or decoder.

Result: Outperforms intra-modal and global cross-modal baselines on MSR-Action3D, DeformingThings4D, NTU-RGB+D 60, and HOI4D; matches heavyweight published 4D methods with a 13x smaller encoder; improves label efficiency and full-label fine-tuning under same budget.

Conclusion: Dense per-point correspondence granularity (rather than teacher modality) drives improvement, demonstrating that richer cross-modal distillation enables more transferable and efficient 4D representations.

Abstract: Automatic understanding of dynamic 4D point clouds, the 3D-point sequences captured over time by depth sensors and LiDAR, is central to robotics and embodied perception. Yet annotating them densely is expensive, making self-supervised pretraining the natural route to transferable representations. Existing pretext tasks, however, are almost entirely intra-modal, and the few methods that transfer knowledge from 2D foundation models rely on a single global embedding per clip, discarding the rich per-patch semantics that these models compute. To address this gap, we propose Cross4D-JEPA, a teacher-student method that distills a frozen 2D foundation model, an image model DINOv2, or a video model V-JEPA 2, into a 4D point encoder. The proposed method combines (1) a dense cross-modal correspondence that maps every 3D point to the teacher patch feature it projects to, and (2) a per-point objective that trains the student to match these features in latent space with no masking, negatives, or decoder. We evaluate Cross4D-JEPA on four benchmarks, MSR-Action3D, DeformingThings4D, NTU-RGB+D 60, and HOI4D, against intra-modal and global cross-modal baselines. Experimental results show that, under a matched protocol, the proposed method consistently outperforms intra-modal and global cross-modal baselines across the four benchmarks and is competitive with heavier published 4D methods; further analysis attributes this gain primarily to the granularity of the correspondence rather than the teacher modality. Beyond recognition accuracy, the dense representation learned by Cross4D-JEPA transfers across domains, improves label efficiency, and improves full-label fine-tuning under the same training budget, while a 13x smaller encoder matches a heavyweight pooling backbone.

</details>


### [57] [ABot-M0.5: Unified Mobility-and-Manipulation World Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00678)
*Ronghan Chen,Yandan Yang,Zuojin Tang,Dongjie Huo,Tong Lin,Haoning Wu,Haoyun Liu,Yuzhi Chen,Lulu Zheng,Botai Yuan,Tianlun Li,Mingxin Wang,Dekang Qi,Bin Hu,Wei Mei,Yuze Xuan,Haolong Yang,Yanqing Zhu,Mu Xu,Zhiheng Ma,Xinyuan Chang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ABot-M0.5 proposes a World Action Model for mobile manipulation that achieves alignment at three levels—temporal granularity (via intermediate latent actions), action space (via dual-level MoT architecture), and train-test consistency (via dream-forcing)—outperforming prior methods on long-horizon and fine-grained tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA policies are reactive and lack world modeling, while current WAMs are misaligned with mobile manipulation's structure: coarse temporal granularity, entangled navigation-manipulation actions, and inconsistent training vs. inference conditions, leading to error accumulation and poor fine-grained control.

Method: Three alignment strategies: (1) intermediate latent actions bridging video latents and embodiment controls for temporal granularity; (2) dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers to disentangle navigation and manipulation action subspaces; (3) dream-forcing training that trains inverse dynamics on model-predicted videos to match autoregressive inference conditions.

Result: State-of-the-art results on challenging mobile manipulation benchmarks, achieving superior long-horizon task success and fine-grained control accuracy.

Conclusion: Granularity-aligned, action-disentangled, and inference-consistent world-action modeling is critical for effective mobile manipulation.

Abstract: Mobile manipulation is a key capability for general-purpose robots, yet remains challenging for current embodied learning methods. VLA policies are typically reactive and lack explicit world modeling, while existing World Action Models (WAMs) are still poorly aligned with the structure of mobile manipulation: they operate on coarse video chunks, model entangled navigation-manipulation actions, and train inverse dynamics under supervision that does not match autoregressive inference. As a result, they often miss fine-grained contact dynamics, suffer from action-distribution conflicts, and accumulate errors over long-horizon rollouts. We propose ABot-M0.5, a new WAM built on the insight that mobile manipulation requires alignment at three levels: temporal granularity, action space, and train-test consistency. To align temporal granularity, we introduce intermediate latent actions that capture local visual state transitions and serve as an bridging action space between video latents and embodiment-specific controls. To align action space, we design a dual-level Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that disentangles both modality representations and heterogeneous action subspaces such as base movement and arm manipulation. To align inference conditions, we propose the dream-forcing training strategy that progressively trains inverse dynamics on model-predicted videos, improving train-test alignment and robustness during autoregressive prediction. Experiments on challenging mobile and fine-grained manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that ABot-M0.5 achieves state-of-the-art performance in both long-horizon task success and finegrained control accuracy. These results highlight the critical importance of granularity-aligned, action-disentangled, and inference-consistent world-action modeling.

</details>


### [58] [Restore3D: Breathing Life into Broken Objects with Shape and Texture Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00522)
*Xiaolong Shen,Zongxin Yang,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Restore3D is a framework that simultaneously restores both shape and texture of broken/ incomplete 3D objects using multi-view images, employing an automated data generation pipeline, a Mask Self-Perceiver module with Depth-Aware Mask Rectifier, and a coarse-to-fine reconstruction strategy to produce high-resolution, view-consistent textured 3D meshes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods focus primarily on geometric completion, neglecting texture restoration, and struggle with complex/diverse objects. There is a need for simultaneous shape and texture restoration of broken 3D objects for cultural heritage preservation, occluded object reconstruction, and artistic design.

Method: 1) Automated data generation pipeline for paired incomplete-complete samples from large-scale 3D datasets. 2) Multi-view model with Mask Self-Perceiver module and Depth-Aware Mask Rectifier for guided image integration. 3) Coarse-to-fine reconstruction strategy to recover detailed textured 3D meshes from refined multi-view images.

Result: Restore3D improves multi-view restoration quality and textured-mesh reconstruction over representative inpainting, completion, and reconstruction baselines on synthetic and real broken-object benchmarks.

Conclusion: Restore3D effectively addresses both shape and texture restoration of broken 3D objects, outperforming existing methods in the evaluated settings, with a project page available at restore3dx.github.io.

Abstract: Restoring incomplete or damaged 3D objects is crucial for cultural heritage preservation, occluded object reconstruction, and artistic design. Existing methods primarily focus on geometric completion, often neglecting texture restoration and struggling with relatively complex and diverse objects. We introduce Restore3D, a novel framework that simultaneously restores both the shape and texture of broken objects using multi-view images. To address limited training data, we develop an automated data generation pipeline that synthesizes paired incomplete-complete samples from large-scale 3D datasets. Central to Restore3D is a multi-view model, enhanced by a carefully designed Mask Self-Perceiver module with a Depth-Aware Mask Rectifier. The rectified masks learned by the self-perceiver guide an image integration and enhancement phase, helping retain observed shape and texture patterns while refining the generated regions and mitigating the low-resolution limitations of the base model, yielding high-resolution, semantically coherent, and view-consistent multi-view images. A coarse-to-fine reconstruction strategy is then employed to recover detailed textured 3D meshes from refined multi-view images. Experiments on synthetic and real broken-object benchmarks show that Restore3D improves multi-view restoration quality and textured-mesh reconstruction over representative inpainting, completion, and reconstruction baselines in the evaluated settings. Project Page: restore3dx.github.io

</details>


### [59] [Creating Impactful Autonomous Driving Datasets: A Strategic Guide from Research Gap to Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00710)
*Richard Schwarzkopf,Jonas Merkert,Frank Bieder,Annika Bätz,Alexander Blumberg,Carlos Fernandez,Felix Hauser,Fabian Immel,Christian Kinzig,Hendrik Königshof,Fabian Konstantinidis,Martin Lauer,Willi Poh,Nils Rack,Kevin Rösch,Yinzhe Shen,Marlon Steiner,Gleb Stepanov,Dominik Strutz,Ömer Şahin Taş,Julian Truetsch,Kaiwen Wang,Royden Wagner,Jan-Hendrik Pauls,Christoph Stiller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a strategic framework for creating impactful autonomous driving datasets, emphasizing diagnosis of data vs. evaluation problems, minimal data operators, and efficient resource allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small and medium-sized labs and startups cannot afford to misallocate scarce resources when creating datasets, yet existing literature only describes what datasets contain rather than how to strategically design them.

Method: The framework involves: (1) diagnosing whether a research question is blocked by a data or evaluation problem, (2) selecting minimal data operator(s) to close the gap, (3) recording new data only when cheaper options are insufficient. Analysis of major AD datasets and a KITScenes case study ground the approach.

Result: A strategic framework for AD dataset creation spanning gap identification, operator choice, sensor suite design, and annotation strategy, demonstrated through the KITScenes dataset family.

Conclusion: Impactful dataset creation requires deliberate strategic design starting from problem diagnosis rather than accumulating data; the framework helps resource-constrained groups build effective datasets.

Abstract: Well-designed autonomous driving datasets have fundamentally shaped research progress, yet existing literature primarily describes what datasets contain rather than how to strategically design impactful ones. This is especially limiting for small and medium-sized labs and startups that cannot afford to misallocate scarce resources. We argue that impactful dataset creation begins with a diagnosis: whether a research question is blocked by a data problem or an evaluation problem, and proceeds by selecting the minimal data operator(s) that closes the resulting gap, recording new data only when no cheaper operator(s) suffices. We analyze the evolution of major autonomous driving (AD) datasets through this lens and distill a strategic framework spanning gap identification, operator choice, sensor suite design, and annotation strategy. We ground the framework in a running case study of our KITScenes dataset family. The datasets are available at: https://kitscenes.com/

</details>


### [60] [SPECSIA: Stylization Dataset for Novel-View Enhancement in Drawing-based 3D Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00525)
*Kyuwon Kim,Sunjae Yoon,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel method (DraViE) with a new dataset (SPECSIA-15K) to remove novel-view artifacts in single-drawing 3D animation, achieving better temporal coherence and lower cost than existing sample-wise refinement approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing drawing-based 3D animation pipelines use sample-wise 2D refinement that overfits to observed views and fails to correct projection-induced artifacts in novel views.

Method: Created SPECSIA-15K dataset (14,980 artifact-corrupted/refinement-target pairs) and proposed DraViE, a lightweight plug-and-play module trained with data-level priors to remove novel-view artifacts.

Result: Consistent gains in novel-view fidelity and temporal coherence with lower per-character adaptation cost than sample-wise fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The data-driven prior approach (DraViE) effectively addresses artifact issues in novel views while preserving style and motion plausibility more efficiently than sample-wise optimization.

Abstract: Generating animation from a single 2D drawing is challenging because the output must preserve character appearance while remaining plausible and temporally coherent under motion. Existing drawing-based 3D animation pipelines often use sample-wise 2D refinement to align animated renderings with the input image, but such optimization tends to overfit to the observed view and fails to correct projection-induced artifacts in novel views. To address this limitation, we introduce SPECSIA-15K, a paired stylization dataset containing 14,980 artifact-corrupted projection/refinement-target pairs from 1,498 3DBiCar characters. We further present DraViE (Drawing-based View Enhancement), a lightweight plug-and-play module trained with data-level priors to remove novel-view artifacts while preserving style and motion plausibility. Experiments show consistent gains in novel-view fidelity and temporal coherence with lower per-character adaptation cost than sample-wise fine-tuning.

</details>


### [61] [Privacy-Preserving Depth-Only Open-Vocabulary 3D Semantic Segmentation Via Uncertainty-Guided Test-Time Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00978)
*Xuying Huang,Sicong Pan,Maren Bennewitz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UTTO is an uncertainty-guided test-time optimization framework that improves depth-only open-vocabulary 3D semantic segmentation for privacy-preserving indoor scene understanding, using uncertainty as a guide to refine unreliable semantic predictions with foundation model priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy-preserving perception is critical for 3D scene understanding in real-world indoor environments, but existing open-vocabulary segmentation methods rely on RGB images that expose sensitive visual information. Depth-only geometry offers privacy but suffers from highly uncertain and unreliable semantic predictions due to the lack of appearance cues.

Method: The paper proposes UTTO, an uncertainty-guided test-time optimization framework that converts uncertainty into a guidance signal to identify unreliable semantic responses, and uses semantic priors from foundation models to regularize their refinement, all without additional training.

Result: On ScanNet20, ScanNet40, and ScanNet200 benchmarks, UTTO consistently improves depth-only open-vocabulary 3D semantic segmentation and outperforms representative baselines under privacy-preserving conditions.

Conclusion: UTTO effectively addresses the uncertainty problem in depth-only open-vocabulary 3D segmentation by converting uncertainty into useful guidance, demonstrating that privacy-preserving 3D scene understanding can be achieved without sacrificing semantic performance.

Abstract: Privacy-preserving perception is a critical requirement for deploying 3D scene understanding systems in real-world indoor environments, yet it remains underexplored in open-vocabulary 3D semantic segmentation. Existing methods typically rely on obtaining rich semantic cues from RGB images, which may expose privacy-sensitive visual information. Depth-only 3D geometry provides a privacy-preserving alternative, but the absence of appearance-based semantic cues makes open-vocabulary predictions highly uncertain and less reliable. Under this setting, we propose to convert uncertainty into a guidance signal to identify unreliable semantic responses and use semantic priors from foundation models to regularize their refinement. We present UTTO, an uncertainty-guided test-time optimization framework for depth-only open-vocabulary 3D semantic segmentation. Without additional training, experiments on ScanNet20, ScanNet40, and ScanNet200 demonstrate that UTTO consistently improves depth-only open-vocabulary 3D segmentation and outperforms representative baselines under privacy-preserving conditions.

</details>


### [62] [NoPA: Non-Parametric Online 3D Scene Graph Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00529)
*Qi Xun Yeo,Seungjun Lee,Yan Li,Gim Hee Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NoPA replaces single 3D Gaussian object approximations with non-parametric distributions to retain geometric detail and improve merging accuracy, enabling real-time 3D scene graph generation with better performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing real-time 3D scene graph generation methods use single 3D Gaussian per object, causing severe loss of 3D geometric detail and inaccurate merging of object candidates. The goal is to preserve 3D information while maintaining real-time inference.

Method: NoPA represents each object as a separate non-parametric distribution (fixed particle set), uses maximum mean discrepancy on kernel density estimates for robust merging of object candidates, and propagates relationships between objects with high affinity to rectify relation loss.

Result: NoPA substantially outperforms current methods without sacrificing real-time inference speed.

Conclusion: Non-parametric object representation effectively preserves geometric detail and improves merging accuracy for real-time 3D scene graph generation, outperforming parametric Gaussian approaches.

Abstract: Classic 3D scene graph generation approaches fail to work in real-time due to the heavy computational cost of environment mapping and the need to generate intermediate point-cloud representations. To alleviate this issue, a recent work eschews point clouds in favor of a lightweight Gaussian distribution for each object. This approximation drastically speeds up inference and enables real-time 3D scene graph generation. However, the representation has two key weaknesses. \textbf{1)} Each object is approximated by a single 3D Gaussian, which causes a severe loss of 3D geometric detail. \textbf{2)} The discrepancy between this approximation and the true object geometry exacerbates the inaccurate merging of object candidates during online inference. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{NoPA}, which represents each object as a separate non-parametric distribution. This formulation retains 3D geometric information while preserving real-time inference of the parametric Gaussian formulation. To build upon our novel object representation, we propose a tailored merging strategy to recover coherent object instances. Specifically, we leverage maximum mean discrepancy on kernel density estimates to enable robust merging of object candidates during online exploration while minimizing added computational complexity. The key is to maintain a fixed particle set per object. Furthermore, to rectify the relation loss caused by misclassified objects, NoPA propagates relationships between objects with high affinity. Experiments show that NoPA substantially outperforms current methods without sacrificing real-time inference speed.

</details>


### [63] [GEAR-Seg: A Grounded Explainable Agent for Reasoning Segmentation and Data Engine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00544)
*Yanan Wang,Wen Li,Yibin Ying,Zhenghao Fei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GEAR-Seg introduces an explicitly decoupled agent for reasoning segmentation that converts visual pixels into attribute-rich text, enabling transparent, trackable logic chains through separate class-agnostic segmentation, semantic description, and LLM deduction modules. It also produces GEAR-131K, a large benchmark for complex reasoning segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end reasoning segmentation models entangle perception and deduction into an opaque black box, limiting interpretability and scalability.

Method: GEAR-Seg decouples the pipeline into three explicit steps: (1) class-agnostic segmentation, (2) semantic description, and (3) LLM deduction, transforming implicit reasoning into explicit, trackable logic chains.

Result: Achieves highly competitive zero-shot performance across reasoning and fine-grained referring segmentation benchmarks; constructs GEAR-131K benchmark with 38k+ images and 656k QA-mask pairs; distillation experiment shows lightweight models supervised by automated pipeline match human-annotated baselines.

Conclusion: Explicitly decoupling perception and deduction improves interpretability and scalability while maintaining strong performance, and the framework can serve as an effective data engine for training lightweight models.

Abstract: Reasoning segmentation requires localizing targets based on complex, implicit queries. Current end-to-end models typically entangle perception and deduction into an opaque black box, severely limiting interpretability and scalability. To address this, we propose GEAR-Seg (Grounded Explainable Agent for Reasoning Segmentation), an explicitly decoupled agent that shifts the paradigm by translating visual pixels into dense, attribute-rich text. By decoupling class-agnostic segmentation, semantic description, and Large Language Model (LLM) deduction, GEAR-Seg transforms implicit reasoning into an explicit, trackable logic chain. As a zero-shot inference framework, it achieves highly competitive performance across diverse reasoning and fine-grained referring segmentation benchmarks. Furthermore, GEAR-Seg inherently functions as a highly scalable data engine. Utilizing this engine, we construct GEAR-131K, a massive benchmark (over 38k images, 656k QA-mask pairs) introducing a multifaceted taxonomy tailored for complex real-world manipulation-oriented reasoning. Finally, distillation experiments demonstrate that lightweight models supervised exclusively by our automated pipeline closely match the upper-bound performance of costly human-annotated baselines.

</details>


### [64] [Towards Metric-Agnostic Trajectory Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01133)
*Markus Knoche,Daan de Geus,Bastian Leibe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a paradigm shift for trajectory forecasting: instead of tailoring training objectives to specific benchmark metrics (which conflict), train with metric-agnostic probabilistic objectives and treat metric optimization as a downstream task via TraDiE policies. Their model DONUT-NLL achieves SOTA on Waymo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SOTA trajectory forecasting models tailor training objectives to different benchmark metrics, but these metrics encourage conflicting behavior, making it suboptimal to optimize them directly during training.

Method: Introduces TraDiE (Trajectory Distribution Evaluation) policies—metric-specific policies that map a predictive distribution to the K trajectories and confidences needed by metrics. Also introduces DONUT-NLL, which adapts DONUT's training to directly optimize the predictive distribution.

Result: DONUT-NLL achieves state-of-the-art results on all metrics of the Waymo motion prediction benchmark using the proposed TraDiE policies.

Conclusion: Trajectory forecasting should separate probabilistic training from metric optimization; metric-specific downstream policies can bridge the gap effectively, leading to better overall performance across conflicting metrics.

Abstract: Accurate trajectory forecasting of surrounding traffic participants is a core capability for autonomous driving, enabling vehicles to anticipate behavior and plan safe maneuvers. We observe that current state-of-the-art forecasting models on Argoverse 2 and the Waymo Open Motion Dataset tailor their training objectives to the different benchmark metrics. Because these metrics encourage conflicting behavior, we propose a paradigm change for trajectory forecasting: training models with metric-agnostic probabilistic objectives and treating metric optimization as a downstream task applied to the predictive distribution. Concretely, we introduce Trajectory Distribution Evaluation (TraDiE) policies, metric-specific policies that map a predictive distribution to the set of $K$ trajectories and confidences required by trajectory forecasting metrics. We evaluate this framework by introducing DONUT-NLL, which adapts the training objective of the state-of-the-art trajectory forecasting model DONUT to directly optimize the predictive distribution. Using our policies, DONUT-NLL achieves state-of-the-art results on all metrics of the Waymo motion prediction benchmark.

</details>


### [65] [ECoSim: Data Efficient Fine-Tuning for Controllable Traffic Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00545)
*Yu-Hsiang Chen,Wei-Jer Chang,Yi-Ting Chen,Masayoshi Tomizuka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a lightweight control adaptation framework that adds multi-modal controllability (sketch, latent codes, text) to pretrained diffusion/autoregressive traffic models using FiLM layers, achieving strong controllability with less than 1% paired data for counterfactual scenario generation and long-tail synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing controllable traffic simulation approaches require retraining large generative models with extensive annotated data, which is inefficient and limits practical deployment.

Method: A lightweight control adaptation framework using identity-initialized FiLM layers to modulate intermediate features of pretrained diffusion/autoregressive traffic models, enabling multi-modal controllability (sketch, latent behavior codes, text) with minimal paired data (&lt;1%).

Result: Demonstrated strong controllability on Waymo Open Sim Agents Challenge with less than 1% of paired control data, achieving counterfactual scenario generation and long-tail synthesis while maintaining stable closed-loop driving realism and safety.

Conclusion: The framework unlocks new possibilities for controllable traffic simulation through lightweight adaptation of pretrained generative models, enabling targeted scenario generation without full retraining.

Abstract: Controllable traffic simulation is critical for testing autonomous driving systems, yet existing approaches often require retraining large generative models with extensive annotated data. We introduce a lightweight control adaptation framework that enables multi-modal controllability (sketch, latent behavior codes, and text) for pretrained state-of-the-art diffusion and autoregressive traffic models. By modulating intermediate features through identity-initialized FiLM layers, our method efficiently adds new control modalities while preserving the base model's generative prior. Evaluated on Waymo Open Sim Agents Challenge, our approach demonstrates strong controllability with less than 1% of the paired control data. Through context-aware condition transfer, our framework enables counterfactual scenario generation and long-tail synthesis while maintaining stable closed-loop driving realism and safety. Our framework unlocks new possibilities for controllable traffic simulation, enabling targeted scenario generation through lightweight adaptation of pretrained generative models. Project page: https://ecosim-web.github.io/

</details>


### [66] [EgoGapBench: Benchmarking Egocentric Action Selection in Multi-Agent Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00547)
*Jihyeok Jung,Jeewu Lee,Sanghyeop Kim,Chanhee Han,Seong Joon Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces EgoGapBench, a benchmark to isolate egocentric perspective understanding (called Egocentric Action Selection, EAS) in multi-agent scenes. Humans perform well on it, but MLLMs struggle, often mimicking other agents. Fine-tuning on standard egocentric data doesn't help, while EgoGapBench training improves but doesn't match human performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing egocentric benchmarks conflate first-person-view perception with true egocentric perspective understanding, failing to isolate the latter—especially when body cues are absent or other agents are present. The authors aim to separately measure egocentric action selection.

Method: The authors construct EgoGapBench, a diagnostic benchmark designed to isolate egocentric perspective by requiring selecting the correct action from the agent's own perspective in multi-agent scenes, where first-person body cues are unavailable.

Result: Humans answer reliably on EgoGapBench; MLLMs (both open-source and proprietary) perform substantially worse and systematically pick actions of other visible agents. Fine-tuning on existing egocentric data fails to close the gap and can be harmful, while fine-tuning on EgoGapBench improves accuracy but not to human level.

Conclusion: Egocentric Action Selection is difficult to learn from first-person-view data alone. MLLMs should be evaluated and trained not just for scene understanding but specifically for egocentric action selection.

Abstract: Existing egocentric benchmarks have primarily constructed the egocentric setting from first-person-view data, which makes it difficult to evaluate egocentric perspective itself in isolation. However, understanding first-person-view input and taking an egocentric perspective are separable abilities, especially when first-person body cues are absent or when other agents are present. To isolate egocentric perspective understanding, we introduce EgoGapBench, a diagnostic benchmark for measuring action selection in multi-agent egocentric scenes. We define the ability measured by this benchmark as Egocentric Action Selection (EAS): selecting an appropriate action from the agent's perspective in the presence of other agents. On EgoGapBench, humans answer reliably, whereas both open-source and proprietary MLLMs perform substantially worse and systematically select actions performed by other visible agents. Fine-tuning on existing egocentric data fails to close this gap and can even be detrimental. In contrast, fine-tuning on EgoGapBench training data improves accuracy but does not reach human performance. These results show that EAS is difficult to acquire from first-person-view data alone, and that MLLMs should be evaluated and trained not only for scene understanding but also for egocentric action selection.

</details>


### [67] [BrainFIBRE: A Foundation Model via Information Decomposition for Brain Microstructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00573)
*Zijian Dong,Yi Lin,Ji Fang,Jianxiong Zhou,Kwun Kei Ng,Juan Helen Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BrainFIBRE is a foundation model for brain microstructure, pretrained on NODDI maps from 55,592 UK Biobank participants using Self-supervised Partial Information Decomposition (SPID) and Counterfactual Candidate Construction (CCC) to disentangle unique, synergistic, and redundant information from NDI, ODI, and FWF maps, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse neuroimaging tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion MRI (NODDI) produces three biophysically interpretable maps (NDI, ODI, FWF), but integrating them is challenging because standard representation learning cannot disentangle their unique, shared, and synergistic information.

Method: BrainFIBRE extends PID-guided multimodal learning to self-supervised learning via SPID, using a CCC paradigm (modality dropping/swapping) to provide contrastive signals for a Mixture-of-Experts architecture, enabling disentanglement without downstream labels.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on both Caucasian and Asian cohorts across diverse tasks (age, sex, cerebrovascular/neurodegenerative markers, cognition) with neurobiologically interpretable representations.

Conclusion: BrainFIBRE establishes a versatile foundation for microstructural neuroimaging analysis.

Abstract: Diffusion MRI probes brain microstructure with particular sensitivity to early cerebrovascular and neurodegenerative changes. Neurite Orientation Dispersion and Density Imaging (NODDI) decomposes the diffusion signal into three biophysically interpretable maps: neurite density index (NDI), orientation dispersion index (ODI), and free water fraction (FWF), capturing neurite packing, fiber coherence, and extracellular fluid. These 3D maps offer a rich substrate for transferable microstructural representations, yet integrating them is challenging: standard representation learning struggles to disentangle the unique information in each map from their shared and synergistic interactions. We present BrainFIBRE, the first foundation model for brain microstructure, pretrained on NODDI-derived maps from 55,592 UK Biobank participants. We propose Self-supervised Partial Information Decomposition (SPID), which extends PID-guided multimodal learning to the self-supervised regime for the first time. A novel Counterfactual Candidate Construction (CCC) paradigm perturbs inter-modality alignment through modality dropping and swapping, providing the contrastive signal for a Mixture-of-Experts architecture to disentangle unique, synergistic, and redundant information without any downstream label. On both Caucasian and Asian cohorts, BrainFIBRE achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse tasks predicting age, sex, cerebrovascular and neurodegenerative markers, and cognition, while yielding neurobiologically interpretable representations that reveal task- and cohort-specific interaction patterns. BrainFIBRE establishes a versatile foundation for neuroimaging analysis at the microstructural level.

</details>


### [68] [Caption Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00578)
*Seref Baris Cagliyan,Umut Ozdemir,Merve Tapli,Emre Akbas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CaBM replaces rigid concept sets in Concept Bottleneck Models with free-form captions from Large Multimodal Models, eliminating information leakage and enabling autonomous concept discovery while maintaining competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CBMs face three key challenges: (1) defining optimal concept sets is difficult and requires expensive expert annotations; (2) static concept sets introduce label bias and limit discovery; (3) traditional CBMs suffer from information leakage where unmodeled visual features bypass the bottleneck, compromising explanation integrity.

Method: CaBM replaces predefined concept layers with free-form natural language captions generated by Large Multimodal Models (LMMs). It uses LMM-generated captions to represent images, trains a classifier strictly on this text (ensuring leakage-free architecture), and analyzes the trained text classifier post-hoc to autonomously discover high-quality, dataset-specific concepts.

Result: CaBM achieves competitive accuracy across fine-grained and coarse-grained benchmarks while preserving interpretability, without relying on external dictionaries or manual labeling.

Conclusion: CaBM successfully addresses the limitations of traditional CBMs by removing the need for predefined concept sets, eliminating information leakage by construction, and enabling autonomous concept discovery, all while maintaining competitive performance.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) provide interpretability by routing predictions through a layer of human-understandable concepts. However, defining an optimal concept set for a specific dataset remains an open challenge. Existing approaches rely on expensive expert annotations or LLM-generated lists based solely on class names. Even "open-vocabulary" variants typically depend on static concept sets, which restrict discovery and introduce label bias. Furthermore, traditional CBMs often suffer from information leakage, where unmodeled visual features bypass the bottleneck and compromise the integrity of the explanations. To overcome these limitations, we propose Caption Bottleneck Models (CaBM), a framework that circumvents the need for predefined concept sets by replacing rigid concept layers with free-form natural language. By representing images via LMM-generated captions and training a classifier strictly on this text, CaBM ensures a leakage-free architecture by construction. Additionally, by analyzing the text classifier post-training, CaBM autonomously discovers high-quality, dataset-specific concepts. Our results across fine- and coarse-grained benchmarks demonstrate that CaBM achieves competitive accuracy while preserving interpretability without the constraints of external dictionaries or manual labeling.

</details>


### [69] [EPO: Boosting 3D Foundation Models with Edge-based Pose Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00579)
*Mattia D'Urso,Christian Sormann,Mattia Rossi,Friedrich Fraundorfer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EPO is a differentiable, trackless optimization framework that uses edge map alignment to improve geometric accuracy of 3D Foundation Model SfM reconstructions, matching or outperforming Bundle Adjustment methods with lower runtime/memory and running on consumer hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Foundation Models achieve fast SfM inference by skipping feature extraction/matching, but their geometric accuracy is inferior to traditional pipelines. Post-processing with Bundle Adjustment requires track extraction, which defeats the speed advantage. There is a need for a refinement method that improves accuracy without requiring explicit correspondences.

Method: EPO is a fully differentiable framework that uses edge map alignment as a proxy for geometric optimization, completely avoiding feature extraction and track construction. It performs optimization through differentiable edge alignment rather than bundle adjustment.

Result: EPO matches or outperforms Bundle Adjustment-like methods across multiple datasets and tasks while requiring significantly lower runtime and memory. Its reduced memory footprint enables operation on consumer-grade hardware where competing methods cannot run.

Conclusion: Edge-based Pose Optimization (EPO) is an effective, efficient alternative to Bundle Adjustment for refining 3D Foundation Model SfM reconstructions, preserving speed advantages while improving geometric accuracy and enabling deployment on consumer hardware.

Abstract: We introduce \textbf{Edge-based Pose Optimization (EPO)}, a trackless geometric optimization framework specifically designed to boost the Structure-from-Motion reconstructions generated by 3D Foundation Models. These models achieve rapid inference by bypassing the time-consuming feature extraction and matching stages of traditional pipelines, where explicit correspondences between each 3D point and multiple images, referred to as tracks, are established. However, their geometric accuracy currently falls short of traditional pipelines. While this can be addressed in a post-processing step via Bundle Adjustment-like refinement, doing so requires extracting feature tracks, thus defeating the original speed advantage. Instead, our fully differentiable framework uses edge map alignment as a proxy for geometric optimization, avoiding feature extraction and track construction entirely. Through extensive evaluation across multiple datasets and tasks, we demonstrate that EPO matches or outperforms Bundle Adjustment-like methods while requiring significantly lower runtime and memory. Notably, its reduced memory footprint makes EPO suitable for consumer-grade hardware, where competing refinement methods cannot run.

</details>


### [70] [Active Spatial Guidance: Eliminating Injected Positional Mechanisms in Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00580)
*Cong Liu,Xiaofang Li,Simon X. Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Active Spatial Guidance (Guidance), a training-only objective that removes explicit positional injection from Vision Transformers and instead uses a 2D coordinate-regression loss on patch tokens to induce spatial organization from data, achieving competitive or better results than traditional positional embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors question whether spatial organization in ViTs can be learned from data rather than explicitly injected through positional mechanisms, motivated by the natural spatial regularities in images.

Method: Guidance is a training-only auxiliary loss that regresses 2D coordinates from final-layer patch tokens, removing positional injection entirely. The guidance head is discarded at inference time, leaving a positional-injection-free ViT encoder.

Result: Guidance consistently outperforms learned absolute positional embeddings and rotary positional embeddings on ImageNet-100, ADE20K segmentation, and Hypersim depth estimation, while also improving robustness under resolution transfer.

Conclusion: Spatial inductive bias in ViTs can be shaped through training-time supervision rather than requiring architectural injection of positional information.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) commonly rely on injected positional mechanisms to address self-attention's permutation invariance. Motivated by the spatial regularities of natural images, we ask whether spatial organization can be induced from data rather than explicitly injected. Under controlled, matched from-scratch training, we propose Active Spatial Guidance (Guidance), a training-only objective that disables positional injection and applies an auxiliary 2D coordinate-regression loss to the final-layer patch tokens. The guidance head is used only during training and removed for inference; the deployed model consists of a positional-injection-free ViT encoder and the task-specific prediction module. Using DINOv3 ViT backbones, Guidance consistently improves performance on ImageNet-100 classification, ADE20K semantic segmentation, and Hypersim monocular depth estimation, outperforming strong injected baselines such as learned absolute positional embeddings and rotary positional embeddings under identical training protocols. On ImageNet-100, broader comparisons against representative injected positional designs further support Guidance's effectiveness. Guidance also improves robustness under resolution transfer, and multi-resolution training further strengthens accuracy across input sizes. Overall, our results suggest that spatial inductive bias in ViTs need not be architecturally injected, but can be shaped through training-time supervision. The code used for training and evaluation is publicly available in https://github.com/cloudlc/asg.

</details>


### [71] [GADA: Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation for Image-Based Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00595)
*Siwoo Lim,Sunjae Yoon,Gwanhyeong Koo,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Gaussian Splatting with warping-based techniques suffers from spatial misalignments due to uncertain geometry. The authors propose Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation (GADA), which uses iterative refinement with deformable offsets to correct misalignments and an implicit confidence weighting mechanism for reliable multi-view fusion, achieving superior quality and 2.13x faster FPS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Warping-based Gaussian Splatting methods suffer from pixel-level inaccuracies caused by uncertain geometry, leading to spatial misalignments that disrupt residual learning and limit correction gains, especially on thin structures and high-frequency details. Additionally, standard visibility checks discard valid pixels and naive mean aggregation is suboptimal.

Method: Proposes Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation (GADA), which includes: (1) an iterative refinement module with deformable offsets to actively correct spatial misalignments and recover displaced visual cues, and (2) an implicit confidence weighting mechanism that selectively suppresses unreliable evidence during multi-view fusion, replacing naive mean aggregation.

Result: Outperforms prior warping-based Gaussian Splatting, preserves high-frequency quality, and achieves 2.13 times faster FPS.

Conclusion: By addressing spatial misalignments and improving multi-view fusion through deformable aggregation and confidence weighting, the proposed GADA method significantly enhances both quality and efficiency in Gaussian Splatting.

Abstract: Gaussian Splatting has achieved significant improvements by incorporating warping-based techniques. However, such methods suffer from pixel-level inaccuracies due to uncertain geometry. This uncertainty leads to spatial misalignments in the warped images, which disrupt residual learning used in warping-based methods and fundamentally limit the gains of correction, particularly on thin structures and high-frequency details. Driven by our insight that useful visual cues are not lost but locally preserved under slight displacement, we propose Geometry-Aware Deformable Aggregation (GADA). This method introduces an iterative refinement module with deformable offsets to actively correct spatial misalignments and recover these displaced cues. Furthermore, to address the limitations of standard pipelines where visibility checks (i.e., thresholding) often discard valid pixels and multi-view warped image fusion relies on naive mean aggregation, our module is coupled with an implicit confidence weighting mechanism that selectively suppresses unreliable evidence. Consequently, our approach outperforms prior warping-based Gaussian Splatting, preserving high-frequency quality while achieving 2.13 times faster FPS.

</details>


### [72] [Semantic-Guided Reading Order Reconstruction in Historical Armenian Newspapers with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00596)
*Chahan Vidal-Gorène,Nadi Tomeh,Victoria Khurshudyan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new dataset of 66 annotated pages from historical Armenian newspapers and presents a hybrid method combining semantic zone detection with a generative LLM to reconstruct reading order, reducing errors by up to 76% over geometric baselines while remaining robust to noise and multi-page layouts. The approach is designed for data bootstrapping in under-resourced scenarios, and the authors also release a specialized Tesseract OCR model for historical Armenian print.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of reading order reconstruction in historical Armenian newspapers, which feature complex layouts and suffer from limited language resources, making existing approaches poorly suited.

Method: The authors compare several approaches: geometric heuristics, YOLO-based layout parsing, an end-to-end document model (ECLAIR), and a hybrid method combining semantic zone detection with a generative LLM. They also introduce a new annotated dataset of 66 pages and release a specialized Tesseract OCR model for historical Armenian print.

Result: The hybrid method achieves the lowest error rates, reducing ordering errors by up to 76% over the strongest geometric baseline, and remains robust in multi-page settings and under noisy OCR.

Conclusion: The hybrid method is not intended for production use but as a data bootstrapping strategy for rapid annotation in highly under-resourced scenarios.

Abstract: This paper addresses reading order reconstruction in historical Armenian newspapers, which combine complex layouts with limited language resources. We introduce a new annotated dataset of 66 pages and compare geometric heuristics, YOLO-based layout parsing, an end-to-end document model ECLAIR, and a hybrid method combining semantic zone detection with a generative LLM. Our hybrid method achieves the lowest error rates of all evaluated approaches, reducing ordering errors by up to 76% over the strongest geometric baseline, and remains robust in multi-page settings and under noisy OCR. Rather than targeting production the method is designed as a data bootstrapping strategy enabling rapid annotation in highly under-resourced scenarios. Alongside the dataset, we release a specialized Tesseract OCR model for historical Armenian print.

</details>


### [73] [Retrieved Images as Visual Thought: Training-Free Multimodal In-Context Learning for the Open-vs-Closed Gap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00606)
*Bingchen Huang,Zhiling Wang,Yifu Chen,Yuanchao Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReVisIT is a train-free framework that enhances visual reasoning by retrieving labeled exemplar images and reasoning over them, achieving SOTA performance (98.5% on VL-ICL Bench) with smaller models and no generation, training, or code synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing "Thinking with Images" approaches rely on generation (tool invocation, code synthesis, or training pipelines), which is costly. The retrieval-based route (using labeled exemplar images) is underexplored despite being train-free and potentially more efficient.

Method: ReVisIT combines (1) structured class definitions, (2) per-query multimodal retrieval of exemplar image-label pairs, and (3) alternating user/assistant injection of exemplars before joint multi-attribute decoding. It degrades gracefully by using only available components.

Result: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B with ReVisIT reaches 98.5% at 4-shot on VL-ICL Bench (vs. 72B LLaVA-OneVision SOTA at 98.7%). Turns alone add 26.1 points to GPT-4.1 on Bongard-OpenWorld. Full stack yields 4-6 point macro gain across backbones on MAAC-Bench. 83% of retrieval gain from quality, not presence.

Conclusion: Retrieval-based visual reasoning is a viable, train-free alternative to generation-based approaches. Retrieval-plus-turns is the universal performance lever; structured definitions are task-adaptive. MAAC-Bench is released with a rubric-grounded LLM verification protocol for subjective attribute annotation.

Abstract: Recent work on Thinking with Images makes vision a dynamic part of reasoning, but does so through generation: the model invokes external tools, synthesizes code, or imagines new imagery, each at the cost of a tool protocol, brittle code, or an expensive training pipeline. A fourth route makes vision dynamic without generating anything, by retrieving labeled exemplar images and reasoning over them, yet it remains underexplored despite being train-free. We present ReVisIT, a train-free framework that realizes this retrieval-based route by treating each retrieved image-label pair as a unit of visual thought. ReVisIT combines structured class definitions, per-query multimodal retrieval of exemplars, and alternating user/assistant injection of those exemplars before joint multi-attribute decoding, and degrades gracefully to whichever components a task admits. On VL-ICL Bench Fast Open MiniImageNet, Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B with ReVisIT reaches 98.5% at 4-shot, statistically indistinguishable from the 72B LLaVA-OneVision SOTA (98.7%) on this near-saturated task at about 1/2.4 the parameters, while the same backbone without the scaffold sits at chance. The turns layer alone adds 26.1 points to GPT-4.1 on free-form concept induction (Bongard-OpenWorld), and the full stack yields a 4-6 point macro gain across three backbones on MAAC-Bench, a new license-clean 27-class, 5-attribute benchmark, significant by paired bootstrap on the curator-derived attributes. Component analysis shows that retrieval-plus-turns is the universal lever while structured definitions are need-adaptive, and that 83% of the retrieval gain comes from retrieval quality rather than from the presence of exemplars. MAAC-Bench is released with a rubric-grounded LLM verification protocol that replaces author spot-check on subjective attributes.

</details>


### [74] [Diffusion-Based Multi-Class Normality for OOD Detection: An Application to CDP Authentication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00609)
*Bolutife Atoki,Iuliia Tkachenko,Bertrand Kerautret,Carlos Crispim-Junior*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a diffusion-based multi-class normality framework using a class-conditional ControlNet trained only on authentic CDPs, with dual template masking, to detect counterfeits via reconstruction error—outperforming baselines on the Indigo 1x1 Base dataset without using counterfeit samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruction-based generative models struggle with multi-class normality modeling for OOD detection, particularly in CDP authentication where authentic and counterfeit samples are visually similar but differ in subtle printing-and-digitization signatures. A single detector must capture multiple in-distribution manifolds and produce comparable anomaly scores across classes.

Method: A diffusion-based multi-class normality framework using a single class-conditional ControlNet trained exclusively on authentic CDPs. Dual template masking hides complementary regions of the input template and scores only withheld pixels to reduce reliance on visible binary structure. Counterfeits are detected through reconstruction error under authentic-class conditioning.

Result: On the Indigo 1x1 Base dataset, the proposed method outperforms traditional and adapted generative baselines under multi-class authentic-versus-counterfeit evaluation, without using counterfeit samples for training or threshold calibration.

Conclusion: The diffusion-based ControlNet framework with dual template masking is effective for unsupervised multi-class OOD detection in CDP authentication, achieving strong performance without requiring counterfeit samples during training or calibration.

Abstract: Reconstruction-based generative models offer a natural framework for unsupervised out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, but multi-class normality modelling requires a single detector to capture multiple in-distribution manifolds and produce comparable anomaly scores across classes. We study this problem in copy detection pattern (CDP) authentication, where authentic and counterfeit samples are visually similar but differ in subtle printing-and-digitisation (P\&D) signatures. We propose a diffusion based multi-class normality framework in which a single class-conditional ControlNet is trained exclusively on authentic CDPs from multiple P\&D classes and detects counterfeits through reconstruction error under authentic-class conditioning. We further introduce dual template masking, which hides complementary regions of the input template and scores only withheld pixels, reducing reliance on visible binary structure. On the Indigo 1 x 1 Base dataset, the proposed method outperforms traditional and adapted generative baselines under multi-class authentic-versus-counterfeit evaluation, without using counterfeit samples for training or threshold calibration.

</details>


### [75] [Identifying Latent Concepts and Structures for Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00620)
*Boyang Dai,Chaoqi Chen,Yizhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CPF-GCD introduces Compositional Primitive Fields to reshape high-rank entangled representations into low-rank compositional structures, enabling better novel category discovery in open-world recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GCD methods focus on clustering objectives but overlook that standard vision backbones produce high-rank, entangled token representations unsuitable for unsupervised discovery of latent concepts and structures.

Method: CPF enforces low-rank compositional organization via a spatial field mechanism inserted between backbone and head, rewriting noisy patch tokens through low-rank primitive mixtures to decompose images into reusable atomic parts and their spatial layouts.

Result: CPF consistently boosts performance across diverse GCD baselines as a generic plug-and-play module.

Conclusion: Identifying and leveraging low-rank compositional structure is a crucial inductive bias for open-world recognition, validated by CPF's effectiveness in enabling novel categories to emerge as new activation patterns over a shared primitive vocabulary.

Abstract: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) aims to recognize known classes while autonomously discovering novel ones in open-world settings. However, current approaches primarily focus on designing clustering objectives, often overlooking a critical bottleneck: standard vision backbones yield high-rank, entangled token representations that are ill-suited for unsupervised discovery of latent concepts and structures. In this paper, we propose Compositional Primitive Fields (CPF-GCD), a novel representation learning framework that reshapes the feature space to make such latent structure identifiable by enforcing a low-rank compositional organization. Our core hypothesis is that all categories, whether known or novel, can be expressed as compositions and spatial arrangements of a finite set of learnable visual primitives that capture reusable concepts. CPF instantiates this geometric constraint via a spatial field mechanism. Inserted between the backbone and the head, it rewrites noisy patch tokens through low-rank primitive mixtures, effectively decomposing images into reusable atomic parts and their spatial layouts. By explicitly modeling the spatial distribution of primitives, CPF enables novel categories to emerge naturally as new activation patterns over a shared vocabulary. This shifts the focus of representation from merely partitioning global embeddings to constructing a structured and separable primitive field. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CPF serves as a generic, plug-and-play module that consistently boosts performance across diverse GCD baselines, validating that identifying and leveraging low-rank compositional structure is a crucial inductive bias for open-world recognition.

</details>


### [76] [Learning to Watch: Active Video Anomaly Understanding via Interleaved Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00622)
*Mengjingcheng Mo,Jiaxu Leng,Xinbo Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Anom-π is a closed-loop video anomaly understanding framework that treats video analysis as active sequential decision-making, using temporal atomic operators and preference optimization to achieve competitive performance with only 2B parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing passive paradigms suffer from observational aliasing, where static sampling fails to disambiguate semantically distinct events.

Method: Proposes a closed-loop framework (Anom-π) with temporal atomic operators (local backtracking, temporal expansion, fine-grained sampling), Interactive Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) for trajectory-level policy alignment, and Active Evidence Inquiry (AEI) utility to balance task success, evidence acquisition, and interaction cost.

Result: With only 2B parameters, achieves highly competitive performance, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art large-scale VAU models in complex scenarios.

Conclusion: Active sequential decision-making outperforms passive sampling for video anomaly understanding, and trajectory-level policy alignment via iDPO effectively learns complex interaction strategies under weak supervision.

Abstract: Video anomaly understanding (VAU) relies on sparse, context-dependent cues. However, existing passive paradigms suffer from observational aliasing, where static sampling fails to disambiguate semantically distinct events. To overcome this, we propose $Anom\text{-}π$, a closed-loop framework that reconceptualizes video understanding as an active sequential decision-making process within a dynamic environment. Inspired by human video-reviewing behavior, this framework unifies internal cognitive reasoning and strategic evidence acquisition into an interleaved policy, utilizing temporal atomic operators such as local backtracking, temporal expansion, and fine-grained sampling to endow the model with perceptual proactivity. To learn such complex interaction strategies under video-level weak supervision, we design Interactive Direct Preference Optimization (iDPO) to achieve trajectory-level policy alignment, guided by an Active Evidence Inquiry (AEI) utility that balances task success, informative evidence acquisition, and interaction cost. This approach enables the agent to learn to actively disambiguate hypotheses while suppressing redundant exploration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework, with only 2B parameters, achieves highly competitive performance, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art large-scale VAU models in complex scenarios.

</details>


### [77] [Uncertainty-aware tree height change regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00638)
*Max Gaber,Dimitri Gominski,Jaime C. Revenga,Stefan Oehmcke,Rasmus Fensholt,Martin Brandt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Canopy Height Change (CHC) dataset for continuous, uncertainty-aware canopy height change estimation at 3m resolution across 10,598 km² in Spain, paired with PlanetScope satellite imagery. It proposes the task of uncertainty-aware change regression and evaluates Geospatial Foundation Models on this task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat canopy height change as binary change detection, ignoring the continuous nature of vegetation change and the uncertainty inherent in the labels.

Method: The authors create the CHC dataset with continuous canopy height differences and spatially resolved uncertainties, propose uncertainty-aware change regression metrics and fine-tuning strategies for GFMs.

Result: State-of-the-art GFMs are evaluated on the new dataset and task, highlighting promising directions and remaining challenges.

Conclusion: Continuous, uncertainty-aware change regression is a more appropriate framing for canopy height change estimation than binary change detection, and the CHC dataset enables advancement in this direction.

Abstract: Monitoring canopy height change is essential for understanding carbon sinks and forest dynamics. Remote sensing enables consistent, large-scale observations of such changes, increasingly integrated with deep learning architectures such as Geospatial Foundation Models (GFMs). However, existing methods and datasets frame the problem as binary change detection, which overlooks both the continuous nature of change, especially for vegetation, and the inherent uncertainty in labels. We present the Canopy Height Change (CHC) dataset, providing 3 $\mathrm{m}$ resolution continuous canopy height differences and associated spatially resolved uncertainties across 10598 $\mathrm{km}^2$ of northern and western Spain. The dataset is paired with a co-located time series of PlanetScope satellite imagery. Based on the dataset, we introduce the task of uncertainty-aware change regression, associated metrics and strategies for fine-tuning GFMs. Furthermore, we evaluate state-of-the-art GFMs and highlight promising directions and remaining challenges for advancing continuous canopy height change estimation.

</details>


### [78] [Not All Prediction Targets Keep Training-Free Diffusion Guidance on the Manifold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00647)
*Yunsung Lee,Hyeongmin Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes x-prediction as a superior alternative to ε- and v-prediction for training-free guidance in diffusion models, showing it better preserves data manifold integrity through theoretical analysis and experiments on bird classification and style transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training-free guidance suffers from manifold drift due to inaccurate clean image estimates at high-noise steps when using ε- and v-prediction. x-prediction outputs clean images directly, eliminating this error source.

Method: The authors provide theoretical analysis of how different prediction targets (ε, v, x) affect clean image estimation accuracy, and introduce Child FID metric to measure manifold damage beyond standard evaluation.

Result: Experiments on fine-grained bird classification and style transfer demonstrate x-prediction keeps guided samples on the manifold most reliably compared to ε- and v-prediction.

Conclusion: x-prediction is the strongest foundation for training-free guidance, as it best preserves manifold integrity during guided sampling.

Abstract: Training-free guidance (TFG) steers a pretrained diffusion model toward a desired attribute at inference. To be effective, this guidance must be applied from the earliest, high-noise steps of sampling. Because its objective (a classifier or energy) is defined on clean images, $ε$- and $v$-prediction models must first estimate the clean image $\hat{x}$ from the noisy state at each step, and the accuracy of that estimate determines how easily guidance drifts off the data manifold. $x$-prediction, a recent alternative, outputs the clean image directly, removing this source of error even at high noise. This is our motivation. We provide a theoretical analysis of how each prediction target shapes this accuracy, and introduce guided-class FID (Child FID), a metric that exposes the manifold damage standard evaluation misses. Experiments on a new fine-grained bird benchmark and on style transfer confirm that $x$-prediction keeps guided samples on the manifold most reliably, making it the strongest foundation for training-free guidance. Code is available at https://github.com/ManLuML/on-manifold-tfg

</details>


### [79] [Linguistic Relative Policy Optimization for Video Anomaly Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00654)
*Jiaxu Leng,Jiankang Zheng,Mengjingcheng Mo,Zhanjie Wu,Haosheng Chen,Ji Gan,Xinbo Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LRPO is a tuning-free, annotation-efficient method for video anomaly detection that distills semantic advantages from reasoning trajectories into linguistic anomaly priors, achieving SOTA results on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VAD methods rely heavily on large-scale annotations or expert-designed priors, limiting their ability to acquire anomaly knowledge with minimal human intervention.

Method: Proposes Linguistic Relative Policy Optimization (LRPO), which distills group-relative semantic advantages from multiple reasoning trajectories into linguistically expressed anomaly experience priors, and injects these priors into context without parameter updates. Builds general experience (cross-scenario anomaly preferences) and scenario experience (context-dependent rules). Uses anomaly alignment reward to match human risk preferences.

Result: Significantly outperforms existing SOTA methods on XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, and UBnormal under tuning-free settings.

Conclusion: LRPO enables effective VAD with minimal human intervention by leveraging linguistic priors and reward-guided optimization without parameter updates.

Abstract: Video anomaly detection (VAD) with multimodal large language models has shown strong potential, yet most existing methods still depend on large-scale annotations or expert-designed priors, limiting their ability to acquire anomaly knowledge with as little human intervention as possible. To address this, we propose Linguistic Relative Policy Optimization (LRPO), which distills group-relative semantic advantages from multiple reasoning trajectories into a linguistically expressed anomaly experience prior, and adapts the model by injecting this prior into the context to steer its output distribution without any parameter updates. LRPO builds two complementary experience representations: general experience captures transferable anomaly preferences across scenarios, while scenario experience models context-dependent anomaly rules for targeted refinement. To further improve the learned experience, we introduce an anomaly alignment reward that guides trajectory optimization to match human risk preferences and reinforce temporally grounded reasoning. Extensive experiments on XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, and UBnormal demonstrate that LRPO significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods under tuning-free settings.

</details>


### [80] [DART: Difficulty-Adaptive Routing for Zero-Shot Video Temporal Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00672)
*Zhengbo Zhang,Mark He Huang,Zhigang Tu,Ming-Hsuan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DART bridges the reasoning gap in zero-shot video temporal grounding by routing simple queries to a Fast path and complex multi-stage queries to a Slow path with structured reasoning, using a DPP-based difficulty indicator, achieving state-of-the-art performance with higher efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot VTG methods rely on frame-query feature matching, which handles simple events but struggles with complex multi-stage queries requiring temporal ordering and causal understanding — referred to as the reasoning gap.

Method: DART uses a query-conditioned Determinantal Point Process (DPP) for dual purpose: selecting diverse keyframes and providing spectral entropy as a difficulty indicator. Simple queries go through a Fast path (direct prediction); complex queries go through a Slow path with Temporal Markup Prompting (global analysis, per-frame annotation, boundary extraction).

Result: On Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, DART achieves SOTA zero-shot performance across ID and OOD settings, improving mIoU by up to 3.5 points over the strongest baseline while using over 7x fewer frames.

Conclusion: Difficulty-aware routing with structured reasoning effectively bridges the reasoning gap in zero-shot VTG, delivering both accuracy and efficiency gains.

Abstract: Zero-shot video temporal grounding (VTG) localizes events in untrimmed videos from natural language queries without task-specific training. Existing methods rely on frame-query feature matching, which suffices for simple events but struggles with complex multi-stage queries that require understanding temporal ordering and causal structure -- a disparity we call the reasoning gap. We propose DART (Difficulty-Adaptive Routing for Temporal Grounding), which bridges this gap by coupling difficulty-aware routing with structured reasoning in large vision-language models. A query-conditioned Determinantal Point Process (DPP) serves a dual role: selecting diverse, query-relevant keyframes as temporal evidence, and providing spectral entropy as a difficulty indicator. Simple queries are routed to a Fast path for direct prediction, while complex queries follow a Slow path with Temporal Markup Prompting, which decomposes localization into global event analysis, per-frame temporal role annotation, and boundary extraction. On Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions, DART achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across both identically distributed and multiple out-of-distribution settings, improving mIoU by up to 3.5 points over the strongest baseline while using over 7 times fewer frames. The project homepage is available at https://dart-vtg.github.io/.

</details>


### [81] [LUMA: Benchmarking Segmentation via a Lightweight Universal Mask Adapter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00687)
*Tobias Christian Nauen,Anosh Billimoria,Federico Raue,Stanislav Frolov,Brian B. Moser,Andreas Dengel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LUMA is a lightweight, backbone-agnostic mask-transformer head that enables fair benchmarking of 20 backbones and 11 pretraining schemes, revealing that plain ViT dominates efficiency and pretraining objective matters more than architecture for segmentation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Comparing transformer backbones for segmentation is confounded by different decoders, recipes, and pretraining—reported differences rarely reflect the backbone itself.

Method: LUMA is a lightweight, backbone-agnostic mask-transformer head treating any backbone as a black-box feature extractor, using queries to read features via cheap cross-attention.
