Our analyses further confirm that stronger priors and finer-grained similarity substantially amplify hallucination. We hope MoHallBench can facilitate future evaluation and mitigation of motion hallucination in VideoLLMs.

</details>


### [135] [SD-RouteFusion: Ego-Trajectory Prediction with SD-Map Route Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01139)
*Sviatoslav Voloshyn,Bruno K. W. Martens,Wangxin Liu,Jakob Vinkås,Junsheng Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SD-RouteFusion is an end-to-end ego-trajectory prediction method that fuses camera, kinematics, and SD-map routes (instead of HD maps), achieving 16.9% ADE improvement over baselines on a large-scale real-world dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ego-trajectory prediction methods rely heavily on High Definition (HD) maps, which are expensive to build and maintain at scale. The authors aim to develop a scalable, production-ready alternative that leverages widely available Standard Definition (SD) map routes.

Method: SD-RouteFusion fuses three input modalities: front-facing camera, vehicle kinematics, and navigation route from SD maps. It employs a dual-hypothesis design with a gated classifier to handle route corruption and visual uncertainty, trained on a dataset of 480k driving scenarios.

Result: On a large-scale real-world dataset (480k scenarios across Europe and U.S.), SD-route conditioning yields 10.5% ADE improvement over image-kinematics baseline; full fusion achieves 16.9% ADE reduction at an 8-second prediction horizon.

Conclusion: SD-RouteFusion provides a practical, scalable path toward robust route-aware ego-trajectory prediction without HD-map infrastructure, and the released SD-route generation toolkit enables broader evaluation on any dataset with ego pose and future trajectories.

Abstract: This paper presents SD-RouteFusion, a deployable end-to-end ego-trajectory prediction method that fuses a front-facing camera, vehicle kinematics, and a navigation route derived from a Standard Definition (SD) map. Unlike approaches that rely on High Definition (HD) map geometry, SD-RouteFusion aligns the learning objective with scalable and production-ready SD-map route inputs, enabling route-aware prediction without requiring HD-map infrastructure. First, we demonstrate that SD-map route prior provides a powerful long-horizon semantic prior. Through a comprehensive study on a large-scale real-world dataset comprising 480k driving scenarios across 10 European countries and the U.S., we quantify the value of SD-route conditioning: incorporating SD-map routes yields a 10.5% ADE improvement over an image-and-kinematics baseline, while our full fusion strategy achieves a 16.9% ADE reduction given a prediction horizon of 8 seconds. The fusion strategy consists of a dual-hypothesis design paired with a gated classifier, to ensure robustness under route corruption and visual uncertainty. Finally, to support broader evaluation, we release an SD-route generation toolkit that enables SD-route-conditioned ego-trajectory prediction on all datasets containing ego pose and future trajectories. Together, SD-RouteFusion establishes a practical path toward robust, route-aware ego-trajectory prediction at scale.

</details>


### [136] [Relation-Centric Open-Vocabulary 3D Gaussian Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01140)
*Eunsung Cha,Hyunjoon Lee,Jaesik Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PairGS reframes 3D Gaussian segmentation as pairwise relation modeling, using sparse edge candidates and hierarchical clustering to achieve SOTA results with 50x speedup over optimization-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing open-vocabulary 3D Gaussian segmentation methods suffer from noisy segmentations or require costly per-scene optimization for per-Gaussian instance features.

Method: Proposes PairGS: (1) constructs a relation graph using view contribution weights and multi-view mask evidence from 3D Gaussians, (2) proposes sparse edge candidates via low-dimensional descriptors, (3) computes precise pairwise affinities only on candidates, (4) builds a hierarchical cluster tree for multi-granular querying.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on open-vocabulary 3D Gaussian segmentation benchmarks; the fast variant is 50x faster than optimization-based instance-feature approaches.

Conclusion: Pairwise relation modeling is an effective and efficient alternative to per-Gaussian language/instance feature optimization for 3D segmentation.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D Gaussian segmentation is challenging because it requires language understanding for diverse queries and accurate separation of Gaussians along object boundaries. Prior approaches either embed language knowledge into individual Gaussians to improve query responsiveness or optimize per-Gaussian instance features to encode object identity. However, these strategies may produce noisy Gaussian segmentations or rely on cost-inefficient per-scene optimization. We propose PairGS, a framework that reframes Gaussian segmentation as modeling pairwise relations between Gaussians. 3D Gaussian representations provide rich signals for relation estimation, such as view contribution weights and multi-view mask evidence. By leveraging these cues, PairGS explicitly constructs a relation graph for segmentation without a heavy optimization process. PairGS first proposes sparse edge candidates using low-dimensional descriptors, computes precise pairwise affinities only on those candidates, and builds a hierarchical cluster tree for multi-granular querying. It achieves state-of-the-art results on open-vocabulary 3D Gaussian segmentation benchmarks, while the fast variant is 50x faster than optimization-based instance-feature approaches.

</details>


### [137] [EquiSteer: Cross-Attention Steering Towards a Fairer Text-Guided Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01147)
*Tatiana Gaintseva,Akshit Achara,Gregory Slabaugh,Jiankang Deng,Ismail Elezi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EquiSteer is a training-free method that debiases text-to-image diffusion models per-sample at inference time by steering cross-attention activations, reducing the gender parity gap by up to 87% across multiple models with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models reproduce demographic biases from their training data (e.g., "a photo of a nurse" skews female, "a photo of a CEO" skews male). Existing debiasing methods require retraining, batch-level control, or prompt-specific tuning, which limits scalability.

Method: EquiSteer is a training-free, per-sample method. It precomputes steering vectors from contrastive prompts for each target attribute. At inference, a prompt-aware gate leaves attribute-specific prompts untouched; for neutral prompts, it clears existing attribute signals from cross-attention (CA) activations and injects a target attribute.

Result: Across SD-1.5, SD-2.1, SDXL, and SANA, EquiSteer reduces the average parity gap by up to 87%, with minimal effect on image quality and text-image alignment.

Conclusion: EquiSteer offers a scalable, training-free solution for debiasing diffusion models at inference time, achieving significant bias reduction across multiple model architectures without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models power everyday creative tasks, but they still reproduce the demographic biases in their training data. On common prompts such as ``a photo of a nurse,'' ``a photo of a CEO'', they skew their outputs toward one gender, driven by the statistics of training data rather than anything in the text. Existing debiasing methods show promise in narrow settings but require retraining, batch-level control, or prompt-specific tuning, limiting their scalability. We propose \emph{EquiSteer}, a training-free method that works per sample by steering cross-attention (CA) activations at inference time. For each target attribute, EquiSteer precomputes steering vectors from contrastive prompts. Then at generation time, a prompt-aware gate leaves attribute-specific prompts untouched, while for neutral ones it clears existing attribute signals from the CA activations and injects a target attribute. Across SD-1.5, SD-2.1, SDXL, and SANA, EquiSteer reduces the average parity gap by up to $87\%$, with minimal effect on image quality and text-image alignment. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Atmyre/EquiSteer}{https://github.com/Atmyre/EquiSteer}.%

</details>


### [138] [High-dimensional Embedding Prior for Noisy K-space Domain MRIReconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01176)
*Yu Guan,Tianjia Huang,Qinrong Cai,Qiuyun Fan,Dong Liang,Qiegen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a high-dimensional k-space reconstruction framework that enhances diffusion-based MRI reconstruction by lifting data representation space, improving performance especially in high-noise regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models struggle to handle noisy reconstruction settings, especially when operating directly in k-space domain, limiting their applicability to realistic MRI acquisition conditions.

Method: A unified high-dimensional k-space reconstruction framework that augments the data representation space (representation lifting) rather than modifying underlying optimization procedures, enabling existing diffusion-based solvers to operate on enriched k-space embeddings with improved expressiveness.

Result: Consistent improvement in reconstruction quality for multiple diffusion-based inverse solvers across varying noise levels and undersampling factors, with largest gains observed in high-noise regimes, supported by theoretical analysis of error propagation.

Conclusion: High-dimensional representation provides a general, model-agnostic mechanism for improving diffusion-based MRI reconstruction in noisy settings, offering a new perspective on robust k-space generative modeling for practical inverse problems.

Abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) reconstruction under realistic acquisition conditions can be fundamentally viewed as estimating the underlying k-space distribution from incomplete and noise-corrupted measurements. While diffusion models have recently shown strong potential as generative prior for inverse problems,existingapproachesstruggletohandlenoisyreconstruction settings, especially when operating directly in k-space domain. In this work, we propose a unified high-dimensional k-space reconstruction framework tailored for noisy inverse problems, whichenhancesdiffusion-based solversthroughrepresentation lifting.Ratherthanmodifyingthe underlying optimization procedures, the proposed framework augments the data representation space, enabling existing diffusion-based solvers to operate on enriched k-space embeddings with improved expressiveness. Extensive experiments on both in-house and public datasets across varying noise levels and undersampled factors demonstrate that the proposed frame work consistently improves reconstruction quality for multiple diffusion-based inverse solvers. Notably, the largest gains are observed in high-noise regimes, which is consistent with our theoretical analysis of error propagation under high-dimensional representation. These results suggest that high-dimensional representation provides a general and model-agnostic mechanism for improving diffusion-based MRI reconstruction in noisy settings, offering a new perspective on robust k-space generative modeling for practical inverse problems. The code will be available at https://github.com/yqx7150/HEP-MRIRec.

</details>


### [139] [Perceive-to-Reason: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01191)
*Hongxing Li,Xiufeng Huang,Dingming Li,Wenjing Jiang,Zixuan Wang,Haolei Xu,Hanrong Zhang,Haiwen Hong,Longtao Huang,Hui Xue,Weiming Lu,Jun Xiao,Yueting Zhuang,Yongliang Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P2R proposes a two-stage framework (Perceiver then Reasoner) with PRA-GRPO reinforcement learning that explicitly decouples perception from reasoning, achieving strong results on fine-grained visual reasoning benchmarks (e.g., 93.2% on V-Star with 4B model).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-grained visual reasoning is difficult because small but critical visual cues in high-resolution images are easily missed. Existing methods use cropping or visual search but fail to explicitly separate perception from reasoning, limiting their effectiveness.

Method: P2R decomposes fine-grained visual reasoning into two stages: (1) a Perceiver that localizes question-relevant visual evidence, and (2) a Reasoner that answers based on the annotated image and cropped regions. It also introduces PRA-GRPO, a role-aware reinforcement learning strategy that alternates perception-focused and reasoning-focused updates with only final-answer supervision.

Result: Built on Qwen3-VL-Instruct (2B/4B/8B), P2R consistently improves across scales. P2R-4B achieves 93.2% on V-Star, 81.9% on HR-Bench-4K, and 80.5% on HR-Bench-8K, significantly outperforming its backbone. Benefits also extend to broader multimodal reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Explicitly decoupling perception from reasoning provides an effective framework for fine-grained visual reasoning, as demonstrated by consistent improvements across model scales and benchmarks.

Abstract: Fine-grained visual reasoning remains challenging for vision-language models, especially when small but critical visual cues are buried in high-resolution images. Existing approaches rely on repeated cropping or test-time visual search to introduce local evidence, but they typically do not explicitly distinguish perception from reasoning. In this paper, we propose Perceive-to-Reason (P2R), a unified framework that formulates fine-grained visual reasoning as a two-stage process: the model first localizes question-relevant evidence as a Perceiver, and then answers the question as a Reasoner based on the annotated image and cropped regions. To better align training with this decoupled formulation, we further introduce Perception-Reasoning Alternating GRPO (PRA-GRPO), a role-aware reinforcement learning strategy that alternates between perception-focused and reasoning-focused updates using only final-answer supervision. Built on top of Qwen3-VL-Instruct-2B/4B/8B, P2R consistently improves performance across model scales. In particular, P2R-4B achieves 93.2% on V-Star, 81.9% on HR-Bench-4K, and 80.5% on HR-Bench-8K, substantially outperforming its corresponding backbone. Further experiments show that the benefits of P2R extend beyond high-resolution benchmarks to broader multimodal reasoning tasks. These results suggest that explicitly decoupling perception from reasoning provides an effective framework for fine-grained visual reasoning.

</details>


### [140] [Linkify: Learning from Interface-Augmented Assembly Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01205)
*Anushrut Jignasu,Daniele Grandi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Linkify is a framework that learns from interface-augmented assembly graphs to perform context-aware part retrieval in mechanical assemblies, using corrected interface geometry and Graph Attention Networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative AI methods for CAD focus on isolated parts or monolithic assemblies, ignoring the rich geometric information at interfaces between parts where function is realized.

Method: Linkify recomputes high-fidelity interface geometry for the Fusion 360 Gallery Assembly dataset, constructs assembly graphs with node (part) and edge (interface) features via a pretrained point-cloud encoder, and trains a GATv2-based Graph Attention Network for masked part prediction.

Result: Linkify outperforms non-graph baselines (logistic regression, k-NN) on Top-K accuracy and F1 scores. Ablation studies show accurate contact computation and dynamic attention over interfaces are critical.

Conclusion: The corrected interface dataset and training pipeline provide a foundation for future interface-aware models in assembly retrieval, validation, and generative design.

Abstract: We present Linkify, a framework for learning from interface-augmented assembly graphs to enable context-aware part retrieval in mechanical assemblies. While recent generative AI methods for CAD have focused largely on isolated parts or monolithic assemblies, the rich geometric information at the interfaces between parts, where function is realized, remains underexplored. We address this gap by recomputing high-fidelity interface geometry for the Fusion 360 Gallery Assembly dataset, correcting missing and erroneous contacts, and generating point-cloud representations of local contact regions. Using this data, we construct assembly graphs whose nodes encode part geometry and whose edges encode interface geometry via a pretrained point-cloud encoder. On top of this representation, we train a Graph Attention Network based on GATv2 to solve a masked part prediction task: given an assembly with one part held out, the model predicts the class of the missing component from a large vocabulary of geometrically clustered parts, thereby approximating a realistic part-retrieval scenario. Compared to non-graph baselines such as logistic regression and k-nearest neighbors operating on aggregated node features, Linkify achieves higher Top-K accuracy and F1 scores. Ablation studies on graph connectivity, edge attributes, and attention mechanisms demonstrate that accurate contact computation and dynamic attention over interfaces are critical for performance. Our corrected interface dataset and training pipeline, released publicly, provide a foundation for future interface-aware models for assembly retrieval, validation, and generative design.

</details>


### [141] [Ink3D: Sculpting 3D Assets with Extremely Complex Textures via Video Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01222)
*Yue Han,Chong Li,Zhening Liu,Cong Huang,Fang Deng,Yong Liu,Fangyun Wei,Yan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ink3D decouples 3D geometry and texture generation by using a white-mesh model for geometry and a video generative model (OrbitPainter) plus neural baking (TextureOptimizer) to synthesize rich, coherent textures from multi-view video.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D generative models produce high-quality geometry but struggle with intricate textures due to limited 3D training data. Meanwhile, large-scale visual generative models excel at complex patterns. This gap motivates leveraging video priors for 3D texture synthesis.

Method: (1) Reconstruct white-mesh geometry via off-the-shelf 3D generation. (2) Use OrbitPainter (conditional video generative model) to produce dense orbit-scan videos across viewpoints. (3) Apply TextureOptimizer (neural baking module) to convert multi-view observations into coherent textures while handling geometry inconsistencies.

Result: Ink3D achieves significantly richer and more faithful texture generation compared to prior approaches, enabling synthesis of extremely complex surface appearances.

Conclusion: Decoupling geometry and texture synthesis and leveraging large-scale pretrained video priors is an effective strategy for high-quality 3D texture generation, overcoming the data scarcity bottleneck.

Abstract: Recent 3D generative models can synthesize high-quality geometry but often struggle to reproduce intricate textures from reference images, largely due to the scarcity of large-scale 3D training data with rich surface appearance. In contrast, visual generative models are trained on datasets several orders of magnitude larger and excel at modeling complex visual patterns. Motivated by this gap, we introduce Ink3D, a framework that bridges 3D generation with large-scale video generative models to synthesize extremely complex textures. Ink3D first reconstructs a white-mesh geometry using an off-the-shelf 3D generation model. It then employs OrbitPainter, a conditional video generative model, to produce dense orbit-scan videos capturing object appearance across viewpoints. To convert these views into coherent textures, we introduce TextureOptimizer, a neural baking module that integrates dense multi-view observations while mitigating geometry inconsistencies arising from video generation. By decoupling geometry and texture synthesis and leveraging large-scale pretrained video priors, Ink3D enables significantly richer and more faithful texture generation than prior approaches.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [142] [EmbodimentSemantic: A Spatial Scene-Graph Dataset and Benchmark for Vision-Language Models on Embodied Manipulation Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00020)
*Hassan Jaber,Refinath S N,Luca Cagliero,Christopher E. Mower,Haitham Bou-Ammar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EmbodimentSemantic is a spatial scene-graph dataset and benchmark for evaluating relational grounding in robotic manipulation, featuring real-world data from a SO101 robot arm and simulated LIBERO data with 60K+ frames, showing that current VLMs struggle with depth-aware and viewpoint-dependent spatial structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatial grounding (understanding object arrangements like support, containment, ordering, occlusion, and depth relations) remains a key limitation in VLA systems for robotic manipulation, as current models lack explicit representation of spatial relations between objects.

Method: The authors introduce EmbodimentSemantic, a dataset using directed object-relation-object triplets for scene representation. It includes real-world SO101 robot arm data and a simulated LIBERO benchmark (60K+ frames, 120K+ scene graphs) with ground-truth relations derived automatically from MuJoCo geometry and camera projections. They test scene graph injection into VLA policy prompts.

Result: Current open-source and commercial VLMs predict plausible relations but struggle with exact depth-aware and viewpoint-dependent spatial structure.

Conclusion: EmbodimentSemantic provides a unified framework for diagnosing spatial grounding in VLM perception and testing its utility for VLA manipulation.

Abstract: Spatial grounding remains a key limitation of vision-language-action (VLA) systems for robotic manipulation. While current models can recognize objects and follow language instructions, they often lack an explicit representation of how objects are arranged in space, including support, containment, ordering, occlusion, and depth-sensitive relations. We introduce EmbodimentSemantic, a spatial scene-graph dataset and benchmark for evaluating relational grounding in embodied manipulation. EmbodimentSemantic represents scenes as directed object-relation-object triplets, where each triplet specifies a spatial relation between an ordered pair of objects using a fixed set of relations. This representation enables direct evaluation of object binding, relation prediction, and spatial consistency. The dataset includes real-world manipulation observations collected with the low-cost SO101 robot arm, together with generated scene graphs for studying spatial grounding in practical robotic settings. To provide controlled validation, we also introduce a simulator-grounded LIBERO benchmark with over 60K manipulation frames and more than 120K camera-specific scene graphs across paired third-person and wrist views, where ground-truth relations are derived automatically from MuJoCo geometry, world coordinates, camera projections, and visibility constraints. We further test whether scene graphs improve downstream control by injecting them into existing VLA policy prompts. Experiments across open-source and commercial VLMs show that current models often predict plausible relations but struggle with exact depth-aware and viewpoint-dependent spatial structure. EmbodimentSemantic provides a unified framework for diagnosing spatial grounding in VLM perception and testing its utility for VLA manipulation.

</details>


### [143] [When to Personalize Household Object Search: A Rigidity-Gated Hybrid Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00022)
*Xianyao Li,Yuhai Wang,Hu Xiao,Kaleb Smith,Gilbert Yang Ye,Eric Jing Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PerSim is a rigidity-gated hybrid policy for service robot search that personalizes object-location priors based on resident personality traits (Big Five) only when placement behavior is variable, using synthetic data generation and human validation to improve search efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Service robots need spatial priors for efficient object search, but object locations vary with resident traits. Collecting real longitudinal, trait-specific in-home data is invasive and difficult to scale.

Method: Proposes PerSim, a rigidity-gated hybrid policy combining trait-conditioned prior with population-frequency baseline. Uses human-calibrated simulation to generate object-placement transitions, injects continuous Big Five trait vectors to predict room-level priors and co-occurrence cues. Personalization is applied only for low-rigidity (variable placement) objects.

Result: Validation with N=200 human study: synthetic transitions are behaviorally plausible (3.85/5, p<1e-6); personalization favored for low-rigidity objects (p=0.005); significant improvement on unseen continuous trait vectors over nearest discrete matching (p=0.035); PerSim reduces expected search cost in home digital twin end-to-end.

Conclusion: Personalization helps selectively for objects with variable placement behavior. The rigidity-gated approach yields a principled decision rule for when to personalize. The synthetic data pipeline and continuous trait interpolation offer scalable, non-invasive personalization for service robots.

Abstract: Service robots searching for household objects rely on spatial priors to reduce search cost, yet object locations can vary with resident traits. Collecting longitudinal, trait-specific in-home trajectories is invasive and hard to scale. We study when personalization helps and propose PerSim, a rigidity-gated hybrid policy that combines a trait-conditioned prior with a population-frequency baseline, personalizing only when placement behavior is variable. To scale resident-conditioned dynamics, we employ a human-calibrated simulation pipeline to generate and validate object-placement transitions in diverse home layouts, and train a predictor that injects continuous Big Five vectors to output room-level priors and within-room co-occurrence cues. In a unified human study (N=200), dual-layer validation shows that (i) synthetic transitions are judged behaviorally plausible (mean 3.85/5, p < 1e-6), and (ii) in a blinded A/B comparison, personalization is favored primarily for low-rigidity objects (p=0.005), while the population-frequency baseline remains strong for universally placed items, yielding a decision rule for when to personalize. In an offline objective test, we observe a small but significant improvement on unseen continuous trait vectors over nearest discrete configuration matching (p=0.035), supporting interpolation in five-dimensional trait space. Finally, in a home digital twin we show that PerSim reduces expected search cost by combining room visitation effort with within-room cue checking, demonstrating end-to-end gains beyond isolated prediction metrics.

</details>


### [144] [Decentralized Geometric Control for Cable-Suspended Payload Transport with Adaptive Mass Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00024)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Benedikt Walter,Paul Schmitt,Miguel Hurtado*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GPAC is a four-layer hierarchical control architecture enabling N quadrotors to cooperatively transport a cable-suspended payload without centralized coordination or inter-agent knowledge of N/payload mass, achieving implicit coordination via local cable measurements, geometric control on manifolds, and a CBF-based safety filter with formal ISSf guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cooperative aerial transport requires controllers that handle nonlinear manifold geometry, operate without centralized coordination, and respect operational safety constraints—challenges not jointly addressed by existing approaches.

Method: A four-layer hierarchical architecture with: implicit coordination via local cable measurements; geometric position/attitude control on the full nonlinear configuration manifold; extended-state observer for wind rejection; concurrent learning-based mass estimation without persistent excitation; and a priority-ordered CBF-based safety filter with input-to-state safety guarantees.

Result: High-fidelity simulation with flexible cables, onboard sensor fusion, and wind turbulence yields a mean payload-tracking RMSE of 33.8 cm (2.8% coefficient of variation over 13 seeds) at low per-agent computational cost.

Conclusion: GPAC demonstrates that fully decentralized, geometrically rigorous, and safety-constrained cooperative aerial transport is achievable with minimal communication and strong formal guarantees.

Abstract: Cooperative aerial transport requires controllers that respect nonlinear manifold geometry, operate without centralized coordination, and respect operational safety constraints. To address these demands, we present GPAC, a four-layer hierarchical architecture that enables $N$ quadrotors to transport a cable-suspended payload without a central coordinator or by exchanging cable states or adaptive parameters. The key insight is implicit coordination: each quadrotor independently estimates its effective load share from local cable measurements, so combined forces converge to the correct total, even without knowledge of $N$ or the payload mass; the payload position is reconstructed locally from each agent's own cable geometry, and the only inter-agent communication is a low-rate neighbor-position broadcast for collision avoidance. GPAC operates directly on the full nonlinear configuration manifold and integrates geometric position and attitude control, anti-swing regulation, an extended-state observer for wind rejection, concurrent learning-based mass estimation without persistent excitation, and a priority-ordered control barrier function (CBF)-inspired safety filter that reduces operational risk, with input-to-state safety (ISSf) margins that hold exactly under single-constraint activation. A compatibility result shows that the filter's force modifications keep the desired attitude within the almost-global stability region of the $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ attitude controller. Finally, high-fidelity simulation with flexible cables, onboard sensor fusion, and wind turbulence -- with all control and estimation loops closed through the estimator -- yields a mean payload-tracking RMSE of 33.8 cm (2.8\% coefficient of variation over 13 seeds) at a low per-agent computational cost.

</details>


### [145] [FLYNN: Robust Neural Network for Robot Navigation using Fly Brain Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00025)
*Benquan Wang,Jingdao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces FLYNN, a recurrent neural network derived from the fruit fly brain connectome, which achieves competitive performance on vision-based navigation while exhibiting superior robustness to out-of-distribution data and sensory loss compared to hand-crafted networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models are brittle under new environments or sensory deprivation, unlike biological systems. The authors aim to address this vulnerability by leveraging biological brain topology for more resilient AI.

Method: The authors construct a recurrent neural network (FLYNN) whose architecture is directly derived from the synaptic-resolution brain connectome of Drosophila melanogaster, and train it for vision-based navigation tasks in MuJoCo.

Result: FLYNN achieves performance comparable to modern hand-crafted networks of similar size, but exhibits superior resistance to out-of-distribution data and tolerance to sensory loss. It remained functional even under total vision loss where hand-crafted networks failed, even with specific training for camera dropout.

Conclusion: Using biological brain connectomes as architectural blueprints offers a promising new direction for designing resilient artificial agents.

Abstract: While deep learning models achieve state-of-the-art performance in complex tasks, they remain brittle when faced with new environments or sensory deprivation. In contrast, biological systems exhibit remarkable tolerance to these challenges. We address this vulnerability by developing a recurrent neural network (RNN) whose architecture is directly derived from the synaptic-resolution brain connectome of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster. We demonstrate the feasibility of training the fly connectome neural network (FLYNN) to perform vision-based navigation in MuJoCo, achieving performance comparable to modern hand-crafted networks of similar parameter counts. Crucially, FLYNN exhibits superior resistance to out-of-distribution (OOD) data and tolerance to sensory loss without further training. It remained functional even under total vision loss while hand-crafted networks largely failed, even when specifically trained with camera dropout. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) of the internal state of FLYNN suggests that it exhibits a particularly high degree of representational modularity, which might be related to its robustness. Our work provides a new direction for designing resilient artificial agents following the topology of biological brains.

</details>


### [146] [Invariant Stochastic Filtering on SE(3) for Inertial-Encoder State Estimation of Serial Rigid Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00026)
*S. Yaqubi,J. Mattila*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An IEKF for serial rigid manipulators is developed on SE(3), featuring group-affine kinematics, physically separated noise modeling, modular chain structure with O(n) cost, and proven exponential stability via Lie algebra Lyapunov analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a state estimation method for serial rigid manipulators that is both theoretically rigorous (exact error covariance propagation) and computationally scalable (linear in link count), while properly handling the nonlinear noise propagation from gyroscopes and accelerometers.

Method: Formulates the kinematic equations within the Lie group SE(3), leveraging the group-affine property for autonomous linearized error dynamics. Uses a physically separated noise model: accelerometer provides translational twist with sample-interval-scaled covariance; gyroscope noise is modeled as state-dependent Coriolis noise. Structures the filter as a modular chain of per-link IEKFs, where covariance propagation is chained via Adjoint transformation, achieving O(n) complexity.

Result: Establishes exponential ultimate boundedness in mean square using a Lie algebra Lyapunov function, with per-link bounds chained through the Adjoint operator norm. Numerical results validate the filter design.

Conclusion: The proposed modular IEKF chain provides a scalable, provably stable solution for state estimation of serial rigid manipulators, with rigorous covariance propagation and physically meaningful noise modeling.

Abstract: An invariant extended Kalman filter (IEKF) is developed for state estimation of serial rigid manipulators with an arbitrary number of links, formulated entirely within the Lie group SE(3). The group-affine property of the kinematic equations makes the linearised error dynamics autonomous, so the Riccati equation governs the true error covariance rather than a local approximation. A physically separated noise model treats gyroscope and accelerometer channels independently: the accelerometer provides translational twist via gravity-compensated integration, yielding a measurement covariance that scales with the sample interval in exact analogy with process noise discretisation; a state-dependent Coriolis noise term captures gyroscope noise propagating through the nonlinear dynamics, vanishing at rest and growing with twist magnitude. The filter is structured as a modular chain of per-link IEKFs in which the predicted covariance of each link depends on its predecessor only through the Adjoint-transformed posterior, giving linear computational cost in link count. Exponential ultimate boundedness in mean square is established via a Lie algebra Lyapunov function, with per-link bounds chained through the Adjoint operator norm to yield a stability certificate that is modular and scalable to arbitrary chain length. Numerical results validate the design.

</details>


### [147] [Urban Deceleration Behavior Modes Under Scene Context: An Early-Kinematic Classifier from Argoverse 2 Multi-Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00027)
*Eni Solomon Laughter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper identifies four stable deceleration modes (anticipatory soft, reactive closing, brake-like jerk, outlier) from urban driving data using clustering, finding that early jerk predicts modes well, pair age modulates behavior, and modes are stable at medium but not low speeds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban deceleration lacks taxonomic organization despite being widely studied in car-following research; the availability of perception-equipped autonomous-vehicle datasets now enables trajectory-anchored mode discovery.

Method: Extracted 1,219 sustained deceleration events from 234 Argoverse 2 Sensor urban driving logs, encoded them in 19-dimensional kinematic feature vectors, applied K-means clustering with bootstrap stability analysis, and used a HistGradientBoosting classifier to predict mode membership from the first 1.0 s.

Result: Four stable modes found: anticipatory soft (62.8%), reactive closing (30.6%), brake-like jerk (4.8%), outlier (1.8%). Bootstrap ARI = 0.897. Pair age had medium effect (ε²=0.085); geometry and VRU proximity had negligible effects. Early-event classifier macro-F1 = 0.758 at 1.0 s; scene context added +0.059 F1. Modes regime-invariant at medium speed (ARI=0.817) but regime-dependent at low speed (ARI=0.166).

Conclusion: A small set of stable kinematic modes structures urban deceleration; early-window jerk is the dominant predictive signal; pair age is the primary contextual modulator.

Abstract: Urban deceleration is one of the most empirically studied yet least taxonomically organized behaviors in car-following research. Recent perception-equipped autonomous-vehicle datasets enable trajectory-anchored mode discovery. We extract 1,219 sustained deceleration events from 234 urban driving logs of the Argoverse 2 Sensor dataset, encode each event in a 19-dimensional kinematic feature vector, discover behavioral modes via K-means clustering with bootstrap stability analysis, and quantify modulation by eleven scene-context variables. A HistGradientBoosting classifier predicts mode membership from the first 1.0 s of each event. Four stable modes emerge with a bootstrap Adjusted Rand Index of 0.897 across 50 resamples: anticipatory soft (62.8%), reactive closing (30.6%), brake-like jerk (4.8%), and an outlier category (1.8%). Only pair age shows a medium effect (epsilon^2 = 0.085); scene geometry and vulnerable-road-user proximity show negligible effects. The early-event classifier achieves macro-F1 = 0.758 at 1.0 s, with scene context contributing +0.059 F1 over kinematics alone. Modes are regime-invariant in medium-speed driving (ARI = 0.817) but regime-dependent at low speed (ARI = 0.166). A small set of stable kinematic modes structures urban deceleration; early-window jerk dominates predictive signal; and pair age is the primary contextual modulator.

</details>


### [148] [Trajectory Learning with Graph Representations for Social Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00028)
*Berke Kartal,Burcu Kilic,Yigit Yildirim,Emre Ugur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes an imitation learning framework with spatiotemporal graph-based social attention and trajectory-level learning for socially compliant robot navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior methods fail to address both spatial and temporal characteristics of real-world pedestrian behavior; RL requires hand-crafted rewards, and Imitation Learning lacks social interaction modeling and suffers from error accumulation.

Method: Graph-based auxiliary network to encode crowd states via attention to pedestrians + navigation module capturing temporal dynamics with trajectory-level learning to mitigate error accumulation.

Result: Outperforms established data-driven baselines on simulation and real-world dataset across diverse social metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed imitation learning framework effectively addresses both spatial and temporal dynamics for socially compliant navigation.

Abstract: Autonomous mobile robots are expected to exhibit socially compliant navigation for minimizing pedestrian disturbance. While capturing social interactions and incorporating pedestrian motion estimations into decision-making are beneficial for compliance, prior methods fail to address both spatial and temporal characteristics present in real-world data. Reinforcement Learning offers high capability, but it requires hand-crafted reward functions that reduce social behavior to static criteria, limiting its ability to reproduce patterns that exist in real pedestrian behavior. Imitation Learning offers direct training from real-world data but lacks modeling of social interactions and suffers from error accumulation. To this end, we propose an imitation learning framework that leverages spatiotemporal dynamics for socially compliant navigation. To represent social context based on interactions, we introduce a graph-based auxiliary network that encodes crowd states by attending to pedestrians. In addition, we present a navigation module that captures temporal dynamics and mitigates error accumulations by incorporating encoded state predictions and employing a trajectory-level learning objective. Our framework outperforms established data-driven baselines on simulation and a real-world dataset across diverse social metrics.

</details>


### [149] [Memory-Native Non-Terrestrial Networks for Embodied Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00029)
*Chengyang Li,Yikun Wang,Jiahui He,Yujie Wan,Shuai Wang,Yuan Wu,Yik-Chung Wu,Chengzhong Xu,Huseyin Arslan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes MemNTN, a memory-native paradigm for non-terrestrial networks that uses a dual-memory architecture (physical + digital) to improve embodied intelligence connectivity, outperforming conventional stateless approaches in satellite-based question answering tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NTN protocols are memoryless and inefficient for embodied intelligence applications because decisions are driven only by local channel conditions and instantaneous service demands, failing in highly-dynamic, resource-constrained, topology-varying environments.

Method: The MemNTN paradigm uses a dual-memory architecture distinguishing physical memory (world state) and digital memory (historical network experience), with mechanisms for acquisition, compression, valuation, update, and utilization enabling cross-layer memory-native decision-making.

Result: Experiments in satellite embodied question answering (SEQA) show that MemNTN significantly outperforms conventional stateless NTN and terrestrial approaches.

Conclusion: Memory-native optimization using long-horizon contexts is an effective paradigm shift for NTN to support embodied intelligence, addressing the limitations of memoryless protocols.

Abstract: Non-terrestrial networks (NTN) provide ubiquitous connectivity for embodied intelligence (EI), enabling robots in wilderness to leverage cloud resources or report critical information to remote centers. However, the synergy is nontrivial due to the highly-dynamic, resource-constrained, topology-varying, and task-oriented environment. Existing memoryless NTN protocols become inefficient, since the decisions are driven by local channel conditions and instantaneous service demands. To address these limitations, this paper proposes the memory-native NTN (MemNTN) paradigm that leverages long-horizon contexts for memory augmented system optimization. To realize this paradigm shift, we establish a dual-memory architecture that distinguishes between physical memory representing the state of the world and digital memory encoding historical network experience. We develop memory acquisition, compression, valuation, update, and utilization mechanisms that facilitate cross-layer, memory-native decision-making, spanning from the physical and access layers up to the network and application layers. Experiments in satellite embodied question answering (SEQA) demonstrate that the proposed MemNTN significantly outperforms conventional stateless NTN and terrestrial approaches.

</details>


### [150] [A Unified Benchmark for RCM-Constrained Visual Servoing: Modeling-Controller Interaction and Robustness Analysis in Laparoscopic Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00030)
*Jing Zhang,Mengtang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an open-source simulation framework for systematically evaluating different RCM constraint formulations and IBVS control architectures in robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery, revealing key structural sensitivities through unified benchmark comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate RCM enforcement is critical for safe automatic FoV adjustment in robot-assisted laparoscopic MIS, but systematic comparison of different RCM formulations and IBVS frameworks is hindered by the lack of a unified, reproducible benchmark.

Method: The authors develop an open-source simulation framework that integrates three RCM modeling approaches and six IBVS-based control architectures under a unified velocity-level formulation, enabling controlled and consistent comparative evaluation through structured case studies.

Result: The framework reveals key structural sensitivities including the impact of tangent-plane definition, constraint dimensionality, open- vs closed-loop enforcement, and robustness issues near kinematic singularities arising from modeling and controller interactions.

Conclusion: The released open-source framework and demonstrations provide a reproducible foundation for future RCM-constrained visual servoing research, enabling systematic benchmarking and comparison in this domain.

Abstract: In robot-assisted laparoscopic minimally invasive surgery (MIS), accurate enforcement of the remote center of motion (RCM) constraint is critical for safe and stable automatic field-of-view (FoV) adjustment. Although control-based RCM strategies are widely adopted due to their flexibility and cost-effectiveness, systematic comparison of different RCM formulations and image-based visual servoing (IBVS) frameworks remains challenging due to the lack of a unified and reproducible benchmark. This paper presents an open-source simulation framework integrating three representative RCM modeling approaches and six IBVS-based control architectures within a unified velocity-level formulation, enabling controlled and consistent evaluation. Through structured case studies, the framework reveals key structural sensitivities arising from modeling and controller interactions, including the impact of tangent-plane definition, constraint dimensionality, open- versus closed-loop enforcement, and robustness near kinematic singularities. All resources are released and demostrations are provided in the supplementary video, providing a reproducible foundation for RCM-constrained visual servoing research.

</details>


### [151] [Joint Discovery of Object and Action Symbols through Effect Prediction for Robotic Manipulation Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00031)
*Burcu Kilic,Berke Kartal,Fatih Dogangun,Erhan Oztop,Emre Ugur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a model that jointly discovers manipulation primitives and object categories through a binary bottleneck layer from random interaction data, enabling effect-driven discrete planning with partial action executions for precise low-level control and few-shot generalization to novel objects, outperforming baselines on tabletop tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of prior work that either categorizes objects by visual appearance (failing to distinguish visually similar but behaviorally different objects) or by interaction effects but limited to predefined actions.

Method: A binary bottleneck layer jointly discovers manipulation primitives and object categories by predicting multi-modal outcomes (motion, contact, force feedback) from random interaction data. A discrete planning method uses intermediate effect trajectory steps for partial action executions. Novel objects are categorized by comparing interaction effects with predicted effects of learned object symbols.

Result: The effect-driven planning approach outperforms both a state-of-the-art method and a visual-based alternative in planning precision on tabletop repositioning and stacking tasks, for both seen and novel objects.

Conclusion: Jointly discovering action primitives and object categories from interaction effects, rather than visual appearance, yields better generalization and planning precision for robotic manipulation tasks.

Abstract: To perform complex manipulation planning, autonomous robots are required to abstract continuous, high-dimensional sensorimotor interactions into discrete object and action representations. Earlier work either categorized objects based on visual appearances, which fails to distinguish objects that appear similar but behave differently, or based on effects under interaction, but was limited to predefined actions. To address these limitations, we propose a model that jointly discovers high-level manipulation primitives and object categories through a binary bottleneck layer, trained to predict multi-modal outcomes, including object motion, contact, and force feedback, from random interaction data. Building on these discovered binary representations, we leverage a discrete planning method that uses intermediate steps in the predicted effect trajectory to enable partial action executions for precise low-level control. Additionally, we evaluate our framework's generalization capabilities on novel objects by assigning object categories through comparing a small number of interaction effects with the predicted effects of learned object symbols, enabling few-shot generalization based on behavior rather than visual similarity. We conduct experiments on tabletop repositioning and stacking tasks, and confirm that our effect-driven planning approach outperforms both a state-of-the-art method and a visual-based alternative in planning precision across seen and novel objects.

</details>


### [152] [Learning Dexterous Manipulation Using Contact Wrench Guidance From Human Demonstration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00033)
*Xinghao Zhu,Zixi Liu,Shalin Jain,Chenran Li,Milad Noori,Huihua Zhao,John Welsh,Michael Andres Lin,Wei Liu,Tingwu Wang,Xingye Da,Zhengyi Luo,Vishal Kulkarni,Naema Bhatti,Yuke Zhu,Linxi Fan,Bowen Wen,Danfei Xu,Soha Pouya,Yan Chang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CHORD is a framework that uses contact wrench space guidance from human demonstrations to enable reinforcement learning for dexterous robot manipulation, achieving high success rates on a large benchmark and transferring to real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between abundant human demonstrations and robot policies for dexterous manipulation, addressing the challenge of transferring demonstration data to effective robot control in contact-rich scenarios.

Method: CHORD uses object-centric contact wrench space guidance, representing human and robot motions by induced forces/torques on objects, enabling similarity measurement through instantaneous motions, combined with reinforcement learning for long-horizon tasks.

Result: Achieved 82.12% success rate on 1,831 benchmark tasks from a dataset of 4,739 bimanual dexterous manipulation tasks; 90.77% success rate on whole-body manipulation; policies transferred to real-world open-loop and closed-loop settings.

Conclusion: Contact wrench space guidance effectively scales reinforcement learning for dexterous manipulation, and the learned policies generalize across tasks and transfer to real-world robots.

Abstract: Dexterous robot manipulation can benefit from the abundance of human demonstrations, but transferring such demonstrations to robot policies remains challenging. We present Contact Wrench Guidance from Human Demonstration in Robotic Dexterous Manipulation (CHORD), a framework for long-horizon manipulation of rigid and articulated objects with reinforcement learning. The key idea is object-centric contact wrench space guidance: we represent human and robot motions by the forces and torques they can induce on the object, enabling similarity to be measured by the induced instantaneous motions. This guidance makes reinforcement learning more scalable for contact-rich dexterous manipulation. We further introduce a large-scale simulation benchmark with 4,739 bimanual dexterous manipulation tasks, constructed from motion-capture datasets and reconstructed in-house videos. Evaluated on 1,831 benchmark tasks, CHORD achieves an average success rate of 82.12%, demonstrating strong scalability. CHORD also generalizes to whole-body manipulation from hand-only and third-person demonstrations, achieving a 90.77% success rate, and the learned policies transfer to the real world in both open-loop and closed-loop settings.

</details>


### [153] [Optimal any-angle path planning in static and dynamic environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00065)
*Yiyuan Zou,Clark Borst*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces Zeta* and Zeta*-SIPP, two optimal any-angle path planning algorithms using elliptical forward expansion and field-of-view scanning techniques, achieving significant speedups over prior methods in both static and dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most any-angle path planning algorithms cannot guarantee optimal solutions, especially in dynamic environments with moving obstacles. The authors aim to develop optimal any-angle planners that work efficiently in both static and dynamic settings.

Method: Two general techniques: 1) elliptical forward expansion (restricts search space using ellipse-based neighborhoods) and 2) field of view (replaces line-of-sight for faster visibility checks). These are integrated via inverted scanning (from open nodes) and forward scanning (from closed nodes). Zeta* uses these for static environments; Zeta*-SIPP extends to dynamic environments.

Result: Zeta* with forward scanning achieves comparable performance to the state-of-the-art Anya algorithm. Zeta*-SIPP is more than 20x faster than the optimal planner TO-AA-SIPP in dynamic environments.

Conclusion: The research identifies key requirements for optimal any-angle path planning and provides a unified approach (Zeta* framework) that works across static and dynamic environments, with Zeta* being more extensible than prior optimal methods like Anya.

Abstract: Any-angle path planning extends traditional graph-based path planning by allowing movement between any pair of vertices, rather than being restricted by predefined edges. It can find straighter and shorter paths in continuous space with graphs, making it particularly suitable for navigation in open areas such as airspaces, warehouses, and oceans. Many any-angle path-planning algorithms have been proposed, but only a few can guarantee optimal solutions, especially in the presence of dynamic obstacles. To address this challenge, this article focuses on optimal any-angle path planning on grids and introduces two general techniques that accelerate computation while preserving optimality in both static and dynamic environments: 1) elliptical forward expansion, which leverages ellipse-based neighborhoods to restrict the search space, and 2) field of view, which replaces traditional line-of-sight methods to speed up visibility checks. To integrate these two techniques, inverted and forward scanning are introduced. Inverted scanning establishes visual connections from open nodes, whereas forward scanning initiates scans from closed nodes. Building on the proposed techniques, Zeta* and Zeta*-SIPP are developed for static and dynamic environments respectively. Zeta*, when combined with forward scanning, is similar to the state-of-the-art algorithm Anya and attains comparable performance. Unlike Anya, Zeta* can be readily extended to other settings, such as dynamic environments (e.g., Zeta*-SIPP). Zeta*-SIPP, with either scanning method, is more than 20 times faster than the corresponding state-of-the-art optimal planner TO-AA-SIPP. Overall, this research identifies the key requirements for achieving optimal any-angle path planning and introduces a unified approach suitable for different environments.

</details>


### [154] [Learning Expert Strategy for Autonomous Robotic Endovascular Intervention via Decoupled Procedural Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00066)
*Yanxi Chen,Tianliang Yao,Shaolong Tang,Jiyuan Zhao,Hengyu Hu,Zhaoxing Li,Antonio J. Sánchez Egea,Peng Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning framework that decouples high-level strategic decision-making from low-level execution to achieve safe, expert-level autonomous endovascular navigation, achieving >96% success rate with 29.3% fewer steps and 13% lower trajectory variance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Endovascular interventions require high precision but manual operation suffers from performance variability. Existing RL approaches for autonomous navigation lack explicit constraint satisfaction and safety guarantees.

Method: A two-level framework: (1) a strategic RL policy generates global navigation intents (high-level), (2) an expert-informed execution module refines these intents to adhere to expert norms, kinematic limits, and vessel safety constraints (low-level).

Result: >96% navigation success rate, 29.3% reduction in operational steps, 13% reduction in trajectory variance, outperforming baselines and replicating expert-level proficiency in both simulation and real-world robotic platforms.

Conclusion: The decoupled framework enhances predictability, safety, and consistency of robotic endovascular interventions, aligning autonomous behavior with clinical norms.

Abstract: Endovascular interventions are high-stakes procedures requiring precise device operation within complex and tortuous vascular anatomies. Autonomous endovascular navigation has the potential to standardize procedural quality and reduce the performance variability inherent in manual operation. Although Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches have demonstrated promise in enabling autonomy in endovascular intervention, they often struggle with explicit constraint satisfaction and safety guarantees. To address these challenges, a learning-based expert strategy is introduced, enhancing procedural consistency in autonomous endovascular intervention by explicitly decoupling high-level strategic decision-making from low-level procedural execution. The proposed framework replicates the expert clinical decision-making process: a strategic RL policy generates global navigation intents, which are subsequently refined through an expert-informed execution module. This module ensures that robot movements strictly adhere to expert operational norms, real-time kinematic limits, and vessel safety constraints. Experimental evaluation across high-fidelity 3D simulations and a real-world robotic platform demonstrates that the proposed framework not only outperforms baseline policies but also effectively replicates expert-level proficiency. The framework achieves a high navigation success rate (> 96%) and a 29.3% reduction in operational steps, which translates to enhanced operative efficiency and minimized device-vessel interaction. Furthermore, a 13% reduction in trajectory variance indicates superior procedural standardization, aligning autonomous behavior with established clinical norms. These results underscore its potential to enhance the predictability, safety, and consistency of robotic endovascular interventions.

</details>


### [155] [AD-MPCC: Adaptive Differentiable Model Predictive Contouring Control for Autonomous Racing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00141)
*Nam T. Nguyen,Binh Nguyen,Ahmad Amine,Thanh Vo-Duy,Rahul Mangharam,Truong X. Nghiem*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AD-MPCC integrates differentiable MPCC with online parameter estimation (Pacejka tire model + moving-horizon estimation) and a machine learning model for adaptive objective weights, achieving safer and faster autonomous racing across varying road surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MPCC methods for autonomous racing struggle with varying road-surface conditions because they lack online adaptation of both vehicle dynamics parameters and objective weights, leading to suboptimal performance or safety failures.

Method: 1) Regularized moving-horizon estimation with exponentially decaying weights for online parameter identification of Pacejka tire model; 2) Differentiable MPCC (Diff-MPCC) for optimal adjustment of objective weights; 3) Pacejka-informed machine learning model trained via supervised learning from Diff-MPCC data for online weight tuning.

Result: AD-MPCC ensures safety and achieves faster lap times than baselines in both single-surface and multiple-surface simulation scenarios.

Conclusion: AD-MPCC effectively handles varying road conditions through online parameter and objective weight adaptation, advancing autonomous racing control.

Abstract: This paper presents Adaptive Differentiable Model Predictive Contouring Control (AD-MPCC), a framework for autonomous racing that integrates differentiable MPCC with online parameter estimation to handle varying road-surface conditions. For online parameter estimation, we leverage a parameterized Pacejka Magic Formula together with a regularized moving-horizon estimation scheme with exponentially decaying weights to capture road interactions and update parameters in real time. Furthermore, we propose a differentiable MPCC (Diff-MPCC) framework that enables optimal adjustment of objective weights based on predefined long-horizon performance costs. To implement Diff-MPCC for online objective weight adaptation, we propose a Pacejka-informed machine learning model that is trained in a supervised manner using data generated by Diff-MPCC to tune the objective weights. Simulation results demonstrate that AD-MPCC reliably ensures safety and achieves faster lap times compared to baseline controllers in both single-surface and multiple-surface scenarios.

</details>


### [156] [Stop Pretending Social Robots Are Inevitable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00142)
*Serge Thill*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper critiques the assumption in HRI conferences that a future human-robot society is inevitable, arguing this framing distorts research priorities by favoring unrealistic scenario design over genuine technical development and societal needs. It advocates rejecting techno-solutionist narratives and refocusing on cognitive mechanisms for the "I" (intelligence) in HRI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors are concerned that the RO-MAN and HRI conferences frame a future human-robot society as inevitable, which shapes research directions in problematic ways, detracting from scientific advancement and prioritizing imagination over real capability development.

Method: Critical analysis and argumentation examining how the "inevitable future" framing in conference themes influences research practices, including user study design, prioritization of scenario over technical development, and deprioritization of societal-needs-based research.

Result: The paper reveals that treating a robot future as fait accompli leads to ecologically irrelevant user studies, overemphasis on scenario design, deprioritization of technical development for genuine social agency, and a self-defeating cycle that stifles the very research needed to make progress.

Conclusion: The field should reject framing HRI around an inevitable social robot future and instead focus on cognitive mechanisms necessary for the "I" (intelligence) in HRI, which would facilitate advances regardless of what the future holds.

Abstract: This paper takes issue with the recent themes of both the RO-MAN and the HRI conferences for their portrayal of a future human-robot society as inevitable. The focus is on discussing how such statements ultimately shape research. By treating a future human-robot society as a fait accompli, license is given for user studies to imagine any scenario they like, no matter whether it has any ecological relevance, and to emphasise the scenario design over actually creating robot abilities needed to fullfill the imagined role. Meanwhile, research that focusses on actual societal needs, without assuming that robots are a solution, is deprioritised, as is technical development, in particular with respect to abilities that are necessary to enable robots that function as social agents rather than a mere automation of tasks. A frame that simply assumes a robot future not only detracts from scientific advancement in favour of a techno-solutionism we ought to resist, it is also self-defeating as it risks stifling the research needed to bring it about. We should therefore reject attempts to frame and promote the field in terms of the inevitable social robot and instead focus on one that facilitates advances in the field regardless of what the future holds. This paper suggests that a renewed focus on cognitive mechanisms necessary for the "I" in HRI would be a good starting point.

</details>


### [157] [Iterated Invariant EKF for 3D Landmark-Aided Inertial Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00145)
*Hilton Marques Souza Santana,João Carlos Virgolino Soares,Marco Antonio Meggiolaro*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper applies the Iterated Invariant Extended Kalman Filter (IterIEKF) to landmark-based inertial 3D localization, showing it outperforms classical SO(3)-EKF, Iterated SO(3)-EKF, and IEKF in estimation accuracy and consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical SO(3)-EKF suffers from the false observability problem, becoming overconfident in unobservable directions; IEKF addresses this but its measurement update lacks full state compatibility.

Method: The authors formulate and apply the Iterated Invariant EKF (IterIEKF) to landmark-based inertial 3D localization, which ensures the estimated state remains on the observed state manifold with uncertainty confined to its tangent space in the low-noise regime.

Result: Numerical simulations show IterIEKF outperforms SO(3)-EKF, Iterated SO(3)-EKF, and IEKF in both estimation accuracy and consistency.

Conclusion: IterIEKF is a superior approach for landmark-based inertial 3D localization, mitigating the false observability problem while maintaining state compatibility.

Abstract: Inertial navigation systems aided by three-dimensional landmark measurements constitute a fundamental problem in robotic perception and state estimation. Classical SO(3)-based Extended Kalman Filter (SO(3)-EKF) approaches provide practical solutions, but suffer from the false observability problem, in which the filter becomes overconfident in unobservable directions, leading to degraded estimation performance. The Invariant EKF (IEKF) addresses this limitation by reformulating the system dynamics as a group-affine system on a Lie group, although its measurement update does not fully satisfy certain state compatibility properties. More recently, the Iterated Invariant EKF (IterIEKF) was proposed to further improve the IEKF by ensuring, in the low-noise regime, that the estimated state remains on the observed state manifold while the uncertainty is confined to its tangent space. In this work, we formulate and apply the IterIEKF to landmark-based inertial 3D localization for the first time. Through numerical simulations, we show that the proposed approach outperforms the classical SO(3)-EKF, the Iterated SO(3)-EKF, and the IEKF in terms of both estimation accuracy and consistency.

</details>


### [158] [3D Point World Models: Point Completion Enables More Accurate Dynamics Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00148)
*Skand Peri,Hung Nguyen,Chanho Kim,Li Fuxin,Stefan Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 3DPWM is a task-agnostic 3D world model that completes partial point clouds and learns action-conditioned dynamics, enabling reliable long-horizon rollouts (100-300+ steps) and successful planning across various robotic embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video-based dynamics models lack explicit 3D structure and suffer from geometrically inconsistent long-term rollouts, while partial point cloud models remain sensitive to occlusions and accumulated prediction drift.

Method: The model first completes partial point clouds, then learns action-conditioned dynamics in the completed 3D scene, operating entirely in 3D space for task-agnostic world modeling.

Result: Achieves reliable long-horizon rollouts (100-300+ steps), supports both open-loop and closed-loop planning, and enables successful sim-to-real transfer across different robotic embodiments and tabletop manipulation benchmarks.

Conclusion: 3DPWM significantly improves geometric consistency and long-horizon prediction reliability for model-based planning, with demonstrated adaptation to new tasks and real-world transfer capability.

Abstract: Learning predictive models of the world enables robotic control through planning, potentially allowing robots to improvise solutions on new tasks. However, large video-based dynamics models lack explicit 3D spatial structure and suffer from geometrically inconsistent long-term rollouts with compounding errors. Emerging 3D dynamics models based on partial point clouds improve geometric consistency but remain sensitive to occlusions and accumulated prediction drift. To address these challenges, we present 3D Point World Models (3DPWM) - a task-agnostic world model that operates entirely in 3D space by first completing partial point clouds and then learning action-conditioned dynamics in this completed 3D scene. By operating on completed geometry, 3DPWM enables reliable long-horizon rollouts and more accurate cost evaluation for model-based planning while supporting adaptation to new tasks. Experiments across different robotic embodiments and tabletop manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that 3DPWM achieves significantly more reliable long-horizon rollouts (100-300+ steps), supports both open-loop and closed-loop planning, and enables successful sim-to-real transfer.

</details>


### [159] [Dual-Informed Vertical Expansion for Multi-Objective Node Selection in Anytime Conflict-Based Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00156)
*Willem van Osselaer,Jiarui Li,Meshal Alharbi,Gioele Zardini*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces DIVE, a node-selection policy for exact CBS that balances best-bound search with depth-oriented diving, providing early feasible solutions, reduced memory usage, and provably exact MAPF solving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard best-first node selection in CBS maintains large frontiers, interrupts parent-child locality, and offers no feasible solutions until optimality is proven. The authors argue node selection should be treated as a first-class design choice rather than a fixed implementation detail.

Method: DIVE (Dual-Informed Vertical Expansion) combines best-bound reanchoring with depth-oriented dives. It starts each dive from the best-bound frontier, follows promising children for locality, uses incumbent pruning, and is proven to maintain exactness under a branch-and-bound framework.

Result: Experiments show DIVE consistently reduces dive breaks, provides early incumbents with certified optimality gaps, uses substantially less queue memory than best-first search, and benefits from warm starts in dense or memory-limited regimes.

Conclusion: Node selection is a critical design axis for CBS. The trade-off map (best-first = node efficient, iterative deepening = memory efficient, DIVE = dive efficient) is validated, and DIVE offers a practical middle ground with strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Conflict-Based Search (CBS) is a leading exact algorithm for Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF), but its high-level node-selection rule is usually treated as a fixed implementation detail. Standard best-first selection is strong for minimizing expanded nodes and closing the optimality certificate, yet it can maintain a large frontier, interrupt parent-child expansion sequences, and provide no feasible incumbent until termination. This paper studies node selection as a first-class design choice for exact CBS. We introduce Dual-Informed Vertical Expansion (DIVE), a policy that is best-bound between dives and depth-oriented within a dive. DIVE starts each dive from the current best-bound frontier, follows promising children to exploit parent-child locality, and uses incumbent pruning to limit unproductive excursions. We formalize CBS node selection through a branch-and-bound view, prove that the traversal policy can be changed without affecting exactness, and analyze the resulting trade-offs among expanded nodes, dive breaks, queue size, and primal-dual bound progress. The analysis predicts three complementary extremes. Best-first search is node efficient, iterative deepening is memory efficient, and DIVE is dive efficient while retaining regular best-bound reanchoring. Experiments on standard MAPF benchmarks support this trade-off map. DIVE consistently reduces dive breaks, provides early incumbents with certified gaps, uses substantially less queue memory than best-first search, and benefits from warm starts and simple responsive variants in dense or memory-limited regimes.

</details>


### [160] [Distributed Multi Robot Lunar Cargo Transportation via Phase Decomposed Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00160)
*Ashutosh Mishra,Elian Neppel,Shreya Santra,Antoine Jonquières,Muhammad Athallah Naufal,Kentaro Uno,Kazuya Yoshida*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A phase-decomposed reinforcement learning framework enables modular robots to cooperatively transport cargo on lunar surfaces through lifting, transportation, and placement stages with centralized training and safety-aware synchronization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modular reconfigurable robotic systems offer scalable solutions for lunar surface operations, but cooperative cargo transportation is challenging due to morphology-dependent topology changes, strong payload-induced coupling, long-horizon decision making, and safety constraints.

Method: Proposes a phase-decomposed RL framework that decomposes the task into lifting, transportation, and placement stages, each with a dedicated joint-state policy. Uses centralized training, a deterministic phase controller with Markov state representation, and a failure-sensitive synchronization mechanism.

Result: Demonstrates reliable cooperative transport across all stages in both simulation and hardware experiments at a JAXA space exploration test facility.

Conclusion: The proposed phase-decomposed RL framework successfully enables safe, coordinated multi-robot cargo transport in lunar-like conditions across all task stages.

Abstract: Modular reconfigurable robotic systems provide a scalable solution for cooperative surface operations in future lunar missions. However, cooperative cargo transportation remains challenging due to morphology-dependent topology changes, strong payload-induced coupling, long-horizon decision making, and safety constraints. This paper proposes a phase-decomposed reinforcement learning framework for cooperative cargo transport with distributed robotic units. The task is decomposed into lifting, transportation, and placement, each optimized with a dedicated joint-state policy capturing inter-agent coupling. Centralized training promotes stable convergence, while deployment uses onboard proprioception for control and OptiTrack motion capture for ground-truth evaluation and post-processed metrics. A deterministic phase controller expressed in Markov state representation regulates transitions between stages, and a failure-sensitive synchronization mechanism ensures coordinated progression and safety-aware halting during real-world execution. The framework is evaluated in simulation and through controlled field experiments at a JAXA space exploration test facility. Results demonstrate reliable cooperative transport across all stages in both simulation and hardware experiments.

</details>


### [161] [HydraCollab: Adaptive Collaborative-Perception for Distributed Autonomous Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00191)
*Luke Chen,Cheng-Ju Wu,David R. Martin,Qilin Ye,Pramod Khargonekar,Mohammad Abdullah Al Faruque*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HydraCollab is an adaptive collaborative-perception framework that selectively transmits the most informative sensor features and dynamically switches between intermediate and late collaboration strategies based on spatial confidence maps, achieving better accuracy-latency trade-offs than existing methods on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing collaborative-perception systems face an inherent trade-off between communication bandwidth and perception accuracy, where more information exchange improves results but increases overhead. Real-world bandwidth constraints require minimizing communication without sacrificing performance.

Method: HydraCollab (i) selectively transmits the most informative sensor features and (ii) dynamically chooses between intermediate or late collaboration strategies based on spatial confidence maps.

Result: Evaluated on V2X-R, V2X-Radar, and UAV3D-mini datasets. Compared to SOTA Where2comm, uses only 41% bandwidth on V2X-R and 26% on V2X-Radar while improving performance by 0.78% and 0.75% respectively.

Conclusion: HydraCollab achieves the best overall trade-off between accuracy and communication cost among existing collaborative-perception methods.

Abstract: Collaborative-perception enables multi-robot systems to enhance situational awareness by sharing perceptual information. Existing collaborative-perception systems face an inherent trade-off between communication bandwidth requirements and perception accuracy, where methods that exchange more information achieve better perception results at the cost of increased communication overhead. However, real-world communication networks impose bandwidth constraints that require minimizing communication overhead without sacrificing perception performance.
