To address this challenge, we propose HydraCollab, an adaptive collaborative-perception framework that (i) selectively transmits the most informative sensor features and (ii) dynamically employs collaboration strategies (intermediate or late) based on spatial confidence maps. Extensive evaluations on the V2X-R, V2X-Radar and UAV3D-mini datasets demonstrate that HydraCollab achieves the best overall trade-off between accuracy and communication cost among existing collaborative-perception methods. Relative to SOTA Where2comm, HydraCollab uses only 41% of the bandwidth on V2X-R and 26% on V2X-Radar while improving performance by 0.78% and 0.75% respectively. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/AICPS/HydraCollab.

</details>


### [162] [ELMP: Efficient Learning for Motion Planning via Analytical Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00215)
*Yixiao Li,Tifanny Portela,Jordis Herrmann,René Zurbrügg,Marco Hutter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ELMP is a self-supervised fine-tuning framework for Neural Motion Planners that adapts policies to new environments without expensive expert data collection, achieving 89.8% success rate in unseen environments with millisecond-level inference latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural Motion Planners require costly recollection of large expert datasets when adapting to new environments, creating a computational bottleneck that limits practical deployment.

Method: ELMP uses self-supervised fine-tuning with a differentiable kinematic layer and dense collision, target-reaching, and smoothness objectives. It replaces expert trajectory generation with rapid problem sampling and encodes tool geometry via point clouds for robust generalization across kinematic chains.

Result: ELMP achieves 84.8% average success rate, reduces per-sample adaptation cost by ~100x, improves success from 57.3% (zero-shot) to 89.8% in unseen environments, and maintains millisecond-level inference latency. Validated on a physical Franka Emika Panda robot.

Conclusion: Self-supervised fine-tuning effectively removes the data collection bottleneck for Neural Motion Planners, enabling orders-of-magnitude faster adaptation while maintaining high success rates and real-time performance.

Abstract: Neural Motion Planners (NMPs) enable fast reactive motion generation, but adapting them to new environments typically requires recollecting large expert datasets, which is computationally prohibitive. We propose ELMP, a framework for data-efficient adaptation via self-supervised fine-tuning. Rather than generating additional expert trajectories with expensive global planners, ELMP directly optimizes the policy through a differentiable kinematic layer using dense collision, target-reaching, and smoothness objectives. This replaces expert data generation with rapid problem sampling, reducing per-sample adaptation cost by roughly two orders of magnitude. To further support robust generalization across changing kinematic chains, we introduce a mechanism to explicitly encode tool geometry via point clouds. Benchmarked against classical and neural baselines, ELMP achieves an 84.8% average success rate with orders-of-magnitude lower cold-start latency than classical methods. In unseen environments, self-supervised fine-tuning improves success rate from 57.3% (zero-shot) to 89.8%, removing the data collection bottleneck. Our approach maintains millisecond-level inference latency and is validated on a physical Franka Emika Panda robot.

</details>


### [163] [ASPIRE: Agentic /Skills Discovery for Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00272)
*Runyu Lu,Yubo Wu,Ethan Kou,Letian Fu,Wenli Xiao,Ajay Mandlekar,Yinzhen Xu,Guanya Shi,Ken Goldberg,Ang Chen,Mosharaf Chowdhury,Yuke Zhu,Linxi "Jim" Fan,Guanzhi Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ASPIRE is a continual learning system that autonomously writes and refines robot control programs, building a reusable skill library over time. It outperforms prior methods by up to 77% on manipulation tasks and enables zero-shot generalization to unseen long-horizon tasks with 31% success vs 4% for baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional robot programming is challenging due to multimodal perception, physical contact dynamics, configuration diversity, and execution failures. The authors aim to create a system that autonomously programs robots and compounds experience into reusable skills.

Method: ASPIRE operates in an open-ended loop with three components: (1) a closed-loop execution engine with fine-grained traces for autonomous failure diagnosis, repair, and validation; (2) a continually expanding skill library distilling fixes into reusable knowledge; (3) evolutionary search generating diverse task sequences and programs beyond single-trajectory refinement.

Result: Surpasses prior methods by up to 77% on LIBERO-Pro manipulation, 72% on Robosuite bimanual handover, and 32% on BEHAVIOR-1K long-horizon tasks. Achieves 31% zero-shot success on LIBERO-Pro Long vs 4% for prior methods. Shows initial sim-to-real transfer evidence.

Conclusion: ASPIRE demonstrates that autonomous code-as-policy programming with continual skill accumulation is effective across diverse tasks, embodiments, and settings, with strong generalization and transfer capabilities.

Abstract: Traditional robot programming is challenging: it requires orchestrating multimodal perception, managing physical contact dynamics, and handling diverse configurations and execution failures. We introduce ASPIRE (Agentic Skill Programming through Iterative Robot Exploration), a continual learning system that autonomously writes and refines robot control programs in a code-as-policy paradigm while compounding experience into a reusable skill library. ASPIRE discovers skills that persist across tasks, simulation and real-world settings, and embodiments. It operates in an open-ended loop with three components: (1) a closed-loop robot execution engine that exposes fine-grained multimodal traces, enabling autonomous failure diagnosis, repair synthesis, and validation; (2) a continually expanding skill library that distills validated fixes into reusable, transferable knowledge; and (3) evolutionary search that generates diverse task sequences and control programs to explore beyond single-trajectory refinement. ASPIRE surpasses prior methods by up to 77% on LIBERO-Pro manipulation under perturbation, 72% on Robosuite bimanual handover, and 32% on BEHAVIOR-1K long-horizon household tasks. Its accumulated library also enables zero-shot generalization to unseen long-horizon tasks: on LIBERO-Pro Long, ASPIRE achieves 31% success versus 4% for prior methods despite their use of test-time reasoning and retries. Finally, simulation-discovered skills provide initial evidence of sim-to-real transfer, substantially reducing real-robot programming effort across different embodiments and robot APIs.

</details>


### [164] [What's Hidden Matters: Identifying Planning-Critical Occluded Agents using Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00283)
*Amirhosein Chahe,Tyler Naes,Jovin D'sa,Faizan M. Tariq,Sangjae Bae,Lifeng Zhou,David Isele*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework using Planning KL-divergence (PKL) to identify and rank occluded agents critical to an autonomous vehicle's trajectory, then uses VLMs (GPT-5) to generate structured annotations. Fine-tuning on PKL-guided data significantly improves VLM performance, with smaller models outperforming larger zero-shot counterparts and achieving ~30% improvement over random sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving approaches treat all occlusions with uniform conservatism (needlessly defensive) or infer hidden spaces without estimating the planner impact. There is a critical gap between perception and planning regarding how occlusions affect the ego vehicle's trajectory.

Method: Proposes Planning KL-divergence (PKL) as an information-theoretic metric to identify and rank occluded agents by their impact on the ego vehicle's plan. Uses an expert VLM (GPT-5) to generate structured annotations based on PKL-guided ranking. Applied to nuScenes dataset to create a benchmark for high-impact scenarios.

Result: Fine-tuning on PKL-guided data yields dramatic performance improvements across all models. Smaller fine-tuned models significantly outperform larger zero-shot counterparts. PKL-guided data selection improves performance by approximately 30% over random sampling.

Conclusion: This is the first systematic approach for training VLMs to focus on planning-critical occlusions, enabling more semantically grounded and efficient risk assessment in autonomous driving.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles must safely navigate complex environments where planning-critical agents may be hidden from view. Current approaches often treat all occlusions with uniform conservatism, yielding needlessly defensive driving, or they infer hidden spaces without estimating the impact on the planner. This work bridges the critical gap between perception and planning by enabling Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to identify and reason about the specific hidden agents that are most critical to the ego-vehicle's trajectory. We introduce a novel framework that uses Planning KL-divergence (PKL), an information-theoretic metric, to systematically identify and rank occluded agents based on their impact on the ego vehicle's plan. Using this planning-aware ranking, we employ an expert VLM (GPT-5) to generate rich, structured annotations that capture the visual evidence and reasoning required for this task. We apply this framework to the nuScenes dataset to create a new benchmark focused on high-impact scenarios. We conduct comprehensive experiments on a wide range of general-purpose and domain-adapted VLMs, demonstrating that fine-tuning on our PKL-guided data yields dramatic performance improvements across all models. Notably, our results show that smaller, fine-tuned models significantly outperform their much larger zero-shot counterparts, and that our PKL-guided data selection strategy improves performance by approximately 30\% over random sampling. Our work presents the first systematic approach for training VLMs to focus on planning-critical occlusions, enabling more semantically grounded and efficient risk assessment in autonomous driving.

</details>


### [165] [NeHMO: Neural Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability Learning for Decentralized Safe Multi-Arm Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00326)
*Qingyi Chen,Zachary Kingston,Ahmed H. Qureshi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A neural Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability learning approach for safe, scalable, decentralized multi-arm motion planning that generalizes across systems and outperforms baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized multi-arm planners scale poorly for real-time use; decentralized methods depend on accurate behavior prediction and fail when other arms act unpredictably.

Method: Learns a safety value function via neural Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability (HJR) that captures worst-case inter-arm collision constraints, then uses it in a decentralized trajectory optimization framework.

Result: Scalable, data-efficient, generalizes across multi-manipulator systems, and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on challenging tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed HJR-based decentralized approach effectively addresses safety and scalability in multi-arm motion planning.

Abstract: Safe multi-arm motion planning is a challenging problem in robotics due to its high dimensionality, coupled configuration space, and complex collision constraints. Centralized planners are capable of coordinating all arms but often face scalability limitations, restricting applicability in real-time settings. On the other hand, decentralized methods are scalable and recent deep learning-based approaches have shown promising results. However, these depend on accurate behavior prediction or coordination protocols and may fail when other arms act unpredictably. To address these challenges, we introduce a neural Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability (HJR) learning-based approach to approximate a safety value function that captures worst-case inter-arm safety constraints. We further develop a decentralized trajectory optimization framework that uses the learned HJR representation for real-time planning. The proposed method is scalable and data-efficient, generalizes across multi-manipulator systems, and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on challenging multi-arm motion planning tasks.

</details>


### [166] [Unleashing More Actions via Action Compositional Training for VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00351)
*Kai Peng,Jie Lu,Xiaojiang Peng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ACT-VLA is an offline data augmentation framework that synthesizes novel, physically valid demonstrations from existing tasks using the model's latent representations, enabling VLA models to compose known sub-skills into new behaviors without costly human data collection, significantly improving generalization and success rates in robotic manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard VLA training causes overfitting to specific behavioral patterns, and models fail to generalize to out-of-distribution scenarios even when those scenarios only require novel combinations of identical sub-skills. Expanding datasets is cost-prohibitive due to expensive human teleoperation for data collection.

Method: ACT-VLA (Action Compositional Training) is an offline data augmentation framework that leverages the VLA model's latent task representations to synthesize novel, physically valid demonstrations directly from existing tasks, eliminating the need for additional manual data collection.

Result: Baseline VLA models generalize poorly due to original distribution overfitting, while policies trained with ACT-VLA synthesized data achieve substantially higher success rates on challenging manipulation tasks in simulation.

Conclusion: Leveraging existing tasks for automated demonstration synthesis provides an effective, scalable, and data-efficient route to broadening VLA generalization without expensive human teleoperation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models excel at robotic manipulation, driven by the scale and diversity of demonstration data. However, standard training paradigms often cause VLA models to severely overfit to specific behavioral patterns, rendering them unable to generalize to out-of-distribution scenarios even when those scenarios merely require novel combinations of identical sub-skills. While expanding datasets can mitigate this overfitting, acquiring high-quality robot data remains notoriously labor-intensive and cost-prohibitive. To resolve this impasse without expensive human teleoperation and to truly unleash more actions,i.e., enable VLA models to compose known sub-skills into a much broader set of executable behaviors beyond the original demonstrations-we propose ACT-VLA (Action Compositional Training for VLA Models), an offline data augmentation framework that leverages the model's latent task representations to synthesize novel, physically valid demonstrations directly from existing tasks for policy training. By eliminating additional manual data collection, our method automatically expands the training distribution and mitigates overfitting. We evaluate our approach on challenging manipulation tasks in simulation. Experiments demonstrate that while baseline VLA models generalize poorly due to original distribution overfitting, policies trained with our synthesized data achieve substantially higher success rates, validating that leveraging existing tasks for automated demonstration synthesis provides an effective, scalable, and data-efficient route to broadening VLA generalization.

</details>


### [167] [Robust Operational Space Control with Conformal Disturbance Bounds for Safe Redundant Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00424)
*Wenhua Liu,Fan Zhang,Qin Lin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a robust operational space control framework for redundant manipulators that combines an extended state observer with conformal prediction to achieve accurate tracking and probabilistic safety guarantees under dynamic uncertainties, validated on a 7-DoF Franka Research 3 arm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Redundant manipulators operating in constrained/human-interactive environments need accurate task-space tracking with safety guarantees under dynamic uncertainties. Classical model-based controllers degrade under disturbances, while pure data-driven methods lack theoretical guarantees and require noisy full-state measurements.

Method: Integrates extended state observer (ESO) for lumped disturbance estimation in operational space + robust control barrier function (CBF) for safety + sliding-window conformal prediction to estimate disturbance-variation bounds online in a distribution-free manner, reducing conservatism.

Result: Millimeter-level tracking accuracy and real-time safe control at 1 kHz on a 7-DoF Franka Research 3 manipulator under various disturbances.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach combining ESO-based model robustness with conformal prediction-based data-driven adaptability achieves practical probabilistic safety guarantees without requiring full-state measurements or known disturbance bounds a priori.

Abstract: Redundant robotic manipulators operating in constrained and human-interactive environments require accurate task-space tracking together with rigorous safety guarantees under dynamic uncertainties. Classical operational space computed torque controller (OSCTC) relies on accurate dynamic models and degrades in the presence of disturbances. In contrast, the data-driven paradigm of residual learning approximates disturbances as functions learned from full-state measurements, which are often noisy in practice, lack rigorous theoretical guarantees, and introduce additional design complexity. This paper proposes a robust OSCTC framework that integrates an extended state observer (ESO) with conformal prediction to combine model-based robustness and data-driven adaptability. The ESO estimates lumped disturbances directly in operational space without requiring full-state measurements as in residual learning, and a robust control barrier function (CBF) is constructed to enforce safety under uncertainty. However, robust CBFs require a known disturbance-variation bound to guarantee absolute safety, which often leads to conservatism in practice. To address this limitation, we further employ a sliding-window conformal prediction mechanism to estimate the bound online in a distribution-free manner, thereby achieving practical probabilistic safety guarantees. Experiments on a 7-DoF Franka Research 3 manipulator demonstrate millimeter-level tracking accuracy and real-time safe control at 1~kHz under various disturbances.

</details>


### [168] [Learning Gait-Aware Quadruped Locomotion with Temporal Logic Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00442)
*Merve Atasever,Cagan Bakirci,Alfredo Reina Corona,Keyan Azbijari,Jyotirmoy V. Deshmukh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework that uses Signal Temporal Logic (STL) constraints to generate dense reward functions for reinforcement learning-based quadruped locomotion, enabling explicit gait specification and control, demonstrated on the Barkour robot with improved velocity tracking and training stability over hand-crafted rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RL for quadruped locomotion relies on fixed, hand-crafted, Markovian reward functions that lack interpretability and explicit gait control. The authors aim to provide a more structured, interpretable, and controllable way to specify gaits and rewards.

Method: The method defines distinct gaits using parameterized STL constraints (safety, synchronization, command tracking, actuation bounds), calibrates parameters from reference rollouts, computes dense rewards via smooth STL robustness approximations, and integrates with PPO training on the Barkour robot in MJX with parallelization and domain randomization.

Result: Compared to hand-crafted rewards, the STL-shaped rewards achieve tighter velocity tracking and more stable training.

Conclusion: STL-based reward shaping is a viable alternative to hand-crafted rewards for quadruped locomotion, offering better performance and explicit gait specification.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for quadruped locomotion commonly depends on fixed, hand-crafted, and Markovian reward functions that limit both interpretability of learned policies and lack explicit control over gait behaviors. We introduce a framework where distinct gaits are specified using parameterized constraints expressed in Signal Temporal Logic (STL). These include safety bounds, gait synchronization constraints, command tracking, and actuation bounds. From these specifications, we develop a reward shaping mechanism that provides learning agents a dense, continuous reward landscape that encodes desired behavior. We define parametric STL templates for three speed regimes (walking-trot, trot, bound), calibrate their parameters from reference rollouts, and compute rewards from using smooth approximations of STL robustness over the rollouts. The generated rewards can be used to provide shaped gradients compatible with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). We instantiate the approach on Google's Barkour quadruped robot in MuJoCo XLA (MJX). We use parallelization within the simulator to improve training speeds and use domain randomization to robustify learned policies. We show that compared to a baseline of hand-crafted rewards, the STL-shaped rewards yield tighter velocity tracking and more stable training. Videos can be found on our project website: https://stl-locomotion.github.io/.

</details>


### [169] [Search-Based Spatiotemporal and Multi-Robot Motion Planning on Graphs of Space-Time Convex Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00444)
*Jingtao Tang,Zining Mao,Lufan Yang,Hang Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents ST-GCS, a graph-based framework for spatiotemporal motion planning using convex sets in space-time, with an Exact Convex Decomposition scheme, achieving substantial speedups for single and multi-robot planning (up to 100 robots) in environments with narrow, transient feasible regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatiotemporal multi-robot motion planning in continuous spaces is challenging because collision-free regions are transient and geometrically constrained, requiring robots to reason about time-varying feasible spaces efficiently.

Method: (1) Graphs of Space-Time Convex Sets (ST-GCS) representing collision-free regions as convex sets in space-time; (2) Best-first search over path-indexed states with admissible heuristics and dominance checks; (3) Exact Convex Decomposition (ECD) to reserve trajectory occupancies; (4) Prioritized planning with windowed coordination for multi-robot settings.

Result: Substantial speedups over various planners in single and multi-robot problems, maintaining high solution quality especially in narrow and transient feasible regions. The multi-robot planner solves instances with up to 100 robots within a few minutes.

Conclusion: ST-GCS with ECD provides an effective algorithmic framework for time-optimal spatiotemporal motion planning, scaling to large multi-robot systems with practical computational efficiency.

Abstract: Spatiotemporal motion planning, especially in multi-robot settings, requires robots to reason about collision-free regions that change over time, which is challenging in continuous spaces when feasible regions are transient and geometrically constrained. We present an algorithmic framework based on graphs of space-time convex sets (ST-GCSs), where collision-free regions are represented as convex sets in space-time and trajectories correspond to paths on the graph together with continuous motions within the selected sets. We formulate time-optimal planning on ST-GCSs as a graph-search problem over path-indexed states and develop a best-first search solver that evaluates partial paths via continuous trajectory optimization, guided by admissible heuristics and dominance checks. We further present an Exact Convex Decomposition (ECD) scheme to reserve trajectory occupancies in space-time, enabling unified handling of dynamic obstacles and multi-robot interactions. For multi-robot motion planning, we integrate ST-GCS planning and ECD into prioritized planning methods and introduce a windowed coordination scheme to improve efficiency. Extensive experiments on single-robot and multi-robot problems demonstrate substantial speedups over various planners while maintaining high solution quality, particularly in environments with narrow and transient feasible regions. Large-scale demonstrations further show that the proposed multi-robot motion planner can solve instances with up to $100$ robots within only a few minutes. Project homepage: https://sites.google.com/view/stgcs

</details>


### [170] [VLM-AR3L: Vision-Language Models for Absolute and Relative Rewards in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00483)
*Kuan-Chen Chen,Winston Chen,Wei-Fang Sun,Min-Chun Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLM-AR3L is a framework that uses Vision-Language Models to generate both absolute and relative rewards for reinforcement learning, outperforming prior VLM-based methods across control, manipulation, and open-world embodied tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing effective reward functions in RL remains challenging, especially in open-ended environments where task goals are abstract and difficult to quantify.

Method: VLM-AR3L leverages VLMs to generate preference labels, learning both absolute rewards (scalar evaluation for individual states) and relative rewards (comparing consecutive observations for progress/regression), then integrating them for stable and robust reward signals.

Result: VLM-AR3L consistently outperforms prior VLM-based reward learning methods across classic control, manipulation, and open-world embodied tasks (especially Minecraft).

Conclusion: Combining absolute and relative rewards from VLMs provides a more effective reward signal for RL in complex, open-ended environments.

Abstract: Designing effective reward functions remains a major challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), particularly in open-ended environments where task goals are abstract and difficult to quantify. In this work, we present VLM-AR3L, a framework that leverages Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to provide both absolute and relative rewards for RL. VLM-AR3L interprets an agent's visual observations in the context of a natural language task goal, and learns both absolute and relative rewards from VLM-generated preference labels. The absolute reward model predicts scalar evaluations for individual states, while the relative reward model compares consecutive observations to infer progress or regression toward the task goal. Their integration combines the stability of state-based evaluation with the robustness of comparative supervision. We evaluate VLM-AR3L across benchmarks spanning classic control, manipulation, and open-world embodied tasks, with a particular focus on Minecraft given its visual complexity and long-horizon decision-making requirements. Experimental results show that VLM-AR3L consistently outperforms prior VLM-based reward learning methods.

</details>


### [171] [From Technical Metrics to User Perception: A User Study of a Multimodal Human-Robot Interaction System for Object Detection and Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00530)
*Jian Song,Tian Zi,Shen Guanting*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A 15% improvement in task success (75%→90%) from replacing perception/language modules in an HRI system is perceptible to users. 70.83% of 24 participants preferred the improved system, with significantly higher ratings for speed, reliability, competence, and fluency (large effect sizes, p<0.001).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Technical benchmark improvements in HRI systems may not translate into perceptible user differences. This study tests whether a 15% success rate gain actually matters to human users during live interaction.

Method: Within-subject user study (N=24) comparing a multimodal baseline (Whisper+Florence-2+LLaMA 3.1+Type-2 fuzzy logic controller) against an improved configuration (Grounding DINO+SAM+Qwen 3.5 9B+same controller) on tabletop object-grasping tasks. Participants rated speed, reliability, competence, fluency on 7-point Likert scales after each interaction.

Result: 17/24 (70.83%) preferred the improved system (p=0.043, h=0.43); all three perceptual constructs significantly higher after Holm correction (p<0.001) with large to very large effect sizes.

Conclusion: The 15% technical improvement in task success is perceptible to users, confirming the need to complement benchmark evaluations with user-centered evidence in robotic manipulation pipelines.

Abstract: Improvements in the technical performance of human--robot interaction (HRI) systems do not automatically translate into differences that human users can detect during live interaction. This paper investigates whether a 15 percentage point gain in end-to-end task success (from 75% in a multimodal baseline system to 90% in an improved configuration identified through a prior ablation study) is sufficient to produce consistent and measurable differences in user perception. The baseline system combines Whisper for speech recognition, Florence-2 for open-vocabulary object detection, LLaMA 3.1 for action extraction, and an interval Type-2 fuzzy logic controller for motion execution. The improved configuration replaces the perception and language modules with Grounding DINO + SAM and Qwen 3.5 9B, respectively, while retaining the same controller. A within-subject user study with 24 participants compared both systems on the same tabletop object-grasping task. After interacting with each configuration, participants rated perceived speed, reliability, and overall competence and fluency on a 7-point Likert scale. Results show that 17 out of 24 participants (70.83%) preferred the improved system (exact binomial test, p = 0.043, h = 0.43), and all three perceptual constructs were rated significantly higher for the improved configuration after Holm correction, with large to very large effect sizes (p < 0.001). These findings confirm that the identified technical improvements are perceptible to users in direct interaction and underscore the importance of complementing benchmark evaluation with user-centred evidence when assessing robotic manipulation pipelines.

</details>


### [172] [Learning from Demonstration via Spatiotemporal Tubes for Unknown Euler-Lagrange Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00534)
*Ratnangshu Das,Puneeth Shankar,Varuni Buereddy,Ravi Prakash,Pushpak Jagtap*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: STT-LfD is a unified Learning from Demonstration framework that uses heteroscedastic Gaussian Processes to learn Spatiotemporal Tubes from demonstrations as safety constraints, enforced by a closed-form feedback controller without system identification, showing superior robustness and speed on real robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LfD approaches decouple motion learning from control, tracking fixed references that lack robustness and cannot capture time-varying precision requirements, while also often requiring explicit system identification.

Method: Uses heteroscedastic Gaussian Processes to learn Spatiotemporal Tubes (STTs) from demonstrations as data-driven safety specifications, then a closed-form feedback controller enforces these learned constraints while respecting actuator limits, all without system identification.

Result: Hardware experiments on a mobile robot and 7-DOF manipulator demonstrate superior robustness to disturbances and computational speed compared to baselines.

Conclusion: STT-LfD provides an efficient, robust unified LfD framework that preserves temporal structure and avoids explicit system identification.

Abstract: We present STT-LfD, a unified Learning from Demonstration (LfD) framework that integrates motion learning with control for unknown Euler-Lagrange systems. Unlike traditional decoupled approaches that track a fixed reference, the proposed method treats demonstrations as a data-driven safety specification. Using heteroscedastic Gaussian Processes, STT-LfD learns Spatiotemporal Tubes (STTs) as an intent envelope that capture time-varying precision requirements of a task. A closed-form feedback controller then enforces these learned constraints while respecting actuator limits, without requiring explicit system identification. The approach preserves the temporal structure of demonstrations, remains computationally efficient, and avoids explicit system identification. Hardware experiments on a mobile robot and a 7-DOF manipulator show that it outperforms baselines in robustness to disturbances and computational speed.

</details>


### [173] [[Preprint] Dynamic Modeling, Gait Synthesis, and Control of a Novel Subsurface Bore Propagator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00569)
*Lina van Brügge,Shruti Kotpalliwar,Anton Koval,Akshit Saradagi,George Nikolakopoulos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents the design, dynamic modeling, gait synthesis, and feedback control of a modular subsurface robot combining earthworm-like anchoring with tunnel boring machine excavation, tested in simulation and achieving 30mm soil penetration over 3 gait cycles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable human-free subsurface exploration and excavation by designing a novel modular robot that can navigate underground environments autonomously.

Method: A 5-module robot design (drill head, two anchoring modules, two propulsion modules) with Euler-Lagrange dynamic modeling, centralized state machine for gait synthesis, feedback control, and sim-to-real testing via Unity + ROS.

Result: The robot successfully anchors in place and achieves 30mm total advancement into soil after completing 3 gait cycles in simulation.

Conclusion: The proposed modular design, controllers, and gait synthesis strategy are effective for subsurface locomotion, with validated performance in physics-based simulation.

Abstract: In this article, we present dynamic modeling, gait synthesis, and feedback control design for a modular novel subsurface robot, designed for human-free subsurface exploration and excavation. The subsurface propagator design is based on two major aspects: 1) anchor and propel movement like an earthworm and 2) excavation similar to tunnel boring machines. This design is decoupled into five separate modules: one drill head to excavate and create cavity for propagation, two modules to anchor the robot, and two modules to enable propagation of the body. In order to design a controller for each of the modules, dynamic models using the Euler-Lagrange framework are developed. These mathematical models are used as a baseline to design controlled decoupled operation of the different joint movements. The operation of robotic assembly is constructed via a centralized state machine for gait synthesis with integration of the designed feedback controller. The controllers are tested on the real robot geometry to aid sim-to-real integration: A physics-based Unity simulation using a CAD model of the robot and integration of the trained controller via ROS verifies the performance of the robot. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed design, controllers and the gait synthesis strategy together are capable of anchoring the robot in place and creating an total advancement of 30\,mm into the soil after completing 3 gait cycles.

</details>


### [174] [Enhancing Robustness in Robot-Environment Interactions through Passive Compliant Degrees of Freedom: A Hybrid Position-Force Control Approach with Feedback Linearization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00571)
*Rahman Ardakanian,Iman Kardan,AliAkbar Akbari,Ali Mousavi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid position-force control architecture with a passive compliant end-effector (spring-damper) to reduce impact shocks and oscillations in robot-environment interactions, showing improved force/velocity error and torque smoothness in simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot-environment interactions in dynamic or unstructured settings suffer from impact shocks, vibrations, and uncertainties in contact geometry and mechanical properties, which degrade performance.

Method: Combines feedback-linearized hybrid position-force control with a passive compliant (spring-damper) end-effector to physically absorb and dissipate impact energy before it reaches the actuated joints and control loop.

Result: Spring-damper configuration outperforms rigid and spring-only setups: 36.5% reduction in fixed-environment tangential force-error std, 25.4% in variable-environment normal force-error std, and 41.1% in variable-environment normal velocity-error std.

Conclusion: Adding passive compliance at the end-effector significantly improves contact stability and reduces oscillations, offering a simple and effective complement to active hybrid control.

Abstract: Robot-environment interactions in dynamic or unstructured settings are often degraded by impact shocks, vibrations, and uncertainties in contact geometry and mechanical properties. This paper proposes an interaction architecture that combines feedback-linearized hybrid position-force control with a passive compliant degree of freedom embedded at the end-effector. Unlike conventional hybrid position-force control, which relies mainly on active feedback, force sensing, and gain tuning, the proposed architecture uses a physical spring-damper interface to store and dissipate impact energy at the contact point before high-frequency shocks propagate to the actuated joints and force-control loop. The approach is evaluated in MATLAB/Simulink on a 2-DOF planar manipulator with three end-effector configurations: rigid, spring-only, and spring-damper. Results under fixed and time-varying interaction conditions show that the spring-damper configuration provides stronger attenuation of contact-induced oscillations, lower force and velocity error variance, and smoother joint-torque response. Representative reductions include 36.5% in fixed-environment tangential force-error standard deviation, 25.4% in variable-environment normal force-error standard deviation, and 41.1% in variable-environment normal velocity-error standard deviation.

</details>


### [175] [From Real-Time Planning to Reliable Execution:Scalable Coordination for Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Fleets in Industrial Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00591)
*Bo Cao,Zhe Liu,Hesheng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCALE is a reactive online coordination framework for heterogeneous robot fleets that enables real-time path planning, reduces conflicts via motion-induced mechanisms, and adapts to disturbances using a Conjugate Action-Precedence Hypergraph (CAPH), validated through experiments and a three-day warehouse deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient coordination of heterogeneous robot fleets in industrial environments is challenging due to high densities, varied motion capabilities, communication delays, and execution uncertainties that cause deviations from planned paths leading to congestion and excessive waiting.

Method: SCALE is a reactive online coordination framework with two key components: (1) a motion-induced conflict reduction mechanism for online generation of feasible paths, and (2) a generalized Conjugate Action-Precedence Hypergraph (CAPH) that adaptively adjusts precedence relations among robots to mitigate disturbance effects.

Result: Extensive validation experiments and a three-day deployment in a warehouse demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

Conclusion: SCALE enables real-time planning with robust execution for heterogeneous robot fleets, effectively handling disturbances and preventing congestion propagation in industrial settings.

Abstract: With the increasing deployment of heterogeneous robot fleets in industrial environments, efficient coordination remains a critical challenge. Real-time path planning must simultaneously accommodate high robot densities and heterogeneous motion capabilities, while communication delays, execution uncertainties, and other disturbances may cause robots to deviate from the temporal assumptions underlying planned paths. Such deviations can lead to excessive waiting and congestion propagation across the fleet. This paper presents SCALE, a reactive online coordination framework that enables real-time planning while maintaining robust execution. Within this framework, we introduce a motion-induced conflict reduction mechanism to support the online generation of feasible paths for online conflict resolution. To mitigate the effects of disturbances, we further design a generalized Conjugate Action-Precedence Hypergraph (CAPH) that adaptively adjusts precedence relations among robots. Extensive validation experiments, together with a three-day deployment in a warehouse, demonstrate the

</details>


### [176] [Domain Arithmetic: One-Shot VLA Adaptation under Environmental Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00666)
*Taewook Kang,Taeheon Kim,Donghyun Shin,Jonghyun Choi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DART adapts VLA models to environmental shifts (e.g., camera pose changes, different robots) using weight vector arithmetic with a single demonstration, outperforming existing methods via subspace alignment to filter noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models fail under environmental shifts (camera pose, robot changes). Existing adaptation requires many costly demonstrations; DART aims to reduce this burden to a single demonstration.

Method: DART performs analogy-based adaptation via weight vector arithmetic (domain-specific information addition). It uses subspace alignment between singular components in weight vectors to isolate and add only relevant domain information while filtering noisy components.

Result: In both simulated and real-world experiments, DART outperforms existing VLA adaptation methods in one-shot scenarios across diverse visual and embodiment shifts.

Conclusion: DART enables efficient VLA model adaptation under environmental shifts with minimal data (one demo) and shows strong generalization across visual and robot embodiment changes.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often fail to perform the same learned tasks under environmental shifts, such as changes in camera pose and shifts to a different but similar robot (e.g., from Panda to UR5e). Adapting these models to the shifted environment (i.e., target domain) often requires training on multiple demonstrations for each task, which are costly to collect. To reduce the burden of data curation and training, we propose an analogy-based method that adapts VLA models under environmental shifts through weight vector arithmetic with domain-specific information addition, named Domain ARiThmetic (DART). Unlike prior approaches, DART requires collecting only a single demonstration, enabling efficient adaptation. To accurately isolate domain-specific information for addition, DART performs subspace alignment between singular components in weight vectors to filter out noisy components. In both simulated and real-world experiments, DART outperforms existing VLA adaptation methods in one-shot scenarios across diverse visual and embodiment shifts. Code is available at https://github.com/snumprlab/dart.

</details>


### [177] [Path Planning in Physically Viable World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00673)
*Su Ann Low,Cheng-Hsi Hsiao,Xingjian Li,Adam J. Thorpe,Ufuk Topcu,Krishna Kumar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a physically viable world model that augments 3D Gaussian splat scenes with physics simulation to evaluate how future terrain changes may affect robot navigation routes, tested with simulated flooding scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots deployed in unstructured outdoor environments rely on stale pre-deployment maps that assume terrain remains unchanged, but physical changes can render planned routes unsafe or unreachable, and operators cannot remap large/remote sites before every mission.

Method: The system augments reconstructed 3D Gaussian splat scenes with physics-based simulation to generate physically modified versions of the same environment without recollecting data. It implements a terrain-aware planner that accounts for simulated physical events, obstacles, and deformations to evaluate route feasibility.

Result: Evaluation on a real outdoor field site in Central Texas with simulated flooding showed that physically viable world models expose long-horizon route failures and rerouting behaviors not apparent when planning only on the original reconstruction.

Conclusion: Physically viable world models allow robots and operators to evaluate how future terrain changes may affect route feasibility before deployment, particularly in constrained environments where retreat may become impossible once conditions change.

Abstract: Robots deployed in unstructured outdoor environments often plan from scene reconstructions collected before deployment because operators cannot remap large or remote sites before every mission. As a result, robots must make long-horizon planning decisions using stale maps that assume the terrain remains unchanged, even though physical changes to the environment may render previously feasible routes unsafe or unreachable at execution time. We present a physically viable world model for evaluating what-if queries for robot navigation under future terrain change. The system augments reconstructed 3D Gaussian splat scenes with physics-based simulation to generate physically modified versions of the same environment without recollecting sensor data or rebuilding the map. We then implement a terrain-aware planner that accounts for physical events, obstacles, and deformations that are simulated by the world model. This allows robots and human operators to evaluate whether planned routes remain feasible before committing to a planned route, particularly in constrained environments where retreat or recovery may become impossible once conditions change. We evaluate the system on a real outdoor field site in Central Texas using simulated flooding across multiple severity levels. We measure route and mission feasibility as terrain conditions deteriorate under physically simulated interventions. Our results show that physically viable world models expose long-horizon route failures and rerouting behavior that are not apparent when planning only on the original reconstructed environment, allowing robots to evaluate how future terrain changes may affect route feasibility before deployment.

</details>


### [178] [From Prediction Uncertainty to Conformalized Distance Fields for Safe Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00776)
*Jaeuk Shin,Yoonseok Ra,Insoon Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces Functional Conformal Prediction (FCP) for safe motion planning, which conformalizes the entire predicted distance field rather than per-obstacle scores. It yields a distribution-free safety lower bound, uses offline PCA-based envelope fitting with online adaptive updates, and embeds this in an MPC controller (FCP-MPC). Results on pedestrian and dense 3D quadrotor benchmarks show favorable safety, feasibility, and efficiency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing conformal prediction methods for safe motion planning aggregate per-obstacle prediction errors into a scalar score, losing spatial coherence and scaling poorly with obstacle density. There is a need for a method that provides distribution-free safety guarantees while being computationally efficient in dense dynamic environments.

Method: The paper proposes Functional Conformal Prediction (FCP) that conformalizes the full predicted distance field at once. It exploits the empirical low-rank and approximate time-invariance of residual distance fields to decompose bounds in coefficient space. An envelope is fitted offline via functional PCA and Gaussian-mixture inductive conformal procedure, then refined online with a lightweight adaptive functional conformal (AFCP) update on a low-dimensional vector. This envelope is embedded as a safety constraint in a sampling-based MPC.

Result: On ETH-UCY pedestrian benchmarks and a dense 3D quadrotor task with up to 280 dynamic obstacles, FCP-MPC achieves a better balance of safety, feasibility, and efficiency compared to pointwise and egocentric conformal baselines. It avoids conservatism and high cost, while keeping per-step computation far below online uncertainty-reasoning baselines.

Conclusion: Functional conformal prediction enables distribution-free, spatially coherent safety guarantees for motion planning with minimal computational overhead. The approach scales well to dense environments and maintains robustness under distribution shift through online adaptive updates.

Abstract: Safe motion planning in dynamic environments requires reasoning about the uncertainty in predicted obstacle motion without sacrificing real-time performance. Existing conformal approaches conformalize a scalar score that aggregates per-obstacle prediction errors, losing spatial coherence and scaling poorly with scene density. We instead conformalize the entire predicted distance field at once. This functional conformal prediction (FCP) framework yields a distribution-free, field-level lower bound, from which safety follows uniformly: any trajectory satisfying the resulting constraint is certified safe, independent of how the control space is sampled. The key enabler is that the residual distance field is empirically low-rank and approximately time-invariant, which makes the bound decomposable in coefficient space. An envelope is fitted offline via functional PCA and a Gaussian-mixture inductive conformal procedure, then refined online by a lightweight adaptive functional conformal (AFCP) update on a low-dimensional vector. This keeps the per-step cost largely insensitive to obstacle count and retains long-run field coverage under distribution shift. We embed the envelope as a tightened safety constraint in a sampling-based model predictive controller, FCP-MPC. On the ETH--UCY pedestrian benchmarks and a dense 3D quadrotor task with up to 280 dynamic obstacles, FCP-MPC attains a favorable balance of safety, feasibility, and efficiency, reaching goals where pointwise and egocentric conformal baselines become too conservative or too expensive, while keeping per-step computation far below online uncertainty-reasoning baselines.

</details>


### [179] [From World Models to World Action Models: A Concise Tutorial for Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00836)
*Xiaoxiong Zhang,Xiong Zeng,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This tutorial clarifies world models as action-conditioned predictive models, categorizing them into observation-space and state-space types, and introduces world action models with four paradigms for embodied control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scope of "world models" remains ambiguous across different communities in embodied intelligence and generative simulation, necessitating a structured taxonomy and conceptual clarification.

Method: The paper presents a design-space view defining world models as action-conditioned predictive models, categorizes them into observation-space vs. state-space types, and introduces world action models with four representative paradigms.

Result: A structured taxonomy comparing trade-offs in visual fidelity, spatial structure, physical interpretability, and control usability; four paradigms: imagine-then-execute, video-feature-conditioned action prediction, joint video-action modeling, and auxiliary video prediction for policy learning.

Conclusion: The tutorial clarifies the conceptual scope of world (action) models and provides a structured taxonomy for embodied prediction and control.

Abstract: World models are increasingly used in embodied intelligence and generative simulation, yet their scope remains ambiguous across communities. This tutorial presents a design-space view of world models as action-conditioned predictive models that estimate the future evolution of task-relevant observations or states. We categorize existing methods into observation-space and state-space world models, comparing their trade-offs in visual fidelity, spatial structure, physical interpretability, and control usability. We further introduce world action models, which connect predicted futures with executable robot actions, and summarize four representative paradigms: imagine-then-execute, video-feature-conditioned action prediction, joint video-action modeling, and auxiliary video prediction for policy learning. The goal of this tutorial is to clarify the conceptual scope of world (action) models and provide a structured taxonomy for embodied prediction and control.

</details>


### [180] [Beyond Line of Sight: Hybrid Validation of V2X Collective Perception in Complex Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00874)
*Markos Antonopoulos,Anastasia Bolovinou,Bill Roungas,Elena Daskalaki,Angelos Amditis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Bayesian fusion algorithm for V2X-enabled Collective Perception, extending autonomous vehicle perception through probabilistic occupancy grids, validated via a hybrid simulation/vehicle-in-the-loop framework showing 260% FOV increase and recall improvement from 0.82 to 0.94.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limited perceptual horizon of individual autonomous vehicles by leveraging V2X communication and collective perception from multiple agents, while providing a certifiable validation methodology.

Method: Bayesian fusion algorithm integrating heterogeneous sensor data into probabilistic occupancy grids; hybrid testing framework combining CARLA simulation with vehicle-in-the-loop experimentation.

Result: 260% increase in field-of-view coverage; occupied-cell recall improved from 0.82 (ego-only) to 0.94 (six-agent CP) under nominal localization conditions.

Conclusion: The approach provides a reproducible, interpretable foundation for validating CP systems, supporting safe and certifiable deployment of cooperative autonomous vehicles.

Abstract: This paper introduces a probabilistic framework and hybrid validation methodology for V2X-enabled Collective Perception (CP) in complex traffic scenarios. The proposed Bayesian fusion algorithm extends the perceptual horizon of connected and autonomous vehicles by integrating heterogeneous sensor observations from multiple agents into a shared probabilistic occupancy grid. Each cell of this grid encapsulates both occupancy likelihood and uncertainty, enabling explainable and trustworthy situational awareness beyond the ego vehicle's field of view. To bridge the gap between simulation and real-world evaluation, a hybrid testing framework is developed, combining CARLA-based virtual environments with vehicle-in-the-loop experimentation. Experimental results in a roundabout scenario demonstrate a 260 percent increase in field-of-view coverage and a rise in occupied-cell recall from 0.82 (ego-only) to 0.94 (six-agent CP) under nominal localization conditions. Overall, the proposed approach provides a reproducible and interpretable foundation for validating CP systems, supporting the safe and certifiable deployment of cooperative autonomous vehicles.

</details>


### [181] [AMBUSH: Collaborative Capture in Complex Environments with Neural Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01029)
*Junfeng Chen,YinHang Luo,Xinyi Wang,Junrui Li,Meng Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes using an "ambush" strategy, optimized via Hybrid Monte Carlo Tree Search with neural acceleration, for multiple slower pursuers to capture faster evaders in complex environments with obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing works on multi-pursuer capture are largely constrained to obstacle-free environments or sparse/regular obstacles, and lack efficient strategies for handling faster evaders in complex environments.

Method: (1) A parameterized ambush strategy considering topological properties, line-of-sight visibility, speed ratio, and capture range. (2) Hybrid Monte Carlo Tree Search (H-MCTS) to optimize parameters via long-term planning. (3) Neural acceleration trained offline to learn parameter rankings and predict scores, replacing the rollout process to speed up online planning.

Result: Validated through extensive simulations and hardware experiments against evaders with different capabilities (up to 2x velocity) and intelligence levels (including human-controlled behavior), showing efficiency and effectiveness.

Conclusion: The ambush strategy alone, when properly parameterized and optimized via H-MCTS with neural acceleration, enables slower pursuers to capture faster evaders efficiently in complex obstacle-filled environments.

Abstract: Collaborative capture of dynamic targets is common in nature as an essential strategy for weaker species against the strong. Similar concepts have shown to be useful for numerous robotic applications, such as security and surveillance, search and rescue. However, most existing works focus on analytical and geometric solutions or end-to-end reinforcement learning methods, which are largely constrained to obstacle-free environments or scenarios with sparse, regularly distributed obstacles. This work tackles the problem from a unique perspective: the renowned strategy of``ambush'' alone would suffice for multiple slower pursuers to capture one faster evader with different levels of intelligence efficiently in complex environments. A parameterized strategy of ambush (including discrete and continuous parameters) is designed first, which takes into account the topological properties of the workspace, the truncated line-of-sight visibility, the relative speed ratio and the limited capture range. Then, a Hybrid Monte Carlo Tree Search (H-MCTS) algorithm is proposed to optimize the associated parameters through long-term planning, enabling the identification of highly promising parameters for future capture. Lastly, the neural acceleration is trained offline to learn the ranking of different choices of parameters across various environments, and to directly predict scores, replacing the rollout process in H-MCTS. The neural acceleration is adopted during online H-MCTS to accelerate the planning procedure while guaranteeing the planning quality. Its efficiency and effectiveness are validated in extensive simulations and hardware experiments, against evaders with different capabilities and intelligence levels, including two-times higher velocity and human-controlled behavior.

</details>


### [182] [DART-VLN: Test-Time Memory Decay and Anti-Loop Regularization for Discrete Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01043)
*Shaoheng Zhang,Zhichen Li,Jie Mei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DART-VLN is a training-free test-time control framework for discrete VLN that combines Test-Time Memory Decay (read-side memory reweighting) and Anti-Loop Regularization (next-hop penalty) to improve navigation reliability and efficiency without retraining or new parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory-based discrete VLN agents suffer from partial observability issues at test time: stale historical evidence during memory readout and inefficient local backtracking during action selection.

Method: DART-VLN introduces two training-free components: (1) Test-Time Memory Decay - a read-side reweighting rule that suppresses stale/redundant evidence without modifying stored content; (2) Anti-Loop Regularization - a lightweight penalty that discourages immediate reversal actions.

Result: Experiments on R2R and REVERIE show decay-only yields stable read-side gains, while decay+anti-loop achieves the best quality-efficiency trade-off with shorter trajectories, lower runtime, and improved navigation performance.

Conclusion: Modest test-time control can make memory-based discrete VLN more reliable and efficient without retraining, improving path efficiency and reducing backtracking under frozen backbones.

Abstract: Memory-based discrete vision-language navigation (VLN) agents must act under partial observability, yet even strong frozen backbones remain vulnerable at test time. Two common failure modes are stale historical evidence at memory readout and inefficient local backtracking during action selection. We present DART-VLN, a training-free test-time control framework for discrete VLN. DART-VLN combines Test-Time Memory Decay, a read-side memory reweighting rule that suppresses stale and redundant evidence without rewriting stored content, with Anti-Loop Regularization, a lightweight next-hop penalty that discourages immediate reversals during action selection. The framework introduces no new learnable parameters and leaves the learned backbone unchanged. Experiments on R2R and REVERIE show a consistent pattern: decay-only provides stable read-side gains, while decay+anti-loop achieves the best overall quality-efficiency trade-off, yielding shorter trajectories, lower runtime, and improved navigation performance in key settings. Behavioral analysis further confirms that anti-loop regularization reduces local backtracking and improves path efficiency under frozen backbones. Overall, the results show that modest test-time control can make memory-based discrete VLN more reliable and efficient without retraining.

</details>


### [183] [Robots Ask the Way: Communication-Enabled Social Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01044)
*Valentino Sacco,Luca Scofano,Indro Spinelli,Fabio Galasso*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CommNav enables robots to proactively ask residents for information to locate target individuals, achieving a 10% improvement in social navigation success, and proving robust to natural language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current social navigation methods rely on reactive collision avoidance and lack proactive information-gathering through human-robot communication.

Method: The authors introduce the CommNav task, extend Habitat 3.0 to Habitat 3.0c with communication protocols, and propose a communication module (COMM) added to a state-of-the-art social navigation model.

Result: Adding COMM yields a 10 percentage-point improvement in Episode Success; pre-training on a communication pretext task handles sparse interaction signals; the policy is robust to colloquial human language.

Conclusion: Explicit communication significantly enhances multi-person navigation, and the approach is robust to natural language variations.

Abstract: Assistive autonomous robots operating in multi-agent environments require efficient strategies to locate specific individuals among multiple residents. Current social navigation methods focus on reactive collision avoidance and trajectory adaptation, but lack mechanisms to proactively gather information through human-robot communication.
  We introduce Communication-enabled Social Navigation (CommNav). In this novel task, robotic agents actively seek assistance from residents to locate target individuals by requesting information about recent sightings, locations, and movements.
  To evaluate CommNav, we extend Habitat 3.0 to create Habitat 3.0c, a communication-enabled variant supporting multi-human environments with information exchange protocols. Adding our communication module (COMM) to a state-of-the-art social navigation model yields a 10 percentage-point improvement in Episode Success. We further investigate the transition from structured data to natural language by evaluating models trained on LLM-generated instructions and on colloquial instructions collected from a human study.
  Our experiments reveal that: (i) explicit human-robot communication substantially enhances multi-person navigation performance; (ii) pre-training COMM on a communication pretext task effectively addresses the challenge of occasional interaction signals; and (iii) the navigation policy is highly robust to natural, colloquial human language, achieving an episode success statistically similar to the model using perfect structured data.

</details>


### [184] [AutoSpeed: Annotation-Free Stage-Adaptive Motion Speed Learning for Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01051)
*Qingda Hu,Ziheng Qiu,Jieru Zhao,Zhongxue Gan,Wenchao Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AutoSpeed is a model-agnostic framework that enables visuomotor policies to execute manipulation tasks with stage-adaptive motion speeds, reducing execution time while improving success rates without requiring speed or stage annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing imitation learning visuomotor policies rigidly imitate expert demonstration speeds and use fixed temporal prediction horizons, which limits flexibility and throughput because different manipulation stages have varying difficulty levels that would benefit from stage-dependent speeds.

Method: AutoSpeed treats future trajectories at different speeds as candidate optimization targets, evaluates them via a composite cost balancing prediction error vs. horizon, and optimizes toward the minimum-cost candidate. Speed modulation is implemented in the frequency domain via discrete cosine transform (DCT) for smooth, non-integer scaling.

Result: AutoSpeed substantially reduces task execution time while also improving success rates, and the inferred motion speeds show strong correspondence with task stages.

Conclusion: Stage-adaptive speed modulation via DCT-based frequency domain optimization is an effective, annotation-free approach for improving both efficiency and success in visuomotor manipulation policies.

Abstract: Different stages of manipulation tasks exhibit varying levels of difficulty, suggesting stage-dependent motion speeds and temporal prediction horizons. However, existing IL-based visuomotor policies typically imitate the execution speed of expert demonstrations and operate with a fixed temporal prediction horizon, limiting flexibility and overall task throughput. In this paper, we introduce AutoSpeed, a model-agnostic learning framework that enables existing visuomotor policies to predict trajectories with stage-adaptive motion speeds, without requiring speed or stage annotations. We treat future trajectories at different speeds as candidate optimization targets, evaluate each candidate using a composite cost that trades off prediction error against prediction horizon, and optimize the policy toward the minimum-cost candidate. With a fixed-length action sequence, speed modulation adjusts the effective temporal prediction horizon: simple stages are executed faster with a longer prediction horizon, whereas complex stages are executed more slowly with a shorter prediction horizon. Specifically, we implement speed modulation in the frequency domain via the discrete cosine transform (DCT), which enables smooth, non-integer speed scaling and thus preserves motion continuity. Extensive evaluations show that AutoSpeed substantially reduces task execution time while also improving success rates. Under the AutoSpeed framework, the inferred motion speeds exhibit a strong correspondence with task stages.

</details>


### [185] [RoboWorld: Fast and Reliable Neural Simulators for Generalist Robot Policy Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01060)
*Byeongguk Jeon,Seonghyeon Ye,JaeHyeok Doo,Sungdong Kim,Minjoon Seo,Hyungmok Son,Kimin Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboWorld is an automated evaluation pipeline for robot policies using a fast autoregressive video world model and task-progress-aware VLM scoring, with Step Forcing to improve long-horizon rollout reliability, achieving near-perfect correlation (r=0.989) with real-world evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video world models offer a scalable alternative to real-world robot evaluation, but suffer from unreliable rollouts due to error accumulation and slow inference limiting throughput.

Method: Proposes RoboWorld pipeline: (1) fast autoregressive video world model with Step Forcing (anchored + one-step self-forwarded contexts), (2) task-progress-aware VLM scoring.

Result: Achieves Pearson's r=0.989 and Spearman's ρ=0.970 correlation with real-world robot evaluations across tasks and environments.

Conclusion: RoboWorld enables reliable, scalable automated evaluation of robot policies that strongly aligns with real-world performance.

Abstract: Video world models are emerging as a scalable alternative for evaluating generalist robot policies, bypassing the physical constraints and engineering burdens of real-world deployment. However, evaluating policies with video world models remains challenging, as world-model errors can make generated rollouts unreliable and slow inference limits large-scale throughput. We introduce RoboWorld, an automated evaluation pipeline that pairs a fast autoregressive video world model with a task-progress-aware vision-language model scoring. To enable reliable long-horizon autoregressive world-model rollouts, we propose Step Forcing, which combines anchored and one-step self-forwarded contexts to reduce train--test mismatch while preserving action--observation dynamics. Together, these components enable RoboWorld to align strongly with real-world robot evaluation across tasks and environments, achieving Pearson's r = 0.989 and Spearman's \r{ho} = 0.970.

</details>


### [186] [Human-Centric Transferable Tactile Pre-Training for Dexterous Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01067)
*Chi Zhang,Penglin Cai,Ziheng Xi,Haoqi Yuan,Hao Luo,Wanpeng Zhang,Sipeng Zheng,Chaoyi Xu,Zongqing Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: H-Tac is a large-scale tactile-action dataset with 160 hours of egocentric human videos (300+ tasks, 135K episodes), and Transferable Tactile Pre-Training (TTP) is proposed to pre-train on human tactile data and transfer to robotic tasks using unified tactile-action spaces and future tactile prediction, achieving superior performance in simulation and real-world experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tactile datasets are small-scale with limited contact coverage, and VLA models with tactile modality are constrained by dynamics-agnostic post-training, limiting performance on downstream tasks.

Method: 1) Collect H-Tac, a large-scale tactile-action dataset (160h, 300+ tasks, 135K episodes). 2) Propose TTP with unified tactile and action spaces across pre-training and post-training for human-to-robot transfer. 3) Use a tactile expert for future tactile prediction to model contact dynamics explicitly.

Result: Extensive experiments in simulation and on real robots show superior performance, robust generalization, and fine-grained manipulation capabilities.

Conclusion: TTP paves the way for scalable tactile pre-training via human-to-robot transfer, demonstrating effective bridging of the human-robot domain gap for tactile-based fine-grained robotic tasks.

Abstract: As an essential modality for dexterous and contact-rich tasks, tactile sensing provides precise force feedback that cannot be reliably inferred from vision. However, limited by hardware and data collection systems, existing datasets with tactility remain small in scale and narrow in contact coverage. Meanwhile, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models with tactile modality are constrained on dynamics-agnostic post-training, which limits the performance ceiling on downstream tasks. In this paper, we present H-Tac, a large-scale tactile-action dataset with 160-hour egocentric human videos containing more than 300 tasks and 135k episodes. Building upon this, we propose Transferable Tactile Pre-Training (TTP), a system of tactile-based pre-training on human data for fine-grained robotic tasks. To bridge the gap between humans and robots, we use unified tactile and action spaces throughout the pre-training and post-training phases, preserving prior knowledge during human-to-robot transfer. By leveraging a tactile expert for future tactile prediction, our framework explicitly models the contact dynamics and precise physical interactions. Extensive experiments in simulation and on real robots demonstrate that our model achieves superior performance, exhibiting robust generalization and fine-grained manipulation capabilities. TTP paves the way for scalable tactile pre-training via human-to-robot transfer.

</details>


### [187] [Where Am I? Semantic Map Grounding via Vision-Language Models for Multi-Modal Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01079)
*Suraj Borate,Aarav Shah,Madhu Vadali*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper reframes robot localization in GPS-denied indoor environments as a semantic reasoning task using a fine-tuned vision-language model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) with a regression head, achieving 96.75% full pose accuracy on in-distribution data and demonstrating robust cross-modal complementarity between camera and LiDAR inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address GPS-denied indoor robot localization by leveraging semantic reasoning from object-level cues and labeled maps, mimicking human localization strategies rather than relying on traditional geometric estimation.

Method: Fine-tunes Qwen2.5-VL-7B with LoRA and attaches a lightweight regression head to predict continuous pose (x, y, theta) from multimodal inputs (front camera image, polar LiDAR scan, top-down semantic grid map), using composite loss and curriculum learning on a custom 120K-sample dataset.

Result: 96.75% full pose accuracy, 0.11m mean position error, 5.7° mean orientation error, 0.62s per sample; 90.99% position accuracy on unseen object categories; 93.72% accuracy with incomplete maps via fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Vision-language models can effectively perform semantic spatial reasoning for localization, with LiDAR serving as a reliable fallback when camera semantics are unavailable, demonstrating strong cross-modal complementarity and adaptability to partial maps.

Abstract: We address robot localization in GPS-denied indoor environments by reframing it as a semantic reasoning task rather than a geometric estimation problem. Motivated by how humans localize using object-level cues and labeled maps, we ask whether a vision-language model, given a front camera image, a polar LiDAR scan, and a top-down semantic grid map, can infer the robot pose. We fine-tune Qwen2.5-VL-7B with LoRA and attach a lightweight regression head that predicts continuous pose coordinates (x, y, theta) directly from the final hidden state, bypassing text generation. Training uses a composite position-and-direction loss with curriculum learning on a custom Gazebo dataset of 120,112 samples and 527 scenes. On the in-distribution test set of 18,017 samples, the model achieves 98.23 percent position accuracy, 98.00 percent direction accuracy, 96.75 percent full pose accuracy, a mean position error of 0.11 m, and a mean orientation error of 5.7 degrees at 0.62 s per sample. Position accuracy drops by only 7.2 percentage points on seven unseen object categories, reaching 90.99 percent, supporting semantic spatial reasoning rather than appearance memorization. With incomplete maps, fine-tuning recovers performance to 93.72 percent position accuracy, showing adaptability to stale or partial map information. Two ablations highlight cross-modal complementarity. Without LiDAR, using only camera and map inputs, position accuracy remains 95.06 percent, only 3.2 percentage points below the full system. However, when the camera sees no visible objects in a wall-facing view, LiDAR sustains 92.33 percent position accuracy, compared with 70.74 percent when neither LiDAR nor visible objects are available. This shows that LiDAR becomes the primary localization signal when camera semantics are unavailable and provides a reliable fallback under occlusion or sparse layouts.

</details>


### [188] [ROSA: A Robotics Foundation Model Serving System for Robot Factories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01088)
*Wenqi Jiang,Jason Clemons,Rowland O'Flaherty,Hugo Hadfield,Alperen Degirmenci,Shuran Song,Yashraj Narang,Christos Kozyrakis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ROSA is a robotics foundation model serving system for robot factories that uses shared GPU-pool serving, multi-model pipelines, and factory-objective-driven scheduling to improve factory productivity by up to 12.06x over conventional dedicated serving systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RFM serving systems assume a single-robot, single-model paradigm with dedicated edge GPUs, which limits inference performance, battery life, and GPU utilization in factory settings with multiple robots and complex multi-model pipelines.

Method: ROSA implements three principles: (1) shared GPU-pool serving for fleet-wide access to server-class GPUs; (2) robotics-aware programming abstraction supporting multi-model pipelines, per-task requirements, and failure handling; (3) factory-objective-driven scheduling maximizing SLO-qualified productivity. Built on Ray Serve with vLLM, PyTorch, and JAX backends.

Result: Evaluated on real robots and synthetic large-scale workloads, ROSA improves factory productivity by up to 12.06x over conventional dedicated serving systems.

Conclusion: Shared GPU-pool serving and objective-driven scheduling significantly outperform dedicated per-robot GPU approaches for factory-scale robotics foundation model deployment.

Abstract: Robotics foundation models (RFMs) are making general-purpose robots increasingly practical for factory deployments. While RFM serving systems are central to this vision, existing systems are largely shaped by a single-robot, single-model assumption: inference is treated as an edge-computing problem handled by an on-robot or dedicated nearby GPU, and the serving objective is to minimize the latency of a single action model. In this paper, we propose ROSA, an RFM serving system for robot factories designed around three key principles. First, ROSA adopts shared GPU-pool serving, allowing a fleet of robots to access powerful server-class GPUs over the network in order to improve inference performance, battery duration, and GPU utilization. Second, ROSA provides a robotics-aware programming abstraction and system design that supports multi-model pipelines, per-task performance requirements, and failure handling. Third, ROSA uses factory-objective-driven scheduling to maximize SLO-qualified factory productivity rather than minimizing individual request latency. We implement ROSA on top of Ray Serve for distributed orchestration, with vLLM, PyTorch, and JAX as model-serving backends, and evaluate it on both real robots and synthetic large-scale workloads.
