The results show that ROSA improves factory productivity by up to 12.06x over conventional dedicated serving systems.

</details>


### [189] [Technical Report: Asynchronous Distributed Trajectory Estimation of Multi-Robot Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01106)
*Adam Pooley,Matthew Hale*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes an asynchronous block coordinate descent algorithm for distributed trajectory estimation that reduces communications by up to 96.9% while maintaining exponential convergence and achieving up to 64% less error than comparable methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing distributed trajectory estimation implementations typically do not account for asynchrony in agents' communications and computations, which limits their practicality in real-world robotic applications.

Method: The authors propose an asynchronous block coordinate descent algorithm where a team of agents observes robots and estimates their states over a sliding window. They derive an approximation of the maximum a posteriori estimation problem, prove exponential convergence of iterates, and validate through simulations and real robot experiments.

Result: The approximation eliminates up to 96.9% of inter-agent communications. The approach achieves up to 64% less error than a comparable state-of-the-art algorithm. Experiments show robustness to delays spanning three orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: The proposed asynchronous method is highly effective for distributed trajectory estimation, significantly reducing communication overhead and error while maintaining robustness to realistic delays.

Abstract: Distributed trajectory estimation arises in many applications across robotics, but existing implementations typically do not consider asynchrony in agents' communications and computations. Therefore, we propose an asynchronous block coordinate descent algorithm for distributed trajectory estimation. We consider a team of agents that observes a team of robots and estimates their states over a sliding window. The agents solve an approximation of the maximum a posteriori estimation problem, which we derive. We show this approximation introduces negligible errors and eliminates up to 96.9% of communications among agents. Next, we prove that agents' iterates converge exponentially fast to the optimal estimate of the robots' states. Simulations show that this approach has up to 64% less error than a comparable state-of-the-art algorithm. Experiments on mobile robots show the robustness of this approach to delays whose lengths span three orders of magnitude.

</details>


### [190] [FAR: Failure-Aware Retry for Test-Time Recovery and Continual Policy Improvement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01111)
*Haoran Hao,Shahram Najam Syed,Jeffrey Ichnowski,Jeff Schneider*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FAR is a framework that enables robots to learn from test-time failures through preference-based adaptation and exploration, improving success rates by ~17.6% in simulation and ~11.7% in real-world tasks without human intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot policies fail in real environments; naive retries repeat mistakes and existing recovery methods often require human intervention. The goal is to enable autonomous failure recovery and learning.

Method: FAR combines Failure-Contrastive Preference Adaptation (constructing preference data from failures to steer policy away from unsuccessful behaviors) with lightweight action perturbations during retries for local exploration, plus continual policy improvement using successful recovery trajectories.

Result: Average success rate gains of 17.6% in simulation and 11.7% in real-world over standard diffusion policy; improved data efficiency under reset and timestep budgets during continual policy improvement.

Conclusion: FAR enables autonomous learning from failures at test time, substantially improving robot policy robustness and success rates while reducing reliance on human intervention.

Abstract: Robot policies inevitably encounter failures when deployed in real environments. Naive retries often repeat the same mistakes, while many existing recovery methods rely on human intervention. In this paper, we propose Failure-Aware Retry (FAR), a framework that enables robots to learn from previous failures at test time, adapt their behavior accordingly, and eventually complete the task autonomously. FAR combines Failure-Contrastive Preference Adaptation, which constructs preference learning data from failures to steer the policy away from previously unsuccessful behaviors, with lightweight action perturbations during retries to encourage local exploration. We further incorporate successful recovery trajectories into a training loop for continual policy improvement. Experiments in both simulation and real-world manipulation tasks show that FAR substantially improves success rates and robustness, with average gains of 17.6% over the standard diffusion policy in simulation and 11.7% in the real world. In addition, FAR significantly improves data efficiency under both reset and timestep budgets during continual policy improvement by exploiting informative failure cases.

</details>


### [191] [Structured 4D Latent Predictive Model for Robot Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01166)
*Zhiyi Li,Peilin Wu,Xiaoshen Han,Ruojin Cai,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Structured 4D Latent Predictive Model that predicts 3D scene evolution in latent space, achieving better 3D consistency and multi-view coherence than 2D video-based planners, with superior performance on robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video predictive models operate on 2D video sequences, lacking 3D geometric understanding necessary for precise spatial reasoning and physical consistency in robotics.

Method: A structured 4D latent predictive model that encodes scene evolution in a structured latent space (decodable into diverse 3D formats), conditioned on observations and textual instructions, combined with a goal-conditioned inverse dynamics module for action generation.

Result: The model produces futures with strong visual quality, substantially better 3D consistency and multi-view coherence than state-of-the-art video-based planners, with superior performance on complex manipulation tasks and robust generalization to novel visual conditions.

Conclusion: The structured 4D latent predictive model serves as an effective planner for robotic systems, proving successful on real-world platforms by bridging 3D geometric understanding with action planning.

Abstract: Video predictive models are emerging as a powerful paradigm in robotics, offering a promising path toward task generalization, long-horizon planning, and flexible decision-making. However, prevailing approaches often operate on 2D video sequences, inherently lacking the 3D geometric understanding necessary for precise spatial reasoning and physical consistency. We introduce a Structured 4D Latent Predictive Model, which predicts the evolution of a scene's 3D structure in a structured latent space conditioned on observations and textual instructions. Our representation encodes the scene holistically and can be decoded into diverse 3D formats, enabling a more complete and 3D consistent scene understanding. This structured 4D latent predictive model serves as a planner, generating future scenes that are translated into executable actions by a goal-conditioned inverse dynamics module. Experiments demonstrate that our model generates futures with strong visual quality, substantially better 3D consistency and multi-view coherence compared to state-of-the-art video-based planners. Consequently, our full planning pipeline achieves superior performance on complex manipulation tasks, exhibits robust generalization to novel visual conditions, and proves effective on real-world robotic platforms. Our website is available at https://structured-4d-model.github.io/.

</details>


### [192] [FastBridge: Closing the Model-Based Realization Gap in Safety Filters on 3D Gaussian Splatting for Fast Quadrotor Flight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01200)
*Tscholl Dario,Nakka Yashwanth Kumar,Gunter Brian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A nonlinear, actuator-aware safety filter for quadrotors using 3D Gaussian Splatting that reduces jerk by 47% and runs 2.25x faster than prior methods, validated in simulation and hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS safety filters use reduced-order models (single/double integrators) that ignore actuator limits and assume instantaneous acceleration realization, which is unsafe for real quadrotor flight.

Method: Derives a high-relative-degree collision cone exponential CBF and a backup CBF preserving QP feasibility under input constraints via forward-simulated backup policy, using full quadrotor dynamics with 3DGS representation.

Result: 47% reduction in trajectory jerk, 2.25x faster runtime compared to state-of-the-art 3DGS safety filter.

Conclusion: The proposed actuator-aware safety filter enables safer, smoother, and faster quadrotor navigation in cluttered environments.

Abstract: Fast quadrotor flight requires safe obstacle avoidance under tight onboard compute limits. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provides a continuous, geometry-aware scene representation for perception-driven navigation, existing 3DGS safety filters use reduced-order models such as single- and double-integrators that ignore actuator limits and assume commanded accelerations are realized instantaneously. Building on an analytic collision cone barrier for 3DGS, we introduce a nonlinear, actuator-aware safety filter enforced through the full quadrotor dynamics. We derive a high-relative-degree collision cone exponential CBF and a backup CBF that preserves QP feasibility under input constraints using a forward-simulated backup policy. Compared with a state-of-the-art 3DGS safety filter, our approach reduces trajectory jerk by 47% and runs 2.25 times faster. We validate the method in simulation and on hardware for real-time navigation in cluttered, perception-derived environments.

</details>


### [193] [Sensorless Four-Channel Control Architecture Using Inverse Dynamics Modeling for Human-Scale Bilateral Teleoperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01201)
*Amir Noohian,Dylan Miller,Justin Valentine,Alan Lynch,Martin Jagersand*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a sensorless four-channel teleoperation architecture based on inverse dynamics modeling that improves transparency and performance in human-scale bilateral teleoperation without requiring external force/torque sensors, validated on a WAM setup and a door-opening task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The four-channel teleoperation architecture is limited in human-scale applications by high inertia, modeling difficulties, and reliance on noisy and costly force/torque sensors.

Method: A sensorless four-channel architecture based on inverse dynamics modeling is proposed, eliminating the need for external force/torque sensors.

Result: The proposed approach outperforms conventional two- and four-channel schemes and transparency-enhancement methods in position and force tracking, reduces operator effort, and increases maximum transmittable impedance. A door-opening case study confirms effectiveness in realistic human-scale tasks.

Conclusion: The sensorless four-channel architecture based on inverse dynamics is an effective solution for improving teleoperation transparency and performance in human-scale applications without external sensors.

Abstract: The four-channel teleoperation architecture is a well-established framework for achieving transparency in bilateral systems. However, its performance in human-scale teleoperation is limited by high inertia, modeling challenges, and reliance on noisy and costly force/torque sensors. This paper introduces a sensorless four-channel architecture based on inverse dynamics modeling. The controller is implemented and validated on a customized WAM bilateral teleoperation setup. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms conventional two- and four-channel schemes as well as transparency-enhancement methods, improving position and force tracking, reducing operator effort, and increasing maximum transmittable impedance without external sensors. A door-opening case study involving sustained whole-body contact along the manipulator further demonstrates the effectiveness of the method in realistic human-scale manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [194] [FurnitureVLA: Learning Long-Horizon Bimanual Furniture Assembly with Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01212)
*Chenyang Ma,Yue Yang,Radu Corcodel,Siddarth Jain,Andrew Wu,Chiori Hori,Diego Romeres*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FurnitureVLA is the first systematic study of real-scale bimanual furniture assembly using Vision-Language-Action models, achieving 80% simulation success and demonstrating real-world feasibility on a Kinova platform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robot furniture assembly focuses on toy-scale or single-arm settings; real-scale bimanual assembly remains underexplored despite its practical importance.

Method: A progress-enhanced VLA that jointly predicts actions and a continuous progress signal, finetuned on semantically grounded subtasks, with a scalable simulation pipeline for expert data generation and a VR teleoperation system for bimanual control.

Result: Improves simulation success from 48% to 80% across three furniture types, with an additional 21% gain from design factor study; only 16% performance drop on the hardest real-world task.

Conclusion: Progress-enhanced VLAs with semantic grounding significantly improve long-horizon furniture assembly performance, and the proposed system bridges the gap between simulation and real-world bimanual manipulation.

Abstract: Current work on robot furniture assembly mostly focuses on toy-scale settings or single-arm manipulation. We introduce FurnitureVLA, the first systematic study of real-scale bimanual furniture assembly using Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs). We formalize the task, develop a scalable simulation pipeline for expert data generation and evaluation, and build a VR teleoperation system for single-operator bimanual control to collect high-quality real-world demonstrations. To address extreme long-horizon assembly with up to 7 subtasks and 1550 control steps, we propose a progress-enhanced VLA, finetuned on semantically grounded subtasks, that jointly predicts actions and a continuous progress signal, enabling automatic subtask transitions and reducing compounding errors during inference. We further study perception and control design factors that critically affect precision in real-scale assembly. FurnitureVLA improves average simulation success from 48% to 80% compared to baselines across three furniture types, with an additional 21% gain from our design factor study. We validate on a real Kinova Gen3 platform with only 16% drop on the hardest task.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [195] [Representation as a Bottleneck for Mechanistic Interpretability: The Manifestation Unit Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00089)
*Hussein Chouman,Wataru Sasaki,Tomokazu Matsui,Hirohiko Suwa,Keiichi Yasumoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Manifestation Units, a typed tuple protocol (E, S, R, D, G) with attention-head primitives (T) for organizing mechanistic interpretability outputs into structured, queryable fields, demonstrating that typed structure outperforms unstructured baselines on retrieval and enables causal sufficiency/necessity verification across vision and language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mechanistic interpretability outputs (selectivity tables, circuit diagrams, feature lists) are locked in per-study notebooks, making them non-composable, not queryable in natural language, and not directly actionable for downstream audit or intervention. The authors aim to solve this by treating the representation layer between analyses and downstream use as an independently evaluable bottleneck.

Method: The authors propose Manifestation Units, a typed tuple protocol (E, S, R, D, G) extended with attention-head primitives (T) for transformers. This protocol organizes per-component statistics into structured fields that are populated automatically and queried through hybrid retrieval. They instantiate it across three model types: beta-VAE (generative vision), CNN (discriminative vision), and GPT-2 (language).

Result: (1) Typed structure substantially outperforms unstructured baselines on retrieval. (2) CNN filters retrieved by the schema satisfy causal sufficiency and necessity criteria under matched-budget controls. (3) The schema absorbs attention-head primitives without modification and set-recovers known IOI circuit members under budget-matched controls. (4) Analysis reveals an irreducible two-field core (S+R) with remaining fields either redundant or actively interfering.

Conclusion: The work presents schema infrastructure for mechanistic interpretability rather than frontier-scale validation, providing a structured protocol for organizing and querying interpretability analyses that is composable, queryable, and actionable for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability has produced a rich inventory of component-level analyses that characterise what neural-network components encode and how they interact. Their outputs, however, are not easily reusable: selectivity tables, circuit diagrams, and feature lists remain locked in per-study notebooks - non-composable, not queryable in natural language, and not directly actionable for downstream audit or intervention. We study the representation layer that sits between these analyses and downstream use as a bottleneck that can be evaluated independently, and introduce Manifestation Units, a typed tuple protocol (E, S, R, D, G) extended with attention-head primitives (T) for transformer architectures, organising per-component statistics into structured fields populated automatically and queried through hybrid retrieval. Instantiated across generative vision (beta-VAE), discriminative vision (CNN), and language (GPT-2), the protocol supports two findings: typed structure substantially outperforms unstructured baselines on retrieval, and CNN filters retrieved by the schema satisfy causal sufficiency and necessity criteria under matched-budget controls. The schema absorbs attention-head primitives without modification, set-recovers known IOI circuit members under retrieval-budget-matched controls, and reveals an irreducible two-field core (S+R) with remaining fields either redundant or actively interfering. We present this as schema infrastructure for mechanistic interpretability rather than frontier-scale validation.

</details>


### [196] [SNAP-FM: Sparse Nonlinear Accelerated Projection for Physics-Constrained Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00095)
*Alaina Kolli,Theodoros Xenakis,Utkarsh Utkarsh,Pengfei Cai,Rafael Gomez-Bombarelli,Alan Edelman,Christopher Vincent Rackauckas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the computational bottleneck of enforcing physical constraints (conservation laws, boundary conditions, invariants) in generative model sampling by exploiting block-sparse Jacobian and KKT structures from sample-wise batching and local PDE couplings, using ExaModels.jl and MadNLP.jl with GPU sparse factorization to accelerate nonlinear constraint projection in Physics-Constrained Flow Matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models used as surrogates for physical simulation lack guarantees that their outputs respect physical laws, boundary conditions, and invariants. Constrained sampling can enforce these exactly at inference, but projection steps become computationally expensive for nonlinear constraints, and standard ML frameworks struggle to exploit the natural sparse structure of physical constraints.

Method: The authors exploit block-sparse Jacobian and KKT systems induced by sample-wise batching and local PDE couplings, exposing this structure via ExaModels.jl and solving the sparse nonlinear programs with MadNLP.jl and GPU sparse factorization, applied to Physics-Constrained Flow Matching (PCFM).

Result: On PDE benchmarks with linear, nonlinear, 1D, and 2D constraints, this approach accelerates nonlinear constraint projection while maintaining constraint satisfaction.

Conclusion: Sparse GPU nonlinear optimization is a practical foundation for constrained generative sampling in scientific machine learning.

Abstract: Generative models have emerged as scalable surrogates for physical simulation, yet they offer no guarantee that their outputs respect the conservation laws, boundary conditions, and nonlinear invariants that govern the underlying physics. Constrained sampling closes this gap, enforcing such constraints exactly at inference time without retraining, but at a computational cost: projection, correction, and trajectory-optimization steps are repeated during sampling, with these steps becoming expensive for nonlinear constraints. Standard ML frameworks exacerbate this: their dense tensor algebra and limited sparse solver composability obscure the structure that physical constraints naturally induce, making efficient batched nonlinear optimization difficult to realize in practice. We address this bottleneck by exploiting the structure that sample-wise batching and local PDE couplings induce in the projection subproblems -- namely, block-sparse Jacobian and KKT systems -- exposing this structure using ExaModels.jl and solving the resulting sparse nonlinear programs with MadNLP.jl and GPU sparse factorization. Applied to Physics-Constrained Flow Matching (PCFM), on PDE benchmarks with linear, nonlinear, one-dimensional, and two-dimensional constraints, this approach accelerates nonlinear constraint projection while maintaining constraint satisfaction. These results show that sparse GPU nonlinear optimization is a practical foundation for constrained generative sampling in scientific machine learning.

</details>


### [197] [SemiScope: Disentangling Classifier Tuning and Joint Optimization in Semi-Supervised Security Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00113)
*Rui Shu,Tianpei Xia,Jingzhu He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that for binary tabular security classification with SSL and tree-based classifiers, most of the performance gains from joint SSL pipeline optimization come from simply tuning the downstream classifier (HPO + threshold optimization), not from SSL-specific components. A simpler recipe—Self-Training + tuned classifier + threshold tuning—reaches within 1 g-measure of Supervised RF with fewer labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Labeled data for security classification is scarce, motivating SSL. However, SSL pipelines are often used as black boxes with default parameters, and recent gains from AutoML-style joint optimization are hard to attribute—they may come from SSL-classifier interactions or simply from tuning the classifier. The authors aim to disentangle these effects.

Method: The authors build SemiScope, which uses Bayesian Optimization to jointly tune SSL settings, confidence filtering, oversampling, and classifier. The key control (Tuned-Clf) fixes SSL to defaults but gets the same 100-trial classifier budget and validation-set threshold tuning. At 10% labels, comparisons use paired TOST with +/-1.0 g-measure smallest effect of interest.

Result: SemiScope beats every default SSL baseline on all five datasets (0.7-12.7 points improvement). Under equal-budget control, Tuned-Clf is statistically equivalent to the full pipeline on 4/5 datasets; Phishing is inconclusive. Classifier HPO alone recovers median 86% of SemiScope's gain over Default ST+RF.

Conclusion: The reusable contribution is the decomposition protocol. A simpler recipe suffices: Self-Training + tuned classifier (Bayesian Optimization) + tuned decision threshold on validation data. This reaches within 1 g-measure of Supervised RF at 20-30% labels on four datasets and 40% on Drebin, at same or lower label rates than Default ST+RF.

Abstract: Background. Labeled data for security classification is scarce. Semi-supervised learning (SSL) propagates labels from a small labeled pool to larger unlabeled pools. Yet security applications often use SSL as a black box: default parameters, a fixed classifier, and no handling of pseudo-label-induced class imbalance. Aims. Recent work reports sizeable gains from optimizing SSL pipelines via joint search, AutoML, or per-component tuning. These gains are hard to attribute: they may reflect useful SSL-classifier interactions, or mostly from simply tuning the downstream classifier. We disentangle these effects for binary tabular security data with classical SSL and tree-based classifiers. Method. We build SemiScope as an analysis instrument, not a deployment recommendation. It uses Bayesian Optimization to jointly tune SSL settings, confidence filtering, oversampling, and the classifier. The key control, Tuned-Clf, fixes SSL to defaults but gets the same 100-trial classifier budget and validation-set threshold tuning as SemiScope. At 10% labels, we compare them with paired TOST using a +/-1.0 g-measure smallest effect of interest. Results. SemiScope beats every default SSL baseline on all five datasets, improving over the strongest by 0.7-12.7 points. Under the equal-budget control, Tuned-Clf is statistically equivalent to the full pipeline on 4 of 5 datasets; Phishing is inconclusive. Classifier HPO alone recovers a median 86% of SemiScope's gain over Default Self-Training (ST) + Random Forest (RF). Conclusions. The reusable contribution is the decomposition protocol. A simpler recipe suffices: use Self-Training, tune the classifier with Bayesian Optimization, and tune the decision threshold on validation data. It reaches within 1 g-measure of Supervised RF at 20-30% labels on four datasets and 40% on Drebin, at the same or lower label rate than Default ST + RF on every dataset.

</details>


### [198] [A Filtered Mixture-of-Generators for Fully Synthetic Survival Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00127)
*Niccolò Maria Rizzi,Eugenio Lomurno,Alberto Archetti,Matteo Matteucci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FoGS reframes synthetic data for survival analysis as a sample-selection problem over a pool of tabular generators, using ensemble scoring and two-level optimization to filter plausible samples, achieving downstream performance that matches or exceeds real-data training on most benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Survival analysis faces data scarcity due to long follow-up times, small cohorts, and privacy restrictions. Tabular generative models are themselves data-hungry and fail on small survival cohorts. A new approach is needed to produce high-quality synthetic survival data without requiring large training sets.

Method: FoGS draws candidate samples from four architecturally distinct tabular generators, scores each sample by an ensemble of seven survival models trained on real data using proper scoring rules, and optimizes a selection policy (generator quotas, scorer weights, random complement, stratified balancing) via a two-level pipeline: outer loop optimizes selection against held-out downstream performance, inner loop tunes XGBoost-Cox.

Result: On 16 public datasets (C-index and IBS, 0-100 scale), FoGS yields mean improvements of +2.17 in C-index and +0.67 in IBS, improves both metrics on 9/16 datasets and at least one on 13/16 (Wilcoxon p=0.039, p=0.035). Matches/exceeds real-data training on most cohorts with no significant privacy degradation.

Conclusion: Sample filtering over a heterogeneous generator pool is a viable substitute for real-data training in privacy-restricted clinical settings, reframing synthetic data construction as selection rather than generation.

Abstract: Survival analysis models time-to-event data, but in clinical settings training data are costly and scarce: events accrue over years of follow-up, cohorts are small, and privacy regulations restrict sharing across institutions. Tabular generative models promise augmentation and privacy-preserving cohort sharing, yet are themselves data-hungry -- on the small cohorts typical of survival analysis, a single generator rarely characterizes the population well enough for downstream models trained on its output to match real-data performance. FoGS (Filtered Mixture-of-Generators for Survival analysis) reframes synthetic-data construction as sample selection rather than generation. A candidate pool is drawn from four architecturally distinct tabular generators, and each sample is scored by an ensemble of seven survival models trained on real data, using proper scoring rules as a per-sample plausibility proxy. A two-level pipeline optimizes, in its outer loop, a selection policy -- generator quotas, scorer weights, a random complement, and stratified balancing on event time and censoring -- against held-out downstream performance, while an inner loop tunes the downstream model (XGBoost-Cox). On 16 public datasets under train-on-synthetic, test-on-real (C-index and IBS, $0$--$100$ scale), FoGS yields mean improvements of $+2.17$ in C-index and $+0.67$ in IBS, improving both metrics on 9 of 16 datasets and at least one on 13 (one-sided Wilcoxon $p=0.039$ and $p=0.035$). It matches or exceeds real-data training on most cohorts, with no significant change in nearest-neighbour privacy margin relative to unfiltered sampling. Sample filtering over a heterogeneous generator pool is thus a viable substitute for real-data training in privacy-restricted clinical settings.

</details>


### [199] [GRPO, Dr. GRPO, and DAPO Are Three Operations on One Number: The Group-Standard-Deviation Identity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00152)
*Yong Yi Bay,Kathleen A. Yearick*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Three popular RL methods for training language models to reason (GRPO, Dr. GRPO, DAPO) are mathematically equivalent—they all adjust based on the standard deviation of answer correctness across sampled responses. This disagreement measure directly controls the training update magnitude: unanimous answers teach nothing, while split answers drive learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods (GRPO, Dr. GRPO, DAPO) are presented as distinct algorithmic fixes, but the paper aims to unify them by showing they are actually the same mechanism with different settings of a single normalization parameter—the standard deviation of answer correctness.

Method: The paper proves a group-standard-deviation identity: for right/wrong rewards, the standard deviation of correctness marks directly equals the size of the training update. GRPO divides by this std, Dr. GRPO removes the division, DAPO filters out zero-std groups. The authors validate this theoretically and empirically on the Big-Math dataset and a controlled training run.

Result: All three methods are equivalent to adjusting a single dial—the standard deviation of answer disagreement. A split group (equal right/wrong) produces the largest updates; a unanimous group (all right or all wrong) produces zero update. This explains which problems get the most training weight and how many samples are needed per problem.

Conclusion: The normalization step in these methods is not a harmless technical detail but the core dial that determines where and how strongly learning occurs. This unification simplifies understanding of these seemingly different RL training techniques.

Abstract: Three of the most popular methods for training language models to reason look like three different tricks. They are not. All three adjust a single number: standard deviation, reflecting how much a prompt's sampled answers disagree. When such a model is trained, it answers each problem many times, and an automatic checker marks every answer right or wrong. The standard deviation of those marks measures the disagreement: largest when the answers split evenly between right and wrong, and zero when they all agree. Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) divides by this number, GRPO Done Right (Dr. GRPO) drops the division, and Decoupled Clip and Dynamic Sampling Policy Optimization (DAPO) discards the groups where it is zero. Each is presented as its own fix, yet this paper proves they are three settings of one dial. That dial is not cosmetic: for right-or-wrong rewards, the disagreement is exactly the size of the training update, the group-standard-deviation identity. A split group teaches the most, while a unanimous group teaches nothing and falls silent. The same result says which problems deserve the most weight and how many tries each one needs. This paper confirms the intuition on a large real difficulty dataset (Big-Math) and in a controlled training run. What looks like a harmless normalization step is the dial that decides where learning happens and how strongly.

</details>


### [200] [EVOTS: Evolutionary Transformer Search for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00154)
*AbdElRahman ElSaid,Damir Pulatov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVOTS is an evolutionary neural architecture search framework that discovers task-adaptive Transformer-like models for multivariate time-series forecasting, achieving competitive results on ETT benchmarks under various settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evolutionary neural architecture design for multivariate time-series forecasting remains underexplored, with most approaches relying on fixed Transformer architectures despite substantial variation across tasks and forecasting settings.

Method: The framework uses a modular genome representation enabling flexible composition of attention, feed-forward, and projection components, with a repair mechanism ensuring structural validity during evolution.

Result: Evaluated on ETTh1, ETTh2, ETTm1, ETTm2 under multiple settings (univariate-to-univariate, multivariate-to-univariate, multivariate-to-multivariate) with horizons 96/192/336/720; achieves competitive/improved MSE against strong Transformer baselines in multivariate-to-multivariate setting.

Conclusion: Evolutionary search can effectively discover flexible and high-performing Transformer-like architectures for multivariate time-series forecasting within practical runtime constraints.

Abstract: Evolutionary neural architecture design for multivariate time-series forecasting remains underexplored, with most approaches relying on fixed Transformer architectures despite substantial variation across tasks and forecasting settings. This paper introduces an evolutionary neural architecture search framework for discovering task-adaptive Transformer-like models for time-series forecasting (EVOTS). Architectures are encoded using a modular genome representation that enables flexible composition of attention, feed-forward, and projection components, while a repair mechanism enforces structural validity throughout the evolutionary process. This formulation allows effective exploration of a diverse architecture space without relying on hand-crafted design rules. The proposed approach is evaluated on four benchmark datasets from the ETT family (ETTh1, ETTh2, ETTm1, and ETTm2) under multiple forecasting settings, including univariate-to-univariate, multivariate-to-univariate, and multivariate-to-multivariate prediction, with horizons of 96, 192, 336, and 720. In the multivariate-to-multivariate setting, the evolved architectures achieve competitive and, in several cases, improved mean squared error relative to a strong Transformer-based baseline. Additional analyses examine performance differences across forecasting settings and report wall-clock training time to provide a coarse indication of computational cost. Overall, the results demonstrate that evolutionary search can effectively discover flexible and high-performing Transformer-like architectures for multivariate time-series forecasting within practical runtime constraints.

</details>


### [201] [FRAME: Learning the Adaptation Domain with a Mixture of Fractional-Fourier Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00162)
*Tom Saliencro,Maya Lindqvist,Rohan Desai,Priya Nair,Daniel Whitmore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FF-MoE introduces a mixture-of-experts adapter where each expert has a learnable fractional-Fourier order, allowing continuous interpolation between spatial (LoRA) and spectral (Fourier) domains for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The choice of domain (spatial vs. Fourier) for reparameterizing weight updates is a design degree of freedom that should be learned, as no single basis is optimal across tasks, layers, or tokens.

Method: Each expert in a MoE adapter carries a learnable scalar fractional-Fourier order that interpolates between spatial and Fourier domains; tokens are routed to experts with different orders; orders are trained with a separate optimizer; the transform uses an O(d log d) chirp-FFT surrogate for efficiency.

Result: Outperforms MoE-LoRA, FlyLoRA, FourierMoE, and HMoRA on commonsense, math, code, and knowledge benchmarks using LLaMA-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B, while keeping a small active-parameter budget.

Conclusion: Learned fractional-Fourier orders specialize by task and layer in interpretable ways, and the mutual incoherence of different orders reduces interference, improving multi-task composition.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) reparameterizes weight updates in a fixed basis: low-rank adapters operate in the spatial domain, while a recent line of spectral methods operates in a fixed Fourier domain. We argue that the choice of domain is itself a design degree of freedom that should be learned, and that no single basis is optimal across tasks, layers, or tokens. We introduce Fractional-Fourier Mixture of Experts, a mixture-of-experts adapter in which every expert carries a learnable fractional-Fourier order that continuously interpolates between the spatial domain (recovering vanilla LoRA) and the Fourier domain (recovering a spectral adapter). Routing tokens through experts that occupy different points on this spatial-spectral continuum lets the model place each low-rank update in the domain where it is most compact, and -- because fractional-Fourier operators of different orders are mutually incoherent -- makes the experts naturally decorrelated, which reduces interference and improves multi-task composition. The order is a single scalar per expert, trained with a separate optimizer, and the transform is computed with an $\mathcal{O}(d\log d)$ chirp--FFT surrogate, so Fractional-Fourier Mixture of Experts adds negligible cost over standard MoE-LoRA. Across commonsense, mathematical, code, and knowledge benchmarks on LLaMA-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B, Fractional-Fourier Mixture of Experts improves over strong MoE-LoRA and spectral baselines -- including FlyLoRA, FourierMoE, and HMoRA -- while keeping the active-parameter budget small, and analysis shows that the learned orders specialize by task and layer in interpretable ways.

</details>


### [202] [Verifiable Rewards for Calibrated Probabilistic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00164)
*Sadanand Singh,Allam Reddy,Manan Chopra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that a 7B model trained with a verifiable, label-free reward (state-conditioned empirical win rate) and gradient isolation achieves betting-market-level calibration for NFL win probability forecasting, outperforming standard RL with verifiable rewards which degrades calibration due to label noise and corrupted reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (e.g., Brier score) degrades calibration in practice because the single stochastic outcome is a noisy target and policy gradient corrupts the chain of thought. The authors aim to train calibrated probabilistic forecasters without human labels or supervised fine-tuning.

Method: The authors introduce (1) a verifiable, label-free reward using state-conditioned empirical win rate estimated from past outcomes to remove label noise, and (2) gradient isolation via direct prediction or gradient mask to prevent corruption of reasoning. They test on NFL in-game win probability forecasting.

Result: A 7B model trained only with this reward reaches betting-market calibration via direct prediction, and is better calibrated than a zero-shot frontier model. It matches the Brier score of both the frontier model and a tabular estimator, revealing the market's remaining edge is live in-game information beyond shared inputs.

Conclusion: Verifiable rewards can train calibrated probabilistic forecasters when label noise is removed and gradient is kept off the reasoning chain. Gradient masking preserves reasoning that chain-of-thought training corrupts.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards can in principle train calibrated probabilistic forecasters, since a proper scoring rule such as the Brier score is computed from outcomes alone and is minimized in expectation by the true probability. In practice it degrades calibration, and existing remedies address epistemic uncertainty, where a model's confidence accompanies a verifiably correct or incorrect answer. We study aleatoric forecasting, where the forecast itself is the output and the label is one stochastic outcome, taking NFL in-game win probability as a testbed with the betting market as a reference. Rewarding the realized per-play outcome fails, because the single outcome is a noisy target and the policy gradient corrupts the chain of thought. We introduce a verifiable, label-free reward, a state-conditioned empirical win rate estimated from past outcomes, that removes the label noise, and we keep the gradient off the reasoning, by direct prediction or a gradient mask, so it cannot be corrupted. Trained with this reward alone, without human labels or supervised fine-tuning, a 7B model reaches the calibration of the betting market by direct prediction and is better calibrated than a zero-shot frontier model. That frontier model and a tabular estimator reach the same Brier score as this model, identifying the market's small remaining edge as live in-game information beyond their shared inputs. Masking the gradient, rather than dropping the chain of thought, preserves reasoning from which the forecast follows, which ordinary chain-of-thought training corrupts.

</details>


### [203] [Scaling Up Thermodynamic AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00170)
*Andrew G. Moore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a scalable backpropagation-based training method for deep convolutional networks designed for thermodynamic inference on Ising machine hardware, achieving 94.9% on CIFAR-10 and 76.0% on CIFAR-100, along with a theory linking inference cost, accuracy, and autocorrelation times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thermodynamic computing devices based on the Ising model show promise for low-power AI inference and edge computing, but scalable methods for training large models for such hardware remain limited.

Method: The authors turn a theoretical correspondence between high-temperature Gibbs-sampled Ising systems and feed-forward neural inference into a scalable backpropagation-based algorithm for training deep convolutional networks for thermodynamic inference on Ising machine hardware.

Result: Image classification models achieve 94.9% on CIFAR-10 and 76.0% on CIFAR-100 under binary Gibbs sampling. Asymptotic results show inference cost is bounded by a well-controlled tradeoff with performance.

Conclusion: The paper develops and experimentally validates a mathematical theory relating inference cost to accuracy and controlling autocorrelation times, and discusses implications for hardware development and the future of high-temperature thermodynamic AI models.

Abstract: Thermodynamic computing devices based on the Ising model show great promise for low-power AI inference and edge computing, but scalable methods for training large models for such hardware remain limited. Prior theory shows that the time-averaged behavior of high-temperature Gibbs-sampled Ising systems can implement feed-forward neural inference. We turn this theoretical correspondence into a scalable and purely backpropagation-based algorithm for training deep convolutional networks for thermodynamic inference on Ising machine hardware. Our image classification models achieve accuracies of 94.9% on CIFAR-10 and 76.0% on CIFAR-100 under binary Gibbs sampling. We then develop and experimentally validate a mathematical theory relating inference cost to accuracy and controlling autocorrelation times. Subsequently, we calculate asymptotic results showing that inference cost is bounded by a well-controlled tradeoff with performance and exhibit algorithms for computing optimal inference schedules. Finally, we discuss implications for hardware development and the future of high-temperature thermodynamic AI models.

</details>


### [204] [TallyTrain: Communication-Efficient Federated Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00173)
*Radhakrishna Achanta,Will Reed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TallyTrain is a federated learning protocol that collapses class-count communication to log₂(C) bits per probe by transmitting only argmax class indices, achieving comparable or better accuracy than soft-label distillation with up to 1000x less communication, and its bandwidth-bridge variant Pareto-dominates FedAvg, FedProx, and FedDF.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning faces two orthogonal bandwidth bottlenecks: model size (limiting parameter-averaging frequency) and class count (making soft-label distillation expensive for large vocabularies). Both worsen as systems scale.

Method: TallyTrain transmits only the argmax class index (log₂(C) bits per probe) instead of full soft-label distributions. For non-IID settings, it uses majority voting on hard labels to filter noise from confidently wrong under-trained peers, unlike soft-label averaging which amplifies it. The bandwidth-bridge variant combines hard-label consensus with sparse parameter merges.

Result: TallyTrain matches or surpasses soft-label distillation while requiring up to three orders of magnitude less communication. The bandwidth-bridge variant Pareto-dominates FedAvg, FedProx, and FedDF across all tested operating points.

Conclusion: Hard-label consensus is not merely a compression technique but can be preferable to soft-label distillation under non-IID conditions, and combining it with sparse parameter merges yields a Pareto-optimal solution for federated learning bandwidth constraints.

Abstract: Federated learning is bandwidth-bound on two orthogonal axes: model size, which limits how often parameter-averaging methods can afford to merge, and class count, which makes per-probe soft-label distillation prohibitive at large vocabularies. Both ceilings tighten as modern systems scale. We collapse the class-count axis to $\lceil \log_2 C \rceil$ bits per probe by transmitting only each peer's $\arg\max$ class index, where $C$ is the number of output classes. The resulting protocol, TallyTrain, is not merely compressed: under non-IID training it can be preferable to soft-label distillation, because under-trained peers are confidently wrong and majority voting filters this noise where soft-label averaging amplifies it. Across standard benchmarks, TallyTrain matches or beats soft-label distillation at up to three orders of magnitude less communication. We also relax the model-size axis: we compose the cheap hard-label consensus with sparse parameter merges to obtain a bandwidth-bridge variant, which Pareto-dominates every tested operating point of the standard FedAvg, FedProx and FedDF baselines.

</details>


### [205] [Play Like Champions: Counterfactual Feedback Generation in Latent Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00190)
*Andrzej Białecki,Adam Mastalerz,Han Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Latent Maps of Performance, a framework using a Guided Variational Autoencoder trained on 23,305 professional StarCraft II replays to generate counterfactual improvement paths for amateur players by traversing from losing to winning gameplay profiles in a learned latent space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL research focuses on defeating humans rather than improving them. Unlike chess/Go where AI aids training, RTS games lack frameworks for translating expert knowledge into actionable feedback for players.

Method: Trained a Guided Variational Autoencoder on 23,305 pro replays. Four traversal strategies (linear interpolation, iterative optimal transport, density-regularized gradient ascent, neural flow matching) generate multi-step improvement paths. Feedback extracted at multiple granularities from amateur OOD data.

Result: Four traversal strategies verified on out-of-distribution amateur replays. Each method generates multi-step improvement trajectories grounded in observed expert behavior while moving toward winning configurations.

Conclusion: There is a trade-off between path-finding methods. Future research should focus on developing model solutions for human improvement in RTS games.

Abstract: Recent advances in reinforcement learning have produced superhuman agents across a wide range of competitive games. As a byproduct, researchers have begun studying how these agents play, extracting behavioral representations, analyzing decision structure, and modeling the latent geometry of expert performance. However, this growing body of work has overwhelmingly focused on defeating human players rather than providing feedback, leaving a critical gap in creating model solutions to improve human players. Unlike chess and Go, where AI has become integral to player training, real-time strategy (RTS) games lack principled frameworks for translating expert knowledge into actionable feedback. We introduce Latent Maps of Performance, a framework for counterfactual path generation. We focus on StarCraft~II data to model player improvement as an algorithmic recourse within a learned representation space. As inspiration for our work, we have looked at the championship model used in sports science. We trained a Guided Variational Autoencoder model on 23,305 professional tournament replays, enabling counterfactual traversal between losing and winning gameplay profiles. To fulfill our goal, we have devised and verified four traversal strategies on out-of-distribution (OOD) data randomly sampled from a dataset of amateur replays, namely linear interpolation, iterative optimal transport, density-regularized gradient ascent, and neural flow matching, each designed to generate multi-step improvement trajectories that remain grounded in observed expert behavior while moving a player's profile toward winning configurations. Feedback is extracted at multiple granularities to support players at different stages of improvement. Finally, we conclude that there is a trade-off between the path-finding methods we employ and hope that future research will focus on developing model solutions for human improvement.

</details>


### [206] [TRIE: An Evaluation Framework for Stochastic PDE Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00196)
*Bharat Srikishan,Javier E. Santos,Nikhil Muralidhar,Charles D. Young*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRIE is a framework evaluating stochastic PDE surrogates on three criteria: reproducing invariant measures, trustworthy uncertainty, and efficient generation. Tests on chaotic SPDEs show generative models outperform pointwise and approximate uncertainty methods, with latent generative models achieving 12x speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific systems with stochastic forcing, unresolved degrees of freedom, or imperfect observations produce uncertainty that is fundamentally distributional. Deterministic neural surrogates fail to capture statistical measures and forecast uncertainty, motivating a dedicated evaluation framework.

Method: TRIE evaluates surrogates on three criteria: (1) reproducing invariant measures, (2) trustworthy predictive uncertainty, (3) scalable probabilistic generation. Tested on two stationary chaotic SPDEs (stochastic Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and stochastic Kolmogorov flow) across 11 parameter values, comparing pointwise-trained surrogates, MC dropout, heteroscedastic Gaussian likelihoods, and generative models.

Result: Pointwise surrogates produce plausible short rollouts but fail on long-time statistics. MC dropout and heteroscedastic Gaussian likelihoods are miscalibrated and overconfident. Generative models best capture invariant measures and achieve lowest CRPS. Latent generative models with automatic dimension discovery retain fidelity while reducing inference time ~12x.

Conclusion: Generative models are the most reliable approach for distributional forecasting of stochastic PDEs, and latent generative models offer an efficient alternative without sacrificing statistical fidelity. The released benchmark supports reproducible evaluation.

Abstract: Many scientific systems exhibit uncertainty from stochastic forcing, unresolved degrees of freedom, or imperfect observations, making reliable surrogate forecasting fundamentally distributional rather than pointwise. For such systems, deterministic neural surrogates fail to capture statistical measures and forecast uncertainty. We introduce TRIE, an evaluation framework for stochastic PDE surrogates that asks whether models reproduce invariant measures, provide trustworthy predictive uncertainty, and scale to efficient probabilistic generation. We demonstrate TRIE on two stationary chaotic spatially extended SPDEs, stochastic Kuramoto--Sivashinsky and stochastic Kolmogorov flow, across 11 parameter values. Our evaluation shows that standard pointwise-trained neural surrogates can produce plausible short rollouts while failing to match long-time statistical structure. Approximate uncertainty methods such as Monte Carlo dropout and heteroscedastic Gaussian likelihoods produce stochastic forecasts, but are often miscalibrated and overconfident under temporal and spatial uncertainty diagnostics. Across these criteria, generative models provide the most consistent performance, accurately capturing invariant measure statistics and achieving the lowest CRPS in all reported probabilistic settings. Finally, we show that latent generative models with automatic dimension discovery retain much of this statistical fidelity while reducing Kolmogorov inference time by roughly $12\times$. We release our code and data at https://github.com/scailab/TRIE-SPDE-Bench to support reproducible evaluation of stochastic PDE forecasting models.

</details>


### [207] [Balancing Expressivity and Learnability in Quantum Kernel Bandit Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01080)
*Yuqi Huang,Vincent Y. F. Tan,Sharu Theresa Jose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using projected/approximate quantum kernels in GP bandit optimization to reduce complexity and regret while maintaining quantum advantages, with theoretical bounds and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum kernels can offer quantum advantage but full high-dimensional kernels increase model complexity and regret, harming learnability in NISQ-era tasks like quantum control and state preparation.

Method: Proposes projected quantum kernels and classical kernel approximation techniques to reduce feature dimensionality while preserving key quantum properties; develops misspecified GP bandit algorithms with regret bounds.

Result: Outperforms full quantum kernels in sample efficiency with substantially reduced computational overhead.

Conclusion: Approximate quantum kernels enable scalable GP optimization for quantum-native applications, with principled guidance for selecting optimal model complexity via regret bounds.

Abstract: We investigate Gaussian process (GP) bandit optimization with quantum kernels, assuming the mean reward function lies in the reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) induced by the quantum kernel. This setting is motivated by NISQ-era tasks such as quantum control, state preparation and variational quantum algorithms. While quantum kernels can offer a `quantum advantage' via domain-specific inductive biases, naïvely using full, high-dimensional kernels increases model complexity and information gain, leading to higher cumulative regret and poor learnability. To address this, we propose projected quantum kernels and classical kernel approximation techniques that reduce feature dimensionality while preserving key quantum properties. Using these approximate kernels, we develop misspecified GP bandit algorithms and derive regret bounds that characterize the trade-off between approximation error and information gain. The regret bounds provide principled guidance for selecting the optimal model complexity. Empirically, our methods outperform full quantum kernels in sample efficiency, while substantially reducing computational overhead, enabling scalable GP optimization for quantum-native applications.

</details>


### [208] [StateFlow: Dual-State Recurrent Modeling for Long-Horizon Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00197)
*Haroon Gharwi,Yue Dai,Kai Shu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StateFlow extends the VARNN model to long-horizon time series forecasting by using a dual-state recurrent backbone (hidden-state trajectory for primary dynamics and residual-memory trajectory for prediction errors) with a chunk-based decoder and two-stage optimization, achieving competitive performance on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon multivariate time series forecasting is challenging due to non-stationarity, regime shifts, and error accumulation. Existing VARNN is limited to one-step regression, so the authors aim to extend it to support multi-step forecasting.

Method: StateFlow uses VARNN as a dual-state recurrent backbone capturing: (1) hidden-state trajectory for primary temporal dynamics (trend, seasonality, level changes, patterns), and (2) residual-memory trajectory for structured local prediction errors via nonlinear recurrent transformation. A chunk-based decoder summarizes these trajectories for direct multi-step forecasting. A two-stage optimization first trains the VARNN encoder via one-step base prediction, then trains a horizon-specific decoder.

Result: StateFlow achieves competitive performance against linear, recurrent, convolutional, and Transformer-based baselines on standard LTSF benchmarks, while maintaining linear recurrent encoding and compact model design.

Conclusion: The proposed StateFlow framework effectively extends VARNN to long-horizon forecasting, demonstrating that a compact recurrent design with dual-state tracking can compete with more complex architectures.

Abstract: Long-horizon multivariate time series forecasting (LTSF) remains challenging due to non-stationarity, regime shifts, and error accumulation. The Variability-Aware Recursive Neural Network (VARNN) is designed to track such variability by maintaining a residual-memory state driven by one-step prediction errors. However, its original formulation is limited to one-step sequence regression and does not directly support multi-step forecasting. In this work, we extend VARNN to long-horizon forecasting and introduce StateFlow, a recurrent forecasting framework that uses VARNN as a dual-state recurrent backbone to capture two complementary signals from the lookback sequence: a hidden-state trajectory representing primary temporal dynamics, including trend, seasonality, level changes, and recurring patterns, and a residual-memory trajectory representing structured local prediction deviations, driven from a nonlinear recurrent transformation of errors between one-step base predictions and observed values. A chunk-based decoder separately summarizes these trajectories and maps them to the future horizon for direct multi-step forecasting. We further employ a two-stage optimization strategy that first trains the VARNN encoder through a one-step base prediction objective to optimize the internal representations over the lookback sequence, and then trains a horizon-specific decoder for direct multi-step forecasting. Experiments on standard LTSF benchmarks show that StateFlow achieves competitive performance against strong linear, recurrent, convolutional, and Transformer-based baselines while preserving linear recurrent encoding and a compact model design.

</details>


### [209] [Device Passport: Enabling Spatio-Temporal Pretrained Models to Generalize Across Input Layouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00249)
*Geeling Chau,Ran Liu,Juri Minxha,Wenhui Cui,Erdrin Azemi,Ellen L. Zippi,Behrooz Mahasseni,Christopher M. Sandino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Device Passport is a new channel embedding technique that uses both functional activity and metadata to improve cross-layout transfer of biosignal foundation models to new device layouts, outperforming prior methods that use only one type of information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: New device layouts lack large datasets for training, so biosignal foundation models need to generalize effectively across layouts; current channel embedding techniques don't adequately handle substantial differences between pretraining and downstream layouts.

Method: Device Passport learns experts and mixture models that take both each channel's functional activity and metadata as input, contrasting with prior methods that use only functional information or only metadata for learned or fixed positional embeddings.

Result: Device Passport is competitive overall and improves over the strongest learned baseline in layout-transfer regimes across controlled subset-transfer experiments and realistic ear-EEG transfer scenarios.

Conclusion: Channel embedding design is a key consideration when reusing large-scale pretrained biosignal models on new devices, and Device Passport offers an effective approach for cross-layout transfer.

Abstract: New device layouts pose a challenging modeling problem due to the lack of large datasets for each specific layout. Biosignal foundation models offer a plausible solution if they are able to generalize to new layouts effectively. To improve cross-layout transfer, we study how different channel embedding techniques behave when pretraining layouts differ substantially from the downstream decoding layout. We propose Device Passport, a new channel embedding technique that learns experts and mixture models that take each channel's functional activity and metadata as input. This contrasts with prior embedding methods, which typically use only functional information or only metadata to look up learned or fixed positional embeddings. Across controlled subset-transfer experiments and realistic transfer to ear-EEG, Device Passport is competitive overall and improves over the strongest learned baseline in the layout-transfer regimes that motivate this work. These results suggest that channel embedding design is a key consideration when reusing large-scale pretrained biosignal models on new devices.

</details>


### [210] [Distributionally Robust Linear Regression With Block Lewis Weights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00252)
*Naren Sarayu Manoj,Kumar Kshitij Patel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents an efficient algorithm for group distributionally robust least squares, achieving (1+ε)-optimal solutions with faster runtime than interior point methods by using block Lewis weights and accelerated proximal methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve group distributionally robust (GDR) least squares problems more efficiently than existing interior point methods, particularly for moderate accuracy regimes, and to bridge the gap between average loss minimization and robust loss minimization.

Method: Uses block Lewis weights (a geometric construction relating GDR to a weighted least squares problem) combined with accelerated proximal methods. The algorithm solves linear systems of the form A⊤BA for block-diagonal B, requiring Õ(min{rank(A),m}^{1/3}ε^{-2/3}) such solves.

Result: Achieves (1+ε)-multiplicative optimal solution with complexity Õ(min{rank(A),m}^{1/3}ε^{-2/3}) linear-system-solves; improves over interior point methods for moderate accuracy; matches state-of-the-art for ℓ∞ regression.

Conclusion: The algorithm provides a practical improvement for GDR least squares in moderate accuracy settings, and can smoothly interpolate between average and distributionally robust loss minimization.

Abstract: We present an algorithm for the group distributionally robust (GDR) least squares problem. Given $m$ groups, a parameter vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$, and stacked design matrices and responses $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, our algorithm obtains a $(1+\varepsilon)$-multiplicative optimal solution using $\widetilde{O}(\min\{\mathsf{rank}(\mathbf{A}),m\}^{1/3}\varepsilon^{-2/3})$ linear-system-solves of matrices of the form $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{A}$ for block-diagonal $\mathbf{B}$. Our technical methods follow from a recent geometric construction, block Lewis weights, that relates the empirical GDR problem to a carefully chosen least squares problem and an application of accelerated proximal methods. Our algorithm improves over known interior point methods for moderate accuracy regimes and matches the state-of-the-art guarantees for the special case of $\ell_{\infty}$ regression. We also give algorithms that smoothly interpolate between minimizing the average least squares loss and the distributionally robust loss.

</details>


### [211] [Learning dynamical systems from noisy data with Weak-form Kernel Ridge Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00257)
*Max Kreider,John Harlim,Daning Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Weak-form Kernel Ridge Regression (WKRR), a method that combines weak formulation with kernel learning to improve prediction of complex dynamical systems from noisy data, demonstrating superior performance on chaotic systems up to 15,000 dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard kernel ridge regression works well on clean data but struggles with noisy measurements. The weak formulation can filter noise, but its mechanisms were not well understood. The authors aim to explain the filtering mechanism and develop a more noise-robust learning strategy.

Method: The authors provide a bias-variance error decomposition to explain the weak formulation's filtering mechanism, then combine it with kernel ridge regression to propose WKRR - a simple-to-implement framework that integrates weak-form noise filtering with kernel learning for dynamical systems.

Result: WKRR outperforms several baseline methods on both clean and noisy data. It was successfully demonstrated on chaotic benchmark systems up to 64 dimensions and real-world fluid data with 15,000 dimensions.

Conclusion: The weak-form kernel ridge regression framework is effective, noise-robust, simple to implement, and scales well to high-dimensional real-world dynamical systems.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of complex dynamical systems from noisy measurements remains a significant challenge in scientific computing. Kernel ridge regression learning strategies are often effective when applied to clean data, but have limited success with noisy data. Recent work has observed that a weak formulation can act to filter noisy data, and different learning strategies have achieved increased noise robustness with a weak-form framework. In this manuscript, we give an overview of the filtering mechanism behind the weak formulation and provide a bias-variance error decomposition. Using these insights, we combine a weak formulation with a kernel learning strategy to propose Weak-form Kernel Ridge Regression (WKRR) for learning dynamical systems. The proposed framework is simple to implement, effective for both clean and noisy data, and outperforms several baseline methods. We demonstrate the performance of WKRR on chaotic benchmark systems in up to 64 dimensions, as well as 15,000-dimensional real-world fluid data.

</details>


### [212] [Validating Causal Abstraction Metrics on Simulated Complex Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00267)
*Maxime Méloux,Tiago Pimentel,François Portet,Maxime Peyrard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a benchmark to evaluate metrics for measuring the validity of high-level causal explanations, finding that only causal metrics with faithfulness testing reliably work, and proposes the Causal Abstraction Error (CAE) as a general-purpose validity metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no consensus on how to measure whether a proposed high-level causal explanation of a complex system is actually valid, despite the central scientific goal of producing such explanations.

Method: The authors created a benchmark of ten complex systems (discrete/continuous, static/dynamical) with ground-truth causal explanations and invalid contrastive conditions, then evaluated over thirty candidate metrics from observational, functional, information-theoretic, and causal families within a unified causal abstraction framework.

Result: Only causal metrics with faithfulness testing over unmapped variables reliably discriminate valid from invalid abstractions. The proposed CAE metric passes all discrimination tests across every system and can converge with as few as 30 sampled interventions.

Conclusion: The Causal Abstraction Error (CAE) with explicit faithfulness testing is offered as a general-purpose metric for discovering and validating high-level causal explanations.

Abstract: A central goal of science is to produce valid explanations of complex systems: high-level causal accounts that faithfully reflect the behavior of lower-level mechanisms. Yet no consensus exists on how to measure whether a proposed high-level explanation is actually valid. We introduce a benchmark of ten complex systems spanning both discrete and continuous state spaces, as well as static and dynamical regimes, each equipped with consensual ground-truth causal explanations and invalid contrastive conditions. Within a unified causal abstraction framework, we systematically evaluate over thirty candidate metrics drawn from observational, functional, information-theoretic, and causal families. Our results show that only the latter reliably discriminates valid from invalid abstractions, and only when incorporating faithfulness testing over unmapped variables. Building on these findings, we introduce the Causal Abstraction Error (CAE), a continuous validity metric with an explicit faithfulness test, which passes all discrimination tests across every system and can converge with as few as 30 sampled interventions. We offer it as a general-purpose metric for the discovery and validation of high-level explanations.

</details>


### [213] [Entropy-Regularized Probabilistic Gates for Sparse Model Discovery in Scarce-Data Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00275)
*Krishna Harsha Kovelakuntla Huthasana,Alireza Olama,Andreas Lundell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes entropy regularization of probabilistic gate distributions to maintain uncertainty in sparse federated learning, preventing early commitment to sparse support and improving generalization under data heterogeneity, partial client participation, and high-dimensional settings (d >> N).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning faces challenges under data heterogeneity and partial client participation, especially in the small-sample high-dimensional regime (d >> N) where sparse models are useful but prone to poor generalization. Existing magnitude-based pruning fails to account for uncertainty exploration in parameter space.

Method: Entropy regularization of gate distributions with an L0 constraint, allowing sampling from competing sparse configurations during training to maintain uncertainty and prevent early commitment to sparse support in federated optimization.

Result: Consistent improvements over Fed-IHT and pruning-after-dense-FedAvg on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, in both test statistical performance and sparsity recovery accuracy.

Conclusion: Entropy regularization effectively maintains exploration uncertainty in sparse federated learning, yielding better generalization and sparsity recovery under heterogeneous conditions and high-dimensional settings.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is a distributed machine learning (ML) paradigm with collaboration among multiple clients without sharing data. FL is challenging under data heterogeneity and partial client participation. Learning sparse models is useful for communication and computational efficiency in FL, but it is especially difficult in the small-sample high-dimensional regime (d >> N) where optimization can yield parameter configurations that fail to generalize to unseen test data. While magnitude-based pruning doesn't account for uncertainty exploration in the parameter space, a formulation with probabilistic gates and an L0 constraint allows sampling from competing sparse configurations during training. In this work, we study entropy regularization of gate distributions as a mechanism to maintain uncertainty in sparse federated optimization by preventing early commitment to sparse support. We examine its impact under data heterogeneity, client participation heterogeneity, and sparsity. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show consistent improvements over federated iterative hard thresholding (Fed-IHT) and pruning after dense federated averaging (FedAvg) training, both in statistical performance on test data and in sparsity recovery accuracy.

</details>


### [214] [Testing Frontier Large Language Models' Physics Literacy in Parallel Physical Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00276)
*Dong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a four-stage diagnostic to test whether LLMs can genuinely reason in unfamiliar physics frameworks (counterfactual and historical) rather than just recalling patterns. Across three models and three worlds, PASS rates are low (0-40%), revealing a qualitative-quantitative asymmetry, unreliable LLM-judge transfer across frameworks, and weak self-review capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM physics benchmarks measure answer accuracy, which cannot distinguish genuine reasoning from pattern recall and offers little insight into where reasoning fails.

Method: A four-stage diagnostic (induction, formulation, prediction, review) using locked pre-registrations, fresh sessions between stages, dual-LLM judging, and a human-audit pathway, applied to three parallel physics worlds: F=mv (counterfactual), Aristotelian mechanics (historical), and Decay World (four-domain counterfactual).

Result: Composite PASS rates: 6/15 for F=mv and Aristotelian, 0/15 for Decay World. Key pattern: qualitative-quantitative asymmetry (models rarely predict wrong direction but often compute wrong ratios). LLM-judge reliability does not transfer across frameworks. Stage 4 self-review wrongly reports no error in ≥2/3 of error-containing trials.

Conclusion: Standard answer-accuracy benchmarks are insufficient for evaluating genuine reasoning. The proposed diagnostic reveals specific failure modes (pattern-slipping, weak self-review) that are invisible to standard metrics. Full prompts, responses, verdicts, and audit records are released.

Abstract: Current large-language-model (LLM) physics benchmarks are usually scored by answer accuracy, which cannot distinguish genuine reasoning from recall of familiar problem patterns and reveals little about where a model's reasoning breaks down. We introduce an auditable four-stage diagnostic that evaluates whether an LLM can reason inside an unfamiliar physics framework through induction, formulation, prediction, and review. The diagnostic combines locked pre-registrations, fresh sessions between stages, dual-LLM judging, and a human-audit pathway, and we apply it to three parallel physics worlds: a single-equation counterfactual world ($F=mv$), a historical framework (Aristotelian mechanics), and a four-domain counterfactual world (Decay World). Across Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3.1 Pro, the three worlds yield composite PASS rates are 6/15, 6/15, and 0/15 respectively (content $\land$ structural for $F=mv$ and Aristotelian, content axis only for Decay World where the structural axis is out of scope). The most pointed empirical pattern is a qualitative-versus-quantitative asymmetry: in Decay World, models almost never predict the wrong direction of change, but frequently compute the wrong ratio by slipping back to standard-physics relations. The protocol also surfaces two methodology findings: LLM-judge reliability does not transfer across frameworks, and Stage 4 self-review is weak in every framework, with the model's own review wrongly reporting no earlier error in at least two-thirds of the trials that actually contained one. We release the full prompts, responses, verdicts, and audit records.

</details>


### [215] [Which Metric Reflects the Spelling Rate Accuracy in Event-Related Potential-Based Brain-Computer Interfaces?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00794)
*Okba Bekhelifi,Naoual El Djouher Mebtouche*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies which performance metrics best reflect spelling performance in ERP-based BCIs, finding that Brier score, MCC, ROC AUC, PR AUC, AP, and pAUC best correlate with spelling rate due to imbalanced class distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard metrics like loss and accuracy are insufficient for ERP-based BCIs, where spelling rate is critical for information transfer rate, and class imbalance requires suitable metrics.

Method: The authors analyzed correlations between spelling rate and 13 different metrics using two ERP datasets (private LARESI and public OpenBMI), examining how trial repetition affects these correlations.

Result: Brier score, MCC, ROC AUC, PR AUC, AP, and partial AUC best correlate with spelling rate, outperforming traditional metrics like accuracy and loss.

Conclusion: Researchers and practitioners should report Brier score, MCC, ROC AUC, PR AUC, AP, and pAUC in ERP-based BCI experiments to better reflect user spelling performance.

Abstract: For predictive models, the often-reported performance metrics are the loss and accuracy. In synchronous Brain- Computer Interface (BCI) systems, these metrics are informative for most BCI paradigms; however, for Event-Related Potential (ERP) applications the spelling rate, which measures the number of characters correctly selected is more important as it influences the estimation of information transfer rate (ITR) and any related metric measuring spelling performance.
