Moreover, ERP-based BCIs hold imbalanced data class distributions, which require reporting metrics that can handle the imbalance, such as the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC AUC). In this work, we study the correlation of the spelling rate with 13 metrics to identify which among them best reflect user spelling performance and how they are affected by trial repetition. The Results of two datasets (a private LARESI ERP dataset and the public OpenBMI ERP dataset) favor the Brier score, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and the metrics that account for class imbalance in binary classification: ROC AUC, area under the Precision-Recall curve (PR AUC), Average Precision (AP), and partial AUC (pAUC). These findings encourage researchers and practitioners to report those metrics in ERP-based BCI experiments.

</details>


### [216] [Understanding Guest Preferences and Optimizing Two-sided Marketplaces: Airbnb as an Example](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00280)
*Yufei Wu,Daniel Schmierer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Airbnb uses economic modeling and causal inference to understand how guests book based on prices, enabling better pricing tools for hosts and personalized guest experiences to improve marketplace connections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better connect guests and hosts by understanding how pricing affects booking behavior and how preferences vary across different guests and listings.

Method: Combining economic modeling with causal inference techniques to analyze how guests book stays based on host-set prices and how preferences differ across various guest and listing segments.

Result: Insights into price responsiveness and preference heterogeneity that can be applied to optimize host pricing tools and personalize guest matching.

Conclusion: Understanding price sensitivity and preference heterogeneity enables Airbnb to better balance demand/supply through competitive pricing tools and improve guest-host matching through personalization.

Abstract: Airbnb is a community based on connection and belonging -- many hosts on Airbnb are everyday people who share their worlds to provide guests with the feeling of connection and being at home; Airbnb strives to connect people and places. Among our efforts to connect guests and hosts, we provide tools to enable hosts to set competitive prices, which helps improve affordability for guests while helping hosts get more bookings. We also personalize the guest experience to show them the listings that match their needs.
  To help inform these efforts, we combine economic modeling and causal inference techniques to understand how guests book stays based on the prices hosts set, among other factors, and how that preference varies across different guests and listings. Such understanding helps us identify opportunities for Airbnb to support the marketplace and better connect guests and hosts. For example, understanding how much guests respond to different prices helps optimize the tools that we provide to hosts, in order to enable hosts to choose and set competitive prices that further balance demand and supply. As another example, understanding heterogeneity in guest preferences helps us personalize the guest experience and better match them with the listings that meet their needs, based on how much they respond to different prices and other factors.

</details>


### [217] [EPC: A Standardized Protocol for Measuring Evaluator Preference Dynamics in LLM Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00297)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces EPC, a standardized protocol for measuring evaluator preference coupling in LLM agent closed-loop systems, along with a versioned reference snapshot and open infrastructure for reproducibility and decay tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field lacks a standardized protocol to reproduce coupling measurements, compare results across evaluators and time points, and detect measurement decay from silent proprietary evaluator updates.

Method: The authors specify EPC as an RFC-style protocol with a four-phase isolation paradigm, covering executor/evaluator configuration, strategy/task design, TTRL update rule, and metric computation (gamma, JSD, ECE, Brier). They provide a Reference Snapshot v1.0 with 122 experimental repetitions across 8 evaluator conditions from 5 studies, with versioning conventions and usage guidance.

Result: A standardized protocol (EPC) and a versioned Reference Snapshot v1.0 containing coupling measurements for 8 evaluator conditions (GPT-4o, Qwen, DeepSeek, etc.) from 122 experimental repetitions across 5 independent studies, with evaluator version identifiers and measurement dates.

Conclusion: The protocol, snapshot, and implementation code are released as open infrastructure to enable third-party researchers to reproduce, compare, and track decay of coupling measurements over time.

Abstract: When LLM agents use evaluator feedback to adapt their behavior in closed loops, evaluator biases propagate through the agent's strategy distribution -- a phenomenon known as evaluator preference coupling. Prior work has documented coupling across multiple evaluator families and model versions, but the field lacks a standardized protocol that enables third-party researchers to (i) reproduce coupling measurements, (ii) compare results across evaluators and time points, and (iii) detect measurement decay as proprietary evaluators silently update. This paper provides the protocol. We specify EPC (Evaluator Preference Coupling) -- a detailed, RFC-style protocol specification for the four-phase isolation paradigm, covering executor and evaluator configuration, strategy and task design, the TTRL update rule, metric computation (gamma, JSD, ECE, Brier), and output schema. We accompany the protocol with a versioned Reference Snapshot v1.0: coupling measurements for eight evaluator conditions (N=122 unique experimental repetitions across GPT-4o, Qwen, DeepSeek, and others) derived from five independent studies, annotated with evaluator version identifiers, API endpoints, and measurement dates. The snapshot is explicitly time-bound: all values are conditional on specific model versions and are expected to decay as proprietary evaluators update. We define a versioning convention (vX.Y-Z, encoding protocol version, snapshot version, and evaluator generation) and provide a usage guide covering adoption, interpretation, and known pitfalls. The protocol, reference snapshot, and implementation code are released as open infrastructure.

</details>


### [218] [Generative Modeling of Quantum Distribution with Functional Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00301)
*Jaehoon Hahm,Tak Hur,Joonseok Lee,Daniel K. Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QFM uses flow matching on spin Wigner functions to learn multi-qubit quantum distributions, accurately capturing physical properties like purity and entanglement entropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning quantum distributions is challenging because traditional deep generative models struggle to model the meaningful physical properties of quantum states accurately.

Method: QFM converts density matrices into spin Wigner functions, then applies functional flow matching to learn distributions in function space for multi-qubit systems.

Result: The model effectively captures physical quantities (trace, purity, entanglement entropy) of generated quantum states, accurately reflecting underlying quantum physics.

Conclusion: QFM demonstrates that flow matching on spin Wigner representations is a promising approach for learning complex quantum distributions with physical fidelity.

Abstract: The emergence of powerful deep generative models based on diffusion and flow matching has enabled the learning and modeling of complex distributions. Learning quantum distributions, however, remains challenging due to the inherent difficulty of accurately modeling the meaningful physical properties of quantum states. We propose Quantum Flow Matching (QFM), a novel generative model designed to learn quantum distribution by utilizing spin Wigner function and flow matching. By converting density matrix into the spin Wigner function and leveraging functional flow matching to learn distributions in function space, QFM enables accurate and effective learning of multi-qubit quantum distributions. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by evaluating physical quantities such as trace, purity, and entanglement entropy of the generated quantum states, accurately capturing the underlying physics of the given quantum distributions.

</details>


### [219] [A Lightweight Self-Supervised Learning Framework for Multivariate Time Series using Hierarchical-JEPA on ECG Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01145)
*Siwon Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ER-JEPA is a lightweight SSL framework for multivariate time series (ECG) that uses a hierarchical two-stage Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture inspired by cardiologists' diagnostic approach, achieving SOTA on ST-MEM benchmark with ~180K pretrained recordings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical data analysis often faces limited target datasets alongside large unannotated datasets. SSL methods effectively leverage large datasets for ECG analysis, motivating a lightweight yet powerful hierarchical approach.

Method: ER-JEPA employs a two-stage hierarchical structure: (1) constructs representations per time interval, then processes them as univariate time series; (2) integrates two Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) with a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, forming a Hierarchical JEPA (H-JEPA).

Result: Pretrained on ~180K 10-second ECG recordings, ER-JEPA achieves state-of-the-art downstream performance on the ST-MEM benchmark with rapid computation and minimal resource usage.

Conclusion: The hierarchical JEPA approach (H-JEPA) successfully applies to multivariate time series (12-lead ECG), demonstrating sensitivity of hierarchical representations and showing SSL can be both lightweight and high-performing.

Abstract: Data analysis in the medical domain often encounters scenarios involving a limited target dataset and a large, unannotated dataset with a general distribution. Under such circumstances, self-supervised learning (SSL) methods are highly effective for utilizing large datasets, making them a popular choice for electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis. This work presents the Event Reconstruction Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (ER-JEPA), a lightweight SSL framework for multivariate time series, whose name and two-fold hierarchical structure are inspired by the diagnostic approach of cardiologists. At its core, ER-JEPA features: (1) a two-stage structure that constructs representations for each time interval and subsequently processes these representations as a univariate time series, (2) the hierarchical integration of two Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), and (3) a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone. The structural concatenation of two JEPAs categorizes the model as a Hierarchical JEPA (H-JEPA), designed to encode multiple levels of abstract representations for enhanced prediction on complex tasks. This study reports a successful application of H-JEPA to 12-lead ECG data as a multivariate time series alongside an analysis of the sensitivity of hierarchical representation during the pretraining stage. Pretrained on approximately 180,000 10-second recordings, the model achieves state-of-the-art downstream performance on the ST-MEM benchmark, with rapid computation and minimal resource usage.

</details>


### [220] [Mapping the Evaluation Frontier: An Empirical Survey of the Bias-Reliability Tradeoff Across Eleven Evaluator-Agent Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00304)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper empirically confirms the bias-reliability tradeoff in LLM evaluation systems: evaluator coupling (gamma), strategy diversity (H), and measurement reliability (CV) cannot all be optimized simultaneously. Expanding from 5 to 11 conditions, the data show that low coupling leads to high noise, strong coupling yields low noise, and coupling suppresses diversity (r=-0.989). No condition achieves both low gamma and low CV.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To empirically test and validate the bias-reliability tradeoff conjecture in LLM evaluation systems, which posits that evaluator coupling (gamma), strategy diversity (H), and small-sample measurement reliability (CV) are fundamentally constrained in a three-way tradeoff at fixed sample sizes.

Method: The authors expand the empirical base from 5 to 11 conditions, measuring gamma and H for all 11 conditions (9 with valid weight vectors) and CV(N=5) for 7 conditions with sufficient seeds. They analyze the correlations between metrics and identify version drift in GPT-4o as an anomalous pattern.

Result: The tradeoff is confirmed: low gamma (<0.2) corresponds to high CV (>1.0), high gamma (>0.9) yields low CV (<0.16). Gamma and H are strongly negatively correlated (r=-0.989, n=5 excluding GPT-4o). GPT-4o conditions show anomalous gamma=0.000 and H=1.000 due to version drift. No condition achieves both low gamma and low CV.

Conclusion: The bias-reliability tradeoff is robustly supported by expanded empirical evidence. The paper releases a standardized benchmark dataset for evaluator comparison, and highlights API version drift as a concern for reproducible LLM evaluation research.

Abstract: The bias-reliability tradeoff conjectures that LLM evaluation systems are constrained in (gamma, H, CV) space, where evaluator coupling (gamma), strategy diversity (H), and small-sample measurement reliability (CV(N)) cannot be simultaneously optimized at fixed sample size N. Prior evidence rests on n=5 conditions with complete metrics from a single study. We expand the empirical base to 11 conditions, measuring gamma and H for all 11 (nine with valid weight vectors) and CV(N=5) for seven with sufficient seeds (N >= 5). Five conditions provide the complete (gamma, H, CV) triple. The data confirm the trade-off: conditions with low evaluator coupling (gamma < 0.2) exhibit high measurement noise (CV(N=5) > 1.0), while conditions with strong coupling (gamma > 0.9) achieve low noise (CV(N=5) < 0.16). The correlation r(H, gamma) = -0.989 (n=5, excluding GPT-4o conditions) confirms that evaluator coupling suppresses strategy diversity. Four GPT-4o conditions show gamma=0.000 and H=1.000 across all seeds -- a pattern we attribute to version drift in the June 2026 GPT-4o API. No condition occupies the region {gamma < 0.2, CV(N=5) < 0.3}. We release all per-condition metrics as a standardized benchmark dataset for evaluator comparison.

</details>


### [221] [Watermarking for Proprietary Dataset Protection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00325)
*John Kirchenbauer,Brian R. Bartoldson,Bhavya Kailkhura,Tom Goldstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores using output watermarking techniques to improve training data membership inference for language models, showing comparable performance to traditional methods under certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training data membership inference is fundamentally hard in modern language modeling settings, motivating the need for alternative approaches to make it more tractable.

Method: The authors compare a watermark-based dataset inference approach against traditional loss-based membership inference methods, leveraging prior findings about residual watermark "radioactivity" in language models trained on partially watermarked data.

Result: Watermark-based membership detection achieves comparable performance to traditional loss-based methods when subset exposure is sufficiently high, under an alternate set of assumptions.

Conclusion: Output watermarking is a promising gadget for making training membership tests more tractable for generative models.

Abstract: A growing body of literature suggests that training data membership inference problems are fundamentally hard tasks in modern language modeling settings. We argue that output watermarking techniques are the right gadget to make training membership tests for generative models more tractable, based on prior results showing that language models exhibit residual watermark "radioactivity" under partially watermarked training datasets. We pit a watermark-based dataset inference approach head-to-head against traditional loss-based membership inference methods and show that watermarking can achieve comparable membership detection performance when subset exposure is high enough, under an alternate set of assumptions.

</details>


### [222] [K-Inverse-RFM: A Modified RFM that Bridges the Gap to Neural Networks for Data-Corrupted Mathematical Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00329)
*Gil Pasternak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RFMs underperform FNNs on corrupted data, but a simple label transformation closes or reverses this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RFMs match FNNs in feature learning but perform worse on noisy, imbalanced, or complex data; the paper investigates why and seeks a fix.

Method: Proposes a transformation applied to training labels that boosts RFM learning in corrupted settings.

Result: The label transformation enables RFMs to match or exceed FNN performance on corrupted data.

Conclusion: RFMs can be made competitive with FNNs even under data corruption through a simple label adjustment.

Abstract: Recursive Feature Machines (RFMs) are a class of kernel machines that utilize the Average Gradient Outer Product (AGOP) as a mechanism for feature learning. They have been shown to effectively replicate the learning dynamics and feature representations of Feedforward Neural Networks (FNNs) across various settings. However, despite comparable capacity for feature learning and the similarities in the features they acquire, RFMs exhibit significantly lower performance than neural networks in certain data-corrupted scenarios. In this work, we investigate these limitations in mathematical problems. As a solution, we introduce a remarkably effective transformation applied to the training labels which promotes learning in noisy, complexly represented, and class-imbalanced data. This simple yet powerful adjustment enables RFMs to close the performance gap with FNNs and, in some cases, even surpass them.

</details>


### [223] [PRISM: Prioritized Channel Importance with Semi-supervised Domain Adaptation for Cross-Subject EEG Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00358)
*Xin Zhou,Xiang Zhang,Hao Deng,Lijun Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRISM is a novel framework for emotion decoding from EEG that uses prioritized channel importance (via differentiable weights from an expert ensemble) and semi-supervised domain adaptation (via confidence-filtered pseudo-labels) to achieve robust cross-subject generalization with limited labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome channel redundancy and inter-subject variability in EEG-based emotion decoding, which hinder scalable cross-subject generalization.

Method: PRISM assigns differentiable, data-dependent channel weights via a lightweight expert ensemble to amplify reliable electrodes and suppress distractors, while leveraging unlabeled data through confidence-filtered pseudo-labels for consistency regularization and domain alignment.

Result: PRISM surpasses state-of-the-art methods on DEAP, DREAMER, and SEED datasets, achieving robust cross-subject generalization with limited annotations.

Conclusion: The proposed PRISM framework effectively enables label-efficient, robust cross-subject emotion decoding by jointly addressing channel redundancy and domain shift.

Abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG) captures endogenous brain activity with high temporal fidelity and holds substantial promise for precise emotion decoding. However, channel redundancy and pronounced inter-subject variability remain key obstacles to scalable generalization. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework termed PRioritized channel Importance with Semi-supervised doMain adaptation (PRISM), enabling label-efficient cross-subject emotion decoding. On the channel side, PRISM assigns differentiable, data-dependent channel weights via a lightweight expert ensemble, amplifying reliable electrodes while suppressing distractors. On the domain side, PRISM leverages unlabeled data through confidence-filtered pseudo-labels to drive consistency regularization and domain alignment, mitigating subject-specific heterogeneity. Extensive experiments show that PRISM surpasses state-of-the-art methods on DEAP, DREAMER, and SEED datasets, achieving robust cross-subject generalization given limited annotations.

</details>


### [224] [SAOT: Self-Supervised Continual Graph Learning with Structure-Aware Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00377)
*Yuting Zhang,Yanbei Liu,Zhitao Xiao,Lei Geng,Yanwei Pang,Xiao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAOT is a Structure-Aware Optimal Transport framework for self-supervised continual graph learning that preserves global relational structure across sequential tasks, outperforming baselines by up to 15%+ on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-supervised CGL methods use instance-level consistency objectives that optimize nodes in isolation, failing to maintain global relational structure and causing progressive distortion of inter-node correspondences under continual learning.

Method: SAOT uses optimal transport theory to capture global inter-node correspondences and incorporates cross-task knowledge distillation to preserve previous structural knowledge across sequential graph tasks.

Result: SAOT outperforms existing self-supervised baselines on four CGL benchmarks, achieving up to 5% accuracy improvement on CoraFull-CL and over 15% on Products-CL in the Class-IL setting.

Conclusion: Explicitly preserving global relational structure via optimal transport is effective for self-supervised continual graph learning, significantly outperforming instance-level consistency methods.

Abstract: Self-supervised Continual Graph Learning (CGL) aims to successively learn from a graph sequence with different tasks without label supervision - a paradigm that has attracted widespread attention. Most existing self-supervised CGL methods rely on instance-level consistency objectives that enforce stability of individual node (or node-pair) embeddings. Due to optimizing nodes in isolation, these methods fail to maintain global relational structure, causing inter-node correspondences to progressively distort under continual learning. To this end, we propose a novel Structure-Aware Optimal Transport (SAOT) framework that explicitly captures and preserves relational structure within graph representations across sequential tasks. Specifically, SAOT leverages optimal transport theory to capture global inter-node correspondences, thereby facilitating and enhancing graph representation learning. Simultaneously, SAOT incorporates a cross-task knowledge distillation mechanism to preserve the previous structural knowledge. Extensive experiments on four CGL benchmark datasets demonstrate that SAOT outperforms existing self-supervised baselines. In particular, SAOT achieves significant performance gains, improving average accuracy by up to 5% on CoraFull-CL and over 15% on Products-CL compared with state-of-the-art methods in the Class-IL setting.

</details>


### [225] [Learning Generalizable Skill Policy with Data-Efficient Unsupervised RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00392)
*Jongchan Park,Seungjun Oh,Seungho Baek,Yusung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GenDa proposes a unified framework for unsupervised reinforcement learning that addresses non-stationary skill semantics via skill relabeling and brittle generalization via Complementary Information Bottleneck (CIB), achieving superior data efficiency and generalizability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current off-policy URL methods suffer from two overlooked bottlenecks: (1) non-stationary skill semantics during pre-training, and (2) brittle generalization to downstream tasks, limiting scalability and data efficiency.

Method: GenDa introduces (1) a skill relabeling mechanism to mitigate non-stationarity and improve data efficiency, and (2) Complementary Information Bottleneck (CIB) to encourage policies to focus on ego-centric features for robust generalization under distribution shifts.

Result: Experiments show GenDa significantly enhances scalability with superior generalizability and data efficiency compared to existing URL methods.

Conclusion: GenDa provides a unified, robust solution to key bottlenecks in unsupervised reinforcement learning, enabling more effective pre-training of skill-conditioned policies for downstream tasks.

Abstract: Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning (URL) aims to pre-train scalable, skill-conditioned policies without extrinsic rewards, serving as a foundation for downstream control tasks. Despite recent progress, we argue that current off-policy URL methods are limited by two critical, overlooked bottlenecks: (1) non-stationary skill semantics and (2) brittle generalization. To address these challenges, we propose GenDa (Generalizable Data-efficient Agent), a unified framework for robust unsupervised reinforcement learning. First, we introduce a skill relabeling mechanism to mitigate non-stationarity and significantly improve data efficiency for pre-training. Second, we propose a Complementary Information Bottleneck (CIB), encouraging the learned skill policy to focus on ego-centric features and become robust to distribution shifts for downstream tasks. Through various experiments, we demonstrate that GenDa significantly enhances the scalability of URL with superior generalizability and data efficiency. Our code and videos are available at https://ihatebroccoli.github.io/official-GenDa.

</details>


### [226] [Timesynth: A Temporal Fidelity Framework for Health Signal Digital Twins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00431)
*Md Rakibul Haque,Shireen Elhabian,Warren Woodrich Pettine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeSynth reveals that standard pointwise metrics misrank forecasting models for health-signal digital twins by failing to detect loss of phase, frequency, and state-transition dynamics; linear and full-sequence attention models systematically lose these properties while localized architectures better preserve dynamical fidelity, making model choice a use-case-driven decision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pointwise metrics used to benchmark forecasting models for health-signal digital twins cannot detect when oscillatory, frequency, phase, and state-transition dynamics are lost, leading to misranked models (e.g., up to 53° phase error invisible to standard metrics).

Method: TimeSynth: a controlled benchmarking framework with (1) a physiologically grounded generator producing signals with analytically known ground-truth dynamics from parametric models fitted to real EEG, ECG, and PPG signals, and (2) diagnostics quantifying amplitude, frequency, phase, and state-transition fidelity.

Result: Across 11 architectures, models with comparable pointwise error diverge by up to 53° in phase accuracy (~123ms for 1.2Hz cardiac rhythm). Linear and full-sequence attention models systematically lose frequency and phase despite acceptable amplitude error; localized temporal architectures better preserve dynamical fidelity but none reliably handles stochastic switching.

Conclusion: The dominant determinant of fidelity is architectural, so model choice becomes a principled, use-case-driven decision rather than a search for a single winner. TimeSynth supplies a needed preclinical stress test before models are coupled to patient data.

Abstract: Forecasting models for health-signal digital twins must preserve the oscillatory, frequency, phase, and state-transition dynamics of physiological signals, yet the pointwise metrics used to benchmark them cannot detect when these fundamental properties are lost. We show that this blind spot misranks models: across 11 architectures, models with comparable pointwise error diverge by up to 53° in phase accuracy, equivalent to roughly 123 ms for a 1.2 Hz cardiac rhythm and invisible to standard metrics. To enable development of models that escape such failures, we introduce TimeSynth, a controlled benchmarking framework with two reusable components: a physiologically grounded generator producing signals with analytically known ground-truth dynamics from parametric models fitted to real electroencephalography, electrocardiography and photoplethysmogram signals, along with diagnostics quantifying amplitude, frequency, phase, and state-transition fidelity. Linear and full-sequence attention models systematically lose frequency and phase information despite acceptable amplitude error, whereas architectures with localized temporal structure better preserve dynamical fidelity and adapt to observable state transitions; none, however, reliably preserves stochastic switching. Because the dominant determinant of fidelity is architectural, model choice becomes a principled, use-case-driven decision rather than a search for a single winner. TimeSynth thus supplies the controlled preclinical stress test missing before models are coupled to patient data, with a reusable generator and diagnostics for fidelity-aware development.

</details>


### [227] [Gauging, Measuring, and Controlling Critic Complexity in Actor-Critic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00452)
*Konstantin Garbers*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces critic complexity as a diagnostic and intervention tool in actor-critic RL, using spectral effective-rank entropy to measure and control complexity, showing measurable associations with training behavior while noting heterogeneous effects across algorithms and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critic quality in actor-critic methods is typically evaluated only indirectly (return, TD error, value loss). The paper aims to introduce a more direct diagnostic dimension—critic complexity—to better understand and potentially control critic behavior during training.

Method: The authors use spectral effective-rank entropy (a rank-like summary of singular-value distributions of critic weight matrices) to measure critic model complexity. They track this metric alongside return and Monte Carlo value-estimation bias across TD3 and PPO experiments. They also test a direct intervention by adding a spectral-entropy penalty to the critic loss.

Result: Critic complexity is measurable throughout training and systematically associated with training behavior, but the relationship varies across algorithms, tasks, and hyperparameters. The spectral-entropy penalty intervention reliably changes the targeted spectral quantity, demonstrating controllability. Return effects are task-dependent rather than universally positive.

Conclusion: Critic complexity can be both measured and controlled during actor-critic RL training, but its relationship to performance is heterogeneous and task-dependent, not a general improvement mechanism.

Abstract: Actor-critic methods depend on learned critics, but critic quality is often evaluated only indirectly through return, temporal-difference error, or value loss. Critic complexity is introduced as an additional diagnostic and intervention dimension for actor-critic reinforcement learning. The analysis uses spectral effective-rank entropy, a rank-like summary of the singular-value distributions of critic weight matrices, to assess critic model complexity. Across TD3 and PPO experiments, critic complexity is tracked together with return and Monte Carlo value-estimation bias. The results show that critic complexity is measurable throughout training and is systematically associated with training behavior, while also making clear that the relationship is heterogeneous across algorithms, tasks, and hyperparameters. A direct complexity-control intervention is then evaluated by adding a spectral-entropy penalty to the critic loss. This intervention reliably changes the targeted spectral quantity, demonstrating that critic complexity can be controlled rather than only observed. Return effects are treated as task-dependent evidence rather than as a general performance claim, because overall complexity-control results vary.

</details>


### [228] [MolSafeEval: A Benchmark for Uncovering Safety Risks in AI-Generated Molecules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00464)
*Tong Xu,Xinzhe Cao,Zhihui Zhu,Keyan Ding,Huajun Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MolSafeEval is a new benchmark that systematically evaluates safety risks (toxicity, reactivity, hazards) of AI-generated molecules by integrating heterogeneous safety knowledge into a structured knowledge graph and using LLM-based reasoning, covering four generative task types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing molecular generation benchmarks focus on novelty, complexity, and property alignment but overlook safety risks—toxic, reactive, or hazardous molecules produced by generative models pose hidden dangers.

Method: MolSafeEval integrates diverse safety knowledge (toxicological databases, hazard rules) into a structured molecular safety knowledge graph, which enables LLM-based reasoning for detecting and explaining unsafe features. It categorizes models into four task types (unconditional generation, property optimization, target protein-based design, text-based generation) with standardized datasets and evaluation protocols.

Result: Systematically reveals safety vulnerabilities across current molecular generative models, providing a new lens for benchmarking beyond traditional metrics.

Conclusion: MolSafeEval offers essential guidance toward safer, more trustworthy molecular design by addressing a critical gap in safety evaluation for AI-generated molecules.

Abstract: Current molecular generation benchmarks emphasize task complexity, molecule novelty, and property alignment; they largely overlook a critical concern: the potential safety risks of AI-generated molecules. In practice, many generative models may produce molecules with toxic, reactive, or otherwise hazardous characteristics - posing hidden dangers that remain insufficiently addressed. To address this gap, we introduce MolSafeEval, a benchmark dedicated to evaluating and analyzing the safety risks of molecular generation. Unlike prior approaches that rely on narrow toxicity predictors, MolSafeEval integrates heterogeneous safety knowledge - ranging from toxicological databases to hazard rules - into a structured molecular safety knowledge graph. This graph serves as a foundation for large language model-based reasoning, enabling systematic detection and explanation of unsafe features in generated compounds. We further categorize molecular generative models into four representative task types - unconditional generation, property optimization, target protein-based design, and text-based generation - and provide standardized datasets and safety evaluation protocols for each. By systematically revealing the safety vulnerabilities of current generative approaches, MolSafeEval offers a new lens for benchmarking molecular models and provides essential guidance toward safer, more trustworthy molecular design.

</details>


### [229] [How Early Is Early Enough? Design-Dependent Observation-Window Sufficiency in Subscription Churn Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00473)
*Xiao Han,Yao Xiao,Chenyu Wu,Tongchen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines how much early behavioral data is needed to predict subscription churn in the KKBox dataset, finding a diminishing-returns knee at 45-90 days—but this curve shifts depending on cohort design, target definition, and feature sets, so such claims must specify these contextual factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine the minimum amount of early behavioral data required for effective churn prediction, particularly for manual-renewal segments, and to understand how different experimental designs affect this sufficiency curve.

Method: The authors use the public KKBox dataset, focusing on the manual-renewal segment. They construct a nine-window sufficiency curve and stress-test across three cohort/task designs (including a moving-target variant) with varying feature sets.

Result: A diminishing-returns knee at 45-90 days in the sufficiency curve; a +0.10 PR gain at 120 days for manual-renewal segments. However, the curve inverts or shifts under different cohort/task designs and feature sets.

Conclusion: Window-sufficiency claims are not universal; they must be reported alongside cohort construction, target definition, and feature families. The mechanism may generalize but exact magnitudes are dataset-specific.

Abstract: How many days of early behavior suffice for subscription churn prediction? In the public KKBox dataset, the early indicator of churn is typically an indicator of someone's contract status; however, when looking in the heavily churned manual-renewal segment, having access to early behavior creates a substantial increase in prediction for that specific segment (PR +0.10 at 120 days). A nine-window sufficiency curve shows a diminishing-returns knee in a 45-90 day band. However, stress-testing over three cohort/task designs shows that this curve is singular to the design being tested; for example, in our test with a moving target, the curve inverts and can shift depending on the feature set used. Therefore, any window-sufficiency claim should state its cohort construction, target definition, and feature families. All evidence is from one music-streaming dataset; the mechanism should generalize but the magnitudes may not.

</details>


### [230] [Interpretable vs Learned Encoders for High-Cardinality Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00477)
*Xiao Han,Jingjing Liu,Moxuan Zheng,Zhen Zhang,Chenyu Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Seven categorical encoding methods were tested on a fraud benchmark dataset. Entity embeddings achieved the highest AUC-ROC (0.9612), statistically tied with CatBoost (0.9602), while CatBoost led in AUC-PR (0.822). No single encoder dominated both metrics. The embedding advantage came from joint multi-column representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To compare categorical encoding methods for high-cardinality features in fraud detection, evaluating both discriminative performance (AUC-ROC) and precision-recall trade-offs (AUC-PR).

Method: Seven encoders tested on IEEE-CIS fraud dataset (590,540 records, 3.5% positives, 8 high-cardinality columns). Stratified 5-fold CV with 3 repetitions. Five encoders used frozen LightGBM learners; CatBoost and TabNet served as cross-paradigm comparisons.

Result: Entity embeddings achieved highest AUC-ROC (0.9612), statistically tied with CatBoost (0.9602), and superior to tier group encoding (0.9548). CatBoost led AUC-PR (0.822 vs. 0.793). No encoder dominated both metrics. TabNet collapsed under data scarcity.

Conclusion: Entity embeddings are best for AUC-ROC; CatBoost for AUC-PR. Choice depends on metric priority. Embedding advantage stems from joint multi-column representation. Tier group encoding offers auditor-friendly interpretability with minimal performance loss.

Abstract: A total of seven categorical encoding methods were tested on the IEEE-CIS fraud benchmark dataset (590,540 records, 3.5% positives, 8 high-cardinality columns). The encoders were evaluated using a stratified 5-fold cross-validation (CV) with three repetitions. Five of the encoders had identical frozen LightGBM learners in the downstream phase, allowing for controlled comparisons of their performance to each other. CatBoost and TabNet were included as comparisons across paradigms using different learners. The entity embeddings produced the highest AUC-ROC (0.9612), with a statistically significant tie with that of CatBoost (0.9602) and statistically superior to tier group encoding (0.9548), whereas target encoding was only 0.0023 worse than tier group encoding and the auditor-friendly tier boundaries were maintained. Off-the-shelf TabNet did not outperform tree-based pipelines and collapsed under data scarcity. On AUC-PR, CatBoost leads (0.822 vs. 0.793); no encoder dominated both metrics. Per-column analysis confirmed the embedding advantage arises from joint multi-column representation.

</details>


### [231] [Ghost in the Kernel: In-Context Learning with Efficient Transformers via Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00479)
*Peilin Liu,Ding-Xuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper theoretically analyzes linear transformers' in-context learning abilities through domain generalization, proving dimension-independent convergence rates and proposing new activation/loss designs for linearizing pretrained softmax LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Softmax transformers have quadratic complexity with context length; linear transformers reduce this but their feature mapping design and theoretical understanding remain unclear.

Method: Investigates linear transformers under a two-staged sampling process from domain generalization; derives generalization bounds showing in-context learning learns a mapping from context distributions to response functions.

Result: Obtains a dimension-independent convergence rate for generalization analysis, revealing a tradeoff between regularities of data distributions and latent features.

Conclusion: Proposes a new perspective on activation and loss design for linearizing pretrained softmax large language models, guided by the theoretical framework.

Abstract: Transformer-based large models have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities across different tasks by leveraging a context-aware attention module for in-context learning. With richer context, transformers adapt more effectively to the current use case without any parameter updates. However, the quadratic computational and memory complexity with respect to context length significantly slows data processing in softmax transformers. Linear transformers were proposed to address this issue by reducing the complexity to linear dependence on context length, but the design and understanding of the feature mapping in linear attention, from a theoretical viewpoint, remain unclear. In this paper, we investigate the approximation and generalization abilities of linear transformers under a two-staged sampling process from domain generalization. We show that linear transformers perform in-context learning as learning a mapping from context distributions to response functions. A dimension-independent convergence rate is obtained for our generalization analysis, which also exhibits the tradeoff between the regularities of data distributions and latent features. Guided by our theoretical framework, we propose a new perspective on activation and loss design for linearizing pretrained softmax large language models.

</details>


### [232] [PAPA: Online Personalized Active Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00486)
*Anindya Sarkar,Nasik Muhammad Nafi,Isaac Lyngaas,Muralikrishnan Gopalakrishnan Meena,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PAPA is a method that fine-tunes diffusion models using real-time user feedback instead of a parameterized reward model, making preference alignment more efficient for personalized recommender systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches require large-scale preference data to train a parameterized reward model, which is often impractical. The goal is to align diffusion models with user preferences under limited feedback.

Method: PAPA bypasses reward model learning by directly optimizing the diffusion model via real-time user feedback using a variational inference framework. An enhanced version (EPAPA) is also proposed for lower computational cost.

Result: Extensive experiments and ablation studies across class-conditioned and fine-grained alignment tasks demonstrate PAPA's effectiveness in feedback-efficient preference alignment.

Conclusion: PAPA provides a practical, feedback-efficient solution for aligning diffusion models with user preferences, suitable for real-world deployment, with EPAPA further improving efficiency.

Abstract: Diffusion models are highly effective at modeling complex data distributions, including images and text. However, in applications like personalized recommender systems, the objective often shifts to modeling specific regions of the distribution that maximize user preferences-initially unknown but gradually uncovered through interactive feedback. This can naturally be framed as a reinforcement learning problem, where the goal is to fine-tune a diffusion model to maximize a reward function based on preferences. However, the main challenge lies in learning a parameterized reward model, which typically requires large-scale preference data-something that is often not feasible in practice. In this work, we introduce Personalized Active Preference Alignment PAPA, a novel method that bypasses the requirement for a parametrized reward model by directly optimizing the diffusion model using real-time user feedback. PAPA enables feedback-efficient preference alignment, drawing inspiration from the variational inference framework. We demonstrate PAPA's effectiveness through extensive experiments and ablation studies across diverse class-conditioned and fine-grained alignment tasks. Additionally, based on theoretical insights, we propose an enhanced fine-tuning strategy, referred to as EPAPA, that requires less computational budget and accelerates the fine-tuning process, further boosting PAPA's suitability for real-world deployment. Our code is made publicly available at https://github.com/NasikNafi/papa.

</details>


### [233] [Prototype Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00510)
*Dan Ley,Giang Nguyen,Himabindu Lakkaraju,Julius Adebayo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRISM is a prototype-based language model architecture using sparse, non-negative mixtures of learned prototypes for each prediction. It matches or nearly matches dense baseline accuracy (within 2.5%) across 130M-1.6B scales trained up to 50B tokens, while enabling ~500x faster training data attribution, localizing loss curvature, supporting traceable model corrections, and enabling targeted behavior removal via prototype suppression without finetuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for identifying which training examples drive language model outputs are expensive, approximate, and post-hoc because standard LLMs distribute training data influence across dense parameters without traceable components. The authors aim to build an interpretable-by-design architecture that makes training data attribution efficient and enables direct model auditing and correction.

Method: The authors design PRISM, a prototype language model architecture where each token prediction is formed via a sparse, non-negative mixture of learned prototypes. Prototypes are trained with clustering objectives that anchor each prototype to coherent neighborhoods of training examples. The architecture is evaluated across 130M to 1.6B parameters trained on up to 50B tokens.

Result: PRISM matches or stays within 2.5 percentage points of dense baseline accuracy. Sparse prototype structure yields a more tractable Hessian and enables training data attribution ~500x faster than post-hoc baselines at equivalent memory. Calibrating linear prototype controllers improves accuracy by ~3 points while enabling tracing corrections to training neighborhoods. Targeted prototype suppression removes model behaviors without finetuning or measurable generation quality loss.

Conclusion: Prototype-based architectures like PRISM offer a viable path toward interpretable-by-design language models that maintain competitive performance while providing built-in mechanisms for efficient data attribution, targeted correction, and behavioral auditing—capabilities currently infeasible for standard dense LLMs.

Abstract: Knowing which training examples drive outputs is fundamental to auditing, correcting, and understanding language models, yet for modern LLMs this remains expensive, approximate, and largely post-hoc. Standard language models generate tokens through a dense network pathway, causing training data's influence to be distributed across parameters rather than organized along explicit, traceable components. We introduce a prototype language model architecture, Prototypes for Interpretable Sequence Modeling (PRISM), that forms each prediction via a sparse, non-negative mixture of learned prototypes, trained with clustering objectives that anchor each prototype to coherent neighborhoods of training examples. Across architectures from 130M to 1.6B parameters trained on up to 50B tokens, prototype language models either surpass or remain within 2.5 percentage points on average downstream accuracy of matched dense baselines. We show that sparse prototype structure localizes curvature in the loss landscape, yielding a more tractable Hessian and enabling training data attribution that is ~500x faster than post hoc baselines when consuming equivalent memory. Calibrating linear prototype controllers can improve downstream accuracy by roughly 3 points while tracing those corrections back to training neighborhoods, and targeted prototype suppression can remove model behaviors without finetuning or measurable loss in generation quality.

</details>


### [234] [From Structural Equation Modelling to Double Machine Learning: Robustness Analysis for Survey-Based Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00512)
*Ka Ching Chan,Qiana Liu,Sanjib Tiwari,Ranga Chimhundu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a staged robustness analysis framework combining SEM, OLS regression, and Double Machine Learning (DML) to assess the stability of structural relationships in survey-based research. Demonstrated on a FinTech Digital Customer Intimacy model, it identifies which relationships are robust across methods and provides a reusable Google Colab template.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SEM path significance is model-specific and may not reveal whether findings remain stable under alternative estimation frameworks, motivating a systematic robustness workflow.

Method: A three-stage framework: (1) SEM for measurement refinement and baseline estimation, (2) OLS regression on SEM-derived construct scores as a transparent benchmark, (3) DML-style residualisation with machine-learning-based adjustment for observed controls, including learner-sensitivity checks (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, SVM) and reverse-direction diagnostics.

Result: The framework identifies which relationships in the FinTech Digital Customer Intimacy model are stable across SEM, OLS, and DML checks, and which require cautious interpretation.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a practical robustness workflow and interpretation guide for survey-based researchers, with a publicly available reproducible Google Colab template.

Abstract: Structural equation modelling (SEM) is widely used in survey-based business and information systems research to assess latent constructs and theory-driven structural relationships. However, SEM path significance is obtained within a particular model specification and may not show whether findings remain stable under alternative estimation frameworks. This study develops and demonstrates a staged robustness analysis framework that connects SEM, ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, and Double Machine Learning (DML). SEM is first used to refine the measurement structure and estimate the robustness-baseline SEM model, in which the full theory-specified structural path system is retained for downstream robustness analysis before final structural path evaluation. OLS regression is then applied to SEM-derived construct scores as a transparent regression benchmark. Finally, DML-style residualisation is used to examine whether each tested focal relationship remains stable after flexible machine-learning-based adjustment for observed controls. Learner-sensitivity checks compare Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Support Vector Machine learners, and selected reverse-direction diagnostics are used to examine directional sensitivity. The framework is demonstrated using a FinTech Digital Customer Intimacy survey model. The findings identify which relationships are stable across SEM, OLS, and DML-style checks, and which require more cautious interpretation. A reproducible Google Colab workbook and generated result files are publicly available, providing a reusable template that researchers and students can adapt to other survey-based latent-construct studies. The paper contributes a practical robustness workflow and interpretation guide for survey-based researchers seeking to complement SEM with conventional and machine-learning-based robustness checks.

</details>


### [235] [Active-GRPO: Adaptive Imitation and Self-Improving Reasoning for Molecular Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00531)
*Xuefeng Liu,Mingxuan Cao,Qinan Huang,Thomas Brettin,Rick Stevens,Le Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Active-GRPO improves molecular optimization by dynamically switching between imitating references and self-reinforcement, while continuously upgrading the reference itself, outperforming both GRPO and RePO on TOMG-Bench MOLOPT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SFT collapses multi-step reasoning and RLVR suffers from sparse feedback; Reference-guided Policy Optimization is limited by reference quality, creating a performance ceiling.

Method: Active-GRPO with two mechanisms: (1) active imitate-reinforce (imitates reference when it outperforms policy candidates, switches to RL otherwise), and (2) active referencing (continuously upgrades reference with best policy-generated candidates).

Result: On TOMG-Bench MOLOPT, Active-GRPO achieves 0.1773 average SRxSim vs 0.0959 (GRPO) and 0.1665 (RePO), with significant gains on LogP, MR, and QED.

Conclusion: Active reasoning paradigm overcomes the reference quality ceiling by letting the policy dynamically decide when to imitate vs. self-improve, and by continuously raising the imitation target.

Abstract: Scientific reasoning is an increasingly important capability of large language models, yet improving the robustness and efficiency of training such reasoning remains a key open challenge. We study this problem in instruction-based molecular optimization, where answer-only supervised fine-tuning (SFT) collapses multi-step reasoning and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse feedback. Reference-guided Policy Optimization mitigates both by anchoring policy updates to dataset-provided references, but its effectiveness is tightly coupled to reference quality: weak or misaligned references impose a performance ceiling. To overcome this ceiling, we propose active reasoning, a paradigm in which the policy actively decides, on a per-instance basis, when to imitate a reference and when to reinforce its own discoveries, while continuously upgrading what it imitates. We instantiate this paradigm as Active Group Relative Policy Optimization (Active-GRPO), realized through two coupled mechanisms: active imitate-reinforce and active referencing. The former performs imitation learning when the reference still outperforms the policy's own candidates, and shifts to self-improvement via reinforcement learning once the policy has generated molecules that surpass the reference. The latter continuously upgrades the reference itself by replacing it with the best policy-generated candidate discovered so far, progressively raising the imitation target and ensuring that reference guidance remains informative-rather than restrictive-throughout training. Across TOMG-Bench MOLOPT, Active-GRPO improves average SRxSim from 0.0959 for GRPO and 0.1665 for RePO to 0.1773 under matched three-seed evaluation, with statistically significant gains on LogP, MR, and QED.

</details>


### [236] [Flow-Map GRPO: Reinforcement Learning for Few-Step Flow-Map Generators via Anchored Stochastic Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00535)
*Zhiqi Li,Wen Zhang,Bo Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flow-Map GRPO is an RL post-training framework that stochasticizes deterministic few-step flow-map generators (like consistency models and MeanFlow) via Anchored Stochastic Flow Map Composition (ASFMC) to enable effective alignment and reward optimization without retraining the original models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deterministic few-step flow-map generators are difficult to optimize with RL post-training because they lack stochastic trajectories and well-defined likelihood ratios. Existing SDE-based stochasticization methods are designed for velocity-based samplers, not long-range flow maps.

Method: The paper proposes Flow-Map GRPO framework with Anchored Stochastic Flow Map Composition (ASFMC) — a path-preserving stochasticization mechanism that introduces randomness via anchor-based conditional resampling while preserving the original marginal probability path. GRPO objectives are derived for both single-time and two-time flow-map parameterizations.

Result: Experiments on few-step FLUX-based text-to-image generators (MeanFlow and sCM) show Flow-Map GRPO improves pretrained deterministic flow-map models across reward-based, perceptual, and task-level evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: Deterministic few-step flow-map generators can be effectively aligned with RL post-training without modifying their original parameterization or retraining them as native stochastic models.

Abstract: Few-step flow-map generators, such as consistency models and MeanFlow, accelerate sampling by directly learning long-range transport maps between noise and data. However, these models are typically deterministic, which makes them difficult to optimize with reinforcement learning (RL) post-training methods that require stochastic trajectories and well-defined likelihood ratios. Existing SDE-based stochasticization techniques are designed for velocity-based samplers with infinitesimal or finely discretized transitions, and therefore do not directly apply to long-range flow maps. In this work, we propose Flow-Map GRPO, an online RL post-training framework for deterministic few-step flow-map generators. The key component is Anchored Stochastic Flow Map Composition (ASFMC), a path-preserving stochasticization mechanism that introduces randomness through anchor-based conditional resampling while preserving the original marginal probability path of the deterministic flow map. We derive GRPO objectives for both single-time and two-time flow-map parameterizations. Experiments on few-step FLUX-based text-to-image generators, including MeanFlow and sCM, show that Flow-Map GRPO improves pretrained deterministic flow-map models across reward-based, perceptual, and task-level evaluation metrics. Our results demonstrate that deterministic few-step flow-map generators can be effectively aligned with RL post-training without modifying their original model parameterization or retraining them as native stochastic models.

</details>


### [237] [Group-Equivariant Poincaré Convolutional Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00556)
*Aiden Durrant,Rahul Baburajan,Georgios Leontidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Equivariant Poincaré ResNets that integrate hyperbolic geometry with discrete symmetry groups (C₄ and D₄), introducing techniques to overcome optimization challenges and achieve spatial-group equivariance in hyperbolic space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hyperbolic networks (e.g., Poincaré ResNet) suffer from computationally expensive Riemannian gradients, strict manifold boundary constraints, and treat spatial transformations as distinct hierarchical concepts, causing redundant parameters and vanishing signals.

Method: Combines hyperbolic geometry with discrete symmetry groups (C₄, D₄); proposes geometrically safe tensor reshaping, left-regular permutations for hyperbolic group convolutions, and joint-orientation Poincaré Midpoint Batch Normalization.

Result: Embedding equivariance reduces optimization space, accelerates convergence, respects Poincaré ball boundary constraints, and preserves spatial-group equivariance.

Conclusion: Equivariant Poincaré ResNets effectively address key limitations of standard hyperbolic networks by incorporating symmetry-aware design, leading to more efficient and principled learning in hyperbolic space.

Abstract: While recent advancements like the Poincaré ResNet have demonstrated the potential of learning visual representations directly in hyperbolic space, their optimisation remains hampered by the computationally intensive nature of Riemannian gradients and the strict boundaries of the manifold. Furthermore, standard hyperbolic networks treat spatial transformations of the same object as distinct hierarchical concepts, leading to redundant parameter usage and vanishing signals. We propose Equivariant Poincaré ResNets, combining hyperbolic geometry with discrete symmetry groups ($C_4$ and $D_4$). We identify critical roadblocks in applying Euclidean equivariance to hyperbolic space and propose geometrically safe tensor reshaping, left-regular permutations for hyperbolic group convolutions, and joint-orientation Poincaré Midpoint Batch normalisation. Empirically, embedding equivariance drastically reduces the optimisation space, accelerating convergence while accelerating convergence while respecting the boundary constraints of the Poincaré ball and preserving spatial-group equivariance.

</details>


### [238] [Decision-focused Sparse Tangent Portfolio Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00581)
*Haeun Jeon,Seunghoon Choi,Hyunglip Bae,Yongjae Lee,Woo Chang Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an end-to-end decision-focused learning framework for sparse tangent portfolio optimization that reformulates Sharpe ratio maximization as a differentiable convex programming layer with a smooth top-k operator, enabling direct optimization of portfolio performance through gradient flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse tangent portfolio optimization is NP-hard due to cardinality constraints, and traditional predict-then-optimize pipelines misalign forecasting accuracy with actual portfolio quality, motivating the need for a decision-focused approach.

Method: The method reformulates Sharpe ratio maximization as a DPP-compliant convex programming layer and replaces discrete selection with a smooth top-k operator to enforce exact cardinality k, enabling differentiable gradient flow through prediction, asset selection, and re-optimization.

Result: Across four major equity markets, the method achieves competitive and often superior out-of-sample Sharpe ratios compared to historical and prediction-focused baselines, with particularly strong gains in larger asset universes.

Conclusion: The end-to-end decision-focused learning framework effectively bridges prediction and optimization for sparse tangent portfolios, outperforming traditional pipelines especially in larger asset universes.

Abstract: Sparse tangent portfolio optimization aims to learn an interpretable, low-cardinality portfolio in the tangency direction of the mean-variance frontier. However, the associated cardinality-constrained formulation is NP-hard, and standard predict-then-optimize pipelines often misalign forecasting accuracy with downstream portfolio quality. We propose an end-to-end decision-focused learning framework that reformulates Sharpe ratio maximization as a Disciplined Parametrized Programming (DPP)-compliant convex programming layer and replaces discrete selection with a smooth top-$k$ operator enforcing an exact cardinality $k$. This enables gradient flow through prediction, asset selection, and re-optimization, allowing the predictive model to directly optimize portfolio performance. Across four major equity markets, our method achieves competitive and often superior out-of-sample Sharpe ratios compared with historical and prediction-focused baselines, with particularly strong gains in larger asset universes. Our \href{https://github.com/feuerwerksh/Diffble-card-SR}{code} is publicly available.

</details>


### [239] [Measuring Dead Directions: Decomposing and Classifying Singular Structure off Canonical Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00603)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a descent-free, alignment-free method to measure singular structure in trained neural networks by recovering the order of dead directions from the directional-Fisher rate at a single frozen checkpoint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To remove the need for canonical alignment and descent preconditions in measuring singular structure, making order-recovery deterministic and architecture-general.

Method: Uses directional-Fisher rate to recover the order k of dead directions from a single frozen checkpoint, with a pluggable detector for various layer types, and assembles per-direction orders into a global coefficient through typed intersection of loci.

Result: Successfully recovers architecture-predicted orders across constructed cells and trained networks, including fine-tuned and from-scratch vision transformers, and matches global coefficients to closed forms.

Conclusion: The method removes alignment and descent preconditions, enabling deterministic singular structure reading, with implications for understanding universal singular fluctuations and multiplicity in trained networks.

Abstract: We give a descent-free, alignment-free measurement of singular structure on trained networks. At a single frozen checkpoint the read recovers the order $k$ of each dead direction from the directional-Fisher rate, the master invariant from which the per-direction learning coefficient $1/(2k)$ follows exactly, in whatever basis the optimizer left. The same read classifies each direction, separating a genuine singularity, whose order the architecture fixes, from a flat gauge symmetry; the directional-Fisher magnitude settles the cases the order cannot. A pluggable detector supplies the directions for transformer, convolutional, and normalisation layers. The read recovers the architecture-predicted order across constructed cells and trained networks, including a fine-tuned vision transformer whose dead structure is the LayerNorm-kernel gauge and a from-scratch one whose compressed MLP forms a node-death at its activation order. Where the singular structure enumerates, the per-direction orders assemble, through the typed intersection of the loci, into the global coefficient $(λ, m)$ matching the closed form. The method removes the canonical-alignment and descent preconditions of the underlying rate result, turning order-recovery into a deterministic, architecture-general reading. We then map its reach into the Watanabe triple: the order determines the universal singular fluctuation $ν(k)$, though a trained network's realized $ν$ falls below it as the live structure absorbs the dead direction's data fluctuation, and the multiplicity recovers from the dominant structure under a single-locus assumption.

</details>


### [240] [Loss Smoothing for Stable Adaptation Under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00634)
*Darshan Patil,Ekaterina Lobacheva,Razvan Pascanu,Sarath Chandar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes loss smoothing, a simple method that interpolates between source and target training objectives during the start of adaptation, helping preserve useful features and improve performance across various settings including supervised shifts, vision adaptation, reinforcement learning, and language model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard adaptation methods abruptly switch from source to target objectives, which can distort learned representations and discard useful features. The authors investigate whether a more gradual transition can alleviate this problem and improve adaptation.

Method: Loss smoothing interpolates between source and target training objectives at the start of adaptation, creating a smooth transition that preserves useful source features while allowing specialization to the target distribution.

Result: Loss smoothing consistently improves performance across diverse settings: controlled supervised shifts, pretrained vision adaptation, offline-to-online and online reinforcement learning, and language model fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Smoother objective transitions are a broadly useful tool for model adaptation, suggesting that gradual interpolation between objectives can better preserve learned representations while enabling task specialization.

Abstract: In settings such as fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, neural networks are often adapted under distribution shift. Standard adaptation methods typically optimize the target objective directly, inducing an abrupt change from the source training objective. This abrupt transition can distort learned representations, including features that may still be useful for the new task. We investigate whether a more gradual transition can improve adaptation. We propose loss smoothing, a simple approach that interpolates between the source and target training objectives at the start of adaptation. This smooth transition helps to preserve useful features from the source distribution while still enabling the model to specialize to the target distribution. Across controlled supervised shifts, pretrained vision adaptation, offline-to-online and online reinforcement learning, and language model fine-tuning, we find that loss smoothing consistently improves performance, suggesting that smoother objective transitions are a broadly useful tool for model adaptation.

</details>


### [241] [Multi-Label Node Classification with Label Influence Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00671)
*Yifei Sun,Zemin Liu,Bryan Hooi,Yang Yang,Rizal Fathony,Jia Chen,Bingsheng He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Label Influence Propagation (LIP), a novel GNN-based model for multi-label node classification that explicitly models and leverages label influence correlations by decomposing message passing into propagation and transformation operations, then propagating high-order influences through a constructed label influence graph to dynamically adjust learning, achieving SOTA results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN methods for multi-label node classification fail to account for intricate influences between labels in non-Euclidean graph data, as they only capture label co-occurrence or proximity but not the complex influence correlations.

Method: (1) Decomposes GNN message passing into propagation and transformation operations. (2) Analyzes and quantifies label influence correlations in each operation. (3) Constructs a label influence graph based on integrated label correlations. (4) Propagates high-order influences through this graph, dynamically adjusting learning by amplifying positive contributions and mitigating negative influences.

Result: The LIP framework consistently outperforms SOTA methods across various settings on comprehensive benchmark datasets for multi-label node classification tasks.

Conclusion: Modeling explicit label influence correlations in non-Euclidean graph data via the proposed LIP approach is effective for improving multi-label node classification performance.

Abstract: Graphs are a complex and versatile data structure used across various domains, with possibly multi-label nodes playing a particularly crucial role. Examples include proteins in PPI networks with multiple functions and users in social or e-commerce networks exhibiting diverse interests. Tackling multi-label node classification (MLNC) on graphs has led to the development of various approaches. Some methods leverage graph neural networks (GNNs) to exploit label co-occurrence correlations, while others incorporate label embeddings to capture label proximity. However, these approaches fail to account for the intricate influences between labels in non-Euclidean graph data. To address this issue, we decompose the message passing process in GNNs into two operations: propagation and transformation. We then conduct a comprehensive analysis and quantification of the influence correlations between labels in each operation. Building on these insights, we propose a novel model, Label Influence Propagation (LIP). Specifically, we construct a label influence graph based on the integrated label correlations. Then, we propagate high-order influences through this graph, dynamically adjusting the learning process by amplifying labels with positive contributions and mitigating those with negative influence. Finally, our framework is evaluated on comprehensive benchmark datasets, consistently outperforming SOTA methods across various settings, demonstrating its effectiveness on MLNC tasks.

</details>


### [242] [Distributed Online Bandit Submodular Maximization with Bounded Sampling Violations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00680)
*Bin Du,Chang Liu,Dingqi Zhu,Lintao Ye,Dengfeng Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified algorithmic framework for distributed online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints, achieving sublinear (1-1/e)-regret guarantees under both full-information and bandit feedback models, with a bounded stochastic pipage rounding scheme that ensures asymptotically vanishing sampling violations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in distributed online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints, particularly handling both full-information and bandit feedback while resolving the sampling violation issue caused by continuous relaxation and rounding.

Method: A unified algorithmic framework for distributed online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints, incorporating a bounded stochastic pipage rounding scheme to address sampling violations from continuous relaxation and rounding.

Result: Sublinear (1-1/e)-regret guarantees under both full-information and bandit feedback models; asymptotically vanishing sampling violation probability; sublinear cumulative sampling violation in T that is not improvable under certain conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed distributed algorithms achieve comparable performance to centralized counterparts, with theoretically grounded guarantees on both regret and sampling violations, validated by numerical results.

Abstract: We study distributed online submodular maximization under partition matroid constraints, in which multiple agents select a limited number of actions from their own subsets sequentially to maximize the cumulative value of a sequence of objective functions. We develop a unified algorithmic framework that accommodates full-information and bandit feedback models. For both feedback models, we prove that the proposed algorithms achieve sublinear $(1-1/e)$-regret guarantees, which are comparable to those achieved by existing centralized counterparts. Furthermore, to tackle the sampling violation issue caused by continuous relaxation and rounding, we develop a bounded stochastic pipage rounding scheme and show that the probability of sampling violation vanishes asymptotically. As a result, the cumulative sampling violation remains sublinear in $T$, which is further shown to be not improvable under certain conditions. Numerical results validate the theoretical findings in this paper.

</details>


### [243] [AdaBoosting Text Prompts for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00684)
*Seokhee Jin,Changhwan Sung,Sunung Mun,Hoyoung Kim,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TPB is an AdaBoost-inspired framework that boosts few-shot text prompting for Vision-Language Models by sequentially aggregating prompt-based classifiers that target misclassified examples, improving accuracy and enabling cross-model transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing few-shot text prompting methods do not explicitly focus on misclassified examples during prompt construction, leading to only marginal improvements even with more shots. The authors aim to fully exploit few-shot supervision by targeting hard examples.

Method: TPB (Text Prompt Boosting) - an AdaBoost-inspired framework that treats each text-prompt-based classifier as a weak learner and sequentially aggregates them into a strong ensemble by explicitly targeting hard, misclassified examples.

Result: Across 11 classification benchmarks, TPB improves accuracy on the source model and preserves shot-driven gains when transferred to larger, more capable VLMs, where existing methods struggle.

Conclusion: TPB preserves task-intrinsic, model-agnostic cues in text space, enabling robust cross-model transfer and outperforming existing methods particularly when transferred to larger VLMs.

Abstract: The classification accuracy of pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) relies on the quality of the text prompts. Handcrafted templates and Large Language Model (LLM)-generated descriptions not only make predictions more interpretable, but also enable reuse of the same prompts across heterogeneous VLMs. Recent works construct task-adapted text prompts with a small number of labeled images. However, existing few-shot text prompting methods do not explicitly focus on misclassified examples during prompt construction, leading to only marginal improvements even as more shots become available. To fully exploit few-shot supervision, we propose Text Prompt Boosting (TPB), an AdaBoost-inspired framework that treats each text-prompt-based classifier as a weak learner and sequentially aggregates them into a strong ensemble by explicitly targeting hard, misclassified examples. Extensive experiments show that TPB preserves task-intrinsic, model-agnostic cues in text space, enabling robust cross-model transfer. Across eleven classification benchmarks, TPB improves accuracy on the source model and preserves shot-driven gains when transferred to larger, more capable VLMs, where existing methods struggle to sustain such improvements.

</details>


### [244] [Generative Refinement for Low-Budget Black-Box Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00691)
*Edouard R. Dufour,Pascal Fua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPARROW is a black-box optimization algorithm that uses a fixed generative prior (any pre-trained sampler) as a structured proposal operator, decoupled from reward signals, enabling effective optimization under very low evaluation budgets even with noisy rewards and complex search spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-box optimization with costly evaluations is challenging when rewards are noisy and high-performing solutions lie in thin, curved, or disconnected regions.
