Motivation: Existing geometry-adaptive neural operators are designed only for forward problems where inputs/outputs share the same domain, limiting their use for boundary value problems (BVPs) and inverse problems where domains differ.

Method: GAIA encodes domain boundaries and interior fields into geometry tokens, then conditions integral transform layers on these tokens via cross-attention, enabling local geometric adaptation without retraining, iterative optimization, or graph construction.

Result: GAIA sets new SOTA on all inverse/BVP tasks (64% error reduction on airfoil flow, 27% on EIT), outperforms baselines on MCB, and matches specialized solvers on forward problems with stable accuracy across resolutions.

Conclusion: GAIA provides a unified, amortized architecture for forward, BVP, and inverse PDE problems on arbitrary geometries, bridging a key gap in neural operator design.

Abstract: Operator learning for partial differential equations (PDEs) on arbitrary geometries builds fast neural surrogates for large-scale simulation. Although recent geometry-adaptive neural operators have made substantial progress, they are mainly designed for forward problems in which inputs and outputs share the same spatial domain. This limits their applicability for boundary value problems (BVPs) and inverse problems, where inputs and outputs may live on different domains. We introduce the Geometry-Adaptive Integral Autoencoder (GAIA), an operator learning model that encodes the domain boundary and the interior field distribution into geometry tokens, and conditions integral transform layers on these tokens via cross-attention, allowing the kernel to adapt locally to geometric features. This yields a single architecture for forward (including BVPs) and inverse problems on arbitrary domains in one pass, without retraining, iterative optimization, or graph construction. We evaluate GAIA on seven 2D and 3D benchmarks, four of which are new or substantially extended benchmarks for inverse problems and BVP: electrical impedance tomography, optical tomography, 3D Darcy flow on varying geometries, and a modified setting of Poisson BVP on mechanical components benchmark (MCB). GAIA sets new state-of-the-art results on every inverse and BVP task, reducing median relative $L^2$ error by 64% on airfoil flow reconstruction and 27% on EIT relative to the next best amortized method, and outperforming all baselines on every shape category of MCB. On other forward problems, GAIA is competitive with specialized solvers while maintaining stable accuracy across point resolutions on which transformer-based baselines degrade.

</details>


### [273] [Sequentially-Controlled Interactive Multi-Particle Flow-Maps for Online Feedback-Driven Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01144)
*Binglin Ji,Anindya Sarkar,Hengchang Lu,Jens Sjölund,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IMPFM is a framework for online feedback-driven search that uses interactive particle systems and flow maps to achieve broad exploration and alignment with unknown preferences, outperforming existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative models for reward alignment struggle with unknown preferences revealed only through sequential feedback, lacking the broad exploration needed to uncover high-utility regions.

Method: IMPFM uses sequentially-controlled interactive multi-particle flow-maps with posterior sample sharing across particles, exploration-exploitation reweighting, and a multi-particle interaction-aware Feynman-Kac corrector to steer toward a KL-tilted target distribution.

Result: Extensive empirical evaluations and ablations across diverse search and alignment tasks confirm IMPFM's efficacy over existing baselines.

Conclusion: IMPFM enables sample-efficient global exploration while preventing mode collapse and mitigating reward over-optimization in preference alignment tasks.

Abstract: While generative models have enabled training-free reward alignment, current methods typically excel in local exploration within narrow regions of the underlying distribution. These approaches struggle when preferences are unknown a priori and only revealed through sequential feedback-a scenario demanding broad exploration to uncover high-utility regions. To address this, we propose Sequentially-Controlled Interactive Multi-Particle Flow-Maps (IMPFM), a framework for sample-efficient online feedback-driven search. IMPFM progressively transports a group of interactive particles toward the target distribution, maintaining the broad coverage essential for heterogeneous preference alignment. IMPFM introduces a principled and efficient posterior sample sharing mechanism across particles powered by flow maps. By correcting individual particle drift with the collective posterior samples of the entire ensemble at each resampling step, the framework maximizes sample utility to enable global exploration while actively mitigating reward over-optimization, typical of standard control frameworks. Paired with a principled exploration-exploitation reweighting mechanism involving multi-particle interaction, this sequentially corrected multi-particle dynamics explicitly preserves structural diversity and overcomes the weight degeneracy inherent to standard SMC samplers. Crucially, we prove that the resulting sampling framework yields a multi-particle interaction-aware Feynman-Kac corrector that progressively steers the multi-particle system toward a KL-tilted target distribution, facilitating global exploration and preventing mode collapse. Extensive empirical evaluations and rigorous ablations across diverse search and alignment tasks confirm the efficacy of IMPFM over existing baselines.

</details>


### [274] [Efficient Compression of Structured and Unstructured Volumes via Learned 3D Gaussian Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01164)
*Landon Dyken,Sharmistha Chakrabarti,Nathan Debardeleben,Steve Petruzza,Qi Wu,Will Usher,Sidharth Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an explicit 3D Gaussian-based model for volume data compression that outperforms implicit neural representations (INRs) by naturally encoding geometry, eliminating mesh storage needs, and achieving faster training with competitive quality on structured data and superior results on unstructured data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing INRs for unstructured volumes do not encode geometry, requiring partial mesh storage that limits compression. The goal is to develop a model that avoids this overhead while maintaining or improving reconstruction quality.

Method: The method reinterprets 3D Gaussian primitives as an explicit scalar field representation, reconstructs values via weighted aggregation of intersecting Gaussians, and uses optimized CUDA pipelines, tailored loss functions, and a sampling-error-based densification strategy.

Result: The explicit model achieves competitive reconstruction quality with significant training speedups on structured volumes, and markedly outperforms INRs on all metrics for unstructured volumes.

Conclusion: Explicit 3D Gaussian representations are a promising alternative to INRs for volume data compression, offering better compression opportunities, faster training, and superior performance especially for unstructured data.

Abstract: Recent work has shown that implicit neural representations (INRs) can be trained to effectively compress structured and unstructured volume data, allowing for direct data querying with a reduced memory footprint. However, as existing INRs for unstructured volumes do not encode geometry, they require partial mesh storage for later sampling, limiting achievable compression. At the same time, novel view synthesis methods have shown that explicit collections of 3D Gaussians can be used to accurately visualize volume data. In this work, we introduce an explicit model for volume data compression based on 3D Gaussian primitives. We reinterpret collections of 3D Gaussians as an explicit representation of a scalar field and use a sampling strategy that reconstructs scalar values at spatial locations through weighted aggregation of intersecting Gaussians. We develop optimized CUDA-accelerated pipelines for structured and unstructured model sampling, loss functions that encourage accurate domain encoding by our models, and a novel sampling-error based densification strategy. Our explicit formulation naturally encodes domain geometry, eliminating the need for mesh storage in unstructured volumes and introducing significantly higher compression opportunities. Compared to existing INRs, we demonstrate that our explicit model achieves competitive reconstruction quality with significant training speedups on structured volumes, while markedly outperforming in all metrics on unstructured volumes.

</details>


### [275] [Decision-Aware Training for Sample-Based Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01171)
*Kornelius Raeth,Nicole Ludwig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a decision-aware training method for sample-based generative models that augments standard scoring rules with a differentiable decision loss to penalize forecast errors where they are most costly, validated on synthetic and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard training objectives (e.g., energy score) are blind to the decision maker's cost structure, allocating training signal proportionally to data density without awareness of where errors are most costly for downstream decisions.

Method: Augments the energy score objective with a differentiable decision loss that directly penalizes the cost incurred by acting on the model's forecast, forming a theoretically grounded combined loss where the decision loss is itself a proper scoring rule.

Result: Validated on one synthetic and two real-world tasks, showing targeted improvements in cost-sensitive regions while retaining full probabilistic forecasts.

Conclusion: Decision-aware training can improve generative model performance in cost-sensitive regions without sacrificing overall forecast quality, making them more suitable for high-stakes decision settings.

Abstract: Sample-based generative models are increasingly used for probabilistic forecasting in high-stakes decision settings, yet their training objectives are blind to the decision maker's cost structure. These models are commonly trained with strictly proper scoring rules, such as the energy score, which allocate their training signal in proportion to data density, with no awareness of where forecast errors are most costly for downstream decisions. We therefore propose decision-aware training for sample-based generative models, augmenting the energy score objective with a differentiable decision loss that directly penalises the cost incurred by acting on the model's forecast. This combined loss is theoretically grounded, as the decision loss is itself a proper scoring rule. We validate our method on one synthetic and two real-world tasks, showing targeted improvements in cost-sensitive regions while retaining full probabilistic forecasts.

</details>


### [276] [QuasiMoTTo: Quasi-Monte Carlo Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01179)
*Michael Y. Li,Anthony Zhan,Kanishk Gandhi,Noah D. Goodman,Emily B. Fox*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QuasiMoTTo uses quasi-Monte Carlo sampling to generate correlated but exact parallel samples from LMs, reducing redundancy and improving sample efficiency by 25-50% across reasoning benchmarks and RL training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling inference compute via parallel independent sampling wastes resources on redundant solutions. The authors argue this waste is not fundamental and explore correlated sampling as a more efficient alternative.

Method: QuasiMoTTo reparameterizes autoregressive sampling as inverse-CDF sampling and draws underlying uniforms via quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) instead of i.i.d. This produces correlated but marginally exact samples that cover the output space more evenly.

Result: QuasiMoTTo matches i.i.d. pass@k with 25-47% fewer samples across four reasoning benchmarks, often saturating the theoretical upper bound. In GRPO RL training, it matches i.i.d. performance with 50% fewer steps.

Conclusion: Correlated parallel sampling via QMC is a viable drop-in replacement for i.i.d. sampling that significantly improves sample efficiency for both inference-time scaling and policy-gradient RL, without sacrificing marginal distribution correctness.

Abstract: Scaling inference compute, by generating many parallel attempts per problem, is a costly but reliable lever for improving language model capabilities. By default these attempts are generated independently, wasting inference compute on redundant solutions. This waste seems unavoidable. After all, independence is what makes parallel sampling trivial to scale. However, this tradeoff is not fundamental: there is a rich design space of samplers that generate correlated but exact samples entirely in parallel. We explore this design space as an avenue for improving sample efficiency in scaling inference compute and reinforcement learning (RL). Concretely, we introduce QuasiMoTTo, which uses correlated samples as a drop-in replacement for i.i.d. samples. To generate these samples, QuasiMoTTo uses a reparameterization of autoregressive sampling as inverse-CDF sampling and draws the underlying uniforms with quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC); because QMC spreads the uniforms out more evenly than i.i.d., the resulting samples cover the output space with far less redundancy. Even though the batch is correlated, each sample is marginally distributed according to the language model, so we can use the batch for policy-gradient training. Our empirical analysis focuses on understanding how efficiently QuasiMoTTo can turn compute into performance. To evaluate correlated samplers, whose dependence breaks standard pass@k estimators, we first develop an unbiased bootstrap estimator. Across four reasoning benchmarks, QuasiMoTTo matches i.i.d. pass@k accuracy with 25-47% fewer samples. Strikingly, QuasiMoTTo often saturates an upper bound on pass@k that holds for any marginal-preserving sampler. We also apply QuasiMoTTo to policy-gradient RL (GRPO) where it matches i.i.d. performance with 50% fewer training steps. These gains come from higher coverage, which yields a stronger learning signal per batch.

</details>


### [277] [Right in the Right Way: LM Training with Verifiable Rewards and Human Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01181)
*Mehul Damani,Isha Puri,Idan Shenfeld,Jacob Andreas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an adversarial generator-discriminator framework that augments verifiable rewards with a learned signal from human demonstrations, improving non-verifiable properties (style, diversity) while preserving accuracy gains of RLVR across bug fixing, story generation, and reward hacking benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods optimize only objectively scorable metrics, neglecting subjective aspects like style and structure, leading to diversity collapse, unnatural responses, and reward hacking.

Method: An adversarial generator-discriminator framework where a generator is trained via RL to maximize both task accuracy and an adversarial reward from a discriminator that learns to distinguish human-written from model-generated outputs.

Result: In bug fixing, lower edit distance while matching end performance; in story generation, improved win rate, diversity, and human-likeness; in reward hacking benchmarks, near elimination of model misbehavior while maintaining high scores.

Conclusion: The approach bridges RL and SFT, offering a scalable path toward jointly optimizing verifiable and non-verifiable properties of a task.

Abstract: RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for training LMs on tasks with well-defined success metrics, such as code generation and mathematical reasoning. However, current RLVR methods optimize only what can be objectively scored, often neglecting subjective, non-verifiable aspects of human-like outputs, such as style and structure. This limitation leads to well-documented failure modes such as diversity collapse, unnatural-sounding responses, and reward hacking. We propose an adversarial generator-discriminator framework that augments verifiable rewards with a learned signal from human demonstrations. A generator model is trained using RL to maximize both task accuracy and an adversarial reward derived from a discriminator. The discriminator, trained alongside the generator policy, learns to distinguish human-written outputs from model-generated ones. The discriminator serves as a learned proxy for the human output distribution, providing feedback on aspects of generation that are difficult to formalize as scalar rewards. Across diverse domains, including bug fixing and open-ended generation, our approach consistently improves non-verifiable properties while preserving the accuracy gains of RLVR. In bug fixing, our method produces solutions with significantly lower edit distance compared to RLVR baselines while matching end performance. In story generation, our method significantly improves win rate while producing stories that are diverse and more human-like. And in a simple reward hacking benchmark, our method nearly eliminates model misbehavior while maintaining high benchmark scores. Together, these results show that our approach bridges RL and SFT, offering a scalable path toward jointly optimizing the verifiable and non-verifiable properties of a task.

</details>


### [278] [Neural Certificate Pricing for Combinatorial Optimization Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01185)
*Jingyi Chen,Xinyuan Zhang,Xinwu Qian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural Certificate Pricing (NCP) is an unsupervised learning framework for combinatorial optimization that trains a neural network to predict certificate-level dual prices, using a structured recovery layer to construct feasible primal solutions with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combinatorial optimization problems are hard due to exponential search space for certifying optimality, yet structural feasibility can be verified in polynomial time. The authors aim to exploit this asymmetry to develop more efficient learning-based solvers.

Method: NCP trains a neural network to predict certificate-level dual prices, then uses a structured recovery layer to construct the induced primal marginal. This is framed as amortized separation—learning residual prices instead of enumerating violated inequalities. Under certificate-consistency, the recovered marginal is globally feasible, with first-order price errors causing only second-order loss in objective value.

Result: Across three classes of CO problems, NCP either outperforms state-of-the-art neural baselines by large margins or matches them at a fraction of computation time, and shows stronger out-of-distribution generalization.

Conclusion: NCP provides an effective unsupervised learning approach for combinatorial optimization that leverages the asymmetry between certification difficulty and verification ease, achieving strong performance with theoretical grounding and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Combinatorial optimization (CO) problems are difficult because certifiable discrete structure induces exponential search. One needs to search over the set exponentially many candidates to certify optimality, however, the structural feasibility of a path, packing, or cover can be verified in polynomial time once supplied. In this study, we introduce Neural Certificate Pricing (NCP) that exploits this asymmetry under an unsupervised learning framework. A neural network is trained to predict certificate-level dual prices, while a structured recovery layer constructs the induced primal marginal. NCP can be viewed as amortized separation: instead of enumerating violated inequalities, it learns the residual prices through which their aggregate effect enters recovery. When the certificate-consistency condition holds, the recovered marginal is globally feasible, and a local theory shows that first-order errors in the predicted price induce only second-order loss in objective value. Across three classes of CO problems, NCP either outperforms state-of-the-art neural baselines by large margins or matches them at a fraction of the computation time, and shows stronger out-of-distribution generalization.

</details>


### [279] [Quantum vs. Classical Machine Learning: A Unified Empirical Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01197)
*Chuanming Yu,Jiaming Liu,Zihao Ge,Xiongfei Wu,Lulu Zhu,Pengzhan Zhao,Jianjun Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper empirically compares seven pairs of quantum and classical machine learning models across supervised learning and reinforcement learning, finding that current QML models do not surpass classical baselines in prediction performance, policy stability, or training time, though QML shows promise for noise filtering and false positive control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evidence supporting the performance and advantages of quantum machine learning (QML) models relative to classical models is insufficient, motivating the need for an empirical comparison.

Method: The authors conduct an empirical study comparing seven model pairs spanning supervised learning and reinforcement learning, evaluating QML models against their classical counterparts.

Result: Evaluated QML models do not yet surpass classical baselines in overall prediction performance, policy stability, or training time. QML shows promise for filtering noise and controlling false positives.

Conclusion: QML faces challenges in hardware environments, training efficiency, and convergence stability, providing a foundation for future research into robustness and parameter optimization of QML.

Abstract: Quantum computing has emerged as a promising computational paradigm for machine learning (ML), with the potential to offer computational advantages over classical approaches. At this stage, the evidence supporting the performance and advantages of quantum machine learning (QML) models relative to classical models is insufficient.To address this gap, this paper presents an empirical study on the performance of QML models and their classical counterparts. We compare seven model pairs spanning supervised learning and reinforcement learning. Our results indicate that the evaluated quantum machine learning models do not yet surpass the classical baselines in overall prediction performance, policy stability, or training time. Nevertheless, QML remains a promising approach for filtering noise and controlling false positives. Our research findings summarize the challenges facing quantum machine learning across hardware environments, training efficiency, and convergence stability, providing a foundation for research into the robustness and parameter optimization of QML. This work is publicly available at https://github.com/Z-537-437/QML.

</details>


### [280] [TiRex-2: Generalizing TiRex to Multivariate Data and Streaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01204)
*Patrick Podest,Marco Pichler,Elias Bürger,Levente Zólyomi,Bernhard Voggenberger,Wilhelm Berghammer,Daniel Klotz,Sebastian Böck,Günter Klambauer,Sepp Hochreiter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TiRex-2 is a recurrent xLSTM-based time series foundation model for multivariate forecasting that operates at constant per-patch cost, integrates past and future covariates, and achieves SOTA zero-shot performance via a synthetic coupling pretraining pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world forecasting is sequential with streaming observations and joint variable evolution, but existing Transformer-based models suffer from quadratic complexity and full-history recomputation; there is a need for efficient, streaming-capable multivariate forecasting with covariate support.

Method: TiRex-2 uses a recurrent xLSTM architecture combining a bidirectional time mixer with an asymmetric grouped-attention variate mixer, enabling future-known covariate integration with strict causality. A synthetic coupling pipeline composes multivariate samples from univariate corpora for scalable pretraining.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench, maintains stable streaming to arbitrary context lengths, constant inference cost per patch, with 38.4M active parameters (univariate) + 44.1M (multivariate).

Conclusion: TiRex-2 is the first time series foundation model combining recurrent streaming efficiency, multivariate forecasting, and future-known covariate support at constant per-patch cost.

Abstract: We introduce TiRex-2, a recurrent xLSTM-based time series foundation model that generalizes the univariate TiRex to multivariate forecasting with both past and future covariates. Real-world forecasting is inherently sequential: observations arrive continuously, variables evolve jointly, and a subset of covariates is known ahead of time. Existing Transformer-based time series foundation models capture cross-variate dependencies but incur quadratic complexity in context length and require full-history recomputation as new observations arrive. TiRex-2 addresses these limitations through a memory-centric recurrent design that operates at constant per-patch cost under streaming. The model combines a bidirectional time mixer with an asymmetric grouped-attention variate mixer, enabling the integration of future-known covariates while preserving strict causality over target variables. To our knowledge, this is the first time series foundation model that achieves this combination of properties. To support scalable multivariate pretraining, we propose a synthetic coupling pipeline that composes diverse multivariate samples on the fly from large univariate corpora. Empirically, TiRex-2 achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench, remains stable when streamed to arbitrary context lengths, and maintains constant inference cost per patch. The model uses 38.4M active parameters in univariate mode, with an additional 44.1M parameters activated for multivariate forecasting.

</details>


### [281] [Language-Critique Imitation Learning from Suboptimal Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01225)
*Chih-Han Yang,Dai-Jie Wu,Yun-Ping Huang,Ping-Chun Hsieh,Kenneth Marino,Shao-Hua Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a language-critique framework (LC-BC and LC-DP) that uses natural language as structured supervision for imitation learning from suboptimal demonstrations, outperforming baselines on control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior imitation learning from suboptimal demonstrations relies on compressed scalar signals (confidence, discriminator scores, importance weights) that cannot express intermediate reasoning about task progress, failure modes, or corrective actions.

Method: The framework constructs language labels describing progress, suboptimal behaviors, and corrective guidance from demonstrations, then introduces a language-critique loss that trains policies using these structured signals without reducing them to scalars. It instantiates this for behavior cloning (LC-BC) and diffusion policies (LC-DP).

Result: Consistently outperforms strong imitation learning and offline RL baselines on diverse continuous control tasks including navigation, manipulation, and gameplay.

Conclusion: Language can serve as a powerful and structured form of supervision for learning robust policies from suboptimal data, with theoretical guarantees showing the objective upper-bounds the expert performance gap.

Abstract: Prior work on imitation learning from suboptimal demonstrations typically relies on compressed supervision signals such as confidence estimates, discriminator scores, or importance weights. These scalar signals are inherently limited, as they cannot explicitly express intermediate reasoning about task progress, failure modes, or corrective actions. We propose a language-critique framework for imitation learning from suboptimal demonstrations that instead leverages natural language as a structured supervision signal, avoiding the collapse of expressive feedback into scalars. Our method first constructs language labels from demonstrations that explicitly describe current progress, identify suboptimal behaviors, and provide fine-grained corrective guidance. We then introduce a language-critique loss that directly trains policies using these structured signals without reducing them to scalars, and instantiate it for both behavior cloning and diffusion policies, yielding LC-BC and LC-DP. We further provide a theoretical result showing that the proposed objective upper-bounds the expert performance gap under standard assumptions. Empirically, we evaluate on diverse continuous control tasks spanning navigation, manipulation, and gameplay, where our methods consistently outperform strong imitation learning and offline reinforcement learning baselines. These results demonstrate that language can serve as a powerful and structured form of supervision for learning robust policies from suboptimal data.

</details>


### [282] [Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01232)
*Zijian Zhang,Rizhen Hu,Athanasios Glentis,Dawei Li,Chung-Yiu Yau,Hongzhou Lin,Mingyi Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL post-training gains in LLMs are concentrated in a small subset of transformer layers—often just one—with middle layers contributing most, while input/output layers matter far less.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL post-training uniformly updates all LLM parameters, assuming each layer contributes equally. The authors challenge this assumption and seek to understand how RL adaptation is actually distributed across layers.

Method: A systematic layer-wise study is conducted, introducing a "layer contribution" metric that measures the fraction of full-parameter RL improvement recovered by training a single layer in isolation. Experiments span 7 models (Qwen3, Qwen2.5), 3 RL algorithms (GRPO, GiGPO, Dr. GRPO), and multiple tasks (math, code, agentic decision-making).

Result: RL gains are highly concentrated in a small subset (often a single) of transformer layers. High-contribution layers concentrate in the middle of the stack, while input/output layers contribute substantially less. Layer rankings are strongly correlated across datasets, tasks, model families, and RL algorithms.

Conclusion: The uniform updating assumption in RL post-training is flawed; selective layer-wise training can recover most (or even surpass) full-parameter gains, suggesting more efficient training strategies are possible.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a central component of post-training large language models (LLMs), yet little is understood about how RL adaptation is distributed across transformer layers. Existing approaches typically update all model parameters uniformly, implicitly assuming that every layer contributes similarly to the gains obtained during RL post-training. In this work, we challenge this assumption through a systematic layer-wise study of RL training. Surprisingly, we find that training a single transformer layer can recover most of the gains achieved by full-parameter RL training, and in some cases even surpass it. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the quantity layer contribution, which measures the fraction of full RL improvement recovered by training a layer in isolation. Across seven models spanning two model families (Qwen3, Qwen2.5), three RL algorithms (GRPO, GiGPO, Dr. GRPO), and multiple task domains including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and agentic decision-making, we observe a remarkably stable pattern: RL gains are highly concentrated in a small subset of, and in many cases even a single, transformer layers. More strikingly, the same structural pattern consistently emerges: high-contribution layers concentrate in the middle of the transformer stack, while layers near the input and output ends contribute substantially less. The resulting layer rankings remain strongly correlated across datasets, tasks, model families, and RL algorithms.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [283] [Active Sensing for RIS-Aided Tracking and Power Control: A Hybrid Neuroevolution and Supervised Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00056)
*George Stamatelis,Hui Chen,Henk Henk Wymeersch,George C. Alexandropoulos*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Dual-Agent (DA) deep learning framework for energy-efficient tracking of power-limited mobile users using a Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS), combining neuroevolution and supervised learning to optimize RIS phase profiles and UE transmit power with low-overhead feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve energy-efficient tracking of power-limited mobile users by reducing localization pilot transmission overhead, which dominates the energy budget of power-constrained devices.

Method: A Dual-Agent (DA) deep learning framework with hybrid training (neuroevolution + supervised learning) that jointly optimizes discrete RIS phase profiles and UE transmit power via low-overhead single-bit feedback, with extensions for single/multi-antenna BSs.

Result: The proposed scheme achieves highly accurate and robust tracking across diverse target motion models, outperforming extended Kalman filters, particle filters, and ML-based trackers; in static localization, it significantly beats fingerprinting schemes, deep RL baselines, and standard backpropagation-based estimators.

Conclusion: The DA active sensing framework enables efficient, accurate, and robust user tracking under strict energy constraints, effectively handling discrete RIS phase responses and limited feedback bandwidth.

Abstract: This paper studies energy efficient tracking of power-limited mobile users with the assistance of a Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS). Since localization pilot transmissions dominate the energy budget of power-constrained devices, we introduce a low-overhead feedback link from the Base Station (BS) to the user to enable dynamic uplink power control. To navigate the discrete and decentralized nature of this active sensing problem, we propose a novel Dual-Agent (DA) deep learning framework that jointly optimizes the discrete RIS phase profiles and the UE's transmit power in real time. Specifically, our approach employs a hybrid training methodology integrating the neuroevolution paradigm with supervised learning, effectively overcoming the non-differentiability of discrete phase responses from the RIS unit elements and the strict information bottleneck of single-bit feedback messages for pilot power control. The proposed DA active sensing framework can be applied with both single- and multi-antenna BSs, the latter with only minor modifications in the structure of one NN: an additional output branch with appropriate structure is included for the latter case to select a valid digital combiner from a finite set. Extensive numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves highly accurate and robust tracking across diverse target motion models, outperforming extended Kalman and particle filters, as well as, machine learning-based trackers. Furthermore, in static localization, it is shown to significantly outperform traditional fingerprinting schemes, deep reinforcement learning baselines, and standard backpropagation-based estimators.

</details>


### [284] [Decision Feedback Differential Detection for Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00121)
*Jiawei Qiu,Harry Leib*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Decision Feedback Differential Detection (DFDD) technique for Differential Reflecting Modulation (DRM) in RIS systems to overcome high error floors and performance degradation caused by time-varying fading channels when using conventional differential demodulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional Differential Demodulation (CDD) receivers for RIS DRM schemes experience high error floors and performance degradation over time-varying fading channels, especially when CSI is unavailable.

Method: The authors propose a Decision Feedback Differential Detection (DFDD) technique for DRM in RIS systems, and evaluate its performance through extensive Monte-Carlo simulations across various RIS scenarios.

Result: DFDD achieves lower error floors than CDD-based DRM at high SNR, performs similarly to CDD at low SNR, but is outperformed by DSTM-coded DRM at low SNR. Proper parameter selection is critical for good performance.

Conclusion: DFDD receivers offer significant advantages for RIS DRM systems over time-varying fading channels by providing low error floors at the cost of a small increase in complexity.

Abstract: This work considers a Differential Reflecting Modulation (DRM) scheme for Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) not requiring channel state information (CSI). When operating over time-varying fading channels, such schemes with Conventional Differential Demodulation (CDD) receivers experience high error floors and performance degradation. To address these issues, we propose a Decision Feedback Differential Detection (DFDD) technique for DRM. We explore the application of DFDD for RIS DRM and conduct extensive Monte-Carlo simulations to analyze performance. Results demonstrate the viability of our DFDD technique across various RIS scenarios and highlight the importance of proper parameter selection to achieve good performance. The DFDD scheme is also compared with uncoded and Differential Space-Time Modulation (DSTM) coded DRM using CDD based receivers. We observe that at low SNR, the DFDD scheme performs almost as well as the DRM with CDD scheme, but worse than the DSTM coded DRM. As the SNR increases however, both CDD-detected systems encounter high error floors while the error rate of DFDD based scheme continues to improve until it reaches a relatively low error floor. It is shown that the chief merits of employing DFDD receivers in such RIS systems is the low error floors they provide over time varying fading channels, albeit at expense of a small increased complexity.

</details>


### [285] [Guesswork Under Linear Constraints: Exact Exponent for Coset Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00205)
*Hassan Tavakoli*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper derives the exact exponential growth rate of the ρ-th moment of constrained guesswork for random binary linear codes under Bernoulli noise, showing it shifts by exactly ρ(1-R) relative to the unconstrained case. It also establishes universality linking guesswork exponent to weight-enumerator growth, with applications to q-ary codes and LDPC ensembles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize the fundamental limits of guessing the noise vector within a coset of a linear code, generalizing Arıkan-Merhav's unconstrained guesswork to the constrained (coset) setting.

Method: Large deviations analysis combining Rényi entropy, weight-enumerator concentration, and a transfer theorem connecting the partition-function exponent to weight-enumerator growth rate g(δ).

Result: Exact exponent: ρ h_{1/(1+ρ)}(p) + ρ(R-1). Universal formula: (1+ρ)ψ_{1/(1+ρ)}(g_ℰ) - ρ ψ_1(g_ℰ).

Conclusion: Constrained guesswork exponent shifts down by ρ(1-R) from unconstrained case, with parity checks contributing equally. Results extend to q-ary codes and LDPC ensembles via the universality theorem.

Abstract: We establish the exact exponential growth rate of the $ρ$-th moment of the constrained guesswork $G_{\mathrm{coset}}$ -- the rank of the true noise vector within its syndrome coset of a random binary linear code under i.i.d.\ Bernoulli$(p)$ noise: \( \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{n}\log_2\Eb\!\left[G_{\mathrm{coset}}^ρ\right] = ρ\,h_{\frac{1}{1+ρ}}(p)\;+\;ρ(R-1), \, ρ>0, \) where $h_α(p)$ is the binary Rényi entropy and $R=k/n$ is the code rate. The exponent shifts down by exactly $ρ(1-R)$ relative to the unconstrained Arıkan--Merhav exponent, with each of the $n(1-R)$ parity checks contributing equally. Finite-length simulations confirm convergence from below. We further establish: (i)~a transfer theorem expressing the partition-function exponent in terms of an arbitrary weight-enumerator growth rate $g(δ)$; (ii)~the exact exponent for $L_n$-list (``$k$-th'') constrained guesswork; and (iii)~a sharp second-order refinement of order $ρ\log_2 n$. Beyond the binary i.i.d.\ setting, we prove a universality theorem: for any code ensemble $\mathcal{E}$ whose weight enumerator concentrates at rate $g_{\mathcal{E}}(δ)$, the guesswork exponent equals $(1+ρ)ψ_{1/(1+ρ)}(g_{\mathcal{E}})-ρ\,ψ_1(g_{\mathcal{E}})$, where $ψ_α(g)=\sup_δ[g(δ)+α\ell(δ)]$. As concrete applications, we instantiate this theorem for the $q$-ary extension, $Λ_q(ρ)=ρ\,h^{(q)}_{1/(1+ρ)}(P)+ρ(R-1)\log_2 q$, and for Gallager's regular LDPC ensemble, obtaining a closed-form guesswork exponent via an exact finite-length identity for the ensemble-average weight enumerator.

</details>


### [286] [SLM, LLM or Agentic AI? Toward Intelligent UAV-Enabled WPT Systems in Low-Altitude Economy Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00255)
*Feibo Jiang,Li Dong,Lei Mao,Kezhi Wang,Xianbin Wang,Abbas Jamalipour*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper explores language models (SLM, LLM, and Agentic AI) for optimizing UAV path planning and energy efficiency in Wireless Power Transfer systems under dynamic, resource-constrained environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAVs are critical for low-altitude economic networks, but achieving efficient, adaptive optimization in resource-constrained and dynamic environments remains a major challenge.

Method: Proposes two approaches: (1) a lightweight SLM using pre-trained BERT with UAV embeddings, contextual features, geometry-aware path decoder, and ensemble inference; (2) an Agentic AI framework with four LLM-based agents (Initializer, Actor, Critic, Reflector) forming a closed optimization loop for iterative path and energy optimization.

Result: Simulations comparing SLM, LLM, and Agentic AI approaches demonstrate the performance trade-offs among complexity, latency, energy efficiency, and optimization capability.

Conclusion: Language models, particularly the Agentic AI framework with collaborative agents, offer promising solutions for adaptive UAV optimization, balancing efficiency with reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have become key enabling platforms for low-altitude economic networks, yet achieving efficient and adaptive optimization under resource-constrained and dynamic environments remains challenging. This paper investigates language models for UAV-enabled Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) systems. First, a lightweight Small Language Model (SLM)-based solution is developed using a pre-trained BERT backbone, enhanced UAV embeddings and contextual features, a geometry-aware path decoder, and ensemble inference to achieve low complexity, low latency, and high energy efficiency. Second, an Agentic AI-based framework is designed to exploit the reasoning and interactive capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). It integrates four collaborative agents-Initializer, Actor, Critic, and Reflector-to form a closed loop of generation, optimization, evaluation, and reflection for iterative UAV path and energy optimization. Finally, simulations compare the SLM-, LLM-, and Agentic AI-based approaches.

</details>


### [287] [Performance Evaluation of A Certain Transceiver Architecture for Multiple-Input Multiple-Output Phase-Modulated Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00356)
*Hengyu Cui,Ru-Han Chen,Zhenyao He,Shijun Zhu,Ruoqi Sun,Yeqin Tai*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper derives upper and lower bounds on the capacity of MIMO phase-modulated channels by transforming them into scalar sub-channels with annulus constellation geometry, showing small gaps between bounds at high SNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the fundamental limits of a specific transceiver architecture for MIMO phase modulation that converts the channel into scalar sub-channels with annulus constellation geometry.

Method: Uses convex geometry to derive two upper bounds on capacity under annulus support constraints, and applies the entropy power inequality to obtain a lower bound.

Result: The gaps between the derived upper and lower bounds are small at high signal-to-noise ratios for Rayleigh fading MIMO and RIS-assisted SIMO symbiotic systems.

Conclusion: The proposed bounds effectively characterize the capacity limits of the MIMO phase-modulated transceiver architecture, with tightness at high SNR.

Abstract: For multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channels with phase modulation, we recently proposed a method of unitarily transforming the channel matrix into a certain row-echelon form, by which the original MIMO channel can be converted into a certain number of scalar sub-channels with two phase inputs, thereby forming an annulus constellation geometry, and corrupted by both the additive white Gaussian noise and weak self-interference. In this paper, several bounds are derived to evaluate the fundamental limit of such a specific transceiver architecture. Two upper bounds are obtained by upper-bounding the capacity of a scalar channel with an annulus support constraint from the perspective of the convex geometry, while a lower bound is obtained by the standard entropy power inequality. Numerical results show that the gaps between these bounds are small at high signal-to-noise ratios for the MIMO phase-modulated channels over the Rayleigh fading and the single-input multiple-output symbiotic communication system assisted by a reconfigurable intelligent surface.

</details>


### [288] [Recovery of Planted Subgraphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00897)
*Wasim Huleihel*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper establishes sharp statistical thresholds for exact recovery of an arbitrary planted subgraph in Erdős–Rényi random graphs, introduces the "minimal maximum subgraph density" as the key quantity, and provides both efficient algorithms and computational lower bounds via the low-degree polynomial framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While prior work has focused on special subgraph families (cliques, bicliques, dense blocks), the exact recovery of a general planted subgraph in random graphs remains poorly understood. This paper aims to fill that gap by identifying the fundamental limits and computational challenges for arbitrary planted subgraphs.

Method: The authors derive sharp necessary and sufficient conditions for exact recovery, characterized by a novel graph-theoretic quantity called the "minimal maximum subgraph density." They propose a computationally efficient recovery algorithm leveraging spectral properties of the adjacency matrix, and derive computational lower bounds using the low-degree polynomial framework. Extensions to semi-random models and weaker recovery notions are also explored.

Result: Sharp statistical thresholds for exact recovery are identified with matching lower bounds. A polynomial-time algorithm is proposed with performance guarantees based on spectral properties. Regimes where recovery is statistically possible but computationally hard are established.

Conclusion: The work provides a comprehensive statistical and computational characterization of planted subgraph recovery for arbitrary subgraphs, introducing the minimal maximum subgraph density as a fundamental quantity and delineating statistical vs. computational feasibility.

Abstract: Understanding the fundamental limits of recovering planted subgraphs in random graphs is a central challenge in high-dimensional statistics and theoretical computer science. While existing work has largely focused on special subgraph families such as cliques, bicliques, or dense blocks, the exact recovery of a general planted subgraph in Erdős--Rényi random graphs remains poorly understood. In this paper, we study the exact recovery of an arbitrary planted subgraph $Γ= Γ_n$ embedded in a dense Erdős--Rényi random graph $\mathcal{G}(n,q_n)$, where edges within $Γ$ are present independently with probability $p_n > q_n$.
  Our main results identify sharp conditions under which exact recovery is possible with high probability, and we establish matching lower bounds showing the necessity of these conditions. The resulting statistical threshold is characterized by a new graph-theoretic quantity, which we term the \emph{minimal maximum subgraph density}. This quantity is defined as the maximum subgraph density of the smallest induced balanced subgraph of $Γ$.
  We then turn to the problem of recovery under polynomial-time constraints. We propose a computationally efficient recovery algorithm that applies to arbitrary planted subgraphs and analyze its performance in terms of certain spectral properties of the adjacency matrix. In addition, we derive computational lower bounds for recovery using the low-degree polynomial framework, establishing regimes where recovery is statistically possible but computationally hard. Finally, we consider several extensions of our setting, including recovery in semi-random models and weaker notions of recovery.

</details>


### [289] [Fundamental Limits of Random Downlink Integrated Sensing and Communication over Rician Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00912)
*Marziyeh Soltani,Mahtab Mirmohseni,Rahim Tafazolli,Mark F. Flanagan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the stochastic performance of a downlink MIMO ISAC system over Rician fading channels, studying two beamforming strategies (SJB and LB) and deriving communication/sensing outage probabilities, scaling laws, and performance trade-offs influenced by the Rician K-factor and LoS components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of analysis on ISAC system performance over Rician fading channels, which are critical for LoS-dominated deployments where deterministic propagation components significantly affect sensing and communication reliability.

Method: The paper analyzes two beamforming strategies: subspace joint beamforming (SJB) and linear beamforming (LB). It derives communication outage probability (OP) and sensing OP based on the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB), identifies special cases, bounds, and approximations, and characterizes large-system and high-power scaling laws.

Result: The Rician K-factor affects communication more strongly than sensing with non-monotonic behavior. LB without DPC is interference-limited at high power. LB with DPC achieves best overall performance in strong LoS and ultra-high reliability in Rayleigh fading. SJB provides a robust lower-complexity alternative across conditions.

Conclusion: LB with DPC is optimal for strong LoS environments and high-reliability Rayleigh fading, while SJB offers a good complexity-performance trade-off across diverse operating conditions.

Abstract: This paper studies the stochastic performance of a downlink multiple-input multiple-output integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system over Rician fading channels. Rician fading is important in line-of-sight (LoS)-dominated deployments, where a deterministic propagation component can strongly affect sensing and communication reliability. The base station (BS) simultaneously serves a user and senses a target. The BS-user channel contains LoS and non-line-of-sight components. The user LoS angle may be fixed or random, and the target angle may follow an arbitrary distribution potentially correlated with the user angle. Compared with Rayleigh fading, the deterministic LoS component introduces angle-dependent terms and leads to generally independent but non-identically distributed random vectors, requiring new analysis.
  We analyze two beamforming strategies: subspace joint beamforming (SJB), optimal for the shared waveform structure, and linear beamforming (LB), a practical alternative using separate sensing and communication beamformers. For both schemes, we derive communication outage probability (OP) and sensing OP based on the Cramer--Rao bound (CRB). We also identify special cases with simpler expressions. For LB, we derive upper and lower bounds on sensing OP and a tractable approximation.
  We characterize large-system and high-power scaling laws. LB without dirty paper coding (DPC) is interference-limited at high power due to radar self-interference. Results show the Rician K-factor affects communication more strongly than sensing, with non-monotonic behavior across regimes. LB with DPC achieves the best overall performance in strong LoS environments and is the only scheme achieving ultra-high communication reliability in Rayleigh fading, while SJB provides a robust lower-complexity alternative across operating conditions.

</details>


### [290] [DRL-Based Joint Beamforming and Surface Shape Optimization for Flexible Intelligent Metasurface-Aided ISAC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00951)
*Maoyuan Wang,Qian Zhang,Jiancheng An,Xuejun Cheng,Zheng Dong,Deqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper uses deep reinforcement learning (DDPG) to jointly optimize beamforming and flexible intelligent metasurface (FIM) shape, minimizing the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) for sensing while satisfying communication QoS constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design ISAC systems that unify high-precision sensing and wireless data transmission, and to overcome the non-convex optimization challenge of jointly designing beamforming and FIM surface shape to minimize CRB under QoS constraints.

Method: Formulated a non-convex optimization problem minimizing CRB subject to power, QoS, and FIM shape constraints; solved using a DDPG actor-critic DRL scheme with a constraint-aware reward function.

Result: Joint optimization of beamforming matrix and FIM surface shape substantially reduces CRB while maintaining communication quality, outperforming traditional rigid arrays.

Conclusion: DRL-based joint optimization of beamforming and FIM surface shape effectively enhances ISAC sensing precision under QoS constraints.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) unifies high-precision sensing and wireless data transmission. In this paper, we investigate the design of ISAC systems enabled by flexible intelligent metasurface (FIM) and aim to minimize the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) with quality of service (QoS) constraints using deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Specifically, we formulate the joint design of beamforming matrix and FIMs surface shape to reduce the CRB subject to transmit power, QoS and the FIMs surface shape constraints. However, the non-convex formulation makes optimization problem difficult to solve. To tackle this issue, we develop a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) actor critic DRL scheme for the joint design, guided by a constraint aware reward to progressively improve sensing performance. Numerical results demonstrate that jointly optimizing the beamforming matrix and the FIMs surface shape substantially decreases CRB while ensuring communication quality compared with existing rigid arrays.

</details>


### [291] [QuaMoE-DRF: Proactive Beam and Rate Adaptation via Multimodal Dynamic Radio Map Forecasting in ISAC Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00974)
*Zhihan Zeng,Kaihe Wang,Zhongpei Zhang,Chongwen Huang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: QuaMoE-DRF is a quality-aware multimodal dynamic radio map forecasting framework that predicts future beam-SINR fields for proactive beam and rate adaptation in ISAC networks, achieving 402.5 Mbps effective rate and 0.0417 outage probability on a dynamic urban benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static radio maps cannot capture short-term blockage from moving objects, and direct sensing-assisted beam prediction is limited because beam indices discard SINR margins, MCS thresholds, BS alternatives, and communication-equivalent neighboring beams. These limitations prevent effective proactive beam and rate adaptation.

Method: QuaMoE-DRF learns a compact reference-BS local field with BS-level supervision, joint BS-beam supervision, and latent network context. It fuses static geometry, event-like motion, structured sensing states, and wireless history via a quality-aware mixture-of-experts module based on inverse-variance fusion under heteroscedastic modality errors, jointly predicting communication-oriented map channels and proactive BS/beam/MCS decisions.

Result: On a dynamic multi-BS multi-UE urban benchmark, QuaMoE-DRF achieves 402.5 Mbps effective rate, 0.0417 outage probability, and 0.1836 map RMSE, improving effective rate by 5.67% and reducing outage by 8.35% over the strongest baseline.

Conclusion: The full multi-BS beam-SINR field is sufficient for finite-codebook threshold-rate decisions. The compact projection alone is insufficient for BS association. Validation uses labels from a blockage/path-loss simulator with ray tracing for calibration only.

Abstract: Static radio maps provide location-dependent propagation priors, but they cannot capture short-term blockage caused by moving objects. Direct sensing-assisted beam prediction is also limited because a beam index discards SINR margins, MCS thresholds, BS alternatives, and communication-equivalent neighboring beams. This paper proposes QuaMoE-DRF, a quality-aware multimodal dynamic radio map forecasting framework for proactive beam and rate adaptation in ISAC networks. Its core representation is a future beam-SINR field. We show that the full multi-BS beam-SINR field is sufficient for finite-codebook threshold-rate BS, beam, MCS, goodput, and outage decisions. For tractability, the implemented model learns a compact reference-BS local field, complemented by BS-level supervision, joint BS--beam supervision, and latent network context; we also clarify that this compact projection alone is not sufficient for BS association. QuaMoE-DRF fuses static geometry, event-like motion observations, structured sensing states, and wireless history through a quality-aware mixture-of-experts module motivated by inverse-variance fusion under heteroscedastic modality errors. It jointly predicts communication-oriented map channels and proactive BS, beam, and MCS decisions. On a dynamic multi-BS and multi-UE urban benchmark, QuaMoE-DRF achieves 402.5 Mbps effective rate, 0.0417 outage probability, and 0.1836 map RMSE, improving the effective rate by 5.67% and reducing outage by 8.35% over the strongest completed effective-rate baseline. The current validation uses labels from a compact blockage/path-loss simulator, with ray tracing used only for calibration and sanity checking.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [292] [Pinching Antennas-Assisted Sensing: A Ziv-Zakai Bound (ZZB) Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00234)
*Hao Jiang,Chongjun Ouyang,Yuanwei Liu,Arumugam Nallanathan,Robert Schober*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the sensing capability of pinching-antenna systems (PASS) using the Ziv-Zakai bound (ZZB), which accounts for sensing ambiguity from multimodal observations. The authors derive ZZB expressions for various prior distributions, study asymptotic behaviors, compare with the Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (BCRB), and propose surrogate objective functions for efficient ZZB-based optimization. Numerical results show ZZB provides tighter lower bounds than BCRB across SNRs and effectively addresses ambiguity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The sensing ambiguity arising from the multimodal observation model inherent to pinching-antenna systems (PASS) motivates the need for a more informative Bayesian bound than the conventional BCRB. The ZZB can capture ambiguity in likelihood functions and provide tight MSE lower bounds across a wide SNR range.

Method: The authors develop an observation model for uplink sensing with PASS, derive general ZZB expressions for arbitrary priors (specialized to Gaussian and uniform cases), characterize asymptotic ZZBs in low/high SNR regimes, study ZZB-BCRB relationship via ambiguity functions, and propose SNR-free and SNR-aware surrogate objective functions for efficient optimization.

Result: 1) ZZB provides a tighter sensing performance lower bound than BCRB across wide SNRs. 2) ZZB's ambiguity-awareness addresses multimodality-induced ambiguity, yielding reliable MSE bounds. 3) Proposed surrogate objective functions enable effective ZZB minimization with lower computational complexity.

Conclusion: The ZZB is a superior Bayesian bound for PASS sensing compared to BCRB, particularly in capturing ambiguity. The proposed surrogate functions allow practical ZZB-based optimization with reduced complexity.

Abstract: The sensing capability of the pinching-antenna system (PASS) is analyzed from a Ziv-Zakai bound (ZZB) perspective, motivated by the sensing ambiguity arising from the multimodal observation model inherent to PASS. In comparison to other Bayesian sensing bounds, the ZZB provides a lower bound on the mean-squared error (MSE) across a broad range of signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) and accounts for ambiguity in the likelihood functions. First, an observation model is developed for an uplink sensing scenario where a single sensing target transmits uplink pilots to a single-waveguide PASS receiver equipped with multiple pinching antennas (PAs). Building on this model, general ZZB expressions are derived for arbitrary prior distributions of the target's position, and are then specialized to the Gaussian and uniform cases. Second, the asymptotic ZZBs in low- and high-SNR regimes are characterized, and the relationship between the ZZBs and the conventional Bayesian Cramér-Rao bound (BCRB) is further studied by introducing the concept of an ambiguity function. Furthermore, to reduce the high computational complexity of direct evaluation of the ZZB, SNR-free and SNR-aware surrogate objective functions are proposed to facilitate ZZB-based optimization for enhancing sensing performance. Numerical results demonstrate that: i) Compared with the BCRB, the ZZB provides a tight sensing performance lower bound over a wide range of SNRs, ii) the ambiguity-awareness of the ZZB can address the multimodality-induced ambiguity in sensing, thereby yielding a reliable lower bound on the MSE, and iii) the proposed surrogate objective functions enable effective ZZB minimization with a lower computational complexity.

</details>


### [293] [ADC-Aware End-to-End Optimization of a Dynamic Metasurface Antenna with Strong Mutual Coupling for Monostatic Scene Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00253)
*Philipp del Hougne*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper studies ADC-aware end-to-end optimization of a monostatic sensing pipeline using a dynamic metasurface antenna (DMA) with strong mutual coupling. It shows that accounting for ADC non-idealities is crucial in low-resolution ADC regimes, as ignoring them causes severe accuracy degradation, while mutual coupling awareness is essential to avoid random-guess performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DMA studies assume ideal analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) and rely on simplified electromagnetic models, ignoring practical ADC non-idealities and mutual coupling effects that limit real-world performance.

Method: The authors model the wave domain using an MC-aware multiport-network model with parameters experimentally estimated from a fabricated chaotic-cavity-backed DMA with 96 one-bit-programmable meta-elements. They perform ADC-aware end-to-end optimization of DMA configurations and a digital classifier, with fixed uniform ADC or jointly learned ADC thresholds.

Result: ADC awareness is essential in low-resolution ADC regimes: with one-bit ADCs, ideal-ADC-trained system accuracy drops from 95.5% to 56.0%, while ADC-aware training achieves 87.2%. Without mutual coupling awareness, accuracy drops to random-guess level. Learning non-uniform ADC thresholds offers modest gains.

Conclusion: ADC awareness and mutual coupling awareness are both critical for practical DMA-based sensing systems, especially under low-resolution ADC constraints.

Abstract: Dynamic metasurface antennas (DMAs) enable programmable wave-domain signal processing that can be jointly optimized with downstream digital processing in an end-to-end manner. Existing studies, however, typically assume ideal analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) and often rely on simplified electromagnetic models. Here, we study ADC-aware end-to-end optimization of a monostatic sensing pipeline based on a DMA with strong mutual coupling (MC). We model the wave domain using an MC-aware multiport-network model whose parameters were experimentally estimated for a fabricated chaotic-cavity-backed DMA with 96 one-bit-programmable meta-elements. We perform ADC-aware end-to-end optimization of the DMA configurations and digital classifier, either with awareness of a fixed uniform ADC or, optionally, with jointly learned ADC decision thresholds, and compare against baselines that assume an ideal ADC and/or ignore MC. Our results show that ADC awareness is essential in low-resolution ADC regimes: with one-bit ADCs and eight DMA configurations, deploying an ideal-ADC-trained system with a uniform one-bit ADC reduces the test accuracy from 95.5% to 56.0%, whereas ADC-aware training with the same fixed uniform one-bit ADC achieves 87.2%. We also show that without MC awareness the accuracy drops to the random-guess level. Learning non-uniform ADC thresholds provides at most modest additional gains over fixed uniform ADCs in the considered DMA-based sensing pipeline.

</details>


### [294] [Evolving Intelligent Complex Systems via Intellicise Networks: Architecture, Technologies, and Pathways](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00316)
*Ping Zhang,Rui Meng,Xiaodong Xu,Song Gao,Zixuan Huang,Yaheng Wang,Yinqiu Liu,Ruichen Zhang,Yiming Liu,Kaiwen Yu,Yaping Sun,Han Meng,Haonan Tong,Huishi Song,Qianqian Yang,Shuoyao Wang,Lexi Xu,Qinghe Du,Geng Sun,Jiawen Kang,Gang Wu,Yiqing Zhou,Haixia Zhang,Zesong Fei,Aimin Hao,Ming Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a cross-domain intelligent communication network architecture based on intellicise networks (Intent-driven, semantic-native, distributed intelligence) for future large-scale, heterogeneous, wireless complex systems. It incorporates information theory, systems theory, game theory, and cybernetics, featuring a cross-layer organizational framework and multi-functional planes (control, user, data, computation, intelligence, security). The paper reviews enabling technologies from semantic extraction to symbodied AI, and presents a case study on embodied agent communications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Future engineering infrastructures are evolving into large-scale, open, heterogeneous, wirelessly interconnected complex systems that pose significant challenges in network resource optimization, high-dimensional information management, and diverse business requirements. Existing paradigms are insufficient, motivating the need for intellicise networks as a new approach.

Method: The authors propose a cross-domain intelligent communication network architecture based on intellicise networks, grounded in information theory, systems theory, game theory, and cybernetics. It includes a cross-layer organizational framework (perception→cognition→decision), multi-functional planes (control, user, data, computation, intelligence, security), and novel information flows (data, knowledge, model, task flows) that interconnect layers and planes in a closed-loop process.

Result: The paper systematically reviews key enabling technologies evolving from semantic extraction to intent understanding, from heterogeneous resource integration to self-configuration/optimization, from generative AI to agentic AI, and from embodied AI to symbodied AI. A case study on intellicise networks for embodied agent communications is presented, along with representative applications and services.

Conclusion: Intellicise networks offer a promising paradigm for enabling future intelligent complex systems, deriving simplicity from high-level intelligence while pursuing enhanced performance. The proposed architecture and identified technology evolution pathways provide a systematic foundation for further research and development.

Abstract: Future engineering infrastructures are evolving into large-scale, open, heterogeneous, and wirelessly interconnected complex systems. These systems present significant challenges in optimizing network resource utilization, managing high-dimensional information spaces, and accommodating diverse business requirements. Intellicise networks, characterized by Intent-driven operation, semantic-native capability, and distributed intelligence, offer a promising paradigm for enabling such intelligent complex systems. We provide a systematic exploration of future intelligent complex systems from the perspective of intellicise networks. Specifically, we propose a cross-domain intelligent communication network architecture based on intellicise networks, grounded in information theory, systems theory, game theory, and cybernetics. The architecture comprises a cross-layer organizational framework, multi-functional planes, and novel information flows. The cross-layer framework defines the vertical evolution from perception and cognition to decision, while the control, user, data, computation, intelligence, and security planes deliver horizontal intellicise capabilities. Moreover, data, knowledge, model, and task flows interconnect the various layers and planes, forming a closed-loop process that derives simplicity from high-level intelligene while concurrently pursuing enhanced. Building on this architecture, we review key enabling technologies, tracing their evolution from semantic extraction to intent understanding, from heterogeneous resource integration to self-configuration and self-optimization, from generative artificial intelligence (AI) to agentic AI, and from embodied AI to symbodied AI. Additionally, we present a case study on intellicise networks for embodied agent communications and discuss representative applications and services for intelligent complex systems.

</details>


### [295] [Semantic-based Internet of Embodied Intelligence: Visions and Frontiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00342)
*Yaheng Wang,Rui Meng,Xiaodong Xu,Yiming Liu,Feiliang Song,Linyuan Hu,Huishi Song,Lexi Xu,Tony Q. S. Quek,Ping Zhang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes the Semantic-based Internet of Embodied Intelligence (SIoEI) paradigm, which uses semantic information as a unified metric to address scalability bottlenecks—such as massive multimodal data transmission overhead and the decoupling of logical reasoning from physical constraints—in multi-agent embodied intelligence networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent advances in generative AI and embodied intelligence enable autonomous agents to interact with the physical world, but scaling these systems into networks of multiple agents (IoEI) faces critical bottlenecks including massive multimodal data transmission overhead and the decoupling of logical reasoning from physical constraints.

Method: The authors envision the Semantic-based IoEI (SIoEI) paradigm, which uses semantic information as a unified metric throughout the agent lifecycle. They define four key dimensions of EI (perception, intelligence, control, communication) and elaborate how semantic empowerment revolutionizes each dimension.

Result: A case study verifies that the semantic-empowered end-to-end process significantly improves channel robustness and reduces end-to-end latency for embodied intelligence systems.

Conclusion: The paper outlines critical open research directions for the SIoEI paradigm, suggesting that semantic communication offers a promising path forward for scaling multi-agent embodied intelligence systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative artificial intelligence (AI) and embodied intelligence (EI) enable autonomous agents to interact with the physical world. However, scaling these systems into networks of multiple agents, namely the Internet of EI (IoEI), faces critical bottlenecks. These include the overhead of massive multimodal data transmission and the decoupling of logical reasoning from physical constraints. To address these challenges, we envision the Semantic-based IoEI (SIoEI), which leverages semantic information as a unified metric throughout the agent lifecycle. We systematically define four key dimensions of EI: perception, intelligence, control, and communication. We further elaborate how semantic empowerment revolutionizes environmental perception, cognition and task planning, action generation and robust control, and communication and networking. We also present a case study to verify that, the semantic-empowered end-to-end process significantly improves channel robustness and reduces end-to-end latency for EI. Finally, we outline critical open research directions for the SIoEI paradigm.

</details>


### [296] [B2X Networks: Joint Design of Communication and Control for Embodied Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00537)
*Yuanwei Liu,Xu Gan,Zhaolin Wang,Chongjun Ouyang,Hao Jiang,Zongyao Zhao,Kaibin Huang,Robert Schober*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces brain-body-to-everything (B2X) networks that integrate wireless communication with embodied intelligence, proposing distributed and centralized brain architectures, redesigning uplink/downlink communication for state acquisition and command delivery, and using a communication-control Pareto boundary to characterize the trade-off between transmission performance and control quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between wireless networks and embodied intelligence by proposing a unified B2X framework that treats the brain (intelligence), body (physical agent), and ecosystem (X) as an integrated loop, addressing the need for redesigned communication protocols that go beyond traditional human-centric or machine-type communications.

Method: The paper proposes two B2X architectures (distributed vs. centralized brains) and focuses on a base-station-side brain setting. It redesigns uplink for state acquisition under event urgency, sensing volume, and multi-body access, and redesigns downlink for coordinated command delivery with conventional services. A communication-control Pareto boundary is introduced to model the loop-level trade-off.

Result: The paper provides conceptual architectures and design principles for B2X uplink/downlink, and introduces a Pareto boundary framework for analyzing the trade-off between wireless transmission performance and control quality in closed-loop embodied intelligence systems.

Conclusion: B2X networks offer a promising direction for integrating wireless and embodied intelligence, but several open problems remain, including scalability, security, latency guarantees, and cross-layer optimization for real-time brain-body interaction loops.

Abstract: This article proposes the concept of \emph{brain-body-to-everything (B2X)} networks to facilitate the integration of wireless networks and embodied intelligence. In this framework, the \emph{brain} refers to the intelligence functions for reasoning, planning, and decision-making, the \emph{body} denotes the physical embodied agent that senses and acts in the real world, and \emph{X} represents the surrounding ecosystem involved in the brain-body interaction loop. Two B2X architectures with \emph{distributed} and \emph{centralized} brains are introduced to characterize different placements of intelligence across the body, base station, and core network. The uplink and downlink designs of B2X networks are then discussed under a representative base-station-side brain setting. For the uplink, communication is redesigned for B2X state acquisition under event urgency, sensing volume, and simultaneous multi-body access. For the downlink, communication is redesigned to coordinate command delivery and conventional service under shared radio resources. Based on these uplink and downlink considerations, a communication-control Pareto boundary is further used to characterize the loop-level trade-off between wireless transmission performance and control quality in B2X networks. Finally, several open research problems are discussed to guide future B2X network design.

</details>


### [297] [Measurement-Based Characterization and Statistical Modeling of 6G Urban Low-Altitude A2G Channels across FR1 and FR3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00541)
*Bin Ao,Jianhua Zhang,Pan Tang,Peijie Liu,Boyang He,Hao Zheng*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents wideband A2G channel measurements in urban environments at 2.85, 4.6, and 7.25 GHz, develops a weakly supervised method for LoS/NLoS identification, and analyzes channel characteristics including PL, SF, RMS-DS, and Rician K-factor across frequency bands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate A2G channel characterization is essential for designing and optimizing low-altitude communication systems in future 6G integrated networks.

Method: Conducted wideband A2G channel measurements at three frequency bands (2.85, 4.6, 7.25 GHz) with 250 MHz bandwidth each; developed a weakly supervised method fusing geometric priors, channel features, and spatial consistency constraints for LoS/NLoS identification; extracted and statistically analyzed channel parameters.

Result: Close-in model fits measured PL better than 3GPP model; NLoS yields higher PL exponents and SF; higher frequencies show fewer MPCs and weaker delay dispersion; mean RMS-DS under LoS decreases from 93.11 to 46.84 ns; mean Rician K-factor increases from 9.16 to 12.88 dB; RMS-DS and K-factor follow lognormal and normal distributions respectively; receiver movement intensifies spatial non-stationarity.

Conclusion: The study provides comprehensive A2G channel models for urban environments across multiple frequency bands, supporting future 6G system design.

Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) communications have been recognized as a key component of future sixth-generation (6G) space-air-ground-sea integrated networks. Accurate characterization and modeling of air-to-ground (A2G) channels are essential for the design and optimization of low-altitude communication systems. This paper presents a wideband A2G channel measurement campaign in an urban environment at 2.85 and 4.6~GHz in FR1 and 7.25~GHz in the FR3 frequency band, each with a bandwidth of 250~MHz. To enable reliable line-of-sight (LoS) and non-line-of-sight (NLoS) propagation state identification, a weakly supervised method is developed by fusing geometric priors, channel features, and spatial consistency constraints.
