Furthermore, based on the measured data, A2G channel characteristics are extracted and analyzed under LoS/NLoS conditions across different frequency bands, including path loss (PL), shadow fading (SF), power delay profile, root-mean-square delay spread (RMS-DS), and Rician $K$-factor. The results show that the close-in model fits the measured PL more accurately than the 3GPP reference model, and that NLoS propagation leads to larger path loss exponents and stronger SF than LoS propagation. For channel delay characteristics, higher-frequency channels exhibit fewer effective MPCs and weaker delay dispersion, indicating increased channel sparsity. Specifically, the mean RMS-DS under LoS conditions decreases from 93.11 to 46.84~ns, while the mean Rician $K$-factor increases from 9.16 to 12.88~dB. The statistical results further show that the RMS-DS and the Rician $K$-factor can be well characterized by lognormal and normal distributions, respectively. Moreover, the movement of the receiver in a complex scattering environment intensifies the spatial non-stationarity of the A2G channel.

</details>


### [298] [Frame-Based AFDM-ISAC Waveform Design With Chirp-Enabled Pulse Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00632)
*Qu Luo,Zilong Liu,Musavian Leila,Thomos Nikolaos,Qihao Peng,Pei Xiao*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an AFDM-empowered ISAC design with a novel frame structure, analog-domain chirp compression sensing, digital-domain sensing fusion, low-complexity channel estimation using GCE-BEM, and optimal power allocation, plus a Kalman filter for intra-frame channel tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate sensing and communications in a unified AFDM-based framework, overcoming challenges such as full-duplex hardware requirements and high-mobility channel estimation.

Method: Proposes an AFDM-ISAC frame structure with ISAC symbols (single chirp subcarrier for sensing/channel estimation) and pure data symbols; analog-domain sensing receiver with local chirp down-mixing for chirp compression gains; digital-domain sensing fusion guided by AFDM modulation parameters; low-complexity channel estimation via GCE-BEM; optimal power allocation between pilot and data; GCE-BEM-based Kalman filter for intra-frame channel estimation.

Result: The proposed design achieves chirp compression gains without full-duplex hardware, enables low-complexity channel estimation in high mobility scenarios, and supports robust frame-based AFDM communications.

Conclusion: AFDM-ISAC effectively combines integrated sensing and communications with improved efficiency and reduced hardware complexity, validated through the proposed frame structure, analog/digital processing, and advanced channel estimation techniques.

Abstract: This paper proposes an Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM)-empowered integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) design, referred to as AFDM-ISAC. We first design a novel AFDM-ISAC frame structure that consists of both ISAC and pure data symbols. Each ISAC symbol consists of a single chirp subcarrier for both sensing and channel estimation, while the remaining subcarriers are allocated for communication. Building upon this structure, we present an analog-domain sensing receiver that down-mixes the received echo with a local chirp to fully exploit \textit{chirp compression} gains avoiding the need for full-duplex hardware. In addition, a sensing fusion algorithm, guided by AFDM modulation parameters, is further proposed in the digital domain. Leveraging the distinct features of the proposed AFDM-ISAC frame, we present a low-complexity channel estimation scheme for high mobility channels based on a generalized complex exponential basis expansion model (GCE-BEM), along with an optimal power allocation strategy between pilot and data symbols. Moreover, to support frame-based AFDM communications, a GCE-BEM-based Kalman filter is also employed for robust intra-frame channel estimation.

</details>


### [299] [Assessing Cardiac Dynamics through RF Sensing for Hemodynamic Monitoring in Pacemakers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00791)
*A. Khaleghi,J. Bergsland,I. Balasingham*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes using RF signal variations between intracardiac transceivers and subcutaneous receivers in pacemakers as a novel method for real-time hemodynamic monitoring, finding strong correlation with cardiac dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore RF channels in cardiac pacemakers as a new sensing modality for hemodynamic monitoring, potentially replacing or augmenting existing sensors.

Method: Analyzed RF signal variations between intracardiac transceivers (RV and RA) and subcutaneous receivers, correlating them with cardiac dynamics.

Result: Temporal RF signal variations closely aligned with cardiac rhythm, enabling estimation of chamber volume, valve behavior, and pressure changes.

Conclusion: RF-based sensing shows strong potential as a novel real-time cardiac monitoring method in pacemaker systems.

Abstract: This paper examines the use of radiofrequency (RF) channels for hemodynamic monitoring in cardiac pacemakers. It analyzes RF signal variations between intracardiac transceivers in the right ventricle (RV) and right atrium (RA), as well as subcutaneous receivers, to determine their correlation with cardiac dynamics. The study shows that temporal RF signal variations closely align with cardiac rhythm, allowing for the estimation of parameters such as chamber volume, valve behavior, and pressure changes. These results underscore the potential of RF-based sensing as a novel method for real-time cardiac monitoring in pacemaker systems.

</details>


### [300] [Meta-Transfer Learning for mmWave Beam Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00860)
*Ahmet Nuri Cevik,Sinem Coleri*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: MTL-BA proposes a meta-transfer learning framework for mmWave beam alignment that freezes a pre-trained CNN backbone and only meta-learns lightweight Scale-and-Shift adapters plus a classifier head, achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning and MAML while updating ~17× fewer parameters and requiring 60% fewer meta-training epochs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing meta-learning for beam prediction adapts entire networks from random initialization (high parameter count, high cost), while transfer learning restricts adaptation but does not exploit episodic meta-learning to optimize the adaptation process itself. The goal is to combine both approaches to reduce adaptation cost and meta-training budget without sacrificing performance.

Method: The paper proposes MTL-BA, which freezes a pre-trained convolutional backbone and meta-learns only lightweight Scale-and-Shift (SS) adapters together with a classifier head. It warm-starts from the pre-trained model, restricting adaptation to SS adapters and classifier head, reducing both adaptation cost and meta-training budget.

Result: On the DeepMIMO ray-tracing dataset, MTL-BA matches accuracy and spectral efficiency of full fine-tuning across various SNR levels while updating ~17× fewer parameters than both full fine-tuning and MAML, outperforms last-layer fine-tuning with comparable parameter updates, and approaches MAML's performance while requiring 60% fewer meta-training epochs.

Conclusion: MTL-BA effectively combines meta-learning and transfer learning to achieve efficient mmWave beam alignment with significantly reduced parameter updates and meta-training costs, making it a practical solution for next-generation wireless systems.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) beam alignment plays a critical role in next-generation wireless systems, yet its efficient implementation remains challenging. Meta-learning and transfer learning have been explored to enable deep learning-based beam prediction models to rapidly adapt to unseen environments; however, existing meta-learning approaches adapt the entire network and are trained from random initialization, leading to a large number of updated parameters and a high meta-training cost, while transfer learning approaches restrict adaptation to part of the network but do not exploit episodic meta-learning, which explicitly trains the model over multiple tasks, to optimize the adaptation process itself. To overcome these limitations, we propose MTL-BA, a meta-transfer learning framework for beam alignment in millimeter-wave multiple-input single-output (MISO) systems that freezes a pre-trained convolutional backbone and meta-learns only lightweight Scale-and-Shift (SS) adapters together with a classifier head. Warm-starting from the pre-trained model and restricting adaptation to the SS adapters and classifier head reduce both the adaptation cost and the meta-training budget without sacrificing prediction performance. Simulation results on the DeepMIMO ray-tracing dataset show that MTL-BA matches the accuracy and spectral efficiency of full fine-tuning across various SNR levels despite updating approximately $17\times$ fewer parameters than both full fine-tuning and Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), outperforms last-layer fine-tuning while updating a comparable number of parameters, and approaches MAML's performance while requiring $60\%$ fewer meta-training epochs.

</details>


### [301] [Lightweight Vision-Aided Beam Tracking for Cross-Environment mmWave Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00936)
*Mengyuan Ma,Ahmed Alkhateeb,Nhan Thanh Nguyen,A. Lee Swindlehurst,Markku Juntti*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a lightweight vision-aided model for cross-environment beam tracking in mmWave communications, using depthwise separable convolutions with hierarchical data augmentation and beam power-based label smoothing. It achieves up to 84% cross-environment beam prediction accuracy while reducing parameters by 52× and complexity by 79× compared to ResNet baselines on DeepSense 6G datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world sensing-aided beam tracking faces challenges from rapid channel variations and environmental differences across deployment scenarios. Existing approaches are complex and fail to generalize across diverse environments, motivating the need for a low-complexity, generalizable solution.

Method: The task is formulated as sequence-to-sequence classification to jointly predict current and future optimal beams from past visual observations. The model uses depthwise separable convolutions for lightweight design, plus hierarchical data augmentation and beam power-based label smoothing to improve robustness and cross-environment generalization.

Result: On two geometrically distinct DeepSense 6G scenarios, the proposed method achieves up to 84% cross-environment beam prediction accuracy across current and three future time slots, outperforming state-of-the-art. It reduces model parameters by ~52× and computational complexity by ~79× compared to a high-capacity ResNet baseline.

Conclusion: Lightweight vision-aided models with proper augmentation and label smoothing can effectively generalize across diverse mmWave environments for beam tracking, offering substantial efficiency gains without sacrificing prediction accuracy.

Abstract: Sensing-aided beam tracking is a promising approach to reduce the overhead for millimeter-wave beam management. However, real-world application remains challenging due to rapid channel variations and substantial environmental differences across deployment scenarios. Developing low-complexity sensing assisted approaches that generalize to diverse environments can alleviate the problem. With this motivation, this paper proposes a lightweight vision-aided model for cross-environment beam tracking. The task is formulated as a sequence-to-sequence classification problem, where the model jointly predicts the current and future optimal beams from past visual observations. We develop a low-complexity model based on depthwise separable convolutions and introduce hierarchical data augmentation and beam power-based label smoothing to improve robustness and generalization. Experimental results on real-world images from two geometrically distinct DeepSense 6G scenarios show that the proposed strategies consistently improve cross-environment beam prediction accuracy up to 84% across the current and three future time slots, outperforming the state-of-the-art solution. Notably, this performance is achieved while reducing the number of model parameters and computational complexity by factors of approximately 52 and 79, respectively, compared with the high-capacity ResNet baseline.

</details>


### [302] [Channel Estimation and Beamforming for Microwave Linear Analog Computers (MiLACs)-Aided Multiuser MISO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00954)
*Qiaosen Zhang,Matteo Nerini,Bruno Clerckx*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes computationally efficient channel estimation and beamforming schemes for MiLAC-aided multiuser MISO systems with limited RF chains, achieving up to 1540× and 16108× computational reduction for channel estimation and beamforming respectively, while maintaining comparable performance to digital baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Channel estimation for MiLAC-aided multiuser systems remains an open problem. Conventional methods require many RF chains and heavy digital processing, undermining MiLAC's advantages in reducing hardware and computational cost.

Method: The proposed schemes exploit rank deficiency of channel correlation matrices to compress full-dimensional received signals in the analog domain via MiLAC, preserving essential channel information while reducing dimensionality. Then low-dimensional channel estimation is performed in the digital domain. Regularized zero-forcing beamforming is realized through a cascade of two MiLACs for greater computational flexibility.

Result: Up to 1540× computational reduction for channel estimation and 16108× for beamforming, while achieving performance comparable to digital baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed schemes enable efficient channel estimation and beamforming for MiLAC-aided MU-MISO systems with limited RF chains, preserving MiLAC's advantages in reducing hardware and computational complexity.

Abstract: Microwave linear analog computers (MiLACs) have recently gained attention for future gigantic multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems by enabling beamforming with greatly reduced hardware and computational cost. However, channel estimation for MiLAC-aided multiuser systems remains an open problem. Conventional channel estimation requires many radio-frequency (RF) chains to access full-dimensional received signals, followed by massive digital processing, which undermines the advantages of MiLAC-aided systems in reducing the number of RF chains and computational complexity. In this paper, we propose computationally efficient channel estimation and beamforming schemes for MiLAC-aided multiuser multiple-input single-output (MU-MISO) systems with a limited number of RF chains. We consider the general case where different user groups experience different channel correlation matrices. By exploiting the rank deficiency of these matrices, the proposed schemes use MiLAC to compress the full-dimensional received signals in the analog domain, making them compatible with the available RF chains while preserving the essential channel information. Then, in the digital domain, only low-dimensional channel estimation is performed based on these compressed observations, substantially reducing computational cost. We further show how regularized zero-forcing beamforming (R-ZFBF) can be efficiently realized from the low-dimensional channel estimates through a cascade of two MiLACs, which offers greater computational flexibility than a single MiLAC. Numerical results show that the proposed schemes reduce computational complexity up to $1540\times$ and $16108\times$, for channel estimation and beamforming, respectively, while achieving performance comparable to digital baselines.

</details>


### [303] [Opportunistic Positioning with LEO Satellites based on SSB from NR NTN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00967)
*Rainer Bachl,Muhammad Nabeel,Tingting Lei*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes using NR NTN's Synchronization Signal Block (SSB) from LEO satellite networks (like Starlink) for opportunistic receiver positioning, achieving <10m mean error without modifying standards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit the existing SSB signals in forthcoming LEO satellite networks for opportunistic positioning, eliminating the need for dedicated positioning signals or hardware modifications.

Method: Doppler shift measurements from SSB are used to derive pseudoranges, accounting for clock bias/drift. Integer ambiguity is resolved via geometry alone without inter-satellite differencing or a-priori position. Multiple SSBs from multiple satellites at multiple occasions are used.

Result: Simulation based on actual Starlink constellation and NR NTN standard shows mean positioning error less than 10 meters.

Conclusion: The proposed SSB-based positioning approach is feasible and accurate without any modifications to the existing NR NTN standard.

Abstract: Forthcoming Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks such as Starlink's Mobile Satellite Service (MSS) will incorporate the New Radio (NR) Non-Terrestrial Network (NTN) standard. The Synchronization Signal Block (SSB) specified as part of NR is periodically broadcast for cell search and initial access. We propose to exploit the SSB for opportunistic receiver positioning. Doppler shift measurements are modeled and pseudoranges are derived from SSB while also taking into account the receiver's clock bias and drift. The resulting per satellite integer ambiguity in the pseudorange is resolved by geometry alone, without inter-satellite differencing or an a-priori position. Measurements are taken from SSBs of multiple satellites and at multiple occasions per satellite, whereby the SSBs are subject to different transmission timings and varying propagation delays. Finally, a simulation model is developed for positioning based on the actual Starlink constellation and the NR NTN standard to evaluate the positioning accuracy to be expected. The proposed approach achieves a mean positioning error of less than 10m without requiring any modification of the NR NTN standard.

</details>


### [304] [Low-Complexity Sensing-Aware PAPR Reduction for AFDM-based ISAC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01064)
*Eya Gourar,Abdul Karim Gizzini,Yahia Medjahdi,Patrick Sondi,Laurent Clavier*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a sensing-aware chirp-subcarrier reservation (CSR) framework for AFDM-based ISAC to reduce PAPR while improving ranging performance via gradient-based minimization and randomized local search for sidelobe suppression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AFDM is promising for ISAC in high-mobility environments but suffers from high PAPR, which the paper aims to address without degrading sensing performance.

Method: A CSR framework combining low-complexity gradient-based PAPR minimization with a randomized local search that exploits phase sensitivity of the AFDM autocorrelation function to suppress delay LAZ sidelobes.

Result: Significant PAPR reduction together with notable sidelobe suppression, leading to improved weak-target detection performance.

Conclusion: The proposed CSR framework effectively balances PAPR reduction and sensing quality, enhancing AFDM-based ISAC systems.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has emerged as a key technology for future wireless networks by enabling communication and environmental sensing through a common waveform and hardware platform. Among the candidate waveforms for ISAC, Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing (AFDM) had attracted significant attention due to its robustness in high-mobility environments, but it suffers from a high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR). In this paper, we propose a sensing-aware chirp-subcarrier reservation (CSR) framework that reduces PAPR while improving ranging performance. The proposed method combines low-complexity gradient-based PAPR minimization with a randomized local search that exploits the phase sensitivity of the AFDM autocorrelation function to suppress delay low-ambiguity-zone (LAZ) sidelobes. Numerical results show that the proposed scheme achieves significant PAPR reduction together with significant sidelobe suppression, resulting in improved weak-target detection performance.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [305] [Constructive Alignment: Governing Preference Dynamics in Human-AI Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00001)
*Max Kanwal,Caryn Tran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes Constructive Alignment, a paradigm shift from treating alignment as static preference optimization to regulating how AI systems shape the evolution of human preferences over time, using a control-theoretic framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI alignment approaches assume human preferences are fixed targets to be inferred and optimized, but this contradicts empirical evidence showing preferences are layered, dynamic, and constructed through interaction—especially with adaptive AI systems that increasingly shape what people value over time.

Method: The authors draw on behavioral economics, psychology, and constructivist social theory to model preferences as layered state variables. They formalize this using a control-theoretic framework where system actions and interaction design jointly influence both world states and human evaluative states.

Result: A formal framework for Constructive Alignment that reframes alignment as regulating how AI systems influence the evolution of human preferences, ensuring value trajectories remain coherent, reflectively endorsed, epistemically grounded, bounded against manipulation, and empowering under uncertainty.

Conclusion: Alignment is not primarily about controlling AI behavior or satisfying static preferences, but about governing long-term value formation—a problem of regulating how AI systems participate in shaping human preference trajectories.

Abstract: Most approaches to AI alignment treat human preferences as fixed targets to be inferred and optimized. This assumption conflicts with extensive empirical evidence showing that preferences are layered, dynamic, and constructed through interaction--particularly with adaptive technologies. As AI systems become more persistent, personalized, and socially embedded, they increasingly participate in shaping what people attend to, value, and endorse over time. We introduce Constructive Alignment, a paradigm that reframes alignment as a control problem over evolving human preference trajectories rather than static preference satisfaction. Drawing on behavioral economics, psychology, and constructivist social theory, we model preferences as layered state variables that evolve under interaction with AI systems. We formalize this view using a control-theoretic framework in which system actions and interaction design jointly influence both world states and human evaluative states. We argue that alignment is not primarily about controlling AI behavior, but about regulating how AI systems influence the evolution of human preferences--ensuring that value trajectories remain coherent, reflectively endorsed, epistemically grounded, bounded against manipulation, and empowering under uncertainty. Alignment thus becomes a problem of governing long-term value formation rather than simply satisfying static preferences.

</details>


### [306] [Bounded Morality: Defining the Space of Moral Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00002)
*Max Kanwal,Caryn Tran,Patrick Mineault*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Bounded Morality, a framework analyzing moral cognition through computational constraints on finite agents, formalizing a tradeoff between moral breadth and depth under resource limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models treat moral cognition as fixed ethical theories (deontology, consequentialism, virtue ethics) implemented as static rules, failing to account for computational limitations of finite agents.

Method: Extends bounded rationality to moral domains by formalizing moral breadth (scope of morally relevant entities) and moral depth (inferential integration for interactions), establishing a tradeoff under resource constraints.

Result: Ethical theories are reinterpreted as locally efficient strategies adapted to different demand regimes; defines moral regret and moral progress under constraints; shows AI alignment depends on scaling reasoning capacity, not imitating human judgments.

Conclusion: Moral alignment in AI systems should focus on computational capacity allocation rather than directly mimicking human moral outputs.

Abstract: Moral cognition has traditionally been modeled as adherence to fixed ethical theories--deontology, consequentialism, virtue ethics--implemented as static rules or value functions. We propose Bounded Morality, a formal framework for analyzing the computational demands of moral problems faced by finite agents. Extending Herbert Simon's notion of bounded rationality, we formalize moral situations along two orthogonal dimensions: moral breadth, the scope of entities treated as morally relevant, and moral depth, the inferential integration required to evaluate their interactions. Limited resources impose an unavoidable tradeoff between these dimensions, defining a feasible space of moral computation. Within this space, ethical theories correspond to locally efficient strategies adapted to different demand regimes rather than competing accounts of moral truth. The framework yields a formal notion of moral regret and moral progress under constraint, and implies that moral alignment in artificial systems depends on the scaling and allocation of moral reasoning capacity rather than on direct imitation of human judgments.

</details>


### [307] [The MMM Data Model -- A Normative Specification for Knowledge Interoperability in a Decentralisable Knowledge Commons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00032)
*Mathilde Noual*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MMM is a data model for knowledge documentation that balances minimal normative constraints with free-text expressiveness, designed for interdisciplinary interoperability without requiring semantic convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional document-centric information systems constrain knowledge structuring, updating, sharing, and reuse; formal approaches fail due to poor usability and adoption, while AI systems lack a portable alternative for human knowledge exchange.

Method: MMM combines a small set of normative constraints with free-text labels, designed for cross-disciplinary interoperability without semantic convergence.

Result: A reference implementation and pilot deployment data demonstrate implementability and early usability.

Conclusion: MMM offers a practical, lightweight data model addressing the limitations of both rigid formal systems and traditional document-centric approaches in collaborative knowledge work.

Abstract: Many information systems are built around documents: self-contained units optimised for print production and linear reading. While effective for large-scale dissemination, the document-centric organisation constrains how knowledge can be structured, updated, shared, and reused. Formal approaches address some of these limitations but struggle to achieve widespread contribution and adoption due to their prioritisation of formal structure over other system properties such as human usability and scope. AI systems are reshaping document production, but without providing a unified portable alternative to traditional documents for humans' expression and exchange of knowledge. This paper presents MMM, a data model for knowledge documentation that emerged from the practical needs of interdisciplinary collaborative research, and positioned here within a comparative analysis of the design space of information systems. MMM combines a small set of normative constraints with the expressive freedom of free-text labels. It is designed for interoperability across disciplines, applications and deployments without requiring semantic convergence. A reference implementation and pilot deployment data demonstrate implementability and early usability.

</details>


### [308] [Making Failure Safe: A Constrained, Verifiable Agent Framework for Open-Web Data Collection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00035)
*Bo Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A constrained agent framework replaces free-form code with typed JSON configurations and rule-based checks to generate reliable, verifiable web scrapers from natural-language requirements, achieving zero LLM token usage at execution time with strong performance on 138 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct LLM generation of web scrapers is unreliable due to dependency errors, broken selectors, schema mismatches, and heterogeneous page structures.

Method: The framework shifts LLM output to typed JSON collector configurations, using a six-type collector taxonomy, template/utility-function constraints, static Airflow DAG execution, rule-based quality checking, and structured feedback correction.

Result: On 138 tasks, the taxonomy supports description-based requirement typing; on 80 independently source-verified tasks, the framework runs with zero execution-stage LLM tokens and the lowest average wall-clock time.

Conclusion: The framework offers a reusable, low-cost, and verifiable execution path for repeated open-web data collection, trading moderate one-shot quality for deterministic and verifiable execution.

Abstract: LLMs and agents can generate web scrapers from natural-language requirements, but direct generation remains unreliable because of dependency errors, broken selectors, schema mismatches, and heterogeneous page structures. We propose a constrained, verifiable agent framework that shifts LLM output from free-form code to typed JSON collector configurations, combining a six-type collector taxonomy, template and utility-function constraints, static Airflow DAG execution, rule-based quality checking, and structured feedback correction. Experiments on 138 tasks show that the taxonomy supports description-based requirement typing, while confirming that stable instantiation requires completing source, field, and execution constraints beyond the initial description. On 80 independently source-verified tasks, the framework runs with zero execution-stage LLM tokens and the lowest average wall-clock time, trading moderate one-shot quality for a reusable, deterministic, and verifiable execution path suited to repeated scheduled collection. These results position the framework as a reusable, low-cost, and verifiable execution path for repeated open-web data collection.

</details>


### [309] [Solution space path planning for supporting en-route air traffic control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00064)
*Yiyuan Zou,Wenying Lyu,Clark Borst*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a conflict-free path-planning algorithm for en-route Air Traffic Control that is interpretable, computationally efficient, and aligned with controllers' decision logic. It integrates three conflict detection methods within a solution-space framework, achieving 3.69 ms average computation time in operational scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing path-planning algorithms are not widely adopted in tactical ATC because they prioritize optimization over interpretability, misaligning with controllers' needs. There is a gap for decision-support tools that are inherently human-centric, flexible, and efficient.

Method: The algorithm integrates three intent-based conflict detection methods (distance-based, time-interval-based, zone-based) within a solution-space framework. Two variants—SSPPV (vertex-based) and SSPPE (edge-based)—are proposed for solution space path planning, evaluated on computational speed and solution quality.

Result: SSPPV paired with zone-based conflict detection performed best, computing conflict-free paths in 3.69 ms on average in operational scenarios based on the Delta sector of MUAC using a 5 nmi grid.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithm achieves fast, interpretable, and conflict-free path planning suitable for tactical ATC, demonstrating that solution-space approaches with appropriate conflict detection can bridge the gap between algorithmic efficiency and controller usability.

Abstract: As technology advances, many path-planning algorithms have been proposed for Air Traffic Management, yet their operational adoption in tactical control remains limited, revealing a misalignment between algorithmic design priorities and air traffic controllers' needs. This underscores the need for decision-support solutions that are inherently interpretable, computationally efficient, and explicitly designed for human use. Focusing on this design challenge, this study develops a conflict-free path-planning algorithm for en-route Air Traffic Control (ATC) designed to be compatible with two guiding considerations: (1) the interpretability and flexibility offered by solution-space displays, which motivate constructing an algorithm that exposes all feasible safe actions and accommodates shifting optimization goals; and (2) the decision logic controllers naturally apply when enforcing operational constraints, such as separation standards, maneuverability limits, waypoint minimization, and routing practicality. Centered on these principles, the algorithm integrates three intent-based conflict detection methods -- distance-based, time-interval-based, and zone-based -- within a solution-space framework to identify conflict-free paths in computationally efficient ways. Additionally, vertex-based and edge-based search nodes are proposed for solution space path planning (SSPP), resulting in two variants -- SSPPV and SSPPE, respectively, which are evaluated in terms of computational speed and solution quality. Empirical results show that SSPPV paired with zone-based conflict detection achieves the best performance, computing paths in 3.69 ms on average in operational-relevant scenarios based on the Delta sector of the Maastricht Upper Area Control Centre (MUAC) using a 5 nmi grid.

</details>


### [310] [RareDxR1: Autonomous Medical Reasoning for Rare Disease Diagnosis Beyond Human Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00147)
*Deyang Jiang,Haoran Wu,Ziyi Wang,Yiming Rong,Yunlong Zhao,Ye Jin,Bo Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RareDxR1 is an end-to-end reasoning-centric LLM for open-domain rare disease diagnosis from unstructured clinical notes, using knowledge internalization, Reflection-Enhanced Reasoning Sampling, and dual-level curriculum reinforcement learning to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI methods for rare disease diagnosis rely on pipeline-based phenotype extraction or RAG, which suffer from information loss due to predefined ontologies, retrieval bottlenecks, and lack of diagnostic logic.

Method: Proposes (1) progressive end-to-end training with knowledge internalization into model parameters, (2) Reflection-Enhanced Reasoning Sampling (RERS) for synthesizing expert diagnostic trajectories without human annotation, and (3) dual-level curriculum reinforcement learning for gradual mastery.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across different benchmarks for open-domain rare disease diagnosis.

Conclusion: RareDxR1 marks a significant breakthrough in open-domain rare disease diagnosis by overcoming limitations of RAG and phenotype restriction.

Abstract: Rare disease differential diagnosis is a critical yet arduous clinical task, requiring physicians to identify precise phenotypes from complex, unstructured patient symptoms and execute intricate reasoning within a vast search space. However, existing AI approaches typically rely on pipeline-based phenotype extraction or retrieval-augmented generation, which suffer from critical information loss due to predefined ontologies, retrieval bottlenecks, and a lack of diagnostic logic. To address these challenges, we introduce RareDxR1, an end-to-end reasoning-centric large language model designed for open-domain rare disease diagnosis directly from unstructured clinical notes. We design a progressive end-to-end training framework by synergizing knowledge internalization with autonomous evolutionary learning, thereby bypassing reliance on structured phenotypes and closed-set decision-making. To overcome the limitations of RAG and phenotype restriction, we enabled the deep internalization of fragmented rare-disease knowledge directly into the model's parameters. Moreover, to bridge the gap between model generation and expert reasoning, we propose Reflection-Enhanced Reasoning Sampling (RERS), a strategy that synthesizes expert-level diagnostic trajectories by learning from failures without human annotation. Additionally, we propose a dual-level curriculum reinforcement learning approach for gradually mastering rare disease diagnosis. Experimental results demonstrate that RareDxR1 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across different benchmarks, marking a significant breakthrough in open-domain rare disease diagnosis. Our code and dataset will be publicly available.

</details>


### [311] [A Contextual-Bandit Oversight Game with Two-Sided Informational Asymmetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00155)
*Yunjin Tong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies human-AI oversight in a bandit game with two-sided asymmetric information—humans know their reward, AI knows action quality—and identifies a "slab of avoidable harm" where myopic humans fail to oversee despite AI knowing the action is harmful.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Runtime human oversight of AI agents faces information asymmetry in both directions: humans know their own preferences but not the AI's situation assessment, while the AI knows action quality but not the human's reward. This mirrors real-world scenarios like autonomous robots operating beyond direct human supervision.

Method: The authors build on Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning (CIRL) and the Oversight Game, introducing a contextual-bandit team game with two-sided asymmetric information and a play/ask/trust/oversee interface. The bandit structure removes physical state transitions, enabling exact one-shot characterizations that would remain conjectural in full POMDP settings.

Result: Two one-shot characterizations are derived: a team optimum and a myopic behavioral rule. The gap between them is a "slab of avoidable harm"—a region where the AI privately knows the action is harmful and shutdown would help, but a myopic human trusting her prior declines to oversee. This gap is identified as the price of non-credible oversight communication.

Conclusion: The gap resolves dynamically over repeated rounds through passive learning and active signaling with one-period-lagged oversight response, offering partial analysis of this dynamic resolution.

Abstract: We study runtime human oversight of an AI agent when private information runs in both directions: the human privately knows her reward function, while the AI privately knows the quality of the action it proposes. This is the kind of asymmetry that arises naturally when an autonomous robot or software agent has inspected a situation its human supervisor cannot directly assess. Building on Cooperative Inverse Reinforcement Learning (CIRL) and the Oversight Game, we introduce a contextual-bandit team game with two-sided asymmetric information and a play/ask/trust/oversee interface. The bandit structure removes physical state transitions and thereby yields exact one-shot characterizations that would remain conjectural in the full POMDP setting, though the common belief remains a dynamically controlled state across rounds. We give two one-shot characterizations, a team optimum and a behaviorally natural myopic rule, whose gap is a slab of avoidable harm: a region in which the AI privately knows the proposed action is harmful and shutdown would help, yet a myopic human, trusting her prior, declines to oversee. We show this gap is the price of non-credible oversight communication, and give a partial analysis of how it resolves dynamically over repeated rounds through passive learning and active signaling with a one-period-lagged oversight response.

</details>


### [312] [Constructing Epistemic AI Literacy: Detecting Epistemic Aims and Processes in Student-AI Co-Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00211)
*Mengqian Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study introduces Epistemic AI Literacy (EAIL), a framework for understanding how students engage epistemically with GenAI in programming. Analysis of human-AI co-programming dialogues reveals that 78.8% of interactions show low epistemic engagement (non-mastery aims, weak strategies), while only 11.1% demonstrate high epistemic engagement with mastery-oriented aims and justification strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to reframe AI literacy not as static knowledge but as a process-oriented epistemic phenomenon emerging through dynamic human-AI interactions, addressing the gap in understanding how students epistemically engage with GenAI in programming contexts.

Method: The study applies the AIR (epistemic Aims, Ideals, and Reliable epistemic processes) framework to analyze a large dialogue dataset of human-AI co-programming, identifying observable dimensions of epistemic aims (e.g., mastery-oriented) and epistemic processes (outsourcing, explanation seeking, verification seeking, prompt monitoring, epistemic justification).

Result: 78.8% of student-GenAI interactions relied on non-mastery-oriented aims and less reliable strategies like outsourcing and verification-seeking. Only 11.1% of interactions showed high epistemic engagement with mastery-oriented aims and advanced strategies like epistemic justification.

Conclusion: Students generally lack Epistemic AI Literacy, suggesting the need for educational interventions that foster deeper epistemic engagement and more reliable epistemic processes when using GenAI for programming.

Abstract: Epistemic thinking plays a central role in students' learning processes when applying generative artificial intelligence (GenAI), particularly in programming contexts where learners must construct queries, evaluate and validate AI-generated outputs, and regulate problem-solving strategies. This study introduces the conceptual framework of Epistemic AI Literacy (EAIL), reframing AI literacy as a process-oriented epistemic phenomenon that emerges through dynamic human-AI interactions across different domains. Drawing on the AIR (epistemic aims, ideals and reliable epistemic processes) framework, this study examines how epistemic aims and epistemic processes are enacted in GenAI-supported co-programming activities and explores scalable approaches for operationalizing these constructs in interaction data. Using a large dialogue dataset of human-AI co-programming, this study identifies observable dimensions of epistemic aims (i.e., mastery-oriented aims) and epistemic processes (i.e., outsourcing, explanation seeking, verification seeking, prompt monitoring, and epistemic justification). The results reveal a prevalent lack of EAIL, with 78.8% of student-GenAI interactions relying on non-mastery-oriented aims and less reliable epistemic strategies like outsourcing and verification-seeking. Conversely, only 11.1% of interactions showed high epistemic engagement, where mastery-oriented aims were coupled with advanced epistemic strategies like epistemic justification in a more reliable epistemic process.

</details>


### [313] [From Signals to Structure: How Memory Architecture Drives Language Emergence in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00233)
*Yashar Talebirad,Eden Redman,Ali Parsaee,Osmar R. Zaiane*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies how LLM agents develop shared language in Lewis signaling games, finding that memory architecture (especially a persistent notebook) matters more than channel capacity for reliable coordination, and that surplus capacity helps while stateless agents collapse under high capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how agents invent shared language from scratch using only interaction history, and to determine which factors (memory architecture vs. channel capacity) drive successful coordination.

Method: Five memory architectures (including persistent notebook and stateless) are tested across varying channel configurations with LLM agents in a Lewis signaling game framework.

Result: Persistent notebook architecture achieves best coordination (0.867 ± 0.023 at capacity 25). Stateless agents peak at moderate capacity then degrade. The information bottleneck (capacity=8) is a fragility point, not optimal; surplus capacity is generally better.

Conclusion: Channel capacity alone cannot predict coordination; memory architecture determines whether agents turn interaction history into stable conventions. Both dimensions are needed to understand how signals become language.

Abstract: How do two agents invent a shared language from scratch? In a Lewis signaling game, a sender and receiver must coordinate on a code using only their interaction history. We study five memory architectures across varying channel configurations with LLM agents and find that memory architecture matters more than channel capacity. Agents with a persistent private notebook benefit from surplus channel capacity and avoid the high-capacity collapse seen in stateless agents, achieving the most reliable coordination ($0.867 \pm 0.023$ at capacity = 25). Stateless agents peak at moderate capacity and then degrade as the vocabulary grows beyond what a rolling context window can track The notebook externalizes learned conventions, freeing agents from having to re-derive codes each round. An information bottleneck-inspired argument predicts an optimal capacity equal to the number of objects. Instead, the bottleneck (capacity = 8) proves to be a fragility point, and surplus capacity is generally better. We show that channel capacity alone cannot predict coordination; memory architecture determines whether agents turn interaction history into stable conventions, and both dimensions are needed to understand how signals become language.

</details>


### [314] [Seed2.0 Model Card: Towards Intelligence Frontier for Real-World Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00248)
*Bytedance Seed*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Seed2.0 is a model series designed to solve complex real-world tasks by addressing long-tail knowledge and complex instruction following, with enhanced reasoning, visual understanding, and search capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve complex, real-world tasks by identifying users' genuine needs and building a reliable evaluation system grounded in realistic scenarios.

Method: Construction of a forward-looking evaluation system based on selected benchmarks from real user needs, targeting long-tail knowledge and complex instruction following challenges.

Result: Seed2.0 achieves world-leading reasoning intelligence, visual understanding, and search capabilities, demonstrating ability to handle initial complex real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Seed2.0 begins to exhibit capability for complex real-world tasks, delivering value to hundreds of millions of users.

Abstract: We present Seed2.0, a model series that takes a meaningful step toward solving complex, real-world tasks. Our approach begins with identifying users' genuine needs and constructing a reliable, forward-looking evaluation system by selecting and abstracting benchmarks grounded in these needs and in realistic, complex scenarios. Guided by this evaluation system, Seed2.0 targets two persistent challenges, long-tail knowledge and complex instruction following, substantially improving the model's reliability on intricate, long-horizon tasks. Beyond these, Seed2.0 delivers world-leading reasoning intelligence, visual understanding, and search capabilities that address the most common needs of a broad user base. Through extensive real-world use cases documented in this model card, we demonstrate that Seed2.0 begins to exhibit the ability to handle initial complex real-world tasks, delivering greater value to hundreds of millions of users.

</details>


### [315] [Mnemosyne: Agentic Transaction Processing for Validating and Repairing AI-generated Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00269)
*Edward Y. Chang,Longling Geng,Emily J. Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Agentic Transaction Processing (ATP), a transaction model treating AI-generated actions as untrusted proposals until verified against constraint sets, implemented in Mnemosyne runtime with low overhead and bounded repair guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generated actions from LLMs, solvers, and agent teams can be syntactically valid yet semantically problematic (stale, infeasible, conflicting, or destructive). There is a need to separate proposal generation from commitment to ensure correctness.

Method: ATP treats generated actions as untrusted proposals that must pass deterministic admission under a declared constraint set C. Mnemosyne runtime implements append-only transition logs, effective-state projection, dependency-safe compensation, and active commitment records with LCRP protocol for localized repair.

Result: Nine falsification tests passed against targeted violations; under 6% projection-and-validation overhead; bounded local repair edits an order of magnitude fewer operations than global recompute.

Conclusion: Committed-state correctness can be made independent of the competence of the proposing layer. The ATP model provides safety properties (authority separation, serial-equivalent generative admission, evidence-preserving repair, obligation containment) with bounded reactive repair guarantees.

Abstract: LLMs, solvers, and agent teams increasingly generate workflow actions, repairs, and plans, but a generated action may be syntactically valid yet stale, infeasible, conflicting, or destructive of the evidence that triggered a repair. We introduce Agentic Transaction Processing (ATP), a transaction model that treats generated actions as untrusted proposals until they pass deterministic admission under a declared, executable constraint set C. The principle is two-sided: a proposal is not truth, and no proposal foresees every disruption: anything may propose, but only the runtime admits and commits, and when an unforeseen disruption strikes it repairs reactively within bounds rather than trusting a fresh proposal. Relative to C, committed-state correctness becomes independent of the competence, honesty, or learning of the proposing layer. We realize ATP in Mnemosyne, a runtime with an append-only transition log, effective-state projection, dependency-safe compensation, and active commitment records, and prove four safety properties relative to C (authority separation, serial-equivalent generative admission, evidence-preserving repair, and obligation containment) together with a bounded-reactive-repair guarantee for its localized repair protocol (LCRP). A reproducible artifact rejects the targeted violations across nine falsification tests while still admitting valid work, at under 6% projection-and-validation overhead, and bounded local repair edits an order of magnitude fewer operations than global recompute. Mnemosyne is open source: https://github.com/eyuchang/Mnemosyne/tree/arxiv-atp-rq1-rq9b-r8-v2.

</details>


### [316] [Managed Autonomy at Runtime: Gear-Based Safety and Governance for Single- and Multi-Agent Cyber-Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00334)
*Srini Ramaswamy,Wang Miaosheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes \system{}, a discrete-time control system with five execution gears for safe autonomous agents, proving stability and safety guarantees. It applies to multi-agent CPS using \smart{} governance, achieving 99.6% anomaly detection in a 3-agent UR5 assembly cell across Monte Carlo simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous agents (LLM-driven or robotic) suffer from safety violations, behavioral instability, and continuity loss when operating without continuous human oversight.

Method: \system{} combines five execution gears (\Gobs{}, \Gsug{}, \Gplan{}, \Gexec{}, \Gint{}) with utility-gated dispatch and event-driven fallback. Uses \smart{} managed-autonomy lifecycle with four governance states. Applies Lyapunov analysis, consensus gating, and rendezvous control for multi-agent safety.

Result: 99.6% anomaly detection rate (vs 2.1% baseline), 3.5× reduced detection latency, formal physical-workspace safety certificate, proven zero collision under stated assumptions.

Conclusion: The execution gears act as micro-level permissions beneath \smart{} runtime governance, separating action control from autonomy governance, enabling provably safe autonomous operation.

Abstract: Autonomous agents, whether LLM-driven software agents or robotic physical agents, face a common class of failure modes when operating without continuous human oversight: safety violations from unverified actions, behavioral instability from unconstrained loops, and continuity loss from unhandled error states. We develop \system{}, a discrete-time control system that combines five execution gears (\Gobs{}, \Gsug{}, \Gplan{}, \Gexec{}, \Gint{}) with utility-gated dispatch and event-driven fallback. For the single-agent case, we prove monotonic stability, execution safety, eventual stabilization, fallback completeness, and equivalence to a gear-constrained Markov decision process. For multi-agent cyber-physical systems (CPS), we apply the established \smart{} managed-autonomy lifecycle and map runtime evidence into its four governance states (\Stable{}/\Meta{}/\Assisted{}/\Regulated{}). Consensus gating, swarm-level Lyapunov analysis, per-agent gear authority, and rendezvous control provide distributed safety and stability guarantees, including zero collision under the stated assumptions. We evaluate the resulting runtime on a three-agent UR5 robotic assembly cell using fault magnitudes calibrated from the NIST \emph{Degradation Measurement of Robot Arm Position Accuracy} dataset across 10,000 Monte Carlo episodes. It achieves a 99.6\% anomaly detection rate versus 2.1\% for the single-agent baseline, reduces detection latency by $3.5\times$, and supplies a formal physical-workspace safety certificate. The execution gears act as micro-level permissions beneath the \smart{} runtime governance states, separating action control from autonomy governance.

</details>


### [317] [Personalization as Inverse Planning: Learning Latent Design Intents for Agentic Slide Generation via Structural Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00407)
*Tianci Liu,Zihan Dong,Linjun Zhang,Haoyu Wang,jing Gao,Emre Kiciman,Ranveer Chandra,Wei-Ting Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPIRE solves Page-level Slide Personalization (PSP) by formulating it as inverse planning and using multi-agent RL with structural denoising to learn design intents without assuming knowledge of specific slide authoring tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI agents fail at fine-grained page-level slide personalization because they rely on prespecified templates or verbose user instructions, failing to capture latent design intents.

Method: Formulates PSP as inverse planning; proposes SPIRE framework that corrupts clean slide visual structures and uses two agents to collaboratively denoise via reinforcement learning.

Result: Proof that structural denoising is a consistent surrogate for PSP; multi-agent formulation reduces policy gradient variance in RL; extensive experiments show superiority.

Conclusion: SPIRE effectively addresses page-level slide personalization without requiring knowledge of specific tools, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Slide design requires personalizing both deck themes and page layouts. Yet, current AI agent-based methods struggle with fine-grained, page-level design. Solely relying on prespecified templates or user verbose instructions, they fail to capture latent design intents, leaving Page-level Slide Personalization (PSP) unresolved. To close this gap, this work formulates PSP as an inverse planning problem. We propose to learn a design intent without assuming any knowledge of the specific executing tools (e.g., PowerPoint, Beamer) being used. However, relinquishing control over these tools makes the problem intractable to optimize end-to-end. To overcome this, we propose SPIRE, a principled framework to solve PSP approximately. By intentionally corrupting the visual structures of clean slides, SPIRE creates a verifiable task to denoise the corruption, whereby two agents learn to collaboratively refine executable designs via reinforcement learning (RL). We present a proof that structural denoising is a consistent surrogate for PSP, and that the multi-agent formulation strictly reduces policy gradient variance in RL. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of SPIRE.

</details>


### [318] [PHREEQC-MCQ-200: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Tool-Augmented Scientific Simulator Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00436)
*Ke Zhang,Sahchit Chundur,Mohammad Javad Qomi,Maziar Raissi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PHREEQC-MCQ-200 evaluates tool-augmented LLM agents on geochemistry simulations. Simulator access improves accuracy but causes regressions; output-access protocols impact results; the paper argues for richer evaluation beyond average accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: It is unclear when tool access makes scientific computation more reliable rather than merely more complex, motivating a diagnostic benchmark for tool-augmented scientific agents.

Method: Introduced PHREEQC-MCQ-200, a 200-question multiple-choice benchmark derived from 21 validated PHREEQC scenarios, requiring agents to build inputs, execute the simulator, parse outputs, and answer questions.

Result: Simulator access improved overall accuracy, but also caused regressions (items answered correctly without tools). A table-of-contents interface reduced token costs and helped stronger models but hurt mid-tier models.

Conclusion: Scientific tool use should be evaluated as an end-to-end diagnostic problem. Evaluations should report accuracy, item-level retention, output-access sensitivity, trajectory failures, and where the computation chain breaks.

Abstract: Large language model agents are increasingly connected to scientific software, yet it remains unclear when tool access makes scientific computation more reliable rather than merely more complex. We introduce PHREEQC-MCQ-200, a benchmark for evaluating tool-augmented agents on deterministic aqueous-geochemistry simulations. The benchmark contains 200 multiple-choice questions derived from 21 validated PHREEQC scenarios, requiring agents to construct simulator inputs, execute PHREEQC, inspect structured outputs, and commit to final answers.
  Across multiple frontier and mid-tier model families, simulator access substantially improves aggregate accuracy, confirming that grounded execution is necessary for many scientific-computation tasks. However, the gains are not monotonic: tool-augmented agents also lose items they answered correctly without tools, revealing regressions that average accuracy alone hides. We further show that output-access protocol matters. A table-of-contents interface can reduce token cost while preserving or improving accuracy for stronger models, but it degrades performance for mid-tier models that cannot reliably navigate structured simulator outputs.
  PHREEQC-MCQ-200 therefore frames scientific tool use as an end-to-end diagnostic problem rather than a simple tool-calling capability. We argue that evaluations of scientific agents should report not only accuracy, but also item-level retention, output-access sensitivity, trajectory failures, and where the computation chain breaks.

</details>


### [319] [Agri-SAGE: Simulation-Grounded Multi-Agent LLM for Context-Aware Agricultural Advisory Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00454)
*Vedant Balasubramaniam,Geetha Charan,Manojkumar Patil,Rohit P Suresh,V Priyanka,Kodur Sai Vinay Sathvik,Y. Narahari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agri-SAGE is a closed-loop framework that combines multi-agent LLM reasoning with APSIM biophysical simulation to generate and validate agronomic advisories, overcoming limitations of both static guidelines and pure LLM-based systems. Three reasoning approaches (Plan-and-Solve, Tree of Thoughts, Reflexion) outperform static baselines, with Tree of Thoughts achieving peak yields and Reflexion offering comparable results at lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agricultural advisory systems face a tension between static agronomic guidelines (consistent but blind to in-season variability) and LLM-powered systems (agronomically credible but physiologically unconvincing). The authors aim to resolve both limitations.

Method: Agri-SAGE integrates retrieval-grounded multi-agent LLM reasoning with APSIM-based biophysical simulation in a closed-loop framework. Three reasoning approaches were evaluated: Plan-and-Solve, Tree of Thoughts, and Reflexion.

Result: All three reasoning approaches significantly outperformed static PoP baselines. Tree of Thoughts achieved impressive peak yields. Reflexion achieved comparable agronomic outcomes with substantially lower computational cost via cross-seasonal episodic memory.

Conclusion: The closed-loop framework effectively resolves the tension between static guidelines and pure LLM-based systems. Reflexion offers the best efficiency-effectiveness trade-off for practical deployment.

Abstract: Agricultural advisory systems face a fundamental tension: static agronomic guidelines offer consistent, evidence-based recommendations, yet remain blind to in-season variability and dynamic uncertainties. Recent advisory systems powered by LLMs are liable for a different risk of generating recommendations that are agronomically credible but physiologically unconvincing. Agri-SAGE is a closed-loop framework designed to resolve the above two limitations by integrating retrieval-grounded multi-agent LLM reasoning with APSIM-based biophysical simulation, to generate and validate agronomic advisories. To assess this framework, we evaluate three reasoning approaches, namely Plan-and-Solve, Tree of Thoughts, and Reflexion, over a 10-year retrospective analysis. All three significantly outperform static PoP (Package-of-Practice) baselines, with Tree of Thoughts achieving impressive peak yields. At the same time, Reflexion achieves comparable agronomic outcomes at substantially lower computational cost by leveraging cross-seasonal episodic memory.

</details>


### [320] [Multi-scale Mixture of World Models for Embodied Agents in Evolving Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00457)
*Jinwoo Jang,Daniel J. Rho,Sihyung Yoon,Hyunsuk Cho,Honguk Woo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MuSix introduces scale-aware world model mixture and evolution for embodied agents, using two-stage routing based on experiential distance and scale-dependent forgetting rates to improve multi-scale reasoning and adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied agents in real-world settings need multi-scale reasoning and adaptive knowledge, but MoE approaches lack explicit scale routing and uniform update policies fail to handle different rates of knowledge obsolescence across scales.

Method: A two-stage routing mechanism: meta-router maps experiential distance (inspired by Construal Level Theory) to a continuous scale weight, then per-scale base routers select world models. Adaptation uses scale-dependent forgetting rates and gated inter-scale transfer for coherence.

Result: MuSix outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on EmbodiedBench and HAZARD benchmarks for multi-scale reasoning and dynamic adaptation.

Conclusion: Scale-aware routing and adaptive forgetting rates effectively address multi-scale reasoning and knowledge evolution challenges in embodied agent settings.

Abstract: Embodied agents operating in the real world require multi-scale reasoning and knowledge adaptation as conditions change. We identify two challenges in applying Mixture of Experts (MoE) to this setting: routing lacks an explicit notion of scale, preventing targeted updates at specific scales, and a uniform update policy cannot accommodate the different rates at which knowledge at each scale becomes outdated. We present MuSix, a framework that addresses both challenges through scale-aware world model mixture and evolution. A two-stage routing mechanism grounds scale selection in experiential distance, a measure of situational novelty inspired by Construal Level Theory: a meta-router first maps this quantity to a weight over continuous scale space, then per-scale base routers select world models within the identified scale. For adaptation, scale-dependent forgetting rates allow low-scale knowledge to refresh rapidly while high-scale abstractions persist, and gated inter-scale transfer maintains coherence across the hierarchy. Experiments on EmbodiedBench and HAZARD show that MuSix improves over state-of-the-art baselines on multi-scale reasoning and dynamic adaptation.

</details>


### [321] [AI Native Games: A Survey and Roadmap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00527)
*Zhiyue Xu,Fandi Meng,Kaijie Xu,Clark Verbrugge,Simon Lucas,Jian Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper defines AI-native games by whether runtime generative AI is constitutive of the core gameplay loop, proposes a G/N taxonomy to classify them, analyzes 53 such games, and argues the key design challenge is organizing semantic openness into stable gameplay through mechanical invariants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a rigorous definition of "AI-native games" (distinct from AI-augmented games, chatbots, or procedural content generation) and to provide a taxonomy for analyzing how generative AI becomes indispensable to core gameplay.

Method: Proposes a counterfactual criterion (if AI were removed, play collapses) to define AI-native games. Introduces a dual-axis G/N taxonomy: G-axis for player-facing game type, N-axis for dominant AI mechanic. Screens and analyzes 53 publicly available AI-native games and prototypes.

Result: The corpus is concentrated on language-forward designs (narrative adventure, epistemic interaction, generative narrative). Less represented categories include semantic adjudication, multi-agent simulation, generative construction, and relationship/companion play.

Conclusion: Central design problem is organizing semantic openness into stable gameplay via mechanical invariants (goals, rules, state, feedback, pacing, agency). Provides a roadmap for controllable generation, AI-as-mechanic design, multimodal/multi-agent systems, inference economics, evaluation, safety, and regulation.

Abstract: Generative AI now enables games to produce dialogue, quests, characters, images, and worlds at runtime. Yet generation alone does not make a game AI-native, nor does it guarantee playability. This paper defines AI-native games by whether runtime generative AI is constitutive of the core loop: if the AI component were removed or trivially replaced, the central form of play would collapse or become fundamentally different. This counterfactual criterion separates AI-native games from AI-augmented games, boundary artifacts, chatbots, tavern-style role-play, procedural content generation, and AI-assisted production. Using this definition, we screen candidate artifacts and analyze 53 publicly available AI-native games and prototypes. We introduce a dual-axis G/N taxonomy: the G-axis captures player-facing game type, while the N-axis captures the dominant AI mechanic that makes generative AI indispensable to play. The corpus is concentrated around language-forward designs, especially narrative adventure, epistemic interaction, and generative narrative, while categories such as semantic adjudication, multi-agent simulation, generative construction, and relationship/companion play remain less represented. We argue that the central design problem is organizing semantic openness into stable gameplay. AI-native design depends on mechanical invariants: goals, rules, state, feedback, pacing, and player agency that make open-ended AI outputs interpretable and consequential. We conclude with a roadmap for controllable generation, AI-as-mechanic design, multimodal and multi-agent systems, inference economics, evaluation, safety, and regulation.

</details>


### [322] [HARC: Coupling Harmfulness and Refusal Directions for Robust Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00572)
*Shei Pern Chua,Fangzhao Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper identifies that jailbreaks succeed by suppressing refusal or harmfulness directions in LLM residual streams before token generation. The authors introduce HARC (Harmfulness-And-Refusal Coupling), a fine-tuning method that pairs these directions across prompt and response positions, achieving strong safety without degrading general capability or causing over-refusal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how aligned LLMs internally represent safety in order to diagnose why jailbreaks succeed and to design more robust alignment strategies.

Method: The authors analyze harmfulness and refusal directions in the residual stream at both prompt-side and response-side token positions. They introduce HARC, a fine-tuning method that couples these two directions across both positions to enhance safety.

Result: Jailbreaks suppress refusal/harmfulness directions; models recognize harmful content during generation even if missed at prompt. HARC achieves the best robustness-capability-usability trade-off across six baselines, and the directions transfer across five model families and two scales.

Conclusion: Coupling harmfulness and refusal directions across prompt and response positions provides an effective safety mechanism that does not degrade general capability or cause over-refusal, and generalizes across model architectures.

Abstract: Understanding how aligned LLMs internally represent safety is critical for diagnosing alignment vulnerabilities, as it explains why jailbreaks succeed and informs the design of robust alignment strategies. Prior work shows that aligned LLMs encode harmfulness and refusal as separable directions in the residual stream at prompt-side token positions. We show that jailbreaks succeed at prompt encoding by suppressing either the refusal or harmfulness direction before any token is generated, with distinct attack classes occupying separable regions of the harmfulness-refusal plane. Extending the analysis to response-token positions, we find that the model recognizes harmful content while it is generating that content, even when it failed to recognize the input as harmful at the prompt side. Motivated by our findings, we introduce HARC (Harmfulness-And-Refusal Coupling), a fine-tuning method that pairs the two directions across both prompt and response positions. Since the intervention is confined to the harmfulness-refusal subspace, it leaves the rest of the residual stream intact and does not degrade general capability or inflate over-refusal. Across extensive experiments, HARC achieves the strongest robustness-capability-usability trade-off among six baselines spanning the major training-time and inference-time safety methods. The harmfulness and refusal directions at prompt and response positions transfer across the five model families and two scales we tested without architecture-specific tuning.

</details>


### [323] [AGI Maze as a Benchmark Framework for World-Modeling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00627)
*Alexey Potapov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces AGI Maze, a framework for testing LLMs on tasks requiring world state representation and memory, finding that vanilla LLMs fail to solve even simple mazes without persistent internal world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs' next-token prediction mode lacks persistent, manipulable representations of external world states, especially in partially observable, memory-dependent environments.

Method: AGI Maze framework: grid-based maze tasks with a clean API and multiple difficulty levels, evaluating vanilla LLMs and a baseline agent using message history as working memory.

Result: Vanilla LLMs fail to represent mazes internally at inference time. Using message history as working memory improves performance but remains insufficient for reliably solving small mazes within generous step budgets.

Conclusion: Current LLMs lack robust world state representation capabilities; constructing persistent internal models remains a significant challenge.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are powerful pattern-completion systems, but their default operating mode - predicting the next token from a static context - does not reliably produce persistent, manipulable representations of an external world. Many tasks that look like "reasoning" in text become substantially harder once the environment is partially observable, stateful, and requires memory and structured hypotheses about hidden state. AGI Maze is a lightweight framework for building such environments without requiring high-dimensional sensory inputs. It provides a family of grid-based maze tasks with a clean API and multiple difficulty regimes. The goal is to create benchmarks where agents must learn and use world state representations, not just infer a local rule over readily provided observations. We provide an initial evaluation of several vanilla LLMs on simple mazes showing that they fail to represent mazes internally at LLM inference time. We also introduce a baseline agent, which is allowed to use its message history as a working memory to construct descriptions of observations at agentic runtime. Although this can improve performance, it is still insufficient for an LLM agent to reliably solve even small mazes within a step budget that is more than enough for humans.

</details>


### [324] [Coachable agents for interactive gameplay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00642)
*Roberto Capobianco,Harm van Seijen,Nolan D. Bard,Neil Burch,Fatima Davelouis,Josh Davidson,Alisa Devlic,Yunshu Du,Ishan Durugkar,Siddhant Gangapurwala,Daniel Hernandez,G. Zacharias Holland,Sahil Jain,Kenta Kawamoto,Raksha Kumaraswamy,Patrick MacAlpine,Dustin R. Morrill,Declan Oller,Francesco Riccio,Akanksha Saran,Craig Sherstan,Kaushik Subramanian,Thomas J. Walsh,Samuel Barrett,Kizza N. Frisbee,Mady Govil,Johannes Günther,Varun R. Kompella,James A. MacGlashan,Maxwell Svetlik,Michael D. Thomure,Jaden B. Travnik,Kevin Waugh,Elahe Aghapour,Florian Fuchs,Andreanne Lemay,Shruti Mishra,Takuma Seno,Peter Stone,Michael Spranger,Peter R. Wurman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a UVFA-based reinforcement learning framework that allows end users to control agent behavior styles in real time, demonstrated successfully across diverse domains including gaming and robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard reinforcement learning typically learns only one near-optimal behavior per task, but many applications need real-time control over how a task is solved (referred to as "styles").

Method: The framework combines universal value function approximators (UVFAs) with carefully selected training scenarios, learning algorithms, and data augmentation to allow agents to exhibit controllable styles.

Result: The approach was successfully applied in AAA video games (Horizon Forbidden West, Gran Turismo) and an open-source humanoid test domain, showing strong coherence to style requests while satisfying main task objectives across car racing, combat, and walking tasks.

Conclusion: The presented framework enables real-time, flexible control over agent behavior styles while maintaining core task performance. It successfully demonstrates style coherence across diverse domains including Horizon Forbidden West, Gran Turismo, and humanoid walking tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has proven to be a valuable tool in the creation of advanced AI and robotic systems, contributing to everything from game playing to robotics to foundation models. Through trial-and-error, these AI systems typically learn one, near-optimal behavior to solve their tasks. However, there are many use cases in which one would like to assert some level of control, preferably in real time, over how the task is solved. We refer to these modifications of a core task as styles. We combine universal value function approximators (UVFAs) with carefully selected training scenarios, learning algorithms, and data augmentation to create a framework for coaching agents that exhibit styles in complex domains. We demonstrate the framework's application in the AAA video games Horizon Forbidden West and Gran Turismo, and in an open-source humanoid test domain. Despite the different nature of the domains -- car racing, stylized game combat, and humanoid walking -- each agent shows strong coherence to the style requests while still satisfying the main task in its domain. Importantly, the techniques outlined in this paper allow an end user to choose the final behavior at run time, giving them flexible control over the final executed performance.

</details>


### [325] [Self-GC: Self-Governing Context for Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00692)
*Xubin Hao,Hongjin Meng,Xin Yin,Jiawei Zhu,Chenpeng Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Self-GC introduces a self-governing context management system for long-horizon LLM agents that treats agent context as indexed, recoverable objects rather than disposable text, achieving 43.95% token pruning with 84.85% no-impact rate on hard tasks and 91-94% no-impact rates in production settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents accumulate structured context (tool results, files, plans, user constraints) that existing heuristic methods (chronological pruning, output masking, final self-summaries) handle poorly—heuristics are blind to future dependencies, and summaries lose exact evidence and editable artifacts.

Method: Self-GC converts user turns, tool spans, and skill states into indexed objects; uses a side-channel planner to propose fold, mask, and prune actions; and enforces recoverable sidecars, safe commit boundaries, and cache-aware commit for lifecycle governance of agent context.
